fields:
Epidemiology

  • GERD prevalence: affects {{c1::18%–27}}% in North America, {{c1::9%–25}}% in Europe, {{c1::2%–8}}% in East Asia, {{c1::9%–33}}% in the Middle East, 11% in Australia, 23% in South America.

Clinical Presentation

  • Typical symptoms: {{c1::heartburn, regurgitation}}
    • With {{c2::reflux esophagitis}}
    • Without {{c2::reflux esophagitis (non-erosive reflux disease)}}
  • Atypical symptoms: {{c3::noncardiac chest pain, hoarseness, chronic cough, asthma, pulmonary fibrosis, hiccups, dental erosion}}
  • Complications: {{c4::esophageal}} erosion and/or ulcer, {{c4::strictures}}, {{c4::Barrett’s}} esophagus, esophageal {{c4::adenocarcinoma}}

Diagnostic Approach

  • Symptom analysis; PPI (proton pump inhibitor) therapeutic trial; GI Endoscopy; Ambulatory pH monitoring, preferably Multichannel intraluminal impedance (MII)
  • Algorithm (Diagnosis):
    • Normal ⟶ [no further workup detailed]
    • Reflux symptoms ⟶{{c1::PPI trial}}
      • Persistent symptoms ⟶ {{c2::Endoscopy}}
        • {{c3::Esophagitis LA B/C/D, Long Segment Barrett’s, Eosinophilic [esophagitis]::What do suspect or from complications}}
          • pH monitoring; Manometry
          • Note: pH monitoring not required for diagnosis
      • If endocopy is normal ->
        • Ph monitoring using manometry to find
        • {{c4::Dysmotility; Esophageal acid exposure; Reflux–Symptom association::What do suspect or from complications}}
        • Then Assessment {{c5::Pre-ARS (Assessment Pre-ARS – Final evaluation before anti-reflux surgery.)}}
  • When to do endoscopy?
    1. Failure of {{c6::PPI}} therapeutic trial
    2. Patients with alarming features at presentation
    3. Patients previously endoscoped and known to have grade {{c7::C or D}} GERD (every 2 months)
    4. Patients >50 yrs with risk factors for {{c8::BE}} and/or {{c8::esophageal adenocarcinoma}}
    5. Chronic patients who report unusual disappearance of {{c9::heartburn}} on no treatment

Los Angeles (LA) Classification of Reflux Esophagitis

  • Grade A: {{c1::One (or more) mucosal break, ≤5 mm, that does not extend between the tops of two mucosal folds}}.

  • Grade B: {{c2::One (or more) mucosal break, >5 mm, that does not extend between the tops of two mucosal folds}}.

  • Grade C: {{c3::One (or more) mucosal break continuous between the tops of two or more mucosal folds, involving <75% of circumference}}.

  • Grade D: {{c4::One (or more) mucosal break that involves ≥75% of the esophageal circumference}}.
LA Grade {{c1::B}} is the most prevalent grade of esophagitis

Ambulatory pH Monitoring (including {{c2::Bravo™ capsule}}) (The gold standard)

  • Purposes:
    • {{c1::To assess acid exposure
    • To investigate symptom-event association}}

Multichannel Intraluminal Impedance (MII)

  • Spatial markers (figure labels): 3, 5, 7, 9, 15, 17 cm; esophageal vs gastric segments; distinguishes acid reflux vs non-acid reflux
  • What MII can determine (measurement of electrical impedance between closely spaced electrodes):
    1. {{c1::Acid or Non-acid content
    2. Direction of flow (antegrade or retrograde) & proximal extent
    3. Composition of intra-esophageal contents (liquid, gas or mixed)
    4. Total number of reflux events
    5. Increases number of reflux events available for symptom association}}

Impedence measures non acid reflux, eg bile -- Also impedence has different measurements for different things, ie solids have an impedence liquids acids etc

Bile is the worst type of reflux

{{c3::High-Resolution Manometry (HRM)}} for Hiatal Hernia

  • Cutoff: LES–CD separation = {{c1::1.85}} cm gives optimal identification of hiatal hernia
  • Performance: Sensitivity {{c2::92}}%, Specificity {{c2::95}}% for HH; exceeds endoscopy or radiography alone (both 73%)

This measures the muscle of the esophagus

Akalesia gets easily mixed up with GERD alot, Akalesia is tightness in the esophagus which makes it not digest properly
Manometry shows that the esophageal contracility is low, thus diagnosing

Treatment Approach

  • Step-up approach: initial {{c1::life-style changes, antacids, H2-receptor antagonists (e.g., ranitidine 150 mg × 2), and, finally, if symptoms persist, switch to PPI::What does it look like}}
  • Step-down approach: initiate with {{c2::PPI 20 mg or 40 mg}}, then step down maintenance to a regimen that effectively controls symptoms ({{c3::10 or 20}} mg)
  • Comparative outcome: {{c4::Step-down}} therapy was superior (71%) vs step-up (42%) of 92 patients in controlling symptoms initially and at 4 and 8 weeks follow-up.

Potassium-Competitive Acid Blockers (PCABs): A New Era in Acid Suppression

  • For many years, proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) have been the mainstay of acid suppression therapy. However, PCABs offer a distinct mechanism of action and potential advantages over PPIs.
  • Mechanism: {{c1::PCABs compete with potassium for binding sites on H+/K+ ATPase (reversible), directly inhibiting acid production}}.
  • Advantages of PCABs:
    • {{c2::Acid-stable: work regardless of stomach pH, unlike PPIs which require activation by acid.
    • May provide longer-lasting acid suppression.
    • Potential for fewer drug interactions}}.

Drugs Other Than PPI’s

  • Prokinetics: Domperidone, Itopride, etc.
    • {{c1::No concrete}} evidence of any effectiveness!!
  • (Slide accents): “we need to think out of the BOX” / “we need to think out of the PPI box” (emphasis in source)
  • {{c2::Baclofen}} (GABA agonist):
    • Zhang et al., 2003: 40% reduction of postprandial TLESR and reflux episodes after baclofen 40 mg orally.
    • Van-Herwaarden et al., 2003: similar reduction in TLESR and reflux episodes; 35%–46% reduction in esophageal acid exposure time.
    • Cange et al., 2003: significant reduction in ambulatory esophageal acid exposure after a single “stat” dose of baclofen 40 mg.

Maintenance Therapy

  • Long-term therapy: All GERD patients and nearly 2/3 of NERD patients will require {{c2::long-term treatment}}; there must be a trial to stop treatment; exceptions: {{c1::LA grade C & D}}, and patients with {{c1::Barrett’s esophagus (BE)}}.
  • For patients requiring long-term PPI therapy, use the {{c3::lowest}} effective dose.
  • While on-demand therapy (ODT) has been associated with higher patient satisfaction vs continuous therapy in GERD, a systematic review of 3 RCTs concluded {{c4::continuous therapy provided better symptom control, quality of life, and higher endoscopic remission rates}}.

Life style modifications (slides 32–35)

  • Lifestyle factors have {{c1::little}} influence on the pathogenesis of GERD
  • There is only {{c1::weak}} evidence that lifestyle factors aggravate GERD symptoms
    • Obesity: severity of esophagitis correlates with weight only when BMI {{c2::>30}} kg/m²
      • Contradictory studies into weight loss indicate no effect/improvement in GERD.
    • Smoking: lowers {{c3::LES (lower esophageal sphincter)}} pressure and the {{c3::acid-neutralising}} effect of saliva.
    • Physical activity: running might {{c4::provoke::provoke or inhibit}} GERD by increasing TLESRs.
      Reference(s): Kotee & Coste 2005
  • Some dietary factors may aggravate GERD symptoms
    • {{c5::Citrus fruits and juices
    • Carbonated drinks
    • Caffeine
    • Heavy meals
    • Fatty foods
    • Spicy foods | weak evidence
    • Alcohol}}
  • Medications may aggravate GERD symptoms
    • Impairment of LES function:
      • {{c6::beta-adrenergic agonists, theophylline, anticholinergics
      • tricyclic antidepressants, progesterone
      • alpha-adrenergic antagonists, diazepam, calcium channel blockers::What drugs can impair Lower esophageal sphincter function}}
    • Damage to the esophageal mucosa:
      • {{c7::acetylsalicylic acid and other NSAIDs
      • tetracycline, quinidine, bisphosphates::What drugs can damage the esophageal mucosa}}

Reflux–airway mechanisms (generated; slide 37)

  • Direct contact mechanism
    • {{c1::Micro-aspiration of refluxate}}
  • Vasovagal reflex mechanism
    • {{c2::Vagal afferent loop from esophageal chemoreceptors}}

1. Direct Contact Mechanism – Stomach contents reach the airway, irritating it.
  2. Micro-aspiration of Refluxate – Tiny amounts of refluxed material enter the lungs, causing inflammation.
  3. Vasovagal Reflex Mechanism – Acid in the esophagus stimulates vagal nerves, triggering airway constriction without actual aspiration.
  4. Vagal Afferent Loop – Esophageal chemoreceptors detect acid → send signals via the vagus nerve → cause reflex bronchospasm or cough.

Cardiac associations (AF) (slide 38)

  • {{c1::Post-prandial AF (atrial fibrillatoin)}} a previously noted phenomenon in case reports and series
    • {{c2::Atrial tachy-arrhythmias}} previously described in patients with hiatal hernia and GERD
    • Mechanisms
      • Direct
      • vagal

H. pylori (slide 39)

  • H. pylori eradication {{c1::does not::Does or does not}} increase the development of esophagitis or GERD symptoms

Barrett’s Oesophagus — definition (slide 40)

  • Metaplasia of the esophagus: Barrett’s esophagus
    • Definition: a {{c2::change in the esophageal epithelium of any length that can be recognised at endoscopy and is confirmed to have intestinal metaplasia by biopsy of the tubular esophagus and excludes intestinal metaplasia of the cardia}}.

Barrett’s Oesophagus — prevalence vs duration (slide 41)

  • The prevalence of Barrett’s esophagus {{c3::increases::Inc or dec}} with the duration of reflux symtpoms

Barrett’s Oesophagus — adenocarcinoma association (slide 42)

  • Barrett’s esophagus is associated with adenocarcinoma 
  • Patients with BE have increased risk of developing adenocarcinoma
  • Estimate of cancer risk is 40-125 fold of general population

Barrett screening 

  • {{c4::Chromoendoscopy}}

  • {{c4::Narrow Band}} Imaging

  • {{c4::Endo}}-microscopy

Barrett Imaging Limitations (“But”)

  • ‘Changes’ in up to {{c5::40}}% of asymptomatic subjects
  • Requires {{c6::expertise, small area, time consuming}}
  • Technologies not available widely & expensive
  • Poor inter/intra-observer agreement
IPCL patterns help grade {{c1::neoplastic}} risk under magnification/NBI

Peptic Ulcer Disease (PUD)

  • Definition:
    • Chronic {{c1::mucosal}} defects (+submucosa) as a result of acid and pepsin attack.
  • Sites: Mostly in stomach & duodenum
  • Lifetime risk: 5–10%
  • Anywhere exposed to the action of acid & pepsin secretion
    • 1- {{c2::First}} part of duodenum
    • 2- Stomach: {{c2::Lesser curvature & pyloric antrum}}.
    • 3- Lower esophagus: {{c3::reflux of acid from stomach}}
    • 4- {{c4::Meckel’s}} diverticulum: congenital remnant of vitello-intestinal duct containing heterotropic gastric mucosa
    • 5- Distal duodenum & jejunum in {{c5::Zollinger Ellison syndrome (hypersecretion of acids)}}
    • 6- {{c6::Gastro-jujenostomy}} stomal ulcer (dumping of acid & pepsin)

Pathogenesis

  • 1. {{c1::HYPER}}ACIDITY
    • H pylori
    • ZES
    • Chronic stress
  • 2. DECREASED {{c2::MUCOSAL DEFENCE MECHANISMS}}
    • Mucus-bacarbonate barrier (duodeno-gastric reflux)
    • Epithelial cell barrier:
      • NSADs
      • Corticosteroids
      • H pylori
      • Smoking
  • In peptic ulcer of stomach, breakdown of {{c3::mucosal defense}} is much more important than excessive acid production
  • In peptic ulcer of duodenum, {{c4::H pylori gastritis with high acid production}} is the main cause

Peptic Ulcer Disease — Gastric Ulcer (GU)

  • Gastric ulcer:
    • Can be malignant or benign gastric ulcers
  • Causes:
      1. H. pylori infection
      2. Delayed gastric emptying
      3. Defective mucosal barrier
      4. {{c1::Bile}} reflux into the stomach due to pyloric sphincter dysfunction which will cause damage to the gastric mucosa and so back diffusion takes place and {{c2::HCl}} is formed
      5. NSAIDs
  • Symptoms:
      1. Pain: provoked by {{c3::food intake}}, localized pain in the {{c4::epigastric}} region. (the main hallmark)
      2. Nausea and vomiting
  • Diagnosis:
      1. Endoscopy
      2. Biopsy
      3. Radiography (Barium meal)
  • Recurrence:
    • 40%–60% recur within 2 years from healing.
  • Additional note:
    • theres no point in getting a biopsy from a duodenal ulcer, cuz most duodenal ulcers are {{c5::benign}}, gastric ulcers can turn malignant

Peptic Ulcer Disease — Duodenal Ulcer (DU)

  • Duodenal Ulcer:

    • Almost always benign

    • Usually small ulcers no larger than {{c1::O.5}}, which penetrate to the muscles.

    • {{c2::Erosion}} is considered a superficial ulcer and it does not penetrate to the muscles.

  • Causes:

      1. Infection with {{c3::Helicobacter pylori}}

      1. Hyper secretion of {{c3::gastric acid}}

      1. Increased number of {{c3::parietal}} cells

      1. Increased sensitivity of parietal cells to {{c3::gastrin}}

      1. Reduced {{c3::gastrin}} inhibition

      1. Increased {{c3::gastric}} emptying

      1. Reduced {{c3::neutralizing}} capacity

    • gastric ulcers are provoked by food intake, duodenal ulcers are releived by food instake

  • Symptoms:

      1. Pain:

      • Usually in the upper abdomen and it radiates to the back.

      • It happens mainly on empty stomach and described as “burning”

      • It is relieved by {{c4::food intake}}

      1. Vomiting:

      • In {{c5::25}}% of the patients

  • Diagnosis:

      1. Endoscopy

      1. Radiography (barium meal)

      2. (no point in a biopsy)

  • Recurrence:

    • Very common in {{c6::95}}% of the patients

    • In 50% within 6–8 months after healing

Diagnosis of H. pylori

  • {{c3::H. pylori serum}} Ab
  • {{c1::Stool}} Ag (most common v v common v v specific)
  • {{c2::Urea breath}} test (Tests for enzymatic action)
  • Endoscopic tests
    • Rapid urease test
    • Histopathology

Complications of Peptic Ulcer Disease

  • Hemorrhage
    • It happens to 20% of the patients.
    • It results from the penetration of an ulcer to the blood vessels.
    • If it penetrates a small vein, the patient will detect it by {{c2::vomiting dark material (Haematemesis) or by tarry black stools (melena)}}.
    • If it penetrates an artery, {{c3::bleeding will be profuse (Haematemesis)}}.
    • Weakness and dizziness are common to those patients.
    • Immediate therapy is required.
  • Perforation:
    • More frequent in patients of {{c1::duodenal}} ulcer
    • when the ulcer perforates the wall to the abdominal cavity.
  • Obstruction (mostly in {{c1::gastric::Where}}):
    • Is defined as a situation where the gastric outlet is either completely or partially obstructed ({{c4::surgical emergency::Is it surgical emergency?}}).

Clinical Course and Additional Complications

  • CLINICAL COURSE
    • PUD may undergo spontaneous healing
    • Recurrent morbidity
    • Higher risk of recurrence if H. pylori present
    • Good rates of healing and prevention of recurrence with {{c1::antibiotic}} treatment
  • COMPLICATIONS
    • Bleeding {{c2::15–20% (25}}% of ulcer deaths)
    • Perforation {{c2::5% (66}}% of ulcer deaths)
    • Fibrosis, stricture, hourglass stomach
    • Obstruction 2%
    • Anaemia
    • Pain
    • Increased risk of malignancy

PUD Treatment — Overview

  • Medical
    • {{c1::PPI’s}}
    • {{c1::H2R}}antagonists
    • {{c1::Antacids}}
    • H pylori treatment
  • Endoscopic treatment
  • Surgical treatment

Who Should Be Tested & Treated for H. pylori (ACG 2017)

  • All patients with a positive test of active infection with H. pylori should be offered treatment, the critical issue is which patients should be tested for the infection (strong recommendation)
  • {{c1::Active peptic ulcer}} disease.
  • A history of {{c1::PUD}}.
  • {{c1::MALT}} lymphoma.
  • History of endoscopic resection of early gastric cancer.
  • Uninvestigated dyspepsia in patients under 55 years without alarm features.
  • Long-term low-dose aspirin use.
  • Patients initiating chronic treatment with NSAIDs.
  • Patients with unexplained iron deficiency anemia despite an appropriate evaluation.
  • Adults with {{c1::ITP}}.

Antibiotic Regimens (Titles/Overview)

  • {{c1::Clarithromycin}}-based (stopped using cause of resistance)
    • Triple therapy
    • Concomitant therapy
    • Hybrid therapy
    • Sequential therapy
  • {{c1::Levofloxacin}}-based(stopped using cause of resistance)
    • Levofloxacin triple therapy
    • Levofloxacin quadruple therapy - LOAD
    • Levofloxacin sequential therapy
  • {{c1::Bismuth quadruple}} therapy, {{c2::First}} line of current therapy
  • Salvage therapy
  • High dose dual therapy (HDDT)
• Just need to know the titles not everythig

Bleeding Peptic Ulcer Disease — Treatment

  • First step is to {{c1::properly resuscitate the patient}}.
  • Second step is endoscopic treatment:
    • 1- {{c2::Injection}}
    • 2- {{c2::Heater probe}}
    • 3- {{c2::Clipping}}
  • If all failed {{c3::surgery}} would be the answer

Gross Picture of PUD

  • Site:
    • 98% of PUD in {{c1::1st part duodenum}} (75%) or {{c1::stomach}} (25%)
    • Also {{c1::GOJ (GORD, Barretts)}}
    • Gastrojejunostomy (the stoma (marginal ulcers) )
    • {{c2::Upper GIT}} in Zollinger-Ellison syndrome
    • Ectopic gastric mucosa ({{c3::Meckel diverticulum}})
  • Morphology:
    • {{c4::90}}% Single
    • {{c4::Well}} demarcated
    • < {{c4::4}} cm, round or oval
    • {{c4::Clean base, punched out::What does it look like}}
    • Surrounding chronic gastritis

• Zollinger–Ellison (acid hypersecretion → multiple ulcers, unusual sites)
    • Meckel diverticulum with ectopic gastric mucosa (acid-producing patch)

ACUTE GASTRITIS

  • Acute inflammation of the gastric mucosa characterised by a {{c1::neutrophil}} infiltrate.
  • Clinically
    • {{c2::Dyspepsia, nausea and vomiting, epigastric pain}}. {{c3::Heartburn}} is not a typical or usual symptom of gastritis.
  • Pathophysiology/Progression
    • {{c4::Decreased protection to the mucosa/direct epithelial damage ⟶ Acid diffusion ⟶ Neutrophil infiltrate, oedema, congestion ⟶ Erosions & haemorrhage ⟶ Ischemia, necrosis, ulceration, perforation, shock}}
    • Mild ⟶ severe
    • Repair ⟶ Chronic gastritis

CHRONIC GASTRITIS

  • Sustained inflammatory response to gastric mucosal damage characterized by chronic inflammation (plasma cells) +/- acute component
  • Clinically
    • Symptoms are {{c1::absent or mild (without complications)}}
    • {{c1::Episodic}} pain
    • Nausea
    • +/- vomiting

Complications of Chronic Gastritis

  • Morbidity
  • {{c1::Pernicious}} anaemia/malabsorption
  • Erosions & bleeding, from the {{c1::blood thinning not erosion}}
  • {{c1::Peptic ulcer}} disease
  • Increased risk of tumours
    • Adenocarcinoma
    • Carcinoid (autoimmune)
    • MALT lymphoma (H. pylori)

Gastritis or Gastropathy?

  • ”itis” includes inflammatory cells
  • “opathy” pathology with {{c1::minimal}} inflammatory cells
    • Chemical or physical
    • E.g. PHG, NSAID

Classification of gastritis ({{c1::Sydney}} classification)

  • this is called the {{c1::sydney}} classification

  • part pathology (endocopic) and part histopathology

Bacterial gastritis / H. pylori. gastritis

  • The mucosa especially of the {{c1::antrum}} is erythematous and mamilated.
  • There is accentuation of the {{c2::areae gastricae}} pattern.
    {{c2::Areae gastricae = the fine, polygonal, raised areas (1–6 mm) seen on the gastric mucosal surface}}.
  • Duodenitis manifests as erythema and erosions.
  • {{c3::Varioliform (raised erosions) gastritis}} may also occur.

Treatment of gastritis

  • Treatment of the cause eg H. Pylori
  • {{c4::PPI, P-CABs, antibiotics}}, etc.
  • Sympotmatic treatment
  • Treatment of the complications

children.name: ICC-2 [111] Overview of the GIT Applied Anatomy

fields:
Introduction to structure

  • The alimentary tract extends from the {{c1::oral cavity}} till the {{c1::anus}}.
  • The GIT extends from the {{c1::stomach}} till the {{c1::anus}}.
  • The alimentary tract is the portal of entry of nutrients, organisms and various antigens to the body.
  • At the beginning (oral cavity, pharynx and oesophagus) and terminal end of the alimentary tract (rectosigmoid and anal canal) the structure–function is mainly directed towards propelling the contents in as well as out of the alimentary tract.

Oesophagus: topography, musculature, constrictions, and clinical points

Thoracic oesophagus

  • Running in the {{c1::superior and posterior}} mediastinum.
  • Important relations are {{c2::trachea and left main bronchus}}.
  • {{c4::Left pulmonary}} vessels; serious haemorrhage during {{c3::transhiatal oesophagectomy}}.
  • Heart: transoesophageal echo.
  • Oesophageal hiatus at level of {{c5::T10}} passing through the {{c6::right crus}} of the diaphragm (antireflux mechanisms).

Topography and constrictions of the oesophagus

  • The oesophagus is a muscular canal extending from pharynx to stomach.
  • 4 narrowed regions where objects may lodge/erode mucosa:
    • {{c7::a) the pharynx becomes continuous with the proximal oesophagus
    • b) the aortic arch crosses the oesophagus
    • c) the oesophagus is compressed by the left main bronchus
    • d) the oesophagus passes through the diaphragm (oesophageal hiatus)}}

Musculature of the oesophagus (labels)

    1. {{c8::Inferior pharyngeal constrictor}} muscle
    2. {{c9::Cricopharyngeal (muscle) part of inferior pharyngeal constrictor muscle}}
    3. {{c10::Circular muscle layer with sparse longitudinal fibers (which the diverticulum comes out of)}}
    4. {{c11::Lateral mass of longitudinal muscle}}
    5. Window cut in longitudinal muscle layer (revealing circular muscle layer)

Musculature of the oesophagus (comment)

  • Muscular coat organized into 2 planes: {{c12::external longitudinal fibers and internal circular fibers}}.
  • Transition from skeletal to smooth muscle as it descends from pharynx to stomach.
  • Clinical:
    • GERD may occur at the {{c13::lower::Lower or higher}} oesophagus and present as dyspepsia, heartburn, dysphagia (oesophageal symptoms), as well as bronchospasm, asthma, dental caries (extraoesophageal manifestations).

Surgical anatomy highlights

  • Points of physiological narrowing are sites of {{c14::caustic burns and possible strictures}}.
  • Options for oesophageal replacement:
    • a) {{c15::Stomach pull-up depending on pattern and rich blood supply of the stomach}}.
    • b) {{c15::Colon bypass transposed based on rich collaterals along the colon}}.

Abdominopelvic cavity and peritoneal relationships

  • The abdominopelvic cavity is a potential space containing a small amount of {{c1::serous lubricating}} fluid ⟶ viscera glide during peristalsis.
  • {{c2::Ascites}} = abnormal accumulation of serous fluid; causes vary.
    • In {{c3::liver cirrhosis}}, portal (sinusoidal) hypertension can contribute up to 10–20 L/day of weeping hepatic lymph ⟶ collects in peritoneal cavity ⟶ greatly distends the abdomen.
  • Abdominal viscera are either suspended in the peritoneal cavity by {{c4::mesenteries}} or positioned between {{c4::the cavity and the musculoskeletal wall}}.
  • Abdominal viscera include:
    • Gastrointestinal system (caudal end of oesophagus, stomach, small and large intestines, liver, pancreas, gallbladder)
    • Spleen
    • Urinary system (kidneys, ureters)
    • Suprarenal glands
    • Major neurovascular structures
  • Categories:
    • A. Intraperitoneal
    • B. Retroperitoneal
  • Three arteries arising from the front of the aorta, the {{c5::coeliac, superior and inferior mesenteric}} arteries, supply, respectively, the foregut, midgut, hindgut and their derivatives

Arterial supply overview by embryologic gut region

  • Three arteries from the anterior aorta supply gut regions and derivatives: coeliac, superior mesenteric, inferior mesenteric.
Region Extension Arterial blood supply Lymphatics Venous drainage
Foregut {{c1::Lower oesophagus, stomach till the middle of the second part of duodenum below the ampulla of Water}}
 Coeliac trunk divides to {{c2::left gastric, common hepatic, splenic arteries}} Lymphatics follow the arterial drainage back to the main trunks originating from the aorta To the {{c3::portal}} vein direct or through the {{c3::splenic vein; Portosystemic anastomosis}} at lower end of the oesophagus
Midgut {{c4::From the middle of the second part of the duodenum, rest of small intestine, right colon till the junction of the right two thirds of the transverse colon}}
 Superior mesenteric artery Lymphatics follow the arterial drainage back to the main trunks originating from the aorta {{c5::Superior mesenteric}} vein that forms the portal vein after joining the splenic vein
Hindgut From the {{c6::end of the midgut to the anus}} Inferior mesenteric artery Lymphatics follow the arterial drainage back to the main trunks originating from the aorta {{c7::Inferior mesenteric}} vein joins the {{c8::splenic}} vein. At the rectum anastomosis with tributaries of the {{c9::middle rectal vein}} to lateral pelvic wall and tributaries of {{c10::inferior rectal veins}} (portosystemic)—rectal varices
Eveything at the end goes to the Portal vein

Omenta, mesenteries, and ligaments

  • Numerous peritoneal folds connect organs to each other or to the abdominal wall.
  • Develop from dorsal and ventral mesenteries suspending the developing GIT in the embryonic coelomic cavity.
  • Some contain vessels and nerves; others maintain positioning.
  • The gut tube is suspended by {{c1::mesenteries}}.

Lesser and Greater omentum

  • Omenta: two layers of peritoneum from the stomach and first part of duodenum to other viscera:
    • Greater omentum (derived from dorsal mesentery)
    • Lesser omentum (derived from ventral mesentery)
  • Lesser omentum:
    • Extends from lesser curvature of stomach and first part of duodenum to inferior surface of liver.
    • Thin membrane, continuous with peritoneal coverings of anterior/posterior surfaces of stomach and first duodenum.
    • Divided into:
      • {{c2::Hepatogastric}} ligament (medial): between stomach and liver (exposure of the hiatus)
      • {{c2::Hepatoduodenal}} ligament (lateral): between duodenum and liver; free border contains common bile duct, hepatic artery, portal vein (Pringle’s manoeuvre).
  • Greater omentum clinical:
    • Migrates to sites of inflammation and forms adhesions ⟶ walls off infection; called the abdomen’s "policeman".
    • Often a site for {{c3::metastatic spread of}} cancer from multiple primaries.

Omental bursa (lesser sac) and foramen of Winslow

  • Continuity between greater and lesser sacs via {{c1::omental (epiploic)}} foramen.
  • Omental bursa lies posterior to stomach and anterior to {{c2::retroperitoneal pancreas}}.
  • Access to lesser sac via {{c3::epiploic foramen of Winslow}}.
  • {{c4::Hepatoduodenal}} ligament (part of lesser omentum) lies anterior to the foramen; contains {{c5::proper hepatic artery, common bile duct, portal vein}}.
  • Duodenum (2nd part) and pancreas are secondarily retroperitoneal.

Clinical correlations

  • Perforated posterior gastric ulcer ⟶ spill into {{c6::lesser}} sac; acidic juice may erode {{c7::pancreas}}.
  • Pancreatic cancer may invade {{c8::duodenum, stomach, spleen}} due to proximity.
  • Pancreas is close to {{c9::duodenum, stomach, spleen, left kidney/adrenal, aorta, IVC}} ⟶ trauma/cancer here impacts multiple structures.

Stomach: position, regions, pylorus, and attachments

  • Surface projection/position:
    • Most dilated GIT part; J-shaped.
    • Between abdominal oesophagus and small intestine.
    • Regions: {{c1::epigastric, umbilical, left hypochondrium::Which regions in the abdomen}}
  • Regions:
    • {{c2::Cardia}}: around oesophageal opening.
    • {{c2::Fundus}}: area above level of cardial orifice.
    • {{c2::Body}}: largest region.
    • {{c2::Pyloric part}}: pyloric antrum + pyloric canal (distal end).
  • Pylorus:
    • Marked by {{c3::pyloric}} constriction; thick {{c4::pyloric}} sphincter (circular muscle) surrounds pyloric orifice.
    • Orifice just right of midline at transpyloric plane ({{c5::lower border LI::Which level of the spine}}).
  • Other features:
    • Greater curvature: attachments—{{c6::gastrosplenic ligament and greater omentum}}.
    • Lesser curvature: attachment—{{c6::lesser omentum}}.
    • Cardial notch: {{c7::superior angle as oesophagus enters stomach::Where is it}}.
    • Bend on lesser curvature with {{c8::crow’s foot–like}} vascularity—landmark for beginning of {{c9::pylorus}}; on barium meal called incisura angularis.
  • Imaging:
    • CT axial positioning image.

    • Double contrast barium radiograph.

Small intestine: organization and distinguishing features

  • Includes duodenum (secondarily retroperitoneal), jejunum (mesenteric), ileum (mesenteric).
  • Jejunum ≈ proximal two-fifths; ileum ≈ distal three-fifths of mesenteric small bowel.
  • Jejunum vs Ileum differences:
    • Jejunum: {{c1::larger diameter; longer straight arteries (arteriae rectae) from arcades; less fat in mesentery; prominent circular folds (plicae circulares)}}.
    • Ileum: {{c1::more Peyer’s patches distally; arcades more complex; featureless appearance on contrast relative to jejunum}}.

Figure: Barium meal and follow-through (labels)

  • Demonstrates stomach and small intestine:
      1. {{c1::Stomach}}
      2. {{c1::Jejunum}}
      3. {{c1::Loops of ileum}}

Large intestine: features, function, and clinical note

  • Includes caecum (appendix), ascending, transverse, descending, sigmoid colon, rectum, anal canal.
  • Characteristic features:
    • {{c1::Omental (epiploic) appendices (fat sacs)
    • Taenia coli (3 longitudinal muscle bands)
    • Haustra}}
  • Function: {{c2::Dehydration and compaction of indigestible material; reabsorption of water/electrolytes; host defense roles}}.
  • Transverse and sigmoid colon are {{c3::intra}}peritoneal with {{c3::mesentery (sites of colostomies)}}.
  • Clinical: {{c4::Colorectal}} cancer is 2nd only to lung cancer in site-specific mortality and ~15% of cancer-related deaths in the United States.

Arterial supply: stomach, liver, and spleen (coeliac trunk)

  • Coeliac trunk supplies foregut derivatives and the spleen (mesodermal derivative).
  • Primary branches: left gastric, common hepatic, splenic.
  • Distribution: liver, gallbladder, pancreas (part), spleen, stomach, proximal duodenum.

Branch details

  1. {{c1::Left gastric}} artery:
    • Arises above pancreas; passes upwards and left to the oesophagus, then turns down into lesser omentum.
    • Anastomoses with {{c2::right}} gastric artery; supplies {{c3::lower oesophagus and stomach}}.
  2. Common hepatic artery:
    • Passes above {{c4::1st part of duodenum}} to enter lesser omentum; ascends in front {{c5::of portal vein}} within free edge.
    • Branches:
      • Right gastric artery: along {{c6::lesser curvature}} ⟶ anastomoses with {{c7::left gastric}}.
      • Gastroduodenal artery: descends behind {{c8::duodenum (clinical relevance is that this is the one that causes hematemsis)}}; divides into:
        • {{c9::Right gastroepiploic}} artery (greater curvature) ⟶ anastomoses with left gastroepiploic.
        • {{c10::Superior pancreaticoduodenal}} arteries: descend between duodenum and pancreas.
      • {{c11::Right and left}} hepatic arteries: ramify in liver; {{c11::right}} hepatic gives a small cystic artery to gallbladder.
    • Clinical: Variations important in {{c12::gallbladder}} surgery.
    • Clinically: {{c12::Gastroduodenal}} artery can be eroded by {{c13::posterior duodenal}} ulcer ⟶ significant haematemesis and melaena.
  3. Splenic artery:
    • Tortuous course along {{c14::upper border of pancreas}}; passes in {{c15::lienorenal}} ligament to splenic hilum; divides into many terminals.
    • Branches:
      • {{c16::Pancreatic vessels}} (several)
      • {{c16::Short gastric}} arteries (via {{c17::gastrosplenic}} ligament to fundus)
      • {{c18::Left gastroepiploic}} artery (in {{c19::gastrosplenic}} ligament to greater curvature, supplying stomach and greater omentum) ⟶ anastomoses with right gastroepiploic.

Since most of the organs have double blood supply, then that would lead to chronic intestinal ischemia

If patient is complaining of chronic intestinal ischemia then its proabably two arteries being narrowed

Innervation and referred pain (epigastric region and anterior abdominal wall)

  • Innervation of Stomach, Liver, Spleen (clinical):
    • {{c1::Epigastric}} region is clinically important; pain is common here.
    • Stomach, duodenum, spleen, pancreas, liver, gallbladder reside here or refer pain to this region and dermatomes {{c2::T5–T9 or T10}} spinal levels.
    • Thorough history and exam needed to localize epigastric pain.
  • Visceral referred pain:
    • Pain afferents from abdominal viscera follow thoracic and lumbar {{c3::splanchnic sympathetic}} nerves (T5–L2) to spinal cord.
    • Visceral pain may be perceived as somatic pain over corresponding dermatomes ⟶ referred pain.
    • Afferent neuron cell bodies in spinal ganglia of respective levels.
  • Clinical:
    • Most visceral pain relates to {{c4::inflammation, ischaemia, distention, compression}}.
    • Knowing body surface referral patterns is important.
    • Some visceral pain from stomach, gallbladder, spleen, liver is referred to both {{c5::anterior and posterior}} body walls.

Superior mesenteric artery (SMA): course and branches

  • Origin/course: Arises below the coeliac artery; descends behind pancreas body, anterior to uncinate process, crosses 3rd part of duodenum, enters root of mesentery.
  • Supplies: Small intestine; ends by dividing into ileocolic, middle, right colic arteries.
  • Accompanied within mesentery by veins, lymphatics, nerves.

Branches

  • {{c1::Inferior pancreaticoduodenal}} artery: between pancreas and duodenum ⟶ anastomoses with {{c6::superior pancreaticoduodenal}}.
  • {{c2::Right colic}} artery: descends right on posterior wall; divides into ascending and descending branches; supplies {{c7::ascending}} colon; anastomoses with {{c8::ileocolic and middle}} colic.
  • {{c3::Middle colic}} artery: passes upward on body of pancreas to mesocolon; supplies {{c9::right two-thirds}} of transverse colon.
  • {{c4::Jejunal and ileal}} arteries: 15–20 branches from left side within small bowel mesentery; tiers of arcades form.
  • {{c5::Ileocolic}}artery: descends right; divides to supply {{c10::caecum, appendix, lower ascending colon}}

Typical cross-sections:
  • Proximal jejunum (a) vs terminal ileum (b):
    • Mesenteric attachment wider in {{c1::jejunum}}.
    • Two leaves of vessels enter bowel wall; wall thicker in {{c1::jejunum}}.
    • Dilated jejunal loops can be used in lengthening operations in {{c2::short bowel}} syndrome due to this vascular pattern.

Non-traumatic pathologies of the GIT (overview)

  • Pathology categories:
    • Congenital
    • Inflammatory
    • Neoplastic
    • Acid–pepsin digestion
    • Functional
        1. {{c1::Oesophageal motility disorders
        2. Pseudo-obstruction}}
    • Vascular accidents:
        1. {{c2::Obstruction–strangulation of herniae
        2. Mesenteric vascular occlusion}}

Congenital lesions by site

  • Stomach: {{c1::Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis}}
  • Small intestine:
    • {{c1::Atresias
    • Malrotation
    • Meckel’s diverticulum (V common)}}
  • Large intestine:
    • {{c2::Hirschsprung
    • Imperforate anus}}

Inflammatory lesions in the GIT

  • Narrow outlet organ inflammation: Appendix, Diverticulum
  • Organ inflammation with a normal outlet
  • Diffuse: {{c2::Enterocolitis}}
  • Autoimmune:
    • {{c1::Ulcerative colitis (diffuse)
    • Crohn’s disease (patchy)}}
  • Specific etiologies:
      1. Physical & chemical trauma
      2. Specific infection: TB, Typhoid, Actinomycosis
      3. Autoimmune Crohn's disease

Neoplastic lesions of the GIT

  • Commonest: {{c1::Adenocarcinoma}}
  • Neuroendocrine tumours (carcinoids)
  • Lymphoma
  • Gastrointestinal stromal tumours (GIST)

Sites where acid–pepsin digestion could occur

  • {{c1::Lower oesophagus}} due to reflux
  • Gastric ulcer
  • Duodenal ulcer
  • {{c2::Stomal}} ulcer after gastrojejunostomy
  • {{c3::Ectopic gastric}} mucosa in Meckel’s diverticulum
  • Anywhere in small intestine in {{c4::Zollinger–Allison}} syndrome

Inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) — branches, marginal artery; SMA–IMA watersheds

  • The inferior mesenteric artery:
    • Arises behind the {{c1::duodenum}} and descends behind the peritoneum to the {{c2::left}}, continuing beyond the pelvic brim as the {{c3::superior rectal}} artery.
    • Branches:
      • {{c4::Left colic}} artery → ascends to the left to the left colic flexure; supplies transverse and descending colon; anastomoses with middle colic and sigmoid arteries.
      • {{c5::Sigmoid}} arteries → enter sigmoid mesocolon; supply sigmoid and lower descending colon.
      • {{c6::Superior rectal}} artery → continuation of IMA; descends in sigmoid mesocolon to back of rectum; supplies rectum and upper anal canal.
    • {{c7::Marginal}} scartery: The terminal arteries supplying the gut form a continuous anastomotic arcade along the mesenteric border of the colon.
  • Figures:
    • Superior and inferior mesenteric arterial supply of the large intestine (for clarity, small bowel removed and large colon lifted up).

    • Comments:
      • SMA supplies adult midgut derivatives (portion of pancreas, distal duodenum, all small intestine, appendix, ascending colon, most transverse colon).
      • IMA supplies hindgut derivatives ({{c8::distal transverse, descending and sigmoid colon, superior portion of rectum}}).
    • Clinical:
      • Anastomotic channels exist between {{c9::SMA and IMA}} branches; if flow from one region is compromised, collateral flow can assist.
  • Watershed points / ischemia:
    • Blood supply defines the three classic intraabdominal gut tube regions; determines watershed points:
      • {{c10::Griffith’s}}point: mid-to-distal transverse colon/splenic flexure ({{c12::SMA}}–{{c12::IMA}} meeting).
      • {{c11::Sudeck’s}} point: rectum ({{c13::IMA}} supply meets branches of the {{c13::internal iliac artery}}).
    • Bowel ischaemia in elderly may result from arterial disease and inadequate flow, threatening viability in the ‘{{c14::marginal’ area at the splenic flexure::Which area}}

Hepatic portal system — microanatomy, tributaries, portocaval anastomoses and shunts

  • Hepatic portal system:
    • Venous blood from digestive tract, pancreas, gallbladder, spleen enters inferior surface of liver through {{c1::large hepatic portal vein}}.
    • This vein ramifies like an artery to distribute blood to small endothelial-lined hepatic sinusoids.
    • After passing through sinusoids, blood collects in short hepatic veins, draining into the inferior vena cava just before it penetrates the diaphragm and enters the right atrium.
    • Normally, vascular beds drained by the portal system interconnect, through small veins, with beds drained by systemic vessels (ultimately to SVC/IVC).
  • Blockage / portal hypertension:
    • Blockage of the hepatic portal vein or of vascular channels in the liver can affect the pattern of venous return.
    • Vessels interconnecting portal and caval systems can enlarge and become tortuous, allowing portal tributaries to bypass the liver → portal hypertension can result in oesophageal and rectal varices and in caput medusae (para-umbilical veins enlarge and become visible).
    • Clinical: Important portacaval anastomotic sites include around the {{c2::oesophagus, paraumbilical region, rectum, and where GI tract is retroperitoneal}}.
    • If portal flow is decreased/blocked, venous blood can still reach the heart via {{c3::these anastomoses}}; likewise, IVC compression can shunt to portal system.
      • Achilles heels of portal hypertension.
  • Anastomoses — when clinically important:
    • Only clinically important when there is a blockage in the portal system (pathological or congenital: portal vein thrombosis, cirrhosis, hepatic vein stenosis).
    • If blocked, new pathways develop between portal and systemic systems:
      1. Lower end of the oesophagus: {{c4::left gastric ↔ azygos veins → large oesophageal varices (prone to haemorrhage)::Describe the anastomosis}}.
      2. Anorectal region: {{c5::superior ↔ inferior rectal veins → rectal varices (may bleed)::Describe the anastomosis}}.
      3. Umbilical region (anterior abdominal wall): {{c6::anterior abdominal wall veins ↔ paraumbilical veins in falciform ligament::Describe the anastomosis}} → {{c7::caput Medusae::What does it look like}}.
      4. Bare area of liver ↔ {{c8::diaphragm veins}}.
      5. {{c9::Retroperitoneal}} varices .
      6. Vessels developing around {{c10::stomas}}.
    • Portal hypertension may require surgery to reduce portal pressure by anastamosing:
      • {{c11::Portocaval}} shunt (portal vein to IVC),
      • {{c11::Mesocaval}} shunts,
      • {{c12::Warren selective distal splenorenal}} shunt.

Lymphatic drainage of the gastrointestinal tract and abdomen/pelvis

  • Lymphatic drainage (text + figures):
    • The intestinal mucosa is richly supplied with lymph vessels which drain, via submucosal and subserosal plexuses, to nodes alongside the viscus.
    • These drain to nodes in the {{c1::mesentery or on the posterior abdominal wall}}, which in turn drain to one of three groups of preaortic nodes around the origins of the {{c2::coeliac, superior and inferior mesenteric}} arteries.
    • {{c3::Inferior}} mesenteric group drains {{c6::distal transverse colon, sigmoid colon and upper rectum}}.
    • {{c4::Superior}} mesenteric group drains {{c7::distal duodenum, small intestine, ascending and proximal transverse colon}}.
    • {{c5::Coeliac}} group drains {{c8::stomach, proximal duodenum, liver, spleen and pancreas, and also takes efferents from the superior and inferior mesenteric groups}}.
    • Efferent vessels pass to the cisterna chyli, which drains into the {{c9::thoracic}} duct.

Functions of the GIT — overview and segmental roles; mechanisms and examples

  • Primary function orchestration:
    • To achieve digestion and absorption with effective propulsion and timed evacuation of the luminal contents is one of the main primary functions of the GI tract.
    • Examples:
      • a) Churning the solid food by the {{c1::powerful antral mill}} pushing the fluid back to the body and fundus: both show receptive relaxation to avoid increases in gastric pressure until proper time for fluid evacuation (neurohormonal control) when the body and fundus contract to evacuate fluid content to the duodenum.
      • b) {{c2::Damped}} propulsion in cases of multiple intestinal diverticulae leads {{c3::to bacterial overgrowth → malabsorption and B12 deficiency}}.

Definitions and Etymology (Slides 4–6)

  • The word “hernia” derived from Latin word meaning “a rupture.”
  • Hernia is an abnormal protrusion of an {{c2::organ or tissue through a defect in its surrounding wall; it might occur between two adjacent cavities (e.g., abdomen and thorax)}}.
  • Hernia can occur in various sites, but it develops most commonly through the abdominal wall.
  • The {{c1::pancreas}} is the only intra-abdominal organ that does not protrude through a defect in the abdominal wall (posterior retroperitoneal position).

Classification (Slides 8–13)

  • According to site
  • {{c1::1) Inguino-femoral (groin)
  • 2) Ventral hernia
  • Incisional (under Ventral)}}

Descriptive Terms (Slide 14)

  • Hernias may be described according to physical or operative findings:
    • {{c2::Reducible}}: The hernia contents can be pushed back into the abdomen.
    • {{c3::Incarcerated (irreducible)}}: The hernia contents cannot be pushed back.
    • {{c4::Obstructing}}: The hernia contains a loop of bowel that is kinked and obstructs the GI tract.
    • {{c5::Strangulated}}: The tissue contained in the hernia is ischaemic and will necrose due to compromise of its blood supply.
    • {{c6::Sliding}}: The wall of the hernia sac, rather than being formed completely by peritoneum, is in part formed by a retroperitoneal structure, such as the colon or the bladder (5% of oblique inguinal hernias).
    • {{c7::Richter's hernia}}: Only one side of the bowel wall is trapped in the hernia (typically, the antimesenteric side) rather than the entire loop of bowel. This is especially dangerous because the incarcerated portion of bowel can necrose and perforate in the absence of obstructive symptoms.

Aetiology of Hernias (Slides 15–17)

  • {{c1::Loss of tissue strength and elasticity
  • Abnormal mainly collagen synthesis as well as elastin synthesis
  • Congenital defects: weakness makes the hernia apparent
    • Oblique inguinal hernia, true umbilical hernia
  • Episodes of sudden unguarded straining
    • Precipitating rather predisposing
    • Epigastric hernia in males
  • Trauma of surgery
    • 80% of tensile strength regained only::What are the 4 main causes of hernias}}
  • Mechanistic categories
    • 1) Chronic stretch: weakening of the fabric (predisposing)
      • Ascites, continuous ambulatory peritoneal dialysis, ventriculoperitoneal shunts, chylous ascites
      • Obesity (male or female patterns)
      • Repeated pregnancies, sizable organomegaly (huge ovarian cyst or splenomegaly)
    • 2) Intermittent physiologic recurring strains ⟶ leading to weak collagen and elastic fibres (predisposing)
      • Thrust at the myopectineal orifice in groin hernia
      • Buffering mechanism of the umbilical segment at straining
      • Pull of respiratory muscles on vertical incisions in the midline aponeurotic zone
    • 3) Chronic recurring pathological straining: causes outside the abdomen (predisposing maybe precipitating in acute episodes)
      • Asthma and COPD, heavy weight lifting
    • 4) Chronic recurring pathological straining: causes within the abdomen (predisposing maybe precipitating in acute episodes)
      • Straining at defaecation or micturition.

Groin Hernia: Pelvic Outlets & Ligaments (Slides 18–21)

  • Bony pelvis viewed from above: note the attachments of the inguinal, lacunar, and pectineal ligaments.

  • Transversalis fascia
    • The transversalis fascia is the fascial envelop of the abdomen and the competency of the {{c1::deep inguinal}} ring depends on the integrity of this fascia:
    • A tear at the ring predisposes to descent of contents in the previously non detected oblique inguinal sac.
    • A direct hernia presents after a tear in the fascia transversalis in {{c2::Hesselbach’s}} triangle

in females, oblique inguinal > femoral; Oblique internal is more common on the {{c2::right}} side 60%}}

Groin Hernias: Core Anatomy and Landmarks

Inguinal Canal: Layered Surgical View

  • Open approach: consider anatomy {{c1::skin → peritoneal cavity}}.
  • Laparoscopic approach: consider anatomy {{c2::abdominal cavity → skin}}.
  • Purpose: approach selection, lower recurrence, avoid complications.
  • Function: inguinal canal allows passage of the {{c3::spermatic cord to the scrotum}}.

Walls and Rings

  • Anterior wall: {{c4::external oblique aponeurosis}}.
    • External inguinal ring: opening in external oblique aponeurosis; bounded by {{c5::superomedial}} and {{c5::inferolateral}} crus, joined by intercrural fibers.
    • Normally the ring will not admit the tip of the little finger.
    • Commonest {{c6::strangulating}} agent in funicular oblique hernia descending to the scrotum.
  • Inferior wall (floor): {{c7::inguinal ligament (Poupart’s)}} and its reflection.
  • Roof (superior): fibers of {{c8::internal oblique}} and {{c8::transversus abdominis}} form the conjoint tendon.
  • Posterior wall: {{c9::fascia transversalis}} and conjoint tendon.
  • Deep inguinal ring: lies lateral to the {{c10::inferior epigastric}} artery.
  • From out to in -> External -> internal-> Transversus abdominus -> Fascia transversalis

Transversalis Fascia and Hernia Formation

  • Transversalis fascia: fascial envelope of the abdomen; competency of {{c11::deep}} ring depends on its integrity.
    • Tear at the ring → descent of contents in a previously undetected {{c12::oblique}} inguinal sac.
    • Direct hernia: presents after a {{c13::tear in fascia transversalis in Hesselbach’s}} triangle.

Anatomical Landmarks: Fruchaud’s Myopectineal Orifice (MPO) and Hesselbach’s Triangle

  • Fruchaud’s MPO
    • Osseo-myo-aponeurotic tunnel; all groin hernias occur through this tunnel
    • Boundaries:
      1. Medially: {{c3::lateral border of rectus sheath}}
      2. Above: {{c3::arched fibres of internal oblique and transversus abdominis}}
      3. Laterally: {{c3::iliopsoas}} muscle
      4. Below: {{c3::pectin pubis and its fascia}}
    • {{c1::Embryologic}} weakness due to egress of testicle/round ligament and iliac neurovascular bundle
    • Common defect: failure of {{c2::transversalis fascia over MPO to retain peritoneum}}
    • 3D shape varies by view:
      • Anterior (open repair): {{c4::egg}}-like
      • Posterior (laparoscopy): {{c4::quadrilateral}}
    • Inguinal ligament and iliopubic tract divide MPO:
      • Superior compartment: {{c5::direct and indirect sacs (both sides of inferior epigastric vessels)}}
      • Inferior space: {{c5::femoral nerve, artery, vein, lymphatics; femoral hernia occurs medial to vein}}

• Hesselbach’s triangle (direct space; medial to inferior epigastric vessels)
    • Covers a large area of posterior wall of inguinal canal
    • Historic boundaries/notes:
      • Divided by {{c1::obliterated umbilical artery (medial umbilical ligament)}} into medial and lateral halves
      • Direct hernia classified as medial or lateral depending on which part it arises from

Contents of the Inguinal Canal and Spermatic Cord

  • Women: {{c1::round}} ligament traverses the inguinal canal.
  • Men: {{c2::spermatic}} cord structures pass into the internal ring, traverse canal, exit external ring into scrotum.

Spermatic cord (compartments)

  • Anterior compartment
    • {{c3::Arteries: testicular, cremasteric
    • Veins: pampiniform plexus}}
  • Posterior compartment
    • {{c4::Vas deferens}}
    • {{c5::Processus vaginalis}}: evagination of peritoneum accompanying descent of testicle and gubernaculum
      • Normally obliterated; remains patent in {{c6::inguinal hernia and forms hernia sac}}
  • Nerves
    • Genital branch of {{c7::genitofemoral}} nerve: within spermatic cord
    • {{c7::Ilioinguinal}} nerve: external to {{c8::cremasteric}} fascia
  • NB: No parasympathetic supply to the testicles

Descent of the Testicles and Processus Vaginalis

  • Formation of processus vaginalis and its passage through lower anterior abdominal wall with formation of inguinal canal occurs in both sexes.
  • Normal fate:
    • Upper part becomes obliterated just before birth
    • Lower part remains as tunica vaginalis
  • Common congenital anomalies:
    1. Persistent (partial or entire) processus as preformed hernia sac for {{c1::indirect inguinal}} hernia
    2. Narrowed lumen but communicates with abdominal cavity → {{c2::congenital hydrocele}}
    3. Upper and lower ends obliterated, leaving {{c3::encysted hydrocele of the cord}}
  • Tunica vaginalis closely related to front and sides of testis → inflammation of testis can cause {{c4::hydrocele}}. Most hydroceles are idiopathic.

Revealing statistics

*The prevalence of hernias as well as likelihood of strangulation increases with age
 *Among all strangulated hernias, most are indirect inguinal hernias.
 *Both indirect and femoral hernias commonly occur in {{c1::right}} side due to
{{c2:: 1) delayed obliteration of processus vaginalis

 2) Due to tamponading effect of sigmoid colon on the left femoral canal.}}

  • Parts of hernia: fundus, body, neck
  • Diagrammatic differences:
    • Indirect: neck {{c1::lateral}} to inferior epigastric artery
    • Direct: neck {{c1::medial}} to inferior epigastric artery
  • Forms (examples): Bubonocele -> Hernia {{c2::limited to ingunial canal}}
  • Funicular-> {{c3::Processus vaginalis is closed above the epidydmis}}, 
  • Bilateral indirect -> {{c4::Hernia extends to lower part of scrotum}}; large indirect may descend and cause {{c5::obstruction }}
