Special Types: Content & Configuration (slides 64–69)

  • Special types of hernia according to content
    • {{c1::Littre’s}} hernia with Meckel’s diverticulum as content.
    • {{c1::Maydl}} hernia (ie two adjacent bowel loops)
  • Sliding hernias (5% of indirect inguinal hernias)
    • {{c2::Sliding bladder | Sliding caecum
      ::Give two organ examples      }}

Special Types: Content & Configuration (slides 64–69)

  • Sliding Inguinal Hernia (Hernia-en-glissade)
    • A type of indirect inguinal hernia where the {{c1::posterior wall of the sac is formed by the sliding viscera}}.
    • Right side: {{c2::caecum}} most commonly involved; Left side: {{c3::sigmoid colon}}; either side: part of {{c4::urinary bladder}}.
    • Exclusively found in {{c5::males}}, most present on the {{c6::left}} side.
    • Suspect in any large hernia that {{c7::cannot be completely reduced}}.
    • Essential to identify early in operation — otherwise inadvertent entry into bowel or bladder may occur.
  • Sliding Inguinal Hernia (Hernia-en-glissade) — management
    • After dissecting the sac from the cord, the sliding hernial sac can be reduced by series of {{c8::inverting sutures (Bevan technique)}} and then reconstruction of the posterior wall of the inguinal canal.
    • Very large sliding hernia: enter the peritoneum through a separate incision, pull the bowel back into the abdomen, fix it to the posterior abdominal wall, then reconstruct floor of the canal.
    • No attempt should be made to dissect the sliding viscera free from peritoneum, as it may cause {{c9::fistula or peritonitis due to devascularization}}.
    • High recurrence rate compared to simple hernia.

Special Types: Content & Configuration (slides 64–69)

  • Types of groin hernia — Sac configuration
    • {{c1::Sliding}} hernia

    • {{c1::Pantaloon}} hernia

Special Types: Content & Configuration (slides 64–69)

  • Types of hernias: incarcerated, strangulated, and {{c1::Amyand’s}} (appendix in sac).

Term Literal meaning What it usually means in hernia surgery Typical use
{{c1::Herniorrhaphy}}
 -{{c1::rrhaphy = suturing}} Tissue repair by suturing the defect (e.g., Bassini/Shouldice), no mesh Consider in contaminated fields or select small defects; higher tension/recurrence vs mesh
{{c2::Hernioplasty}}
 -{{c2::plasty = reconstruction}} Reinforcement with mesh (e.g., Lichtenstein open, TEP/TAPP lap) Standard of care for most primary adult inguinal hernias; lowest recurrence
{{c3::Herniotomy}}
 -{{c3::otomy = opening}} Open the hernial sac, reduce contents, ligate/excise the sac Children’s inguinal hernia—hernia is from patent processus vaginalis; in adults, not enough by itself
this is what the Dr said to know

Complications of Inguinal Hernia Repair (slide 94)

  • The complications of inguinal hernia repair
      1. {{c1::Urinary retention, especially in males
      2. Wound infection and haematomas
      3. Scrotal swelling
      4. Orchitis and testicular atrophy
      5. Recurrence
      6. Iatrogenic bladder, bowel, nerve and vascular injuries
      7. Chronic groin pain}}.

Femoral Hernia: Peculiarities, Anatomy, Diagnosis & Repair (slides 95–113)

  • Peculiarities of femoral hernia
    • Occurs more frequently in women than in men due to wider pelvis and a smaller bulk of {{c1::pectineus}} muscles in females.
    • However, an {{c2::indirect inguinal}} hernia is the most common hernia in both sexes.
    • Protrusion of extraperitoneal fat or abdominal contents through the femoral canal.
    • Femoral canal is surrounded by bone and ligaments on three sides. The narrowness of the sac and the rigidity of femoral ring makes it most likely to be {{c3::strangulated}}. One-third of femoral hernias present with {{c4::incarceration or strangulation}}.
    • On clinical examination, rounded bulge appears in the upper thigh just below the {{c5::inguinal ligament}}, {{c5::below and lateral}} to the pubic tubercle
    • N.B tender femoral hernia without features of intestinal obstruction -> think {{c6::richters hernia}}

Femoral Hernia: Peculiarities, Anatomy, Diagnosis & Repair (slides 95–113)

Femoral ring anatomy (multiple annotated figures)


Defect in femoral hernia is through the {{c1::femoral}} canal, but otherwise involves similar structures and insertions as a {{c2::direct inguinal}} hernia.

Differential diagnosis of femoral hernia
    1. {{c1::Inguinal}} hernia
    2. An enlarged {{c1::Cloquet}} lymph node of any cause
    3. Psoas abscess — psoas spasm with flexed hip but difficulty in extension
    4. {{c1::Lipoma}}
    5. Femoral {{c1::aneurysm}}
    6. Distended {{c1::psoas bursa (Disappears on hip flexion)}}
    7. Saphena varix — enlarged terminal portion of {{c2::long saphenous vein}}. Soft, disappears on lying down, fluid thrill, impulse on coughing and venous hum on auscultation present. Associated varicose veins on leg
    8. Haematoma in the region

Zieman’s test
  • {{c1::Index finger on deep ring ({{c2::indirect inguinal}} hernia); middle finger ({{c2::direct inguinal}} hernia) on superficial ring and ring finger over saphenous opening ({{c2::Femoral hernia}}) are placed after reducing the content::Describe the test}}.
  • Patient coughs; impulse is felt in finger corresponding to the existing hernia

  • NB: bilateral inguinal hernias

MALGAIGNE BULGING
  • Protrusion of abdominal wall muscles during leg raising test as weak, soft, supple, swelling, which signifies {{c1::poor abdominal muscle tone}}.
  • More commonly seen in patients with {{c2::direct}} hernia.
  • Concludes that particular hernia requires {{c3::mesh repair (hernioplasty)}} as surgical treatment.
  • Common in old age, obese patient.
  • Head or {{c4::leg raising or Valsalva}} manoeuvre is important to elicit abdominal muscle tone

Complicated Herniae: Irreducible, Obstruction, Strangulation, Loss of Domain (slides 115–129)

Hernial complications
  • Hernias should be repaired electively to prevent major complications.
  • {{c1::a- Irreducibility (incarceration)
  • b. Intestinal obstruction.
  • c. Intestinal strangulation with bowel perforation.
  • d- Loss of domain with huge ventral hernias::What are the 4 main complications}}

Complicated Herniae: Irreducible, Obstruction, Strangulation, Loss of Domain (slides 115–129)

B) Intestinal obstruction
  • Once the most common cause, hernia is now the third most common cause (first is adhesions, second is cancer).
  • ABCs of bowel obstruction: A = adhesions, B = bulge (hernia), C = cancer
  • Diagnosis
    • An obstructed hernia is {{c1::tense and irreducible}}, and the abdomen is {{c1::distended}}.
    • {{c2::High-pitched}} bowel sounds with {{c2::frequent}} rushes.
    • Plain abdominal X-ray:
        1. {{c3::Classic dilated loops of bowel with air-fluid levels in the erect position}}
        2. {{c3::Paucity of gas distal to obstruction}}
    • Computed tomography may be helpful if clinical diagnosis is uncertain.
  • Treatment
    • Patient sedated and placed in {{c4::Trendelenburg}} position.
    • Taxis maneuver elongates neck to allow contents to pass into abdomen.
    • Grasp hernia neck with one hand and apply intermittent pressure with other hand on distal neck of hernia.
    • Abort attempt at reduction if unsuccessful after two or three tries because reduction of gangrenous bowel or reduction en masse is possible.
    • Postreduction
      • Reduction followed by resuscitation and urgent surgery via a direct approach over the hernial site.
      • Viable bowel reduced back into the abdomen.
      • Nonviable bowel resected with anastomosis prior to hernia repair

Complicated Herniae: Irreducible, Obstruction, Strangulation, Loss of Domain (slides 115–129)

C) Strangulation of contents
  • Predisposing
    • Narrow neck e.g. {{c1::femoral}} hernia
    • Tough constricting structures: e.g. {{c2::external ring in funicular oblique hernia and lacunar ligament in femoral hernia}}
    • Adhesions
    • Irreducibility
    • Long-time, large hernia with adhesions

• Strangulation signs are due to the {{c1::toxic fluid in the sac}}; {{c2::omentum}} can be strangulated without symptoms and signs of intestinal obstruction (beacuse it doesn't contain any organisms)

  • Strangulated inguinal hernia will be {{c3::tender and irreducible}} without {{c3::impulse on coughing}}.

Mechanism: Pressure on bowel trapped in sac ⟶ {{c1::venous congestion ⟶ edema of bowel and accumulation of toxic fluid in the sac ⟶ pressures so high that arterial inflow is obstructed ⟶ bowel becomes gangrenous}}.

Diagnosis
  • {{c2::Tender, irreducible}} masses with systemic symptoms such as toxicity, dehydration, and fever.
  • Symptoms of bowel obstruction may be absent if {{c3::Richter’s}} hernia or contents are omentum.
  • The hernia is tense and very tender; overlying skin changes (erythematous or bluish discoloration). Warm and with induration. {{c4::No}} impulse on cough.
  • Leukocytosis common, metabolic acidosis may be present.
  • Lactic acid may be normal because no venous outflow to circulate lactic acid.
Treatment
  • Strangulated hernias {{c5::should not be reduced}}.
  • {{c6::NG}} tube suction, fluid and electrolyte replacement and {{c6::antibiotics}} are started.
  • Emergency surgery follows with exposure of the hernia, opening of the sac at the fundus to {{c7::evacuate the toxic fluid}}.
  • Inspect the contents; any gangrenous viscera should be {{c8::resected}}, followed by {{c8::hernia repair}}.
The hernia repair is no longer a sterile field in this setting. Synthetic mesh must {{c9::NOT be used}}

D) Loss of domain
  • Massive hernia with prolonged duration
  • Large portion of abdominal contents situated in hernia sac; lose their right of domain; thus, replacement with fascial closure puts patient at risk of {{c1::abdominal compartment}} syndrome, skin edema, and cellulitis after closure.
  • {{c2::Pneumoperitoneum (blowing air into it)}} may help when returning contents to the abdominal cavity over 3 weeks prior to repair.
  • A {{c3::prosthesis}} is usually required because of the size of the defect.

children.name: ICC-2 [157] Principles of Management of Complicated Peptic Ulcer Disease

fields:
Bleeding Peptic Ulcer

Case Scenario (1)

  • “A 57-year-old man with multiple medical problems presents to the emergency department feeling lightheaded with hematemesis and melena... NGT yielded bright red blood clots... dialysis-dependent ESRD... improved with fluids... DRE: dark, tarry stool (guaiac positive).”

Conservative Management

  • Correction of hypovolemia by {{c1::IV crystalloids}} and {{c1::blood transfusion (Fresh blood in sufficient amount is the best single item to resuscitate and promote arrest of bleeding).}}
  • Careful monitoring of pulse, blood pressure, and hematocrit.
  • {{c2::IV H2 receptor}} blockers or {{c2::omeprazole}}.
  • {{c3::Antacids}} should be given frequently and liberally.
  • Correction of any bleeding tendency.
  • Lavage of the stomach by cold saline is recommended by some surgeons.

Management (General)

  • Most cases stop bleeding {{c4::spontaneously}}.
  • Conservative treatment should always be considered whenever possible.

Bleeding Peptic Ulcer

Case Scenario (1)

  • “A 57-year-old man with multiple medical problems presents to the emergency department feeling lightheaded with hematemesis and melena... NGT yielded bright red blood clots... dialysis-dependent ESRD... improved with fluids... DRE: dark, tarry stool (guaiac positive).”

Endoscopic Management (Slide 9)

  • Ulcer usually covered by a blood clot or there may be an actual spurting artery.
  • {{c1::Gastroscopy}} provides diagnosis and can achieve hemostasis.
  • Indications of endoscopic hemostasis:
    1. {{c2::Actively bleeding ulcer}}.
    2. {{c2::Stigmata of recent bleeding (likelihood for rebleeding):
      • Visible vessel in the ulcer base
      • Fresh clot covering the ulcer}}
  • Methods of endoscopic hemostasis: {{c3::laser}} coagulation, {{c3::thermal}} coagulation, diathermy, or injection of the ulcer base with {{c4::adrenaline or alcohol}}.

Bleeding Peptic Ulcer

Case Scenario (1)

  • “A 57-year-old man with multiple medical problems presents to the emergency department feeling lightheaded with hematemesis and melena... NGT yielded bright red blood clots... dialysis-dependent ESRD... improved with fluids... DRE: dark, tarry stool (guaiac positive).”

Case Conclusion (Slide 13 and 17)

  • Patient stabilizes after a total of four units of PRBCs. Three days after his initial EGD he develops recurrent hematemesis with an associated drop in his blood pressure. Blood transfusion and fluids are started
  • Exploratory {{c1::laparotomy}}, {{c1::pyloroduodenotomy}}, and oversewing of a posterior duodenal bulb ulcer; {{c2::H. pylori}} positive on gastric biopsies → {{c3::PPIs and H. pylori}} treatment (full narrative provided in later slide cluster).

Arterial Blood Supply (Anatomy)

  • Coeliac trunk; 3 branches:
    • {{c1::Left Gastric}} Artery: supplies the {{c2::cardia of the stomach and distal esophagus}}.
    • {{c3::Splenic}} Artery → {{c4::Left Gastroepiploic}}, {{c4::Short Gastric}} arteries ({{c5::greater curvature}} supply).
    • Common Hepatic:
      • {{c6::Right Gastric}}: supplies a portion of the {{c7::lesser curvature}}.
      • {{c8::Gastroduodenal artery}}→ {{c9::Right Gastroepiploic}} (helps supply greater curvature with Left Gastroepiploic).
  • Lesser curvature → {{c10::left and right Gastric}}; Greater curvature → {{c10::left and right Gastroepiploic}}.
  • SMA arises from {{c11::behind the uncinate process of the pancreas}}.

Operative Treatment (Bleeding)

  • Simple knot on the {{c1::proximal and distal}} segments around the bleeder.
  • When suturing the pyloric hole (that led to the ulcer): suture {{c2::horizontally}} (incision {{c2::vertically}} along muscular fibers) → this is called pyloroplasty.

Bleeding Peptic Ulcer — Key Facts

  • The most common complication.
  • Self-limited in {{c1::75}}% of cases.
  • {{c1::25}}% will bleed or re-bleed in hospital.
  • Important to identify the high-risk group early with clinical and endoscopic parameters.

Anatomy (Innervation — notes)

  • INNERVATION
    • Embryologically: posterior part of stomach is {{c2::right}}, anterior is {{c2::left}}.
    • Anterior and posterior of {{c3::Latarjet}} supply the stomach from the vagus nerve

Types of Surgical Procedures (selected)

  • VAGOTOMY
    • Cuts vagus nerve → eliminates {{c1::acid-secretion stimulus}}.
    • Notes: “wont digest anything”; “could do vagotomy with pylorotomy → would usually lead to {{c2::alkaline reflux and NERD}}.”
    • Evolution: Selective vagotomy (Latarjet) → then the highly super selective vagotomy.
    • Then developed antrotomy, but the issue: removing pylorus with antrum (Billroth).
  • PYLOROPLASTY
    • Widens the pylorus to guarantee {{c3::stomach emptying even without vagus nerve stimulation}}.

TAKE HOME POINTS (bleeding-focused cluster)

  • Ensure adequate {{c1::intravenous}} access and an adequate {{c1::airway}} prior to any procedure.
  • {{c2::EGD (Esophagogastroduodenoscopy)}} is the most important assessment tool (diagnostic and therapeutic).
  • Treat with a {{c3::PPI}} and evaluate for H. pylori infection.
  • EGD reasonable for {{c4::rebleed if not profoundly unstable}}.
  • {{c5::Angiographic embolization}} for patients who fail endoscopic therapy or have contraindications to surgery, if readily available.
  • Operative approach: {{c6::cessation}} of bleeding followed by an {{c6::acid reduction procedure}}, if appropriate.

Types of Surgical Procedures (Resection/Reconstruction)

  • {{c1::ANTRECTOMY}}/ SUBTOTAL {{c1::GASTRECTOMY}}
    • {{c2::Lower}} half of stomach ({{c2::antrum}}) makes most of the acid
    • Removing this portion (antrectomy) decreases acid production
  • SUBTOTAL GASTRECTOMY
    • Removes {{c3::½ to 2/3::What percentage}} of stomach
    • Remainder must be reattached to bowel:
      • {{c4::Billroth I
      • Billroth II::What are they called}}

Billroth I

  • {{c5::Distal}}portion of the stomach is removed
  • Remainder is anastomosed to the {{c6::duodenum
    }}
  • Note: “very failed procedure”

Billroth II

  • {{c7::Lower}}portion of stomach removed
  • Remainder is anastomosed to the {{c8::jejunum}}

  • Note: “also kinda bad operation”

Postoperative Care

  • {{c9::NG}} tube—care and management
  • Monitor for post-operative complications

TAKE HOME POINTS

  • Although decreasing in incidence, peptic ulcer remains the most {{c1::common}} cause of upper GI bleeding, with significant associated mortality.
  • {{c2::H. pylori infection and NSAID use}} are the most frequent inciting factors in bleeding gastric ulcers.
  • {{c3::Endoscopy}} is first-line diagnostic and therapeutically effective in a majority.
  • Surgery is indicated in {{c4::massive bleeding, failure of endoscopic therapy, recurrent hemorrhage, or neoplasm}}.
  • For non-refractory disease without prior failure: {{c5::Anterior gastrotomy, ulcer oversewing, and biopsy}} is the procedure of choice.
  • {{c6::Truncal vagotomy}} only for ulcers refractory to adequate PPI therapy and H. pylori eradication.
  • {{c7::Distal gastrectomy}} (including ulcer in specimen) is preferred in stable patients with refractory large antral ulcers.
  • {{c8::Acid suppression and H. pylori}} clearance decrease risk of recurrent bleeding gastric ulcer

1. Anterior gastrotomy, ulcer oversewing, and biopsy

→ For patients without a history of refractory ulcer disease
  • Reason:
    • In non-refractory (i.e. first-time or responsive) ulcers, the main goal during emergency surgery is hemostasis (to stop bleeding) and diagnosis (to rule out malignancy).
    • Anterior gastrotomy gives access to the posterior gastric wall where most bleeding ulcers occur.
    • Oversewing stops the active bleeding.
    • Biopsy ensures the ulcer isn’t malignant, as gastric ulcers can mimic or harbor carcinoma.
    • There’s no need for acid-reducing surgery (like vagotomy) because the ulcer isn’t chronic or treatment-resistant.

2. Truncal vagotomy

→ For ulcers refractory to adequate PPI therapy and H. pylori eradication
  • Reason:
    • The vagus nerve stimulates acid secretion through parietal cell activation.
    • In patients whose ulcers persist despite modern medical therapy (PPI + H. pylori eradication), the cause is excessive acid secretion unresponsive to medication.
    • Truncal vagotomy cuts both vagal trunks, significantly reducing acid output and promoting ulcer healing.
    • It’s reserved for refractory cases, because most ulcers today heal medically—surgery is now the last resort.

3. Distal gastrectomy with ulcer inclusion

→ For stable patients with refractory, large antral ulcers
  • Reason:
    • Large antral ulcers often lie near the pylorus and may involve mucosal changes or fibrosis, making local repair inadequate.
    • Removing the distal stomach (including the ulcer) ensures:
      • Complete excision of diseased tissue (eliminates possible malignancy).
      • Reduced acid secretion (since most parietal cells lie in the proximal stomach).
    • It’s best suited for stable patients, as this is a definitive, curative procedure that prevents recurrence.

4. Acid suppression and H. pylori eradication

→ Decrease the risk of recurrent bleeding gastric ulcer
  • Reason:
    • H. pylori infection and acid hypersecretion are the two major causes of ulcer formation and rebleeding.
    • PPIs suppress acid, allowing mucosal healing and clot stability.
    • Eradicating H. pylori removes the underlying inflammatory trigger that perpetuates ulcer formation.
    • Together, they promote mucosal healing and prevent rebleeding or recurrence.
✨ In summary:
  • Simple oversewing + biopsy → for acute, nonrefractory ulcers.
  • Truncal vagotomy → for refractory acid-related ulcers.
  • Distal gastrectomy → for large, chronic antral ulcers needing definitive excision.
  • PPI + H. pylori clearance → cornerstone for long-term prevention.

Perforated Peptic Ulcer

Clinical Scenario

  • ““A 70-year-old man presents to the emergency department (ED) with a 1 hour history of generalized abdominal pain that began abruptly in the epigastric region and now radiates to both shoulders. The patient has a prior history of peptic ulcer disease but is not currently on acid-suppression therapy. He is also on low-dose aspirin for peripheral vascular disease. He has never had an abdominal surgery. He has no known drug allergies. He does not smoke tobacco or drink alcohol. On physical examination, his temperature is 36.5°C, heart rate is 100, blood pressure is 125/70, and his abdomen is diffusely tender to palpation..”

Overview / Natural History

  • {{c1::2nd}} most common complication.
  • Initially: chemical peritonitis (from acid and alkaline mix) ➔ lucid interval ➔ bacterial peritonitis (from the undigested food).
  • Presentation = acute abdomen:
    • History of PUD
    • Sudden severe abdominal pain
    • Picture of shock and sepsis, {{c2::neurogenic}} shock ({{c2::vagal}}) or {{c2::septic}} shock
    • Abdominal exam: {{c3::tenderness, guarding, rigidity, rebound tenderness}}
    • Erect X-ray abdomen: {{c4::free air under diaphragm (~80%)}}, especially in the {{c7::right}} area under the diaphragm
    • Notes:  if inflammation is very acute, omentum {{c5::can’t}} fix; if not very acute, omentum {{c5::can}} fix 
    • generally {{c6::don’t endoscope::Do we endocscope}} in perforation (air insufflation worsens perforation; novel procedures may staple before air)

Differential Diagnosis

  • Perforated acute appendicitis, acute cholecystitis, acute pancreatitis, intestinal obstruction.
  • At onset, differentiate from {{c8::myocardial infarction}}.

Epidemiology Note

  • Now most commonly seen in elderly, particularly women, majority attributable to {{c9::NSAID}} use.

Perforated Peptic Ulcer

Clinical Scenario

  • ““A 70-year-old man presents to the emergency department (ED) with a 1 hour history of generalized abdominal pain that began abruptly in the epigastric region and now radiates to both shoulders. The patient has a prior history of peptic ulcer disease but is not currently on acid-suppression therapy. He is also on low-dose aspirin for peripheral vascular disease. He has never had an abdominal surgery. He has no known drug allergies. He does not smoke tobacco or drink alcohol. On physical examination, his temperature is 36.5°C, heart rate is 100, blood pressure is 125/70, and his abdomen is diffusely tender to palpation..”

Pathology (Stages)

  • {{c1::[A] Stage of perforation: Yielding of ulcer base → gastric contents into peritoneal cavity.
  • [B] Stage of chemical peritonitis:
    • Peritoneum reacts by producing serous alkaline fluid, neutralizing acid and bringing antibodies to hinder bacterial growth temporarily.
    • Length depends on:
      1. Size of perforation
      2. Contents: longer duration with empty stomach
      3. General resistance of the patient
  • [C] Stage of septic peritonitis:
    • Occurs when body resistance fails → microorganisms invade peritoneal cavity → pus forms.}}

Duodenal vs Gastric Perforation (Pathology Notes)

  • Duodenal ulcer perforation is {{c2::less}} serious, with a longer lucid interval, because:
    1. Contents more {{c3::sterile}}
    2. Contents less {{c3::irritant}}
    3. {{c3::Smaller}} size
    4. Poured out contents flow along right {{c4::paracolic gutter}} and tend to localize in {{c4::right iliac fossa}}
  • Gastric ulcer perforates directly into the general peritoneal cavity → {{c5::generalized}} peritonitis.
  • Additional note: Only {{c6::first}} part of duodenum is intraperitoneal; the rest retroperitoneal → most perforations retroperitoneal → peritonitis usually less.

Perforated Peptic Ulcer

Clinical Scenario

  • ““A 70-year-old man presents to the emergency department (ED) with a 1 hour history of generalized abdominal pain that began abruptly in the epigastric region and now radiates to both shoulders. The patient has a prior history of peptic ulcer disease but is not currently on acid-suppression therapy. He is also on low-dose aspirin for peripheral vascular disease. He has never had an abdominal surgery. He has no known drug allergies. He does not smoke tobacco or drink alcohol. On physical examination, his temperature is 36.5°C, heart rate is 100, blood pressure is 125/70, and his abdomen is diffusely tender to palpation..”

Clinical Picture

  1. [A] Stage of shock (usually short; patient often not seen in it)
    • {{c2::Sudden severe::Describe }} upper abdominal pain
    • {{c1::Vomiting}} (may occur once)
    • {{c1::Neurogenic}} shock
    • Upper abdomen: {{c3::tenderness and board-like rigidity}}
  2. [B] Stage of lucid interval
    • Patient relieved from shock, vital signs improve
    • Pain {{c4::diminishes}}; patient comes walking to hospital
    • On examination:
      • {{c5::Spreading tenderness and rigidity towards abdomen}}
      • {{c5::Obliteration}} of liver dullness
      • {{c5::Shifting}} dullness may be present
  3. [C] Stage of septic peritonitis
    • {{c6::Vomiting, distention, dead silent abdomen & absolute constipation}}
    • {{c7::Hypovolemic}} shock sets in and becomes more profound
    • Patient dies in few days if untreated

Perforated Peptic Ulcer

Investigations

  • Laboratory: {{c1::Electrolytes; blood picture (leukocytosis); ABGs -> {{c2::lactate}} }}.
  • X-ray erect: {{c5::Air under the copula of the diaphragm}}.

  • CT Abdomen.
  • Notes:  Most perforations also increase {{c3::amylase}}, but if amylase is 3×, then definitely {{c4::pancreatitis}}.

Radiology / Figures

  • Posterior wall perforation (image).

  • 37-year-old woman with perforated gastric ulcer: focal defect in lesser curvature of gastric body caused by deep ulcer, associated with surrounding mural thickening; {{c6::small air bubble}} on anterior peritoneal surface of liver

Perforated Peptic Ulcer

Treatment

  • Urgent Operation: {{c1::Open or Laparoscopic}}.
  • Preoperative preparation and resuscitation:
    1. {{c2::Ryle’s}} tube (sucks out stomach content) with continuous aspiration.
    2. IV {{c3::fluids}} guided by electrolyte and pH estimations.
    3. IV {{c3::antibiotics}}.
    4. IV {{c4::H2 receptor}} blockers or {{c4::PPIs}}.
    5. Careful attention to {{c5::respiration}}.
    6. Late cases with toxemia require {{c6::blood transfusion}}.
  • At operation (simplest procedure):
    1. Closure of the perforation with a {{c7::covering omental patch}}.
    2. Thorough {{c8::peritoneal toilet (wash the belly)}}.
    3. Abdomen closed with drainage.
    4. Perforated gastric ulcer: {{c9::biopsy}} must be obtained.
  • Postoperative: Patients are treated {{c10::medically}} for their ulcer disease.

Perforated Peptic Ulcer

Subacute Perforation

  • {{c1::Small}} perforation allowing minimal contents to enter peritoneal cavity; rapidly sealed.
  • Same early signs but much reduced.
  • If diagnosed correctly, can be treated {{c2::conservatively}}.

Obstructing Peptic Ulcer (Gastric Outlet Obstruction)

Fibrous Contractures of Peptic Ulceration

  • Types:
    1. {{c1::Pyloric stenosis}}
    2. {{c1::Hourglass}} stomach
    3. {{c1::Teapot or handbag}} stomach
  • These are complications of healing ulcers rather than complications of their activity.

Pathology

  • Pylorus: {{c2::Cicatrized and stenosed (basically super narrow) }}, showing the specific pathology.
  • Stomach: At first {{c3::hypertrophied}} then {{c3::dilated}}, may reach down to the pelvis.
    • Mucosa: Initially {{c4::hypertrophic}} gastritis, then {{c4::atrophic}} gastritis and {{c4::true hypoacidity}}, due to stagnation and fermentation.
  • Intestine: Normal and collapsed.

Obstructing Peptic Ulcer (Gastric Outlet Obstruction)

Complications (due to repeated vomiting)

  • {{c1::Metabolic alkalosis and may be tetany::Whats the ABG}}.
  • {{c2::Hyponatremia}} (Na {{c2::loss}}) and {{c2::hypokalemia}} (K {{c2::loss}}).
  • Dehydration, loss of weight, avitaminosis.
  • Antral {{c3::stasis}} releases {{c4::gastrin}} leading to other ulcers.
  • {{c5::Respiratory}} tract infection from inhalation of vomitus.
    • Note: “from the alkalosis, the H decreases, freeing Albumin; Albumin binds to calcium causing tetany.”

Obstructing Peptic Ulcer (Gastric Outlet Obstruction)

Clinical Picture

  • Long history of ulcer dyspepsia or first presentation.
  • Vomiting: {{c1::Projectile, not bile-stained, contains food of previous meals or days, foul odor revealing fermentation (characteristically in the evening)::Describe vomit}}.
  • Loss of {{c2::initial periodic ulcer}} pain → replaced by epigastric discomfort and fullness.
  • Progressive constipation.
  • Picture of complications.
  • Signs:
    • General: Usually {{c3::under}} weight; see complications.
    • Abdominal:
      • Epigastric {{c4::fullness (may show contour of enlarged stomach)}}.
      • {{c5::Succussion}} splash can be heard.
      • Peristaltic waves propagating from {{c6::left to right}}.
        • Notes: “left to right is not normal”; shows in skinny people and kids.

Obstructing Peptic Ulcer (Gastric Outlet Obstruction)

Differential Diagnosis

  • Traumatic: {{c1::Impacted foreign body, corrosive ingestion, after pyloroplasty}}.
  • Inflammatory: {{c1::Commonest is cicatrized duodenal or prepyloric gastric ulcer; less commonly TB, Crohn’s disease, Syphilis}}.
  • Neoplastic: {{c1::Cancer pylorus, less commonly sarcoma or leiomyoma}}.
  • Pressure from outside: {{c2::Lymph nodes, bands, tumors of head of pancreas}}.
  • Functional: {{c3::Pylorospasm or achalasia}}.

Investigations

  • {{c4::Hb/Hct, serum electrolytes}}.
  • Endoscopy:
    • {{c5::Pyloric ring}} is inactive and will not pass the tip of the endoscope.
    • Main value: detect a tumor at the pylorus and obtain {{c6::biopsies}}.
  • Barium meal shows:
    • {{c7::Dilated stomach often reaching the pelvis ({{c8::Soup dish}} appearance)
      }}.
    • {{c9::Delayed}} gastric emptying.
    • {{c10::Irregular filling}} defect in case of carcinoma (may miss small pyloric carcinoma).

Obstructing Peptic Ulcer (Gastric Outlet Obstruction)

Management

Pyloric stenosis requires surgery. It is not an emergency and needs Proper Preoperative Preparation:
  1. {{c1::Suck and wash}} the stomach via nasogastric tube.
  2. Correct {{c2::fluid, electrolytes, and acid-base}} balance.
  3. High-{{c3::protein}} fluid diet, supplemented by intravenous alimentation.
  4. Parenteral {{c3::vitamins}}.
  5. Packed {{c4::cells}} to correct anemia.
  6. Chest physiotherapy and antibiotics.
  7. Some pyloric stenosis cases due to {{c5::muscle spasm and marked inflammatory}} reaction around an active duodenal ulcer respond well to medical treatment.
Surgery (Options)
  • Standard: {{c6::Truncal vagotomy and gastrojejunostomy}}.
  • Other options:
    1. {{c7::Gastrojejunostomy}} alone in elderly or unfit patients.
    2. {{c7::Endoscopic}} balloon dilatation.
    3. Partial gastrectomy + gastrojejunostomy.
  • Additional note (endoscopic vs surgical): Balloon dilatation is {{c8::transient, ultimately fails}}.
    Standard is Vagotomy + Antrectomy; gastrojejunostomy + V.

Peptic Ulcers: Definition, Pathophysiology, Subjective Data

Definition

  • A circumscribed ulceration of the gastrointestinal mucosa occurring in areas exposed to acid and pepsin and most often caused by Helicobacter pylori infection.

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY


Subjective Data

  • Pain—“{{c1::gnawing”, “aching”, or “burning}}”
  • Other symptoms: Nausea, vomiting, belching, dyspepsia, bloating, chest discomfort, anorexia, hematemesis, and/or melena may also occur.

Differential Diagnosis

  • {{c2::MI}} —not to be missed if having chest pain
  • Neoplasm of the stomach
  • Pancreatitis
  • Pancreatic cancer
  • Diverticulitis
  • Nonulcer dyspepsia (functional dyspepsia)
  • Cholecystitis
  • Gastritis
  • GERD

Duodenal Ulcers

  • Duodenal sites are {{c3::4}}× as common as gastric sites
  • Most common in {{c4::middle}} age; peak 30–50 years
  • Male:female = 4:1
  • Genetic link: 3× more common in 1st-degree relatives; more common in blood group {{c5::O}}
  • Associated with increased serum {{c6::pepsinogen}}
  • H. pylori infection common—up to 95%
  • Smoking is {{c7::twice}} as common

Gastric Ulcers

  • Common in {{c8::late middle}} ages; incidence increases with age
  • Male:female = 2:1
  • More common in patients with blood group {{c9::A}}
  • {{c10::NSAID}}s: associated with a three- to four-fold increase in risk of gastric ulcer
  • Less related to H. pylori than duodenal ulcers—about {{c11::80}}%
  • 10–20% of patients with a gastric ulcer have a concomitant {{c12::duodenal}} ulcer

Bleeding Severity in peptic ulcer

  • Microscopic: part of disease process.
  • Mild: from {{c1::granulation tissue}} in ulcer floor.
  • Moderate: {{c1::erosion of a small vessel}} in ulcer floor.
  • Severe: penetration of a {{c1::large extra-gastric vessel (e.g., gastroduodenal artery)—usually fatal}}.

Clinical Picture of Bleeding

  • Hematemesis: Dark coffee ground due to {{c2::acid hematin}}.
  • Melena: Altered blood in stools.
  • Manifestations of progressive shock.
  • Signs:
    • Exclude false hematemesis by examining {{c3::nose and pharynx}}.
    • Exclude {{c4::portal hypertension}} as a cause (commonest cause of hematemesis being bleeding varices).

Higher-Risk Patients (for bleeding)

  • Presentation in shock
  • Requirement of 4–6 units of blood
  • Repeated hospitalization
  • Ulcers >{{c1::2}} cm
  • Age >60
  • Concurrent indication for surgery (perforation or obstruction)
  • Lack of availability of endoscopic therapy
  • Lack of availability of blood transfusions

Post-operative Complications (selected)

  • Bleeding
    • Occurs at the anastomosed site
    • First {{c1::24}} hours and post-op days {{c1::4–7}}
  • {{c2::Duodenal stump}} leak (Billroth II)
    • Severe {{c3::abdominal}} pain
    • {{c3::Bile}}-stained drainage on dressing
  • Gastric retention
    • WILL NEED TO PUT {{c4::NG}} TUBE BACK IN
  • Dumping Syndrome
    • Prevalent with {{c5::subtotal gastrectomies}}
    • Early—30 minutes after meals
      • {{c6::Vertigo, tachycardia, syncope, sweating, pallor, palpitations}}
    • Late—90 minutes–3 hours after meals
    • Rx: Decrease {{c7::CHO}} intake, Eat {{c7::slowly}}, Avoid {{c7::fluids during meals}}, Increase {{c7::fat}}, Eat {{c7::small}}, frequent meals
  • Anemia
    • Rapid gastric emptying decreases absorption of {{c8::iron}}
  • Malabsorption of fat
    • Decreased {{c9::acid}} secretions, {{c9::decreased}} pancreatic secretions, increased upper GI motility

Functional GI Disorders: Definition (Slide 4)

  • A functional gastrointestinal disorder comprises symptoms arising in {{c1::the mid or lower gastrointestinal tract that are not attributable to anatomic or biochemical defects}}
  • The symptoms include abdominal pain, early satiety, nausea, bloating, distention, and various symptoms of disordered defecation
  • The 3 most common functional bowel disorders are {{c2::irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), constipation, and functional dyspepsia}}

Functional GI Disorders: Classification (Slides 5–7)

{{c1::Esophageal}} Disorders

  • Globus
  • Functional heartburn
  • Reflux hypersensitivity

{{c2::Gastroduodenal}} Disorders

  • Functional dyspepsia
  • Nausea and vomiting disorders
  • Belching disorders

{{c3::Bowel}} Disorders

  • Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)
  • Functional constipation
  • Functional diarrhea
  • Functional abdominal bloating/distension

{{c4::Centrally Mediated Disorders of Gastrointestinal Pain}}

  • Centrally mediated abdominal pain syndrome (CAPS)
  • Narcotic bowel syndrome (NBS) / Opioid-induced GI hyperalgesia

{{c5::Gallbladder and Sphincter of Oddi (SO)}} Disorders

  • Biliary pain
  • Functional pancreatic SO disorder

{{c6::Anorectal}} Disorders

  • Fecal incontinence
  • Functional anorectal pain
  • Functional defecation disorders

Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS): Definition (Slide 9)

  • IBS is a gastrointestinal syndrome characterized by {{c1::chronic abdominal pain and altered bowel habits in the absence of any organic cause}}
  • It is the most commonly diagnosed gastrointestinal condition

Clinical Manifestations of IBS (Slide 10)

  • Chronic abdominal pain
    • Usually described as a {{c2::crampy sensation}} with variable intensity and {{c2::periodic}} exacerbations
    • The location and character of the pain can vary widely
    • {{c3::Emotional stress}} and {{c3::eating}} may exacerbate the pain, while {{c3::defecation}} often provides some relief
  • Altered bowel habits
    • {{c4::Diarrhea}} (IBS-D)
    • {{c4::Constipation}} (IBS-C)

Subtypes of IBS (Slide 11)

  • IBS with constipation
    • {{c5::hard or lumpy}} stools ≥25 percent / {{c5::loose or watery}} stools <25 percent of bowel movements
  • IBS with diarrhea
    • {{c6::loose or watery}} stools ≥25 percent / {{c6::hard or lumpy}}   stools <25 percent of bowel movements
  • Mixed IBS
    • {{c7::hard or lumpy}} stools ≥25 percent / {{c7::loose or watery}}  stools ≥25 percent of bowel movements
  • Unsubtyped IBS
    • {{c8::insufficient abnormality of}} stool consistency to meet the above subtypes

Rome IV Criteria of IBS (important)

  • Criteria must be fulfilled for the past {{c1::3}} months, with symptom onset at least {{c1::6}} months prior to diagnosis
  • IBS — Recurrent abdominal pain on average at least {{c2::1}} day/week in the last 3 months that is associated with at least 2 of the following:
    1. Related to {{c3::defecation}}
    2. Associated with a change in {{c3::frequency of stool}}
    3. Associated with a change in {{c3::form (appearance) of stool}}
  • IBS-D subtype identified with: {{c4::>25}}% Bristol stool types 6 or 7 and {{c4::<25}}% Bristol stool types 1 or 2

Diagnostic Approach to IBS (Slides 13–14)

  • "Alarm" or atypical symptoms which are not compatible with IBS include:
    • {{c1::Rectal bleeding}} vv imporatnt
    • {{c1::Nocturnal or progressive}}abdominal pain
    • Weight {{c1::loss}}
    • Laboratory abnormalities such as {{c1::anemia, elevated inflammatory markers, or electrolyte disturbances}}
  • Patients with one of these alarm symptoms require further imaging studies and/or {{c2::colonoscopy}}
  • In patients with no alarm symptoms and no family history of IBD or CRC, a limited number of diagnostic studies can rule out organic illness in the majority of patients and a sizable number require {{c3::no}} testing at all

Diagnostic evaluation depends on predominant symptom (diarrheoa vs constipation)

  • Diarrheoa
    • {{c4::Stool}} cultures and testing for giardiasis (not routine)
    • {{c6::Celiac}} disease screening
    • Fecal {{c5::calprotectin/lactoferrin + CRP}}
    • {{c7::24 hours stool collection}} if malabsorption is suspected
    • {{c8::Colonoscopy or flexible sigmoidoscopy and biopsy}} (in alarming features presence)
  • Constipation
    • Colonoscopy or flexible sigmoidoscopy (in alarming features presence)

Treatment of IBS (Slides 15–21)

Overview (Slide 15)

  • Patient Education
  • Dietary Modification
  • Management of Constipation
  • Management of Diarrhea
  • Pain Management

Patient Education (Slide 16)

  • The most important component of treatment lies in the establishment of a therapeutic physician–patient relationship:
    • The doctor should be {{c1::non}}-judgmental
    • Establish {{c1::realistic}} expectations with {{c1::consistent}} limits
    • Involve the patient in treatment decisions
  • Patients should be informed of the {{c2::chronic}} and {{c2::benign}} nature of IBS

Dietary modification (Slides 17; merged with extracted content)

  • {{c3::FODMAPs}} restriction
  • {{c3::Gluten}} Avoidance
  • {{c3::Lactose}} Avoidance
  • {{c3::Food allergies}}  testing
  • {{c3::Dietary}} Fiber is good
  • {{c4::Physical activity}} should be encouraged

Management of Constipation (Slide 18)

  • In patients with IBS-C who failed a trial of soluble fiber (e.g., {{c5::psyllium/ispaghula}}):
    • {{c6::Osmotic laxatives}} (e.g., {{c10::Polyethylene Glycol (PEG)}})
    • {{c7::Lubiprostone}}: locally acting {{c11::chloride channel activator}}
    • {{c8::Linaclotide}}: {{c12::Guanylate cyclase}} agonist
    • {{c9::Tegaserod, Prucalopride}}: {{c13::5HT4 R}} agonists

Management of Diarrheoa (Slide 19)

  • In patients with IBS-D:
    • Anti-diarrheoal agents: {{c14::loperamide, eluxadoline}} ({{c15::mu/delta opioid}} receptor agonist/antagonist)
    • {{c16::Bile acid}} sequestrants: {{c17::cholestyramine, cholestipol}}
    • {{c18::5HT3 R}}antagonists: {{c19::Alosetron}} (ischemic colitis as a side effect)

Pain and Bloating (Slide 20)

  • {{c20::Antispasmodics}}:
    • {{c21::Clinidium bromide/chlordiazepoxide}}
    • {{c21::Hyoscyamine-sulfate}}
    • {{c21::Mebeverine}}
  • {{c22::Antidepressants}}:
    • TCAs: {{c23::Amytriptyline hydrochloride}}
    • SSRIs: {{c23::Fluoxetine hydrochloride / Duloxetine hydrochloride}}
  • {{c24::Rifaximin}}and Probiotics
  • {{c25::Peppermint}} oil
  • Refractory patients:
    • Behavioural therapy
    • {{c26::Fecal microbiotal}} transplantation

Functional dyspepsia: Definition (Slide 22)

  • The ACG defines functional dyspepsia as:
    • {{c1::Predominant epigastric}} pain lasting at least {{c1::1}} month
    • This can be associated with any other upper gastrointestinal symptom such as {{c2::epigastric fullness, nausea, vomiting, or heartburn}}, provided epigastric pain is the patient’s {{c3::primary}} concern

Approach to Functional Dyspepsia (Slide 23)

Bone Surgery

  • {{c1::Osteotomy}}: to cut bone
  • {{c2::Osteosynthesis}}: fix a fracture
  • ORIF / CRIF
  • {{c3::Bone Graft}} to fill voids (Autograft / Allograft / Synthetic)
  • Drain / drill infection
  • {{c4::Lengthen bone}} (osteotomy + distraction)
  • {{c5::Cheilectomy}}: Excision of extra bone around a joint

Osteotomy

  • to correct deformity, to change the shape of the bone, or to redirect load trajectories in a limb so as to influence joint function.
  • Wedge, crescent, Z shape
  • Opening or closing wedge
  • Stabilise

Osteosynthesis

  • External fixation
    • {{c6::Monoaxial
    • Circular}}
  • Internal fixation
    • {{c7::Intra-medullary}} e.g. nail
    • {{c7::Extra-medullary}} e.g. plate and screws

C.R.I.F (Closed Reduction Internal Fixation)

  • Fracture reduced under X-ray guidance
  • {{c8::Kirschner wires (K-wires)}} are sterilized, sharpened, smooth stainless-steel pins that can be used to stabilize bone fragments.
  • wires can be drilled through the bone to hold the fragments in place

Bone Graft

  • Osteo-{{c9::inductive}}: they are able to stimulate osteogenesis through the differentiation of mesenchymal cells into osteoprogenitor cells
  • Osteo-{{c9::conductive}}: they provide linkage across defects and a scaffold upon which new bone can form.
Types
  • Autograft: one site to another in the same individual
  • Allograft: from one individual (alive or dead) to another of the same species.
  • Synthetic: {{c10::Calcium phosphate, Hydroxyapatite}}

Joint Surgery

Orthopaedic Operations on Joints

  • {{c1::Arthrodesis}}: to fuse a joint
  • {{c2::Arthrotomy}}: to open a joint
  • {{c3::Arthroscopy}}: to look inside a joint with scope
  • {{c4::Arthroplasty}}: to refashion or replace a joint
  • {{c5::Synovectomy}}: to remove a joint lining
  • Manipulate a stiff joint

Arthrotomy

  • May be indicated to:
    1. {{c6::inspect the interior or perform a synovial biopsy
    2. drain a haematoma or an abscess
    3. remove a loose body or damaged structure
    4. excise inflamed synovium::4 things}}.

{{c7::Arthrodesis}}

  • {{c7::Arthrodesis}} is a reliable operation for a painful or unstable joint; where stiffness does not seriously affect function, this is may be the treatment of choice.
  • Examples are the spine, tarsus, ankle, wrist, thumb metacarpophalangeal joint and the interphalangeal joints.

Arthroplasty

  • The surgical refashioning of a joint, aims to relieve pain and to retain or restore movement.
Types
  1. Excision arthroplasty
    • Sufficient bone is excised from the articulating parts of the joint to create a gap at which movement can occur.
    • Fibrous tissue forms in the gap.
    • e.g. {{c8::Trapeziectomy}}
  2. {{c9::Partial}} replacement
    • One articulating part only is replaced (e.g. a femoral prosthesis or hemiarthroplasty without an acetabular component); or one compartment of a joint is replaced (e.g. the medial half of the tibiofemoral joint)
  3. {{c9::Total joint}} replacement
    • Both the articulating parts of the joint are replaced by prosthetic implants

Soft Tissue Surgery

  • Tendon surgery
    • {{c1::Tenotomy}}: to cut tendon
    • Lengthen a tendon
    • Move or transpose a tendon
    • {{c2::Tenodesis}}: Stabilise tendon to bone
    • Repair a torn tendon
  • Ligament
    • Repair a ligament
    • Replace a ligament

Orthopaedic Practice

  • Adjuncts
    • Physical therapy
    • Mobility aids
    • Occupational Therapy: Home / environmental modifications
    • Orthosis
    • Prosthetics

Conservative treatment

  • Casts and slabs
    • Require close supervision
    • {{c1::Fracture}} treatment

Orthotics

  • An orthosis is an externally applied device that improves function by supporting, correcting or compensating for skeletal deformity or weakness
  • Examples:
    • Ankle-Foot {{c2::Orthosis (AFO)}}
    • Dynamic {{c2::splints}}
    • Insoles
    • Braces

Operative Treatment

Planning

  • Corrective osteotomies and implant positioning can be simulated on X-ray, paper cut-outs or templating software before the operation is undertaken.
  • Before new or complex reconstructive operations are undertaken they should, ideally, be rehearsed using artificial bones and joints. 3D printing of models could also be used.

Basic Orthopaedic Equipment

  • {{c1::Drill}}(for boring holes)
  • {{c1::Saw}}(for cutting cortical bones)
  • {{c1::Osteotomes}}(for cutting cancellous bone)
  • {{c1::Chisels}}(for shaping bones)
  • {{c1::Gouges}} (for removing bones)
  • Hammer, Plates, screws and screwdrivers (for fixing fractures)

{{c2::Intraoperative Fluoroscopy (C-arm)}}

  • {{c2::Intraoperative fluoroscopy}} is often helpful and sometimes essential.
  • Fracture reduction, osteotomy alignments and the positioning of implants and fixation devices can be checked before completing the procedure.
  • Radiation exposure precautions
  • Radio-lucent table

Magnification

  • The improved view minimizes the risk of inadvertent injury to structures and allows more accurate apposition of tissues during reconstruction.
  • Loupes (x2.5 to x3.5)
  • Microscope

Tourniquet

  • Operations on limbs can be done more rapidly and accurately if bleeding is prevented and a bloodless field is created.
  • This is usually achieved by the application of a tourniquet
  • A tourniquet pressure of {{c3::150}} mmHg above systolic is recommended for the lower limb and 80–100 mmHg above systolic for the upper limb
  • Elevation for 30 seconds or exsanguination
  • Tourniquet ⟶ ischaemia
  • 2 hours maximum
  • Complications
    • Nerve palsy
    • Chemical burns
    • Beware of patients with PVD

Theatre ventilation

  • Laminar air with {{c4::High Efficiency Particulate Air (HEPA)}} Filters

Surgical attire

  • Indicator gloves
  • Eye protection
  • Body-Exhaust system in some centers

Blood grouping basics (What is meant by blood grouping?) (pp. 5–7)

  • Definition
    • Blood Groups are antigens on the blood cell surface
    • 400 Blood groups described
    • ABO and Rh Blood groups are the most important:
      • discovered by Landsteiner in 1901
      • ABO expressed on many tissues (epithelial and endothelial cells) as well as RBC
      • Secreted in soluble form in body fluids
      • Rh group includes: CcDEe
  • Red cell antigens
    • Are there other clinically important blood groups?
      • Yes, Other clinically important groups e.g. {{c1::Duffy, Kell, Kidd and S}}

Red cell antigens → ABO system (pp. 8–30)

Molecular basis

  • The early glycoproteins (macromolecules) formed of a peptide backbone (15 amino acids) and sugars
  • Function of the peptide backbone
    • The peptide backbone doesn’t influence the serological specificity of the molecule, but holds sugars in chain
  • Function of the terminal monosaccharide of sugar chain
    • The terminal monosaccharide of the sugar chain is responsible for the specificity of the ABO antigens and are recognized by specific antibodies, anti-A or Anti-B

H gene and H substance

  • What is the H gene?
    • The H gene codes for a transferase that adds a {{c1::fucose}} to the precursor substance to become the H substance

A, B, O genes and enzymes

  • A and B groups and genes
    • A and B genes code for specific glucosyl transferases that add a sugar to the H substance
    • A gene codes for an enzyme that adds {{c1::N-Acetyl-D galactosamine (GalNAc)}}
    • B gene codes for an enzyme that adds {{c1::D-Galactose}}
  • The O gene is a {{c2::silent}} allele that does not alter the H substances

Bombay (hh) phenotype

  • What is the Bombay blood group? h/h
    • A very rare abnormality
    • High in area around Bombay in India
    • No {{c1::H}} gene (h/h), the substance H is not formed, the red cells cant react with any antibody of ABO or anti-H
    • Their sera contains a high concentration of anti-{{c2::H}}
  • Difference between O blood group and the Bombay blood group
    • Group O have {{c3::no antigens of A or B; however, they do possess H-antigen and can react with anti-H, but not anti-A or anti-B}}
    • You need one H gene at least otherwise the A or B genes will have no effect (Bombay phenotype)
Bombay can only receive from other bombays, can give to anyone ABO wise but not anyone Rh-wise

Inheritance

  • A, B and O genes on chromosome {{c1::9}}
  • The H and h genes on chromosome {{c1::19}}
  • A and B genes show Co-dominant inheritance
  • O gene is recessive

ABO system: Antibodies

  • Types
    • Naturally occurring antibodies
    • Immune antibodies
  • Naturally occurring antibodies
    • Present without any prior exposure of the individual to the foreign antigen and are present in all individuals except the {{c1::newborn infant (3–6 months from birth)}}
    • Antibodies are Ig{{c2::M}} and can agglutinate and activate complement
    • These are IgM antibodies and are complete i.e. they will agglutinate red cells suspended in saline
    • E.g {{c3::Anti-A, Anti-B, Anti-AB}}
  • Immune antibodies
    • Develop in response to introduction of red cells possessing antigens that the subjects lack
      • By transfusion or trans-placental passage during pregnancy
    • Usually Ig{{c4::G}}, warm antibodies
      • react optimally at body temperature
    • Most important immune antibodies are those of the rhesus system and in particular {{c5::anti-D}}
    • Clinical significance
      • Their clinical significance lies in the {{c6::haemolytic transfusion reactions and haemolytic disease to the newborn}}
      • Most important: in particular {{c5::anti-D}}

Clinical importance of the ABO system

  1. Incompatible transfusions can be {{c1::fatal}}.
  2. {{c2::Anti-A or Anti-B}} antibodies that cross the placenta can cause haemolytic disease of the newborn (only Ig{{c3::G}} not Ig{{c3::M}} – Ig{{c3::M}} antibodies do not cross the placenta).
  3. Solid organ transplantation.

Important definitions

  • Iso-agglutinins: antibodies that {{c1::agglutinate blood cells of some individuals of the same species}}.
  • Glycosyltransferases: enzymes that facilitate the {{c2::transfer of carbohydrate (sugar) molecules onto carbohydrate precursor molecules}}.
  • Immuno-dominant sugar: the sugar molecule that complete the {{c3::antigenic determinant when combined with the precursor substance}}.

Rh system (pp. 32–39)

  • Overview
    • The most complex with at least 50 antigens defined by specific Rhesus antibodies
    • Found only on red cells and are embedded in the red cell membrane and are important for the integrity of the membrane
    • The most important antigen is {{c1::D}} because it is immunogenic
  • Genetics and expression
    • Composed of two related structural genes, Rh D and Rh CE, which encode membrane protein that carry the D, Cc and Ee antigens
    • Rh D gene may either be present or absent, giving the Rh D+ or Rh D- phenotype, respectively
    • Mostly are immune (i.e. results from previous transfusion or pregnancy)
  • RhD specifics
    • RhD is the most important
    • Two genes for RhD groups:
      • D - codes for D antigen on RBC and is dominant
      • d - codes for no antigen and is recessive
      • dd = no D antigen = RhD negative
      • DD or Dd = D antigen present = RhD positive
      • {{c2::85}}% of people are RhD positive; {{c2::15}}% are RhD negative
  • Clinical significance of the Rh D
  • Rhesus hemolytic disease of the newborn
    • {{c3::A D negative woman sensitized through pregnancy by a D positive partner
    • This do not usually cause a problem for her first Rh D positive pregnancy
    • Subsequent Rh D positive pregnancies run the risk of developing rhesus haemolytic disease of the newborn::Describe what happens}}
    • Without prophylaxis, a second pregnancy has a 20% chance of fetal loss and with subsequent pregnancies the risk rises to 40%
  • Future transfusions in sensitized individuals
    • Patient must, in future, have {{c4::Rh D}} neg. blood
      • otherwise anti-D would react with Rh D pos. blood and causes {{c5::delayed haemolytic transfusion reaction as well as anaemia; high bilirubin; jaundice etc}}

Other blood group systems (pp. 40–43)

  • Examples: {{c1::Duffy, Kell, Kidd and S}}
  • Frequency/temperature
    • Less frequently of clinical importance
    • Antibodies of P, Lewis, MN system are not uncommon but, they usually react at low temperature
    • Immune antibodies against antigens of these systems are detected {{c2::infrequent::Frequent or not}}
  • Clinical significance
    • Many of the antigens are of low antigenicity and others e.g. Kell, although comparatively immunogenic, are of {{c3::relative low frequency::high or low frequency}} in multiply transfused patients

Blood ordering policies & sampling (pp. 44–47) 

  • Who is in charge of ordering blood transfusion?
    • Blood can be prescribed only by a {{c1::registered doctor}}
  • Blood request form (read)
    • The number of units
    • date and time of transfusions
    • For elective surgery, the hospital’s maximum surgical blood ordering schedule (MSBOS) should be consulted
    • Addressograph labels may be used on request forms, but not on samples
  • Sampling for blood transfusion
    • The conscious patient must be positively identified by {{c2::asking him/her to state the name and date of birth; this information must be checked with the wrist band::How to make sure}}
    • Samples must be labelled only {{c3::after::Before or after}} blood has been placed in the container
    • Information on the sample container must be handwritten and must include full patient identification as stated above and dated and signed by the sampler
    • N.B. Most immediate haemolytic transfusions occur due to {{c4::clerical/misidentification}} errors

Pre-transfusion testing (pp. 48–82)

Intro to Pre-transfusion testing

  • It is impossible to provide identical or fully matched red cells
  • Only two blood group systems are routinely taken into account - {{c1::ABO and RhD}}
  • Group donor and recipient
  • An antibody screen should be performed, in parallel with grouping, on the serum of patients
  • Why ABO matters
    • The ABO system is important because of the universal presence of naturally occurring antibodies
    • Antibodies are IgM and can agglutinate and activate complement
    • They are, therefore, able to cause {{c2::intravascular haemolysis}} if incompatible blood is transfused
Cross matching is also routine unless its an emergency

Pre-transfusion testing (pp. 48–82)

Blood grouping

  1. The red cells are tested against known antibodies (monoclonal) – forward group
  2. The plasma is then tested against known ABO and Rh cells for the ABO group – reverse group
Forward Blood grouping
  • ABO typing uses {{c1::anti-A and anti-B}} reagents. These are IgM/complete antibodies, mostly monoclonal, and will bind to the relevant red cell antigens leading to clumping or agglutination of red cells.
    • If the reaction is strong, lysis may occur.
    • Tests are carried out at room temp (20°C).
    • Where there is agglutination, then that means the {{c2::antigen is present}}
  • Figure (forward typing examples; AB Rh+):

    • “Agglutination in all three test tubes indicates that all three antigens are present in the blood: A, B and Rh. Accordingly, the blood type is {{c3::AB Rh+}}”
    • Figure key takeaway: Agglutination with anti-A, anti-B, anti-D → AB Rh+.
  • Figure (forward typing examples; AB Rh−):

    • “Agglutination in the test tubes containing A antibodies and B antibodies. Accordingly, the blood type is {{c3::AB Rh−}}.”
    • Figure key takeaway: Agglutination with anti-A and anti-B only → AB Rh−.
Reverse Blood grouping
  • The serum of the subject is also tested for the presence of {{c4::anti-A and anti-B}} by adding it to known group A, B and O cells (reverse grouping)
    • The pattern of reaction will indicate whether anti-A and/or anti-B is present
    • The results of the serum group should correspond to the red cell ABO group

    • Where there is agglutination that means {{c5::the anti antigen is there thus the original antigen is the other one, ie if the agglutination happens on the A cells that means the serum has anti-A and its a B type blood}}
  • Figure (Forward and Reverse Blood grouping):
    • Forward: Patient RBC + test antibodies
    • Reverse: Patient sera + test RBC cells

Pre-transfusion testing (pp. 48–82)

Rh D typing
  • c. Rh D typing uses anti-{{c1::D}}
    • Most examples of anti-D used nowadays are monoclonal IgM, agglutination denoting a positive reaction
    • It is usual to use two different anti-D reagents when typing for the Rh D antigen
  • Combined interpretation

  • {{c2::First one is A+, second is B-}}
Selection rules (ABO/Rh choice)
  • When can you give Rh positive blood to an Rh negative recipients?
    • in times of {{c3::shortage males and un-immunised elderly women can be given D positive blood}}
  • General selection {{c4::(read)::Read}}
    • Blood selected for transfusion should be ABO and D compatible but need not always to be identical
    • Rh positive blood can be given to unimmunised Rh negative male recipients
    • group AB patients can receive group A blood
    • D negative girls and women of childbearing age should always receive D negative blood

Antibody screening

  • Definition
    • Plasma from recipient is tested against panels of red cells where all important antigens are expressed
    • looking at the pattern of reactivity tells you which antibodies the recipient has
    • Sometimes you cannot immediately differentiate and more panels are needed
    • This is to see if the recipient has any {{c1::atypical antibodies e.g. Anti-M}}
    • These would have usually been acquired through pregnancy or previous transfusion
  • Screen outcome handling
If Positive If negative
The antibody must be {{c2::identified with the use of a large panel of red cells}} If the screen is negative, any donor blood which is ABO and D compatible can be given.
Donor units which {{c2::lack}} the corresponding antigen are chosen for
Cross matching with the recipient’s {{c2::serum}} prior to transfusion.


Compatibility testing (X-matching)

  • So far, have done a Group and Atypical Antibody screen
  • This is enough in some situations to issue blood
  • Rules differ in different hospitals
  • However, often a {{c3::cross-match}} is needed

Pre-transfusion testing: Compatibility testing — Indirect antiglobulin test (generated)

  • Indirect antiglobulin test
    • To {{c3::detect antibodies directed against red blood cell antigens, in preparation for a blood transfusion, or during pregnancy and at delivery}}.
    • Looks for antibodies against foreign red cell antigens in the patient’s serum.

Pre-transfusion testing: Compatibility testing — Direct antiglobulin test (generated)

  • Direct antiglobulin test
    • To detect the {{c1::cause of haemolytic anaemia, to investigate a transfusion reaction, and to diagnose haemolytic disease of the newborn}}.
    • Also known as {{c2::Coombs}} test or {{c2::DAT}}.
    • Looks for antibodies already stuck on the cells of the patient in vivo.

StudyFix Simplification: IAT checks the patient’s serum for antibodies against foreign RBC antigens by incubating with test cells and adding anti-globulin to see if they agglutinate.

StudyFix Simplification: DAT checks patient RBCs for in vivo–bound antibodies by adding anti-globulin and seeing if they agglutinate.

Pancreas: Overview & Organization (p.6–12, merged)

  • Anatomy
    • Elongated {{c1::retro::retro or intro}}peritoneal organ
    • Large head near the {{c2::duodenum}}; more narrow body and tail extending to the left
  • Mixed exocrine–endocrine gland
    • Exocrine
      • Secretion of {{c3::alkaline fluid (bicarbonate ions)}} into the {{c4::duodenum}} via the pancreatic duct → {{c5::buffers acid concentration of chyme::What does it do}}
      • Secretion of {{c3::digestive enzymes}}
    • Endocrine
      • Secretion of {{c6::insulin and glucagon}} into blood
      • Islets of {{c6::Langerhans}}: discrete clumps surrounded by exocrine tissue
  • Stroma and Lobulation
    • Surrounded by a {{c7::very thin connective tissue capsule that invaginates to form septae}}
    • Septae serve as scaffolding for {{c8::large blood vessels and divide the pancreas into lobules (closely packed acini)}}

Exocrine Pancreas — Acini & Duct System (p.11–12, p.13–16, merged)

  • Acini
    • Each acinus consists of several serous cells around a very small lumen
    • Acinar cells: {{c1::pyramidal serous}} cells with {{c1::round basal}} nuclei, {{c1::basophilic}} cytoplasm (rER), numerous {{c1::apical eosinophilic zymogen granules}} (secretory granules)

  • Duct System
    • Intercalated ducts: {{c2::simple squamous}} epithelium
      • Initial cells extend into the acinar lumen as {{c3::centroacinar}} cells (pale-staining; unique to pancreas)

    • Intralobular ducts: {{c4::small cuboidal cells}}
    • Interlobular ducts: larger, lined by {{c5::mucin-producing columnar}} cells
    • Main pancreatic duct (of {{c6::Wirsung}}) and accessory pancreatic duct (of {{c6::Santorini}})
      • Wirsung joins the {{c7::common bile duct (CBD)}} and opens at the {{c8::major}} duodenal papilla
      • Santorini ends separately at a {{c8::minor}} duodenal papilla
      • Both lined by {{c9::mucin-producing columnar cells with greater goblet cells}}

Endocrine Pancreas (Islets of Langerhans) (p.17–19)

  • The endocrine pancreas secretes hormones that regulate blood glucose levels

  • Islet cells: {{c1::polygonal/rounded, smaller, more lightly stained than surrounding acinar cells; arranged in cords separated by fenestrated capillaries::Describe how they are, what they are seperated by, how they arranged}}

  • Cell types and immunohistochemistry
    • {{c2::α (A)}}cells: secrete glucagon; usually peripheral
    • {{c2::β (B)}} cells: produce insulin; most numerous, central
    • {{c2::δ (D)}} cells: secrete somatostatin; scattered, less abundant
    • Adult pancreas lacks {{c3::G}} cells, however gastrinomas are {{c4::common}}
    • Islet cells: generally positive for {{c5::synaptophysin}}, {{c5::chromogranin}}, CD{{c5::56}}; typically {{c6::pancytokeratin}} negative

Pancreatitis — Definition & Types (p.27–28)

  • Pancreatitis: inflammation of the pancreas caused by {{c1::enzymatic autodigestion}} of pancreatic tissue; classified into acute and chronic
    • Acute pancreatitis: {{c2::reversible; function can return to normal if underlying cause is removed::Are they reversible or not}}
    • Chronic pancreatitis: {{c2::irreversible destruction of exocrine pancreatic parenchyma}}

Acute Pancreatitis — Epidemiology & Causes (p.28–29)

  • Definition/severity: Reversible inflammatory disorder; severity ranges from focal edema and fat necrosis}}focal edema and fat necrosis to widespread hemorrhagic parenchymal necrosis
  • Incidence: 10–20 per 100,000 in western society
  • What causes acute pancreatitis
    • {{c1::Chronic alcoholism and biliary disease}} (70% of cases)
    • {{c1::Idiopathic}} (20% of cases)
    • ERCP, trauma, hyperlipidaemia, hypercalcaemia, drugs, infections (e.g., mumps, CMV), ischaemia (e.g., atheroembolism)

Acute Pancreatitis — Core Pathogenesis (Trypsin Activation) (p.30–31)

  • Trypsin is produced as trypsinogen (zymogen); normally activated in the duodenum by {{c1::enteropeptidase}}
  • Early/inappropriate activation of trypsin → {{c2::acute}} pancreatitis
    • Trypsin activates itself and other proenzymes (e.g., {{c3::phospholipases, elastases}}) → autodigestion
    • Trypsin → activation of factor {{c4::XII}} ({{c4::Hageman}} factor) → clotting

Acute Pancreatitis — Three Initiating Pathways of Enzyme Activation (p.31–34)

  • Duct obstruction pathway (p.32)
    • Pancreatic duct obstruction →{{c1::↑ intraductal pressure → accumulation of enzyme-rich interstitial fluid::What happens in obstruction?}}
    • {{c1::Lipase (secreted active) → local fat necrosis
    • Injured tissues (periacinar myofibroblasts, leukocytes) release proinflammatory cytokines → local inflammation and interstitial edema (leaky microvasculature) → edema further compromises local blood flow → ischemic injury to acinar cells}}
  • Direct acinar cell injury (p.33)
    • Alcohol, drugs, trauma, ischemia, viruses → {{c2::release of intracellular proenzymes and lysosomal hydrolases → enzyme activation (intra- or extracellular) → acinar cell injury::What happens}}
  • Defective intracellular transport (p.34)
    • In normal acinar cells, digestive enzymes (intended for zymogen granules) and hydrolytic enzymes intended for lysosomes) are transported in discrete pathways after ER synthesis
    • In some animal models, pancreatic proenzymes and lysosomal hydrolases become {{c3::packaged together}} thus leading to activation and autodigestion
  • Alcohol question prompt: By which mechanism alcohol causes acute pancreatitis? {{c4::3 Mechanisms}}

Acute Pancreatitis — Morphology (Gross & Micro) (p.36–45, merged)

  • Acute interstitial pancreatitis (mild form) (p.37)
    • {{c1::Pancreas swollen (edema); superficial white plaques of fat necrosis which chalky white appearance::Describe the photo below}}

  • Acute necrotizing pancreatitis (more severe) (p.38–39)

    • {{c2::Black-brown areas of hemorrhage and necrosis; chalky, yellow-white fat necrosis::Describe the photo above}}
    • Peritoneum: usually {{c3::brown}}, {{c3::serous}} fluid, globules of {{c3::fat}}, {{c3::chalky}} deposits
    • Sectioned pancreas: {{c4::dark hemorrhage in head; focal pale fat necrosis in peripancreatic fat}} (arrow)

    • In the photo above, if treated, the nectroized area would be fibrosed but the rest works out, in the photo below, its all done for

Acute Pancreatitis — Morphology (Gross & Micro) (p.36–45, merged)

  • Histology (p.40–45)
    • Acute Interstitial Pancreatitis (less severe): {{c1::neutrophilic}} infiltrates with early fat {{c1::necrosis}}

    • Acute pancreatitis: interstitial {{c2::oedema}}, fat {{c2::necrosis}}, {{c2::haemorrhage}}

    • Acute Necrotizing Pancreatitis (more severe): Has {{c3::Acute}} inflammation, fat {{c3::necrosis}}, and {{c3::necrotic}} parenchyma

Acute Pancreatitis — Clinical Course & Complications (p.47–49)

  • Clinical course
    • {{c1::Abdominal}} pain ({{c2::cardinal; mild → severe upper abdominal pain, may radiate to back::Describe the pain}})
    • Nausea, vomiting, tachycardia, high WCC, hypotension, oliguria, steatorrhea
    • 3–4 fold increase in plasma {{c3::lipase (48 h)}} or {{c3::amylase (24 h)}}
    • Most episodes mild/self-limiting, but {{c4::1/5}} severe → may progress to shock (due to {{c5::hemorrhage and kinins released into blood), peritonitis, organ failure, death}}
    • Severe cases: {{c6::Cullen’s}} sign ({{c7::periumbilical}} hemorrhage) and {{c8::Grey Turner’s}} sign ({{c9::flank}} hemorrhage)
    • Mild episodes: resolve with {{c10::supportive treatment and minimal organ dysfunction}}
    • Severe episodes: considerable pancreatic {{c11::necrosis}} ({{c12::CT with contrast}} is investigation of choice), {{c11::clinical organ failure}}, death

Acute Pancreatitis — Clinical Course & Complications (p.47–49)

  • Complications of acute pancreatitis
    • Local: {{c1::pancreatic pseudocyst, abscess, duodenal obstruction}}
    • Systemic: {{c1::multiorgan failure, DIC, ARDS, infection}}
  • Medical emergency (full-blown cases) (p.50)
    • Mortality from severe acute pancreatitis: {{c2::30}}%
    • Early deaths: come from {{c3::organ failure}}
    • Late deaths: come from {{c3::infection (necrotic debris may become infected)}}
    • {{c4::5}}% die from shock within first week

Chronic Pancreatitis — Definition, Epidemiology, Presentation (p.50–52)

  • Definition: Sustained inflammation → irreversible exocrine parenchymal destruction and fibrosis → loss of function; late: endocrine {{c1::parenchyma}} also destroyed
  • Incidence: ~10 per 100,000 in western populations
  • Presentation
    • Usually {{c2::5}}th decade
    • {{c2::Episodic::Describe the}} abdominal pain (recurrent bouts of acute pancreatitis)
    • {{c2::Mal}}digestion
    • Diabetes mellitus
    • Occasional jaundice

Chronic Pancreatitis — Pathogenesis & Morphology (p.52–56)

  • Pathogenesis
    • {{c1::Chronic alcoholism}} (most common)
    • Biliary disease
    • Chronic hypercalcaemia
    • Hyperlipidaemia
    • Genetic factors: rare {{c2::autosomal dominant}} hereditary pancreatitis (predisposes to {{c3::pancreatic carcinoma}}) and {{c4::cystic fibrosis}}
    • 40% idiopathic
    • Repeated bouts of inflammation → parenchymal damage → fibrosis, gland atrophy, distortion, calcification
  • Morphology
    • Gross
      • Initially: {{c5::enlarged pancreas with septal fibrosis and intra-pancreatic fat necrosis}}
      • Advanced: {{c5::shrunken, firm pancreas with extensive fibrosis, calcification, variable dilatation of pancreatic ducts}}
      • {{c6::Pseudocysts}} common
    • Microscopy (three cardinal features)
      • {{c7::Fibrosis}}
      • Loss of {{c7::exocrine glands}}
      • {{c7::Chronic}} inflammation (lymphocytes, plasma cells, monocytes)

Chronic Pancreatitis — Pathogenesis & Morphology (p.52–56)

Figures (p.54–56)
  • Gross: Pancreatic head shows diffuse sclerosis with small calculi in main pancreatic duct;
  • Ectatic ducts, fibrosis, calcifications inside ducts

  • Histology: {{c1::Fibrosis}}; {{c1::atrophic}} lobules with {{c1::lobular}} arrangements of small ducts; {{c1::chronic}} inflammation; relative sparing of {{c1::endocrine islets }}(thick arrow)

    N.B

Chronic Pancreatitis — Complications (p.57–58)

  • ~{{c1::70}}% → local complications
    • {{c2::Pseudocyst
    • Biliary or intestinal obstruction
    • Haemorrhage
    • Ascites
    • Gastric varices}}
  • Metabolic/functional
    • {{c3::Diabetes}} due to islet damage (also common)
    • {{c3::Fat}} malabsorption from acinar damage → {{c4::steatorrhea}} and {{c4::decrease}} in fat-soluble vitamins
  • Oncologic
    • Increased risk of {{c5::pancreatic carcinoma}}, probably relates to alcohol and smoking

Pancreatic Pseudocyst (p.59–64, merged)

  • Definition & frequency
    • {{c1::75}}% of pancreatic cystic lesions
    • Circumscribed collection of fluid rich in {{c2::pancreatic enzymes leaked from damaged ducts, blood, and necrotic tissue}}
    • Wall made of: {{c3::granulation tissue}} (acute) → {{c3::fibrous tissue}} (chronic) forming a cystic space
    • Develop after pancreatitis (acute or chronic) or trauma
    • Not a true cyst because {{c4::no epithelial lining}}
    • Presents with {{c5::persistent abdominal pain & raised pancreatic enzyme levels}}
  • Diagnosis (p.60)
    • {{c6::Imaging ± biopsy
      }}
  • Gross features (p.61–63)
    • Usually {{c7::solitary}}; up to {{c7::10}} cm in diameter; within or adjacent to the pancreas

    • Necrotic debris encased by {{c8::granulation tissue and fibrous walls lacking an epithelial lining}}
    • Often {{c8::calcified}}; {{c8::no}} epithelial lining

  • Outcome (p.64)
    • Many spontaneously {{c9::resolve}}
    • Secondary infection → {{c10::abscess, peritonitis}}
    • Erosion into blood vessels → {{c11::hemorrhage of splenic artery}}
    • {{c12::Compression}} effects in large pseudocysts
    • Perforation into adjacent structures

Tumors of the Pancreas — Classification (p.66–67)

  • Tumors of {{c1::exocrine}} pancreas (~95%)
  • Neuroendocrine tumors (balance)
  • Tumors of the exocrine pancreas
    • Non-cystic epithelial tumors
      • Ductal derivation (M): {{c2::Pancreatic adenocarcinoma / pancreatic carcinoma / duct adenocarcinoma (> 90% of pancreatic tumors)}}
      • Acinar derivation (M): {{c2::Acinar cell}} carcinoma
      • Pancreatoblastoma (M)
    • Cystic epithelial tumors (<5% of pancreatic neoplasms) — {{c3::Cystadenoma and cystadenocarcinoma}}
      • {{c4::Mucinous}} (B, Bo, M)
      • {{c4::Serous}} (B, M)
      • {{c4::Solid-pseudopapillary neoplasm}} (Bo, M)
    • Non-epithelial tumors (B, M)
    • Metastatic tumors

AbbreviationMeaningBehavior Type
BBenignNon-cancerous, doesn’t invade or spread
BoBorderlineHas potential to become malignant
MMalignantCancerous, invasive, can metastasize

Pancreatic Carcinoma — Definition, Incidence, Presentation, Site (p.68–71)

  • Definition: {{c1::Malignant epithelial}} tumor derived from the pancreatic ducts; represents 90% of all pancreatic tumors
  • Incidence: 10 per 100,000 per annum; {{c2::5}}th most common cause of cancer death in western society
  • Presentation
    • Usually ≥{{c3::60}} years, more common in {{c3::males}}
    • Insidious onset with non-specific features: abdominal pain (secondary), weight loss, anorexia, vomiting
    • 50% {{c4::jaundice}}
    • 10% {{c4::Trousseau’s}} sign
    • {{c4::Courvoisier’s}} sign
    • Pain secondary to {{c5::nerve compression or invasion}}, at which point cure is not possible
    • {{c6::NOT tender::tender or not}} tho, big diffrential compared to stone
    • Diagnosis by imaging and tissue biopsy
  • Site distribution & gross (p.70)
    • Site: {{c7::Head}} (60%), {{c7::Body}} (15%), {{c7::Tail}} (5%), Diffuse (20%)

    • Gross features: {{c8::gritty, hard pale mass with poorly defined infiltrating edges; eventual invasion into adjacent structures}}

Pancreatic Carcinoma — Definition, Incidence, Presentation, Site (p.68–71)

      Histology & infiltration (p.71–72)
      • Usually {{c1::moderately to poorly}} differentiated adenocarcinomas with {{c1::infiltrating}} pattern of cell clusters
      • {{c2::Well differentiated adenocarcinoma::What is the diffrentiation of the picture below}} 

      • {{c3::Moderate to poorly}} differentiated invasive adenocarcinoma with marked {{c3::desmoplastic stromal}} reaction; tumor cell nuclei are {{c3::hyperchromatic with nuclear pleomorphism}}

      • Pancreatic carcinoma with {{c4::Perineural}} invasion illustrated|

Pancreatic Carcinoma — Grading & Staging (p.73–74)

  • Grading
    • Based on morphology; attempts to predict aggressiveness on morphological appearance
    • Cellular and architectural features:
      • {{c1::Mitoses
      • Pleomorphism
      • Differentiation
      • Necrosis
      • Vascular invasion
      • Molecular markers}}
  • Staging (based on tumor spread: pathological & radiological)
    • Helps determine treatment (surgery ± chemotherapy, DXT)
    • {{c2::Tumor(T1–4)
      • Size
      • Local extent (invading adjacent organs)
    • Nodes (T1–2 or 3)
      • Local vs distant
    • Metastases
      • Present/absent}}

Pancreatic Carcinoma — Outcome (p.75)

  • Generally considered {{c1::fatal::Fatal or not}}
  • Usually {{c2::late}} presentation due to late and nonspecific symptoms
  • Often pain secondary to nerve compression/invasion (cure usually not possible at this point)
  • Overall 5-year survival: {{c3::3–4}}% (early stage with radical resection may be {{c3::25}}%)
  • {{c3::15}}% are resectable at diagnosis; surgery often for palliation
  • Often spread locally into {{c4::peritoneum, spine, liver}}
  • Often already metastatic — {{c4::lungs and bone}} at diagnosis

Gastric Cancer — Predisposing Factors

  • {{c1::Adenomatous}} polyps
  • {{c2::Chronic}} gastritis and {{c2::Peptic}} ulcer
  • Blood group {{c3::A}}
  • Diet: Same as Cancer {{c4::oesophagus}}
    • Smoked food and Food containing {{c5::nitrates}}

Gastric Cancer — Aetiology

  • Genetic factors:
    • Autoimmune gastritis [NETs and gastric cancer through hyperplasia of {{c6::G}} cells]
    • Family history (8–10%)
  • Host factors:
    • {{c7::H. pylori}} infection [chronic gastritis → atrophy → intestinal metaplasia
    • {{c8::Hypo}}chlorhydria
    • {{c9::Partial gastrectomy::What kind of gasterctomy}}
  • Environmental Factors:
    • Smoking
    • {{c10::Nitrates}} from food (salted or smoked food)
    • Nutritional deficiencies – low fruit and vegetable intake

Gross Picture — Bormann Classification (Types I–IV)

  • Type I — {{c1::Polypoid: Well circumscribed polypoid tumors::What is it and describe what it looks}}
  • Type II — {{c2::Fungating: Fungating tumors with marked central infiltration::What is it and describe what it looks}}
  • Type III — {{c3::Ulcerated: Ulcerated tumors with infiltrative margins::What is it and describe what it looks}}
  • Type IV — {{c4::Infiltrating: Diffusely infiltrated tumors::What is it and describe what it looks}}

Gastric Carcinoma — Microscopic Picture (Types)

  • Microscopic types
    • Adenocarcinoma
      • {{c1::Intestinal}} type → forms {{c2::glands}}
      • {{c3::Diffuse (Dis-cohesive)}} type → isolated cells

      • Mucoid carcinoma
      • Anaplastic carcinoma

      • {{c6::Small cell}}neuroendocrine carcinoma
