Herditary [sic] Diffuse Gastric Cancer [DGC] (linitis plastica) — CDH1 Testing & Management

  • When to consider CDH1 testing in the family?
    • Initial criteria developed by the International Gastric Cancer Linkage Consortium required:
      1. {{c1::Two or more documented cases of diffuse gastric cancer in first- or second-degree relatives}}
      2. {{c1::At least one diagnosed before the age of 50, or three or more first- or second-degree relatives with diffuse gastric cancer at any age}}
  • How to deal with a CDH1 mutation carrier?
    • {{c2::Endoscopic}} surveillance for Diffuse Gastric Cancer is inadequate
    • Carriers are sometimes advised to undergo prophylactic {{c3::gastrectomy}} to remove their risk of DGC.
    • Female carriers also have a 60% lifetime risk of {{c4::lobular breast}} carcinoma.

Clinical Course & Spread (Gastric Carcinoma)

  • Local spread:
    • Depth of infiltration
    • Penetration into adjacent organs
    • breach of serosa
    • peritoneal carcinosis
    • Extension into oesophagus and duodenum
  • Metastases:
    • Lymph nodes
    • Distant (liver → lungs, ovary (Kruckenberg))
    • Note: “GIT tumor the spreads to ovary is {{c1::kruckenberg}} tumor”

Complications (Gastric Carcinoma)

  1. Gastrointestinal bleeding → {{c1::Haematemesis (vomiting of blood) and melena (dark fecal matter containing digested blood)}}
  2. {{c1::Pyloric}} obstruction
  3. Hypo{{c2::chlorhydria}} and {{c2::intrinsic factor}} deficiency
  4. Anemia due to {{c3::hemorrhage and intrinsic factor deficiency}}

Gastrointestinal Stromal Tumor (GIST) — Main Features & Epidemiology

  • Main features
    • Most common mesenchymal tumor of the gastrointestinal tract
    • Arises from or shares common phenotype with the {{c1::interstitial cells of Cajal}} within the {{c1::myenteric}} plexus of the {{c1::muscularis propria}}
    • The {{c2::stomach}} is the most common site of GIST (60%) followed by the {{c2::ileum and jejunum}} (30%)…
    • Micro / mini / subclinical GIST: minute growths (1–10 mm) of interstitial cells of Cajal / GIST-like cells
  • Epidemiology:
    • M:F = {{c3::1:1}}, no clear sex predilection
    • Mean age at diagnosis: 60–65 years old
  • Prognosis correlates with:
    • Tumor {{c4::size}}
    • {{c4::Mitotic}} index
    • Location ({{c4::gastric}} GISTs are less aggressive than those in the small intestine)

GIST — Pathogenesis & Molecular Notes

  • Mutations
    • {{c1::C-KIT (KIT)}}: mutations of gene encoding tyrosine kinase {{c1::C-KIT}} (receptor for stem cell factor) (75–80%)
    • {{c2::PDGFRA}}: mutations that activate platelet-derived growth factor receptor A (5%)
    • Thus: activating mutations in tyrosine kinase
  • Cell of origin
    • GISTs appear to arise from, or share a common stem cell with, the {{c3::interstitial cells of Cajal}}
    • Interstitial cells of Cajal express c-KIT and are located in the muscularis propria; they serve as pacemaker cells for gut peristalsis.
  • Slide note: “if C-Kit is negative then we do the {{c4::PDGFRA}} test”

GIST — Diagnosis (Clinical/Imaging/Pathology)

  • Most common presentation: {{c1::gastrointestinal}} bleeding or {{c1::abdominal}} pain
  • Incidental findings during workup for other issues:
    • CT: usually shows a {{c2::solid, heterogeneous}} mass
    • Endoscopy: shows as a subepithelial lesion
    • Endoscopic ultrasound: reveals a {{c3::hypoechoic solid}} mass
  • Definitive diagnosis: only through {{c4::histologic}} examination

GIST — Morphology (Gross & Microscopic)

  • Gross picture:
    • {{c1::Solitary, well-circumscribed, fleshy, submucosal / intramural}} mass
    • Mean size 6 cm {0.4 cm – 40 cm}
    • Metastases may form multiple small serosal nodules or fewer large nodules in the liver
  • Microscopic picture:
    • 3 morphologic types:
    • {{c2::Spindle}} (70%)

    • {{c2::Epithelioid}} (20%)

      Mixed (10%)
  • GISTs can be composed of {{c3::thin, elongated}} spindle cells or {{c3::plumper}} epithelioid cells or a mixture of both
{{c1::Ulcerated GIST originating from the muscle layer::Describe}}

GIST — Morphology (Gross & Microscopic)

    • “GISTs originate from the interstitial cells of Cajal”
    • “They may show differentiation towards {{c1::smooth muscle cells, neural cells, mixed (smooth muscle and neural), or neither cell types}}”
    • “Most specific marker: {{c2::DOG1}}”
    • “Most useful marker: {{c3::c-KIT (CD117)}} {targeted therapy}”

GIST — Prognosis & Treatment

  • Prognosis
    • GIST can have a clinically {{c1::malignant::malignant or benign}} behavior
    • Depends on: {{c2::tumor size, mitotic rate, site of origin(important)}}
    • Intraoperative tumor rupture → poorer prognosis
    • {{c3::Complete}} surgical resection improves local recurrence rate and overall survival
    • {{c3::Incomplete}} resection → higher risk of recurrence
  • Treatment
    • Most GISTs → surgical resection
    • {{c4::Imatinib mesylate (Gleevec)::Name the drug}}: tyrosine kinase inhibitor of {{c5::KIT and PDGFRα}}
      • “Gleevec is a Tyrosine Kinase (TK) inhibitor”
      • “Small molecule targets Receptor tyrosine kinase (RTK); they are high affinity cell surface receptors for many growth factors, cytokines and hormones”
      • “New mutations allow resistance to drug”

Gastric Lymphoma — Overview & Types

  • Primary lymphomas comprise nearly {{c1::5}}% of all gastric malignancies
  • Types:
  • Lymphomas of mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue (MALT), or {{c2::MALTomas}} (the most common type)
    • “could be Caused by {{c3::H pylori}}”
    • “orgininates from the marginal zone, is a secondary lymphycotic infilatrainot from the irritation of H pylori”
    • “the most important thing for diagnosis is that the lymhocytes are intraepithelial thus becoming an intraepihelial lesion”
  • Diffuse large B cell lymphoma

MALT Lymphoma — Definition & Key Histology

  • Definition
    • A gastric lymphoma composed of morphologically heterogeneous small {{c1::B}}-cells
    • The infiltrate is in the marginal zone of the reactive B-cell follicles and extends into the {{c2::interfollicular}} region
    • Neoplastic cells typically infiltrate the epithelium, forming {{c3::lymphoepithelial}} lesions
  • Microscopy (expanded slide)

    • {{c4::Dense, monotonous population of lymphoid with residual germinal centers and lymphoepithelial lesions::Describe and point out which kind of legion is present above}}
    • May have {{c5::plasmacytoid}} differentiation
    • Common findings:
      • {{c6::lymphoepithelial}} lesions (infiltration of glandular epithelium by lymphocytes) or follicular colonization
      • Adjacent mucosa:
        • epithelial erosion (61%)
        • intestinal metaplasia (59%)
        • H. pylori (57%)
        • lymphoid follicles (39%)
        • atrophy (37%)
        • atypical regenerative changes (19%)
        • dysplasia (4%)

MALT Lymphoma — Treatment & Clinical Course

  • Treatment
    • {{c1::Antiobiotics}} for H. pylori gastritis (patients may have positive serology even if H. pylori negative by histology; these patients may also {{c2::respond}} to treatment)
    • H. pylori eradication therapy produces a long term favorable outcome
    • Radiation therapy
    • However, t({{c3::11;18}})({{c3::q21;21}}) tumors unlikely to respond only to H. pylori eradication
  • MALT, clinically
    • Usually indolent
    • When it spreads, tends to involve other mucosal sites
    • 5-year survival is 94%
  • To sum-up MALT Lymphoma
    • Extranodal neoplasm of B cells associated with ‘acquired lymphoid tissue’
    • 8% of GI malignancies
    • {{c4::Stomach}} the most common site for Extra-Nodal Lymphoma (20%) & most are MALT Lymphomas
    • 80% arise on the background of H pylori-associated gastritis (acquired MALT)
    • Heterogeneous B cell morphology
    • No specific immunophenotype
    • Clonal proliferation of B cells in mucosa with lymphoepithelial lesions
    • 50% may be treated with antibiotics [for H pylori with excellent remissions] Antigen-driven T cells support B cell growth initially
    • Tumours with trisomy 3, translocation t(11;18) do not respond
    • Indolent clinical course

The Oesophagus (Anatomy & Histology)

  • A 25 cm TUBE connecting the pharynx to stomach
  • It enters the abdomen via the diaphragmatic hiatus
  • The oesophageal sphincter is physiological smooth muscle
  • The crura of the diaphragm is at the angle of the gastro-oesophageal junction
  • Layers:
    • Mucosa (made of epithelium, then lamina propria then muscularis mucosae, then the submucosa)
    • Submucosa
    • Muscularis propria

  • Epithelium (Mucosa):
    • The lining epithelium is: {{c1::Stratified squamous epithelium}}
    • State true or false
      • “The mucosal lining is Keratinized stratified squamous epithelium”
        False, it is non-keratinized
  • Submucosa: {{c2::glands}}
  • Muscular layers:
    • Longitudinal and circular muscle layers
    • 5% skeletal muscle
    • 45% mixed muscle
    • 50% smooth muscle
  • Innervation:
    • Extrinsic: {{c3::Vagus & sympathetic}}
  • Submucosal gland figure labeling:

    • G: {{c4::Submucous glands of oesophagus}}
    • D: {{c4::duct}}

The Gastro-Oesophageal Junction (GOJ): A Medically Important Region

  • {{c1::Dysphagia}}(difficulty swallowing)
  • {{c1::Acid reflux}} (manifested as heart burn)
  • Barrett’s oesophagus / intestinal metaplasia:
    • What is metaplasia? [question]
    • What is intestinal metaplasia? [question]
    • What are the complications of intestinal metaplasia? [question]

Dysphagia

  • Dysphagia (difficulty swallowing) ⟶ a sensation of impairment of the normal progression of the bolus from the mouth into the stomach)

Oesophageal Pathology 

Hiatus Hernia

  • Definition: Partial or total herniation of the stomach or gastro-oesophageal junction through the diaphragmatic hiatus
  • Epidemiology: 25% of adults, age 50’s, 10% of patients are asymptomatic
  • Aetiology (cause):
    • {{c1::Increased intra-abdominal pressure: obesity, pregnancy, low residue Diet, trauma
    • Diaphragmatic laxity: previous surgery, connective tissue disorders::2 things}}
  • Types of herniation:

    • {{c2::Sliding (Axial)}} — 95% of hiatus hernias
      • Involves the stomach and the Gastro-oesophageal junction
    • {{c2::Rolling (Para-oesophageal)}} — 5% of hiatus hernias
      • Only the stomach “usually the {{c3::greater }}curvature”
        • [the whole stomach may herniate]
  • Complications: result from {{c4::reflux oesophagitis}}

Oesophageal Pathology 

Oesophagitis

  • Definition: An inflammatory condition caused by damage to the oesophageal mucosa
  • Symptoms:
    • Oesophageal: {{c1::Heartburn, regurgitation +/− dysphagia}}
    • Extra-oesophageal: {{c1::persistent cough, asthma, bronchitis, laryngitis, precordial pain}}
  • Most common cause: {{c2::GORD [gastro-oesophageal reflux disease]}}
  • Other causes include: infections, chemicals, alcohol, radiation therapy
  • Role of endoscopy in diagnosis:
    • May be seen grossly yet remember that {{c3::50–60}}% of symptomatic patients with evidence of reflux have normal mucosa endoscopically
    • Take a biopsy, so that you could see it microscopically ( v clear in the microscope)
So treating the GORD would treat the oesophagitis

Gastro-Oesophageal Reflux Disease (GORD)

General

  • Most common cause of oesophagitis
  • Due to reflux of gastric or duodenal contents into lower esophagus
  • Esophageal squamous epithelium is prone to injury from acid
  • May be erosive or nonerosive
  • Long term consequences are bleeding (almost never massive), stricture, Barrett esophagus with possible Barrett ulcer

Aetiology

  • Increased intra-abdominal pressure: obesity, pregnancy, low residue diet, chronic coughing...
  • Gastro-oesophageal laxity: alcohol, h. hernia, smoking

Pathophysiology

  • Transient reflux is normal, especially after meals and is usually asymptomatic
  • When the threshold is exceeded, {{c1::GORD}} occurs
  • Chronic exposure to gastric juices impairs reparative capacity of esophageal mucosa; gastric acid injury to mucosa is critical to pathophysiology of GORD; bile reflux may also contribute

Diagnosis

  • Symptoms:
    • Heartburn, dysphagia
      • Pain may be mistaken for {{c2::myocardial infarction}}, note that MI can also be misdiagnosed as reflux
    • Remember: severity of symptoms is NOT related to histology
  • Endoscopy:

    • {{c3::Erythema or edema}}
    • {{c3::Linear}} ulcers at distal esophagus, often with exudate
    • Normal in 50–60% of symptomatic patients — thus biopsy is {{c4::required::required or not}} if clinically suggestive of reflux esophagitis even if endoscopy is normal
  • Histologic examination:
    • Multiple biopsies are indicated

Microscopy 


Very mild disease

  • Hyperemia may be the only finding
  • Up to 1/3 of patients with chronic GORD symptoms have normal endoscopic findings

 Acute lesions

  • Intraepithelial {{c5::neutrophils, eosinophils and T-lymphocytes}}
  • Basal cell {{c5::hyperplasia}}
  • Elongated {{c5::RR}}
  • Epithelial {{c5::erosion and ulceration}}
  • {{c5::Granulation}}tissue
  • Reactive {{c5::atypia}}

Microscopy — Case-based figure (biopsy features)

  • Clinical vignette: A 45 year old man is admitted to the hospital with heartburn and dysphagia. Endoscopy appears within normal. However, multiple endoscopic biopsies are taken for microscopy. WHY? This is the microscopic picture of one of the biopsies. What are the features to which the numbers point?

  • Answers:
  • {{c6::1: Increased epithelial thickness
  • 2: Basal and suprabasal hyperplasia
  • 3: Intraepithelial inflammatory cells: Neutrophils – eosinophils – lymphocytes
    • P.s. Usually its around  9 layers, if its more then its thick indicative of hyperplasia}}

Microscopy — Chronic lesions

  • Intraepithelial {{c7::eosinophils and lymphocytes}}
  • ++ Basal layer {{c7::hyperplasia}}
  • ++ Elongation of {{c7::papillae}}
  • {{c7::Congestion}}
  • {{c7::Oedema::Describe the microscopic picture}}

These are all ->Reactive changes to continuous injury (acid reflux) which cause

  • {{c8::Columnar metaplasia}}— Protective response within Lower 1/3 of oesophagus

Complications of chronic GORD

  • Reflux of gastric contents into lower oesophagus ⟶ oesophagitis
    • {{c9::Mucosal erosion/ulceration
    • Haemorrhage +/− haematemesis/melaena
    • Stricture formation
    • Risk of carcinoma
    • Barrett’s oesophagus (columnar metaplasia with risk of adenocarcinoma)}}

Barrett’s Oesophagus

  • A benign condition of the oesophagus, characterized by {{c1::glandular}} metaplasia of the distal oesophageal mucosa
  • Considered a protective mechanism
  • It affects:
    • 10% of patients with gastro-oesophageal reflux disease
    • 40% of peptic strictures
  • Patients may be asymptomatic
  • Diagnosis of Barrett’s oesophagus is {{c2::endoscopic::using what}} and is based on microscopic findings
    ----- 
    • GOJ = Gastro-oesophageal junction; BO = Barrett’s oesophagus
  • Microscopy:
    • Replacement of squamous epithelium by glandular epithelium (either {{c3::gastric/cardiac or intestinal}} types)
    • {{c4::Goblet cells::Wich type of cells are in the intestinal type of metapalsia}}
  • Cancer risk and follow-up:
    • A {{c5::30–40}} fold increase in the risk of developing adenocarcinoma of the oesophagus
    • Patients undergo endoscopic follow up and repeated biopsy aiming at the identification of dysplastic changes or early malignancy
    • {{c6::50}}% of patients with high grade dysplasia already have malignancy
  • What to do after the diagnosis of Barrett’s Oesphagus?
  • {{c7::  (Has an image occlusion card for it) }}

Oesophageal Carcinoma — Overview

  • Presentation in the {{c1::50–80::what age}}’s with dysphagia, weight loss, vomiting, haemorrhage and pain
  • Diagnosis is by endoscopy and biopsy
  • Types:
    • {{c2::Squamous cell carcinoma}}
    • {{c2::Adenocarcinoma}}

Oesophageal Carcinoma — Histological Classification (by site & risk)

  • Upper 2/3
    • {{c3::Squamous cell}}carcinoma
    • {{c4::Smoking & alcohol::What is it caused by}}
  • Lower 1/3
    • {{c3::Adeno}}carcinoma
    • {{c4::GORD & Barrett’s::What is it caused by}}

Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) of the Oesophagus

Aetiology

  • Tobacco
    • {{c1::Methylating nitrous compounds are carcinogenic}}
  • Alcohol
    • Contains many carcinogenic compounds
    • {{c1::Nitrosamines}} and {{c1::polycyclic}} hydrocarbons
  • Diet
    • Food with high {{c1::nitrite/nitrosamine}} levels
    • Smoked {{c2::fish and fungal contamination}}
    • Low intake of {{c2::fresh fruits & vegetables (antioxidants)}}

Pathogenetic mechanisms

  • Inflammation (oesophagitis) caused by irritants
    • Inflammation leads to epithelial cell damage
    • Increases epithelial cell turnover
    • Increases risk of dysplasia and then malignancy

Stepwise progression (figure)


Gross picture (location & endoscopic types)

  • {{c3::Upper 2/3}} of the oesophagus

  • Endoscopy:
    • {{c4::Protruding}} (60%)
    • {{c4::Excavating}} (25%)
    • {{c4::Flat}} (15%)

Microscopic picture

  • {{c5::Well-, moderate(Left)}}- and {{c5::poorly(Right)}} differentiated squamous cell carcinoma

  • Which grade is this SCC? 

    {{c6::Poorly diffrentiated}}

Adenocarcinoma of the Oesophagus

Pathogenesis

  • Reflux of acid/bile leads to epithelial cell damage
  • Increases epithelial cell turnover
  • Also leads to metaplasia (squamous → gastric → intestinal-type)
  • Increases risk of dysplasia and then malignancy
  • Adenocarcinoma because of previous metaplasia

Pathogenesis (continued)

  • Barrett’s oesophagus → sequence of dysplasia from low to high grade →Invasive adenocarcinoma [Mutations in oncogenes {{c1::p53}} and Loss Of Heterozygosity (LOH) in chromosome {{c2::17p}}]
  • {{c3::30–40}}fold increased risk of developing adenocarcinoma
  • 10% of patients with Barrett’s will develop adenocarcinoma
  • If diagnosed with high grade dysplasia – 50% association with adenocarcinoma

Gross picture

  • Distal oesophagus (lower 1/3)
  • {{c4::Flat, ulcerating, infiltrative::Describe it}}

Progression figure

  • Normal ⟶ {{c5::Gastric metaplasia (Barretts Oesophagus)::Just Read, has an image occlusion card}} ⟶ Adenocarcinoma

Microscopic picture

  • Types of adenocarcinoma: {{c6::intestinal, gastric and mucinous}} types
  • Grading: well, moderate and poorly differentiated
• Captioned example: {{c1::Tubular adenocarcinoma. Irregular-shaped and fused neoplastic glands with intraluminal mucus and debris::Describe and diagnose}}

Oesophageal Carcinoma — Complications and Spread

Complications

  • Esophageal {{c2::obstruction}}
  • {{c2::Hema}}temesis (vomiting of {{c2::blood}})
  • {{c2::Tracheo-esophageal}} fistula
  • Spread

Spread

  • Local spread:
    • Extension along {{c1::entire}} oesophageal length
    • Infiltration through oesophageal wall and into the surrounding organs [pleura, trachea, bronchi, lung, aorta, …]
  • Lymph node metastasis:
    • Deposits within draining lymph nodes depends on which segment of the oesophagus is harbouring the tumour
  • Distant metastasis to the liver and lungs ….

Case Study

  • Case:
    • A 54 year old man with known oesophageal reflux is admitted to the ER. He has severe heartburn and is vomiting blood. Endoscopy reveals a large ulcer with necrotic floor and fixed base at the GO Junction. A biopsy is taken for microscopic examination.
    • Which is the most likely type of ulcer detected?
      • A. Acute inflammatory ulcer
      • B. Adenocarcinoma ulcer
      • C. Basal cell carcinoma ulcer
      • D. Squamous cell carcinoma ulcer
      • E. Tuberculous ulcer
        Answer :         {{c1::B. Adenocarcinoma ulcer}}
  • Case continued:
    • Examination of the excised biopsy has shown: A moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma invading into the adventitia with five positive draining lymph nodes. There are no reported data regarding distant metastasis. Which is the most accurate TNM stage in this case?
    • Answer: {{c2::T3 N2 Mx}}

Histology of the gastric mucosa (generated) [Slides 4–10]

  • Gastric mucosa [Slides 5–6]

  • Gastrin [Slide 7]
    • Gastrin is a peptide hormone responsible for {{c1::enhancing gastric mucosal growth, gastric motility, and secretion of HCl into the stomach}}
  • Other less identified cells [Slides 8–9]
    • Stem cells: at {{c2::junction of pit with isthmus}}
    • ECL cells: secrete {{c3::histamine}} at base of the gland; situated near {{c4::Parietal}} cells (histamine aids in the production of gastric acid)
    • Gastrin in the {{c5::fundus}}

    • Normal {{c6::antral}} type mucosa ⟶ Normal body type mucosa

Gastric glands (extra)

  • Mucous neck cells → mucus (thinner, less alkaline than surface cells).
  • Parietal (oxyntic) cells → {{c1::HCl (acid) + intrinsic factor (B₁₂ absorption)}}.
  • Chief (zymogenic) cells → {{c1::pepsinogen (→ pepsin in acid)}}.
  • Enteroendocrine (G cells) → {{c1::gastrin (stimulates acid secretion)}}.
  • D cells → {{c2::somatostatin (inhibits gastrin, acid)}}.
  • ECL cells → {{c3::histamine (stimulates parietal cells)}}.

H. pylori–induced chronic gastritis [Slides 24–27, 28–31]

  • Epidemiology/association [Slide 24]
    • 90% of patients with chronic gastritis affecting the {{c1::antrum}} are caused by H. pylori
  • Complications [Slide 24]
    • H. pylori gastritis ⟶ increased {{c2::acid}} secretion ⟶ peptic ulcer of the stomach or duodenum
    • H. pylori infection ⟶ increased risk of {{c2::gastric cancer}}
  • Pathogenesis [Slides 25–26]
    • H. pylori adapt to the niche provided by gastric mucus
    • Virulence depends on:
      1. {{c3::Flagella}}—allowing bacterial motility in the viscous mucus
      2. {{c4::Urease}} enzyme—generates ammonia from endogenous urea ⟶ elevates pH around organisms, protecting them from high gastric acidity
      3. {{c5::Adhesins}}—enhance adherence to surface foveolar cells
      4. {{c6::Toxins}}—may be involved in ulcer or cancer development
    • These factors create an imbalance between mucosal defenses and damaging forces
    • When the damaging effect supervenes ⟶ {{c7::pangastritis}} develops, followed by {{c8::multifocal atrophic gastritis}} with:
      • {{c9::Reduced}} acid secretion
      • {{c9::Intestinal}} metaplasia
      • Risk of {{c9::gastric adenocarcinoma}}
    • This progression is due to an interaction between host immunity & the bacterium
  • Microscopy [Slide 27]
    1. {{c10::H. pylori}} within mucous overlying epithelial cells
    2. {{c11::Neutrophils}} within the {{c12::lamina propria, intraepithelial, and within gastric pits (abscesses)}}
    3. Lamina propria: aggregates of {{c13::plasma cells, lymphocytes, and macrophages ⟶ lymphoid follicles ⟶ possibly complicated by lymphoma}}
    4. {{c14::Intestinal}} metaplasia ({{c15::Goblet}} cells and villi) ⟶ possibly complicated by {{c16::adenocarcinoma}}

  • Special stains/Immunohistochemistry [Slides 28, 31]
    • {{c17::Warthin–Starry silver}} stain



    • {{c18::Lymphocytic}} infiltrate forming lymphoid follicles
      IHC → positive for {{c18::H pylori}}

Diagnosis and characteristics [Slide 36; OCR matched]

  • Autoimmune gastritis
    • {{c1::10}}% of cases of chronic gastritis
    • Characteristically spares the {{c2::antrum}}, usually in the {{c2::body alone}}
  • Characteristics
    1. Antibodies to {{c3::parietal cells}} and {{c3::intrinsic factor}} in {{c4::serum and gastric secretions}}
    2. Reduced serum {{c5::pepsinogen I}} level
    3. {{c6::Antral endocrine}} cell hyperplasia
    4. Vitamin {{c7::B12}} deficiency
    5. {{c8::Achlorhydria (defective gastric acid secretion)}}

Pathogenesis (flow) [Slide 37; p.37 OCR]

  • Anti-parietal cell antibodies ⟶ Loss of parietal cells
    • ⟶ Deficient acid ⟶ {{c9::Gastrin}} release ⟶ Hypergastrinaemia ⟶ Hyperplasia of {{c10::antral G-cells}}
    • ⟶ Deficient intrinsic factor ⟶ ↓ Vit B12 absorption ⟶ {{c11::Megaloblastic (“pernicious”)}}  anaemia
  • ALSO: Chief cell loss ⟶ reduced concentration of pepsinogen I

↓ Pepsinogen I → chief cells lost with parietal cells (body/fundus).
Antral endocrine hyperplasia → no acid → ↑ gastrin → cell overgrowth.

  • {{c3::NSAIDs}}
  • {{c3::Smoking}}
  • {{c3::Alcohol}}
  • {{c3::Stress}}
  • {{c2::Bile}} reflux
  • {{c2::Zollinger–Ellison}} syndrome (hypergastrinaemia)

Peptic Ulcer Disease (PUD) 

Endoscopic/gross picture — Sites and morphology [Slides 54–56; p.54–56]

  • SITE :
    • 1st part duodenum (75%)
    • Stomach (25%)
  • Other sites [p.54 OCR]:
    • {{c1::Gastro-oesophageal}} junction ⟶ GORD, Barretts’ oesophagus
    • {{c1::Gastrojejunostomy}} stomal lesions
    • {{c2::Upper GIT}} in {{c3::Zollinger–Ellison}} syndrome
    • Ectopic gastric mucosa ({{c4::Meckel diverticulum}})
  • Gross picture 
    • {{c5::90}}% Single
    • {{c5::Well}}demarcated
    • < {{c5::4}} cm, {{c5::round or oval}}
    • {{c5::Clean base, punched out}}
    • Surrounding chronic gastritis
  • Most common site of a gastric ulcer?
    • {{c6::Lesser curvature at junction of fundus and antrum}}

Peptic Ulcer Disease (PUD) 

  • Representative images:

    • {{c1::Gastric ulcer (lesser curvature) with a punched-out ulcer base with a whitish exudate}}

    • {{c2::Duodenal ulcer: example in 65-year-old man with osteoarthritis on daily Naproxen, presenting with hematemesis and melena::Describe and diagnose}}
    • The patient took {{c3::Naproxen}} on a daily basis


  • {{c4::Peptic ulcer disease. Gastric ulcer with a punched-out ulcer base and whitish fibrinoid exudates::Describe}}

Differentiating benign vs malignant ulcers [Slides 57–58; p.57–58]

  • Benign ulcers:
    • {{c1::Smooth, regular, rounded edge
    • Flat smooth base
    • Surrounding mucosa shows radiating folds}}

  • Malignant ulcers:
    • {{c1::Irregular overhanging margins
    • Ulcerated mass often protrudes into the lumen
    • Folds surrounding the ulcer crater are often nodular and irregular}}

Histology of a peptic ulcer — Zones and micro [Slides 60–62; p.60–61]

  • Histological layers/zones:
    1. {{c1::Surface fibrinous debris}}
    2. {{c1::Acute inflammation of the mucosa}}
    3. {{c1::Granulation tissue}}
    4. {{c1::Fibrosis / fibrous scar
      }}
  • Representative micro (Slide 62):

    • Peptic ulcer: {{c2::sharply demarcated with normal gastric mucosa on one side (*) falling away into a deep ulcer; Ulcer base contains inflamed, necrotic debris (I)::Describe and diagnose}}

Clinical course and complications [Slides 63–65]

  • Clinical course
    • PUD may undergo spontaneous {{c1::healing}}
    • Recurrent morbidity
    • Higher risk of recurrence if {{c2::H. pylori}} present
    • Good rates of healing and prevention of recurrence with {{c3::antibiotic}} treatment
  • Complications
    • {{c4::Bleeding}}15–20% (25% of ulcer deaths)
    • {{c4::Perforation}}5% (66% of ulcer deaths)
    • {{c4::Fibrosis, stricture, hourglass}} stomach
    • {{c4::Obstruction}}2%
    • {{c4::Anaemia}}
    • {{c4::Pain}}
    • Increased risk of {{c4::malignancy}}

Normal Liver Structure 

  • Liver
    • Surrounded by fibrous capsule
      • Thin
      • Fibroblasts, collagenous fibres, few elastic fibres
    • Hepatocyte: the functional cell of the liver parenchyma
  • Functions of Liver
    • Metabolism
      • Substances absorbed by gut pass through liver before entering general circulation
      • {{c1::Lipid}} metabolism; including cholesterol, TGs, phospholipid, vitamin A, lipoprotein and fatty acid metabolism and storage
      • {{c1::Protein}} metabolism; synthesis and degradation of amino acids and ptns, and neutralisation of nitrogenous waste. Important ptns synthesised include albumin, clotting factors
      • {{c1::CHO}} metabolism; glucose and glycogen formation, storage and breakdown
    • {{c2::Bile}} Production
      • Emulsify & help fat absorption in SI
    • Blood Regulation
      • Phagocytosis of {{c3::old/damaged RBCs, pathogens, debris}}
    • Xenobiotic metabolism; i.e., {{c4::drugs and toxins, 2ry haematopoiesis}}

Liver Architecture and Lobular Organization

  • Hepatic Lobules
    • Hexagonal or pentagonal, bounded by thin trabeculae of reticular CT
    • Portal tracts at the periphery
    • Hepatocytes arranged as thin plates radially around a small {{c1::central vein}}

    • Plates separated by fine vascular sinusoids through which blood flows
      • Close association of liver cells and the circulation allows absorption of nutrients and secretion of products into blood
    • Blood flow into sinusoids comes from terminal branches of both the {{c2::hepatic portal}} vein and {{c2::hepatic}} artery
  • Portal Tract (Triad)
    • Condensation of CT at the corners of hepatic lobules containing:
      • {{c3::Portal}} venule (branch of portal vein): blood rich in nutrients but low in O₂
      • {{c3::Hepatic arteriolar}} (branch of hepatic artery): supplies oxygen
      • {{c3::Bile}} ducts (branches of the bile conducting system)

  • Liver architecture = Hepatic Lobules
    • Liver parenchyma is organized as thousands of hepatic lobules separated by very thin trabeculae
      • Reticular CT (type of CT characterized by predominance of reticular fibers made of type {{c4::III}} collagen (demonstrated by silver stain))
      • The lobules are evident in animals, not human

Portal Tract (Triad) — Microscopic
  • Portal venule
    • {{c1::Wide}} vessel
    • {{c1::Thin}} wall
    • {{c1::Flat}} endothelial lining

  • Hepatic arteriole
    • {{c2::Narrow}} vessel
    • {{c2::Thick muscular}} wall
    • {{c2::Flat}} endothelial lining

  • Bile ducts: lined by {{c3::cuboidal}} epithelium

Definition of interface
  • The meeting of {{c4::the portal tract}} with {{c4::the limiting plate of hepatocytes}} is called the interface, and is one of the sites of inflammatory activity in hepatitis|


  • {{c5::Interface hepatitis}}

Hepatic acinus (Rappaport)
  • Zone {{c1::1}} of the hepatic acinus is closest to blood entering the liver
  • Zone {{c1::3}} is the least perfused area, is prone to various types of damage, particularly viral, toxic and ischaemic injury

Cell Types in Hepatic Lobules

  • 1. Hepatocytes
    • Joined to each other by cellular junctions enclosing small {{c1::bile canaliculi}}
    • {{c2::Polygonal}} cell with abundant {{c2::eosinophilic}} {{c2::vacuolated}} cytoplasm
    • {{c3::Central round vesicular}} nucleus with prominent nucleolus
    • Vascular sinusoids lined by a {{c4::discontinuous, fenestrated}} endothelial layer without basal lamina
    • {{c5::Kupffer}} cells ({{c5::resident macrophages}}) adhered to endothelium; phagocytic functions
    • {{c6::Stable}} cells, able to replicate and regenerate (e.g., post-resection hyperplasia)
  • 2. Sinusoidal endothelial cells
    • {{c7::Discontinuous, fenestrated; surrounded by sparse reticular fibers::Describe what they are and what they are surrounded by}}
  • 3. Ito cells and Von Kupffer cells

    • Hepatic stellate cells (Ito cells)
      • Quiescent: storage of {{c8::vitamin A}}
      • Activated by injury/cytokines: {{c9::myofibroblast-like, lay down collagen::What happens to them}}
    • Stellate macrophages (Kupffer cells)
      • Phagocytose microbes/RBCs, LDL, immune complexes; store metals (Cu, Co, Fe)
    • {{c10::Pit}} cells: NK lymphocytes attached to endoluminal surface of endothelium
  • Perisinusoidal Space (Space of Disse)
    • Between {{c11::hepatocytes}} and {{c11::endothelial/Kupffer cells}}
    • Microvilli project into this space from hepatocytes

Bile Canaliculi and Biliary Flow

  • Bile canaliculi
    • Enclosed between {{c1::hepatocytes}}
    • Exocrine secretion of bile
    • Bile flow {{c2::opposite}} to blood, i.e., from {{c2::center of the lobule}} to {{c2::its periphery}}
    • Drain into canals of {{c3::Hering}} and bile ductules, then portal bile ducts
    • Then into larger septal ducts, segmental ducts, and main right and left hepatic ducts
  • Porta Hepatis (Hilum)
    • Dual blood supply from {{c4::portal vein}} and {{c4::hepatic artery}} enters
    • {{c5::Lymphatics}} and {{c5::common hepatic}} duct exit

Patterns of Hepatic Injury

  • Patterns of hepatic injury
    • Hepatocyte {{c1::degeneration and intracellular accumulation}}
      • Water
      • Fat
      • Bilirubin
    • Hepatocyte {{c1::apoptosis}}
    • Hepatocyte {{c1::necrosis}}
    • Inflammation
    • Regeneration and fibrosis

Degeneration and Intracellular Accumulation

  • Cellular swelling due to accumulation of {{c1::water}}
    • Occurs in acute and chronic liver diseases
    • {{c2::Cloudy}} swelling: small water accumulation
    • {{c2::Hydropic}} change (ballooned hepatocytes): large water accumulation
       --> Hydropic change
  • Steatosis
    • Accumulation of {{c3::neutral fat (TGs)}} inside parenchymal cells

    • GP: liver {{c4::enlarged, soft, yellow, greasy}}\\
  • Macrovesicular steatosis
    • {{c5::Common, non-specific::Is it common/specfic}} reactive change with many causes (alcohol, diabetes mellitus, obesity, drugs/toxins, starvation, etc)
    • Usually {{c6::reversible::Reversible or not}} on removal of the stimulus
    • MP:

      • {{c7::Single large vacuole}} distends hepatocyte; nucleus {{c7::displaced to one side}}
      • Zonal distribution variable:
        • Most often {{c8::centrilobular}}: e.g., {{c9::alcoholic liver disease, obesity}}
        • {{c8::Periportal}}: e.g., after {{c9::total parenteral nutrition, chronic venous congestion}} (CVC)
        • Severe → {{c10::panlobular}}
      • Etiology not definable by lipid distribution pattern alone; {{c11::clinicopathologic}} correlation required
      • Also with HCV, NASH
  • Microvesicular steatosis
    • Rare but important; indicates {{c12::severe metabolic derangement}} (e.g., {{c13::acute fatty liver of pregnancy, Reye’s syndrome, mushroom poisoning}})

  • IC bilirubin (feathery degeneration)

    • Cholestasis
    • Caused by Marked reduction/arrest in {{c14::bile secretion/flow}} 
    • Etiology:
      • Disturbance in {{c15::hepatocyte secretory dysfunction}}
      • Obstruction of bile anywhere from {{c15::canaliculi to ampulla of Vater (e.g., Primary biliary cirrhosis)}}

Hepatocyte Cell Death: Apoptosis and Necrosis

  • Hepatocyte apoptosis (acidophil bodies/{{c1::Councilman}} bodies)

    • Appears either
    • {{c2::Condensed shrunken}} cell with {{c2::eosinophilia}} and nuclear {{c2::pyknosis or clusters of cell fragments ± pyknotic nuclear material}}
    • Or may be {{c3::naked}}, near {{c3::mononuclear inflammatory}} cells, or inside {{c3::phagocytosing}} macrophages
    • {{c4::No rupture::Is there rupture}}
  • Hepatocyte necrosis
    • Cell {{c5::swells and ruptures::What happens to the cell}}
    • {{c6::Spotty}} necrosis ({{c7::focal}} necrosis, {{c7::lobular}} inflammation): small cluster of mononuclear cells marks sites of {{c8::dropout}}
    • Above is example of {{c9::single cell necrosis in the liver}}. There is marked cell swelling and karyorrhexis in a necrotic hepatocyte (arrow) and a nearby small focus of inflammation (arrowhead), most likely secondary to cell rupture

Hepatocyte Cell Death: Apoptosis and Necrosis

  • Confluent necrosis Necrosis of contiguous hepatocytes
    • Zonal necrosis: affects a particular zone within each hepatic lobule
      • Centilobular (perivenular)
      • Midzonal: {{c1::yellow fever}}
      • Peripheral zonal necrosis (interface hepatitis): E.g., in {{c1::chronic hepatitis}}
    • Bridging necrosis: P–P, P–C, C–C
    • Panlobular and multilobular necrosis (submassive liver necrosis):
      • Corresponds clinically to {{c2::fulminant}} hepatitis with acute {{c2::LCF and coma}}
      • Panlobular: confluent necrosis of the {{c3::entire liver lobule}}
      • Multilobular: confluent necrosis affecting {{c3::several adjacent lobules}}
      • In surviving patients, multilobular necrosis results in {{c4::cirrhosis}}
  • Centilobular (perivenular) necrosis — causes (Part 1, p.39)
    • {{c5::Acute viral hepatitis, chronic venous congestion (CVC), some drugs and toxins}}

    • Figure key takeaway: {{c6::Perivenular}} zones are the biochemical/oxygen “low points,” so toxins/CVC and some viruses localize damage here.
  • Portal inflammation
    • May be limited to {{c7::portal tract}} or spill over into {{c7::parenchyma}}

Regeneration, Fibrosis, and Cirrhosis

  • Regeneration and fibrosis
    • HC regeneration signified by {{c1::mitosis}}, thickening of the {{c1::hepatocyte}} cords and some disorganization of parenchymal structure
    • Chronic diffuse liver disease disturbing the framework leads to fibrosis accompanied by irregular proliferation of hepatocytes
      • This makes the liver {{c2::diffusely}} nodular ({{c2::regeneration}} nodules) and the condition is known as cirrhosis
      • Examples: cirrhosis due to chronic alcoholism and chronic hepatitis
  • Cirrhosis
    • A chronic {{c3::diffuse irreversible::is it local or diffused? reversisble or irreversible}} liver disease characterized by:
      • Progressive diffuse necrosis of liver cells, associated with framework destruction
      • Repair by both {{c4::regeneration (in the form of nodules)}} and {{c4::fibrosis}}, i.e., the normal hepatic lobular architecture is lost
      • Restoration of the normal liver functions does not occur since the regeneration nodules have no {{c5::regular relation to portal tracts}}, no {{c5::eccentric}} veins and {{c5::irregular}} sinusoids
      • Moreover, hepatocytes in these regeneration nodules are commonly degenerated
    • Liver Cirrhosis — {{c7::Masson trichrome::Whats the stain}} (figure)

      • {{c6::Fibrous tissue}}, blue
      • Normal hepatic lobule

    • Liver Cirrhosis (gross)
      • The cirrhotic liver is {{c8::nodular and firm}} due to fibrosis

Hepatotropic viruses (merged from slides 6–8)

  • {{c3::Hepatitis A}} virus (HAV)
    • {{c1::Fecal–oral::Mode of transm.}}
    • Self-limited {{c2::acute}} hepatitis
    • Rarely leads to {{c2::fulminating}} hepatitis
    • No chronic hepatitis or carrier state
  • Hepatitis B virus (HBV)
    • {{c4::Blood, sexual intercourse, transplacental transmission::Mode of tran}}
    • {{c5::Acute, self-limited}} hepatitis
    • {{c5::Non-progressive}} chronic hepatitis
    • Progressive chronic disease culminating in {{c6::cirrhosis (and increased risk of hepatocellular carcinoma)}}
    • {{c7::Fulminant}} hepatitis with massive liver necrosis (1–4% of HBV infections)
    • Asymptomatic carrier state
  • Hepatitis C virus (HCV)
    • Mode of transmission: similar to {{c8::HBV}}
    • Causes similar clinical patterns as HBV but, as opposed to HBV, {{c9::progression to chronic disease occurs in most (80%–85%) HCV-infected patients and cirrhosis occurs in 20%–30%; fulminant hepatitis in 2–5% of HCV infections}}
    • HCV is the most common cause of chronic hepatitis in Egypt
  • Hepatitis D virus (HDV)
    • {{c10::Defective RNA::RNA or DNA}} virus
    • HDV infection can develop only with {{c11::concomitant HBV}} infection
      • Acute co-infection by HDV and HBV ⟶ {{c12::hepatitis ranging from mild acute to fulminant, but chronicity rarely develops}}
  • Hepatitis E virus (HEV)
    • Mode of transmission: similar to {{c13::HAV}}
    • Typically {{c14::self-limiting acute}} hepatitis with no tendency to chronicity
    • High rate (20%–50%) of {{c15::fatal fulminant}} hepatitis in pregnant women

Acute viral hepatitis — microscopy (slides 9–11)

  • {{c1::Lobular}} changes predominate
  • Portal inflammation, usually {{c2::mild}}
  • {{c3::Interface}} hepatitis, more association with chronic hepatitis
Lobular changes of acute viral hepatitis
  • Lobular disarray due to heterogeneous appearance of parenchymal cells; evidence of hepatocyte damage:
    • {{c4::Hydropic}} change in some parenchymal cells
    • {{c4::Apoptosis}} in others
    • {{c4::Spotty}} necrosis (focal necrosis, lobular inflammation)?
  • With increasing severity: {{c5::centrilobular confluent}} necrosis
  • Higher degree: {{c6::portal–central (P–C)}} bridging necrosis
  • In fulminant hepatitis, {{c7::massive hepatic}} necrosis is the dominant feature
  • Cholestasis: accumulation of bile in hepatocytes and bile canaliculi (obstructed by swollen hepatocytes)
    • {{c8::Kupffer}} cells engulf bile and undergo {{c9::hyperplasia}}
Portal inflammation in acute viral hepatitis
  • Mild mononuclear cell infiltration: {{c10::lymphocytes, macrophages, and some plasma cells; usually confined within the portal tract (PT) ± interface hepatitis}}
Fate of acute viral hepatitis (slide 12)
  • Complete recovery in {{c11::95}}% or more of hepatitis A infections
  • {{c12::Acute fulminating}} hepatitis: more common with HBV, HCV, and {{c13::HEV}} (during pregnancy) than HAV
  • Chronic hepatitis or chronic carrier: not due to hepatitis {{c14::A or E}}

Chronic hepatitis (slides 13–17)

  • Active ongoing inflammation of the liver persisting >6 months, detectable by serological and histological means
  • Causes
    • Hepatotropic viruses: HCV, HBV ± HDV, mixed infections
    • Auto-immune
    • Rarely {{c1::drug}}-induced, as liver toxicity is usually identified and medication ceased or changed

Microscopy of chronic hepatitis (portal changes predominate; slides 14–16)

  • {{c2::Portal inflammation and interface hepatitis}} predominate
  • Lobular parenchymal changes also present
Chronic hepatitis — portal changes

  • Portal inflammation: mild, moderate, or marked; mononuclear cells mainly {{c3::lymphocytes, plasma cells, histiocytes}}; {{c4::follicles}} sometimes form (especially in {{c5::HCV}})
  • Interface hepatitis: extension of the portal lymphocytic infiltrate beyond PT limits, associated with {{c6::piecemeal}} necrosis of hepatocytes and trapping of surviving hepatocytes within inflammatory infiltrate (mild, moderate, or severe)
  • Portal tract fibrosis progressing to ⟶ {{c7::bridging}} fibrosis or {{c7::bridging}} fibrosis with regenerative nodules (cirrhosis) if the disease process continues
    • {{c8::Assessment of fibrosis}} is the STAGE of disease
Chronic hepatitis — parenchymal (lobular) changes
  • {{c9::Hydropic}} change
  • {{c9::Spotty}} necrosis (lobular inflammation): usually mild
  • {{c9::Confluent}} necrosis (zonal, bridging, or multiacinar) occurs in severe cases
  • {{c9::Apoptosis}} may also occur
  • Other features
    • Steatosis of hepatocytes (mainly in {{c10::hepatitis C}})
    • {{c11::Ground-glass}} hepatocytes (HBV) best seen with {{c12::orcein}} stain
      • {{c13::Paler eosinophilic finely granular}} cytoplasm; often separated from the cell membrane by a {{c14::clear halo (artifact)}}
      • Cause: {{c15::HBsAg within proliferated SER throughout the}} cell

    • Hepatocellular dysplasia (precancerous)

Chronic hepatitis (slides 13–17)

Grading of inflammation and staging of fibrosis (slide 17)

  • Most popular systems: {{c1::Ishak (American) & METAVIR (French)}}
  • Ishak score
    • Grade of inflammation (maximum 18)
      • {{c2::Interface hepatitis (0–4)
      • Confluent necrosis (0–6)
      • Spotty necrosis (lobular inflammation) (0–4)
      • Portal inflammation (0–4)
    • Stage of fibrosis (0–6); 6/6 = cirrhosis}}
  • METAVIR
    • {{c3::Grade A0–A4
    • Stage F0–F4; F4 = cirrhosis}}

Autoimmune hepatitis (AIH) (slide 18)

  • Presentation
    • May present acutely, although considered a chronic liver disease
    • Predominantly affects females (70%)
    • High serum auto-antibody titres (anti-smooth muscle, anti-nuclear, and/or anti-mitochondrial antibodies)
    • Dramatic response to {{c1::immunosuppressive}} treatment
    • Negative viral serologic markers
  • Microscopic picture (MP): {{c2::chronic hepatitis pattern with prominent plasma cells in portal tract inflammation and lobular inflammation}}

Drug-induced and toxic liver injury (slides 19–20)

  • Liver is the main site of biotransformation of drugs
  • The same drug may cause different patterns of liver damage in different patients
  • Patterns may be:
    • {{c1::Predictable}}, dose-dependent toxicity due to intrinsic hepatotoxicity of the molecule, in all exposed population (e.g., paracetamol)
    • {{c1::Unpredictable}}(idiosyncratic) reactions, in only a small % of the exposed population (e.g., antibiotics such as {{c2::flucloxacillin}})

Morphology (diverse)

  • Drug-induced acute hepatitis similar to {{c3::acute viral hepatitis}}
  • Drug-induced chronic hepatitis: prolonged or repeated exposure to some drugs (e.g., {{c4::phenytoin}})
  • Drug-induced cholestasis

Complications of chronic hepatitis (slide 21)

  • {{c1::
  • Post-hepatitic cirrhosis
  • Liver cell failure
  • Hepatocellular carcinoma
    • }}

Alcoholic liver disease (slides 22–30)

  • NB. NAFLD is associated with obesity (BMI >30), type II diabetes mellitus, and dyslipidemia
  • Parallel disease spectra:
    • Alcoholic fatty liver disease
      • {{c1::Hepatic steatosis
      • Alcoholic steatohepatitis (ASH)
      • Alcoholic cirrhosis (Laennec’s cirrhosis)::Whats the sequence}}
    • Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD)
      • {{c1::Hepatic steatosis
      • Non-alcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH)
      • Nutritional cirrhosis (Laennec’s cirrhosis)::Whats the squence}}

Alcoholic liver disease, hepatic steatosis — figure (slide 23)


Steatohepatitis, fibrosis, and cirrhosis (slide 24)

  • ASH and NASH have the same pathogenesis and histology, although a greater proportion of patients with {{c2::ASH}} develop cirrhosis due to {{c3::superimposed toxicity of alcohol}}

Alcoholic steatohepatitis (slides 25–29)

  • History of alcohol abuse
  • Minimum of three histologic findings:
    • {{c1::Steatosis (>5%)
    • Lobular inflammation (arrowhead)
    • Hepatocyte ballooning
      • }}
  • Macrosteatosis is obvious; two ballooned hepatocytes shown containing {{c2::Mallory–Denk}} bodies (top and bottom arrows) 

  • {{c3::Apoptotic hepatocytes commonly seen in alcoholic steatohepatitis}} (top arrow)
  • satellitosis
  • lymphocytes also seen
  • portal inflammatory infiltrates, predominantly neutrophils 

  • {{c4::Mallory–Denk bodies}} (long thin arrow) with both {{c5::neutrophilic}} (upper arrowhead) and {{c5::lymphocytic}} (tailed arrow) inflammation; inflammation is usually mixed (figure)

  • {{c6::Trichrome-}}stained section shows dense pericellular/perisinusoidal fibrosis in a patient with alcoholic steatohepatitis; collagen bands are highlighted with multiple arrowheads (figure)

Hepatic failure (slides 31–34)

Acute liver failure (slide 32)

  • {{c1::Acute liver illness + encephalopathy::What happens}} within 6 months of diagnosis
  • Pathology = massive hepatic necrosis

Chronic liver failure (slides 33–34)

  • Chronic liver disease
    • End point of chronic hepatitis
    • Pathology = {{c2::cirrhosis}}
  • Hepatic failure without {{c3::overt}} necrosis
    • Hepatocytes viable but unable to perform function
    • Examples: {{c4::Tetracycline}} toxicity, acute fatty liver of pregnancy

Liver tumors (slides 35–43)

Benign tumors (slide 35)

  • {{c1::Cavernous}} hemangioma
  • {{c1::Hepatocellular}} adenoma
  • {{c1::Bile duct}} adenoma

Malignant tumors (slide 36)

  • {{c2::Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC)}} — the most common primary malignant liver neoplasm
  • Other primary neoplasms
    • {{c3::Cholangiocarcinoma}}— associated with {{c5::ulcerative colitis, PSC, liver flukes}}
    • {{c4::Angiosarcoma}}— rare; associated with exposure to {{c6::vinyl chloride or arsenic}}
    • {{c7::Hepatoblastoma}}— rare neoplasm of young childhood
    • Lymphoma and sarcoma can also affect the liver
  • Metastatic involvement of the liver is {{c8::common::Common or not}}

Liver tumors (slides 35–43)

Hepatocellular carcinoma — gross patterns (slides 37–39)

  • {{c1::Multinodular pattern: multiple tumor nodules::Describe}} (figure)
  • {{c1::Single mass::Describe}} (figure)
    • .
  • {{c2::Diffuse liver involvement or massive::Describe}} (large mass replacing most of the liver) (figure)
    • .

HCC — histologic patterns (slides 40–42)

  • {{c3::Trabecular architecture with thickened cell plates separated by little vascular stroma::Describe}} (figure)
    • .
  • {{c4::Acinar pattern, little vascular stroma::Describe}} (figure)

  • {{c5::Solid pattern — malignant hepatocytes showing moderate to marked cellular anaplasia::Describe}} (figure)

Liver tumors (slides 35–43)

HCC, fibrolamellar variant (slide 43)

  • Columns of {{c1::large eosinophilic hepatocytes with very well-differentiated features, separated by parallel (lamellar) bundles of collagen}}

  • Tends to occur in {{c2::younger, non-cirrhotic individuals & has much better prognosis than the classic type}}

Rheumatoid Arthritis: Overview & Epidemiology

  • Commonest severe inflammatory arthritis in the UK.
  • Multisystem inflammatory disease with prominent involvement of the joints.
  • Affects people of all ages, peak age of onset ≈ 30 years.
  • 75% of people with RA are rheumatoid factor positive.
  • Prevalence in the population is 1%.
  • Female:Male = {{c1::3:1}}.
  • Possible genetic component (increased susceptibility in first degree relatives).
  • HLA haplotypes {{c2::DR4}} and {{c2::DR1}} are associated with increased susceptibility.
  • Possibly provoked by a virus in some genetically susceptible people.

Pathogenesis: Pannus Formation and the RA Joint

  • Localisation of the arthrotropic agent in the joint and antigen presentation causes T-cell activation and recruitment to the joint.
  • T-cells secrete cytokines that stimulate other cells (B-cells, plasma cells, macrophages).
  • Arrangement of cells:
    • {{c1::T-cells and B-cells}} in the center.
    • {{c1::Plasma cells and macrophages}} in the periphery (pannus formation).
  • {{c2::B-cells::Which cells}} are the RF producing cells.

Clinical Picture & Distribution of Joint Involvement

  • {{c1::Mainly the small joints of the hand (except DIP), wrists, elbows, shoulders, knees and ankles::Which joints}} are affected.
  • At first effusion, {{c2::pain and affection of hand function::What does it look like clinically}}.
  • Later, deformities develop due to the effect of the {{c3::proliferative synovium, destruction of ligaments, and imbalance of the muscle weakness created by the reflex inhibition}}.
  • Incidence of affected joints
    • {{c4::MCPs, PIPs and MTPs}} 90%
    • {{c4::Knees, wrists and ankles}} 80%
    • {{c4::Hip, elbow}} 50%
    • {{c4::Cervical spine}} 40%
    • {{c4::Temporomandibular and acromioclavicular joints}} 30%
    • {{c4::Cricoarytenoid joint}} 10%

Patterns of Presentation & Hand Deformities

  • Pattern of presentation
    • {{c1::Insidious}} onset affecting the small joints of the hand and feet, progressing to the elbows, shoulders, knees and ankles 70%.
    • {{c2::Abrupt acute polyarthritis}} (hard to diagnose) 20%.
    • Acute monoarthritis.
    • {{c3::Palindromic rheumatism}} (it is inflammaitoin of a joint, that then skips to another joint) with variable episodes of polyarthritis 10%.
  • Hand deformities can be in the form of:
    • {{c4::Ulnar}} deviation of MCP’s
    • {{c4::Radial}} deviation of wrists
    • {{c5::Boutonniere}} deformity of fingers
    • {{c6::Swan-neck}} deformity of fingers
    • {{c7::Z-deformity}} of thumb
How to know if there is MCP effusion?
{{c8::make them make a wrist, if there is space between the knuckles then no effusion}}

Extra-articular Features

General & Skin

  • {{c1::Fatigue and weight loss (early on)::Describe the systemic symtpoms}}.
  • {{c1::Morning stiffness that gets better with movement::Describe the stiffness}}.
  • {{c2::75}}% develop one or more extra-articular manifestations within 5 years of onset of RA.
  • Skin affection:
    • {{c3::Subcutaneous}} nodules occur in 20% of patients who are rheumatoid factor positive.
      • Develop at sites of {{c4::pressure e.g. elbows, scalp, fingers::Where does it develop}}.

Extra-articular Features

Ocular

  • {{c2::  {{c1::Keratoconjunctivitis sicca}} (Sjӧgren’s Syndrome) 
  • Episcleritis
  • Scleritis
  • Scleromalacia
  • Scleromalacia perforans::what are the five ocular factors}}
Sjӧgren’s Syndrome describe as dryness in all external glands

Extra-articular Features

Pulmonary

  • Involvement is frequent.
  • {{c1::Pleuritis – chest pains, pleural effusions.
  • Pulmonary nodules—peripheral (1–8 cm), may cavitate and cause effusions.
  • Pulmonary fibrosis (healing of the nodules) – usually mild, non progressive and lower lobe in location.
  • Caplan’s syndrome – {{c2::pneumoconiosis and multiple peripheral nodules (Present alot in coal mines' workers)}} .::What are the 4 main signs}}

Extra-articular Features

Cardiac & Renal

  • Cardiac
    • {{c3:: {{c1::Pericarditis}} is the most common cardiac manifestation.
    • Endocarditis.
    • {{c2::Myocardial}} disease results from nodular granulomatous lesions seen in RA::What are the 3 main signs}}.
  • Renal
    • {{c4::Renal amyloid is well described. ( can cause nephrotic syndrome)
    • Glomerulonephritis (related to drugs)::What are the two main signs}}.

Extra-articular Features

Vasculitis

  • Vasculitis
    • {{c1::Nailfold splinter haemorrhages – mild, chronic
    • Small vessel vasculitis – Nodule formation::What are the two main signs}}
    • Systemic vasculitis that complicates RA is uncommon.

Extra-articular Features

Neurological

  • Cervical cord compression
    • {{c1::Subluxation of cervical spine at Atlanto-axial joint (To test, make chin touch chest)}::How does that happen}}
  • Entrapment neuropathies
    • e.g. {{c2::carpal tunnel syndrome}}
  • Peripheral neuropathy
    • {{c3::Mild glove and stocking sensory loss::What does that cause}}
  • Mononeuritis multiplex
  • Comes from {{c4::the inflammation of the vessels spreading to the nerves, making it not work}}
If the sublaxation gets worse or with wrong movements ( like chin lift in anesthesia), could affect spinal cord, could lead to quadriparesis

Extra-articular Features

Haematological

  • {{c1::Normochromic, normocytic}} anaemia
    • Degree of {{c2::anaemia correlates with disease activity through bone marrow depression and iron utilisation is impaired::What causes anemia}}.
  • {{c3::Thrombocytosis::What else is there on top of the anaemia}}

Clinical Evaluation

History

  • Chronological onset of disease
  • Acute or chronic
  • Location of pain
  • Pattern of joint involvement
  • Duration and frequency of attacks
  • Associated symptoms
  • Early morning stiffness
  • Present status

Present Status

  • Review of number of joints affected
  • Extra-articular features
  • Functional capacity
  • Activities of daily living

{{c1::Functional Activity}} (Good to use in egypt)

  • Normal
  • Some disability but adequate for normal activity
  • Activities restricted and use of special devices
  • Totally dependent

Activities of Daily Living

  • Mobility: walking, climbing, bed-chair.
  • Personal care: eating, dressing, washing, use of toilet.
  • Special hand function: door handles, keys, jars, pen, scissors
  • House work, shopping

Examination

  • Eyes
  • Skin: {{c2::warmth, nodules, vasculitis, surgical scars, pallor for anemia::What am i looking for}}
  • Joint {{c2::tenderness and swelling::What am i looking for}}
  • Joint deformities: {{c2::Swan neck, Boutonniere deformity, Z-deformity, ulnar deviation/ MCP sublaxation, hallux valgus, hammer toes::What am i looking for}}
  • Muscle testing for {{c2::weakness and disuse atrophy}}
  • Respiratory
  • Cardiovascular
  • Neurological

Investigations

Baseline Labs & Serology

  • {{c1::CBC}}
  • {{c1::ESR}}
  • {{c1::Urea and creatinine, LFTs (To make sure kidney and liver work well) }}
  • Rheumatoid factor
    • {{c2::IgM}} antibody directed against patient’s {{c2::IgG}} immunoglobulin
    • High titre in {{c3::extra-articular disease, nodules and in severe disease::where}}
  • Anti-CCP antibody (more specific)
    • Anti-cyclic citrillunated peptide {{c4::97}}% specific
    • {{c4::78}}% sensitive

Seropositive Rheumatoid Arthritis

  • {{c5::Not all patients with RA have positive autoantibodies::T or F Not all patients with RA have positive autoantibodies}}.
  • 70–80% have rheumatoid factor BUT healthy people can also have RF.
  • In contrast fewer patients have anti-CCP but it rarely occurs in healthy people.
  • The combination of RF +ve, anti-CCP +ve makes RA {{c6::very likely::Very or less likely}}.
  • These seropositive patients have a worse outlook and need more aggressive treatment.
  • Inflammatory markers tend to be high and Hb may be low.

Diagnosis of RA (Laboratory Support)

  • Diagnosis supported by the identification of rheumatoid factor (RF), an anti-IgG antibody present in >80% patients.
  • Most of these antibodies are of the IgM subtype, with specificity for the Fc portion of the IgG molecule.
  • High levels associated with {{c1::worse}} prognosis.
  • Traditionally detected by agglutination methods.
  • Inflammatory markers CRP and ESR are usually raised.

Imaging & Joint Aspiration

  • X-rays
    • {{c2::Soft tissue swelling
    • Periarticular osteopenia
    • Joint erosions
    • Subluxations and deformities::What are the 4 things to look for}}


  • Other imaging methods include musculoskeletal ultrasound and MRI.
  • Joint aspiration
    • {{c3::Clear yellow
    • Variable wcc–lymphocytes
    • Low glucose
    • High protein
    • RF::What am i looking for 5 things}}

Classification Criteria

  • ACR classification criteria for RA (1987)
    • Patients must have 4 of 7 criteria:
      1. Morning stiffness lasting at least 1 hour *
      2. Swelling in 3 or more joints *
      3. Swelling in hand joints *
      4. Symmetric joint swelling *
      5. Erosions or decalcification on X-ray of hand
      6. Rheumatoid nodules
      7. Abnormal serum Rheumatoid factor
      • must be present at least 6 weeks
In the new 2010 criteria, the items are
{{c1::Joint involvement, serology. Acute phate reactants. Symptoms duration

Need one joint with unexplained synovitis }}

Treatment

General Principles

  • Diagnosis
  • Multidisciplinary approach
    • Doctor, specialist nurse, physiotherapist, occupational therapist
  • Pain relief
    • NSAIDS, simple analgesia
  • {{c3::DMARDs}}: Main line of treatment.
  • Rest, relaxation of joints splinting
  • Aids to daily activities
  • Exercise to prevent disuse atrophy
  • Psychotherapy and counselling
  • Surgery – synovectomy, arthroplasty

{{c1::NSAIDS}}

  • Reduce pain and inflammation
  • Many inhibit COX-1 as well as COX-2
  • Important contraindications
    • {{c2::Peptic ulcer history
    • Renal impairment
    • asthma}}

Corticosteroids (not used alot nowadays)

  • Local {{c4::intra-articular or systemic oral, iv::How to give}}
  • Monitor for {{c5::diabetes & hypertension}}
  • Prevent osteoporosis: {{c6::HRT/ calcium + vitamin D, bisphosphonates}}.

Treatment

General Principles

  • Diagnosis
  • Multidisciplinary approach
    • Doctor, specialist nurse, physiotherapist, occupational therapist
  • Pain relief
    • NSAIDS, simple analgesia
  • DMARDs: Main line of treatment.
  • Rest, relaxation of joints splinting
  • Aids to daily activities
  • Exercise to prevent disuse atrophy
  • Psychotherapy and counselling
  • Surgery – synovectomy, arthroplasty

DMARDs: {{c1::Hydroxychloroquine and Chloroquine}}

  • Stabilises lysosomes in neutrophils
  • Side effects:
    • {{c2::Skin pigmentation
    • Retinopathy (fundus examination is needed)
    • Myopathy}}

DMARDs: {{c3::Salazopyrine}}

  • 5-aminosalicylic acid + sulphapyridine
  • Inhibits lymphocyte function & neutrophil migration
  • Side effects: {{c4::rashes, liver abnormalities}}
  • Monitor {{c5::liver function and CBC}} regularly 

Leflunomide and Gold

  • {{c6::Leflunomide}}
    • Inhibits pyrimidine synthesis
    • Side effects include {{c7::diarrhoea, alopecia, rash and abnormal liver function tests}}
  • Gold IM or oral (not available and no longer used)
    • Inhibits neutrophil function & lymphocyte activity
    • Side effects: rashes, proteinuria, bone marrow suppression

Other DMARDs: {{c8::Penicillamine}}

  • Inhibits neovascularisation, action of neutrophils and lymphocytes
  • S.E: {{c9::rashes, bone marrow suppression, proteinuria}}

Treatment

Immunosuppressives

  • {{c1::Azathioprine}}
    • S.E: {{c4::bone marrow suppression, malignancy}}
  • {{c2::Cyclophosphamide}}
    • S.E: {{c5::Myelosuppression, gonadal toxicity, hemorraghic cystitis}}
  • {{c3::Cyclosporin}}
    • S.E: {{c6::hypertension & creatinine rise}}

Biologic Drugs: {{c7::Etanercept and Infliximab}}

  • Selective immunosuppressants which inhibit the activity of {{c8::tumour necrosis factor}}.
  • {{c9::Infliximab}}(given {{c10::IV}} infusion) must be combined with {{c11::methotrexate}}.
  • Expensive costing about £8,000 per patient per annum.
  • Side effects of etanercept — {{c12::site irritation (25 mg sc twice a week).}}

Systemic Lupus Erythematosus — Overview & Epidemiology (Slides 5)

  • Chronic autoimmune disease
  • Incidence 1:1000
  • Female: Male {{c1::9:1}}
  • Presents 15–50 yrs
  • Afrocaribbean, Asian : Caucasian 9:1
  • Multisystem

SLE — Pathogenesis (Slide 6)

  • Complex interplay between genetics, environment and immune response

Genetics

  • High concordance in {{c1::monozygotic}} twins
  • 5–12% of relatives with lupus have the disease
  • No single lupus gene
  • Disease is polygenic
  • At least 30 susceptibility genes identified
  • {{c2::HLA-DR2}}, {{c2::HLA-DR3}}, {{c2::HLA-DR4}}, {{c2::HLA-DR8}} (present in 75%)
  • Homozygous deficiency of {{c3::C1q}} complement

Forms of the disease (Slide 7)

  • Mild disease—for {{c1::most}}
  • Life threatening—for {{c1::few}}
  • Several types
    • Discoid lupus
    • Systemic lupus erythematosus 
    • Drug induced lupus
    • Neonatal lupus

Presentation (Slide 8)

  • Fatigue, lethargy, fever, weight loss
  • Skin {{c2::rashes}}
  • Mouth {{c2::ulcers}}
  • Hair {{c3::loss}}
  • {{c4::Raynaud’s}} phenomenon
  • {{c5::Aching}}joints
  • Chest {{c6::pain}}
  • {{c7::Renal or neuropsychiatric}} manifestations
  • {{c7::Hematologic}}manifestations

Clinical Features (Slides 9–22)

Cutaneous Manifestations (Slide 9)

  • Most common rash is {{c1::photosensitive, raised erythematous malar}} rash. 55–85% develop at some point in disease
  • Discoid Lupus Erythematosus (DLE): 15–30% {{c2::circular, scaly hyperpigmented}} lesions with {{c3::erythematous rim, atrophic}} center—can be disfiguring
  • {{c4::Mouth/vaginal/nasal::Where}} ulcers
  • {{c5::Alopecia}}: may be diffuse or patchy. Occurs in 50% of patients

Clinical Features (Slides 9–22)

Musculoskeletal Manifestations (Slides 13–15)

  • {{c1::Polyarthritis}}, mild to disabling, occurs most frequently in {{c2::hands, wrists, knees}}. Occurs in 90% of patients
  • Joint deformities occur in only 10%
    • {{c3::Jaccoud’s arthropathy}} – deformities similar to rheumatoid arthritis but correctable (i.e., pathology of {{c4::tendons}}, not joints)
  • Arthritis of SLE tends to be transitory
  • If single joint has persistent pain, consider {{c5::osteonecrosis (prevalence increased in SLE over general population, especially if on steroids)}}
  • {{c6::Myositis}} with elevated {{c6::CK}} and {{c6::weakness}} rarely occurs

Clinical Features (Slides 9–22)

Cardiovascular manifestations (Slides 16–17)

  • {{c1::Pericarditis}}: most common cardiac manifestation and usually responds to {{c2::NSAIDs}}
  • {{c3::Myocarditis and fibrinous endocarditis}} may occur.
  • {{c4::Myocardial infarction}} due to {{c5::atherosclerosis}} can occur in <35 yrs
  • {{c6::Vasculitis::Does vasculitis occur}}

Clinical Features (Slides 9–22)

Renal manifestations (Slides 18–20)

  • {{c3::50}}% white patients
  • {{c3::75}}% in black patients
  • Mild {{c1::glomerulonephritis::Whats the type of renal disease}}
  • Rapidly progressive {{c2::lupus nephritis}} on biopsy
  • Renal failure is rare—5%
Renal Disease — Classes (Slide 19)
  • Class 1 {{c4::Normal (light microscopy)}}
    • Or {{c4::Minimal change (immunog. or complement deposit)}}
  • Class 2 {{c5::Mesangial lupus nephritis}}
  • Class 3 {{c6::Focal Proliferative glomerulonephritis}}
  • Class 4 {{c7::Diffuse Proliferative glom}}.
  • Class 5 {{c8::Membranous glomerulonephritis}}
  • Class 6 {{c9::Interstitial and tubular nephritis}}

Clinical Features (Slides 9–22)

Neurological manifestations (Slides 21–22)

  • {{c2::Central nervous}} system
    • {{c1::Epilepsy
    • Hemiparesis
    • Cranial nerve lesions
    • Brain stem/cord lesions
    • Aseptic meningitis
    • Psychoses::What are the 6 main signs of the CNS}}
  • {{c2::Peripheral}} nervous system
    • Neuropathies
  • {{c3::Microvascular}}
    • Infarcts due to {{c4::vasculitis}}

Diagnosis (Slides 23–25)

  • History
  • Examination
  • ACR Revised criteria for classification of SLE (although now more recent criteria as the SLICC and the newest ACR-EULAR criteria for diagnosis)

ACR Classification criteria (4 out of 11) (Slide 24)

  • {{c1::Facial}} rash  ({{c1::butterfly}} rash) sparing the nasolabial folds
  • {{c1::Discoid}} rash
  • Photo{{c1::sensitivity}}
  • {{c2::Oral or nasopharyngeal}} ulcerations
  • {{c3::Nonerosive}} arthritis/arthralgia
  • Serositis
  • Renal disorder
  • Haematological disorder
  • {{c4::anemia, leukopenia, and thrombocytopenia ( so all WBCs are pretty low, because of all ABs attacking them making them low)::What happens in the haem. disorder}} 
  • Neurological disorder
  • Immunological (anti dsDNA, anti-Sm. Or +ve APS antibodies)
  • ANA positive

Investigations (Slide 25)

  • CBC, ESR, liver functions, Kidney functions, urine analysis and 24 hour urine protein.
  • Immunological investigations: Complement and antibodies assessment:
    • {{c5::ANA}} 100% (Highly sensitive)
    • {{c5::dsDNA}} 60%
    • Anti-RNP 30%
    • Anti-{{c5::Sm}} 20%
    • Anti-Ro 30%
    • Anti-La 10%
  • Kidney biopsy and sometimes skin biopsy for rash are required
  • MRI brain in cases of {{c9::CNS lupus}} may be required
  • Specifically in SLE we test complement {{c6::C3 C4}}, could have SLE but not show positive {{c6::C3 C4}} but usually it tests positive
  • {{c7::dsDNA}} and {{c7::Anti-Sm}} are v v speicifc
  • ANA not specific to just SLE {{c8::but has to be there for SLE::But does it have to be there or not}}
  • Anti-RNP is in another disease, mixed Connective tissue disease

Management (Slides 26–28)

  • Explanation and reassurance as a form of patient education
  • Symptomatic
  • Minimize lupus flare and infection
  • Minimize major organ damage: monitor BP and urine

Pharmacologic / Local Measures (Slides 26–27)

  • Analgesics
  • Local treatment including sun blocks with {{c1::high SPF}}
  • NSAIDs
  • Steroids (intra-articular, pulsed, oral, topical)
  • DMARDs: {{c2::Antimalarials i.e., Hydroxychloroquine}}
  • Immunosuppressive {{c3::azathioprine and cyclophosphamide}} according to organ involvement and severity of affection
  • Biologic treatment in severe cases and those not responding to conventional treatment

CNS lupus management (Slide 28)

  • Psychoses and seizure
    • {{c4::anticonvulsants
    • antipsychotics}}
  • Status epilepticus, coma
    • {{c5::pulse methylprednisolone, IV cyclophosphamide and/or plasmapheresis}}

Antiphospholipid syndrome (from SLE) (Slides 29–31)

  • Main problems ares
  • {{c6::Thrombosis}}
    • {{c6::venous}}
    • {{c6::arterial}}
  • Recurrent {{c6::foetal}} loss
  • {{c6::Thrombocytopenia::what does it cause to the WBCs}}
  • {{c6::Livedo reticularis::What does it cause to the skin}}

Diagnosis is confirmed by specific antibodies

  • {{c7::Lupus anticoagulant}}
  • {{c7::Anticardiolipin antibody}}

Cause of death in SLE (Slide 32)

  • {{c1::Infection (most common) }}
  • {{c1::Cardiovascular}}
  • Organ failure especially {{c1::renal}}
  • {{c1::Malignancy}}
  • other

Pregnancy and SLE (Slide 33)

  • Fertility {{c1::normal}}
  • {{c2::Foetal loss (active disease, antiphospholipid syndrome)}}
  • Renal disease may {{c3::deteriorate}}
  • {{c4::Premature}}birth
  • {{c4::Preeclampsia::What other issue can happen in SLE preg.}}
Treatments
  • {{c5::Prednisone, azathioprine and hydroxychloroquine}} safe during preganncy

Lupus variants (Slides 34–41)

Chronic discoid lupus DLE (Slides 34–36)

  • Skin lesions {{c1::photosensitive}}
  • ( often ANA {{c2::negative or low::Negative or positive}})
  • Small % of patients develop systemic form (5%)
  • the only variant does not need ANA 

Subacute cutaneous lupus SCLE (Slides 37–38)

  • {{c3::Photosensitive}} rash, non-scarring
  • Mild illness
  • {{c4::Low}} incidence of renal disease
  • ANA {{c5::positive}}
  • {{c6::Anti-Ro}} antibody

Neonatal lupus (Slides 39–40)

  • Born to mothers with {{c7::Ro}} antibody
    • Incidence 1 in 20
  • Rash {{c8::transient}}
  • {{c9::Complete heart block 2%::What heart pathology is present}}
    • Mortality 15–25%
    • Recurrence in a subsequent pregnancy 15%

Lupus in the elderly (Slides 41–42)

  • 15% of cases disease begins after 60 yrs
  • Onset {{c10::insidious}}
  • {{c11::Interstitial lung}} disease is common
  • {{c11::CNS and renal}} disease is uncommon

Drug induced lupus ( Very important) (Slides 43–44)

  • {{c1::Procainamide
  • Hydralazine
  • Isoniazid
  • Anticonvulsants
  • Chlorpromazine::What 5 drugs are most commonly associated with inducing luous}}
  • Rarely
    • Penicillin, penicillamine, sulphonamides
    • Sulphasalazine, oral contraceptives.

Features of drug induced lupus (Slide 44)

  • Common
    • {{c3::Poly}}arthritis
    • Rash
    • {{c4::Pleurisy / pericarditis::What regarding the CVS}}
    • Pulmonary {{c2::infiltrates}}
    • {{c2::Positive}} ANA (mainly histones and ssDNA)
    • Resolution when drug stops
  • Rare
    • Renal and psychiatric disease

Sjogren’s syndrome (Slides 45–48)

  • {{c2::Dry}} eyes
  • {{c2::Dry}} mouth
  • Positive Schrimer’s
  • {{c1::Dryness in the eye, we put a filter paper in the eye to see how wet the eye is::Describe the shircmers test}} 
  • Histology salivary glands
  • {{c3::Objectively dry}} mouth
  • Serology {{c4::Ro+/-La}}

Overlap syndromes (Slides 49–50)

  • SLE – {{c1::20}}% patients Sjogren’s syndrome
  • SLE – {{c1::5}}% patients myositis
  • SLE / scleroderma
  • SLE / scleroderma / rheumatoid arthritis
  • Mixed connective tissue disease:
    • SLE, polymyositis and scleroderma

Osteoarthritis 

Definition (slide 5)

  • Loss of the {{c1::cartilage at the end of the joints and associated changes at the bone ends and the joint margins}}.
  • {{c2::It can affect individual joints such as the knee or hip, groups of joints such as the hand or the spine in the neck and back, or combinations of the above::What does it mostly affect}}.

Osteoarthritis 

Pathophysiology (slide 7)

  • OA is characterized by biochemical, molecular and biomechanical changes of both cells and matrix which lead to {{c4::softening, ulceration and loss of articular cartilage, sclerosis of subchondral bone, osteophytes and subchondral cysts::List the 5 things that happen to the bones}}.
  • Articular cartilage is a connective tissue composed of chondrocytes and extracellular matrix (ECM) as the ground substance containing mainly collagen type II as well as type IX and XI and proteoglycans.
  • In the early stages of disease, {{c1::water}} content increases leading to tissue edema and weakening of the collagen network. Type {{c2::II}} collagen and {{c2::proteoglycan}} content progressively decrease leading to impairment of mechanical function.
  • Chondrocytes alter their gene expression and take a {{c3::catabolic}} phenotype, synthesizing increased levels of proteolytic enzymes that degrade ECM.

Structural and periarticular changes (slides 8–10)

  • The cartilage becomes rough and thin.
  • The bone at the edge of the joint grows outwards, forming bony spurs called {{c5::osteophytes}}.
  • The {{c6::synovium}} may swell and produce extra fluid, causing the joint to swell.
  • The capsule and ligaments slowly thicken and contract.

Osteoarthritis 

Epidemiology (slide 11)

  • OA is by far the most common form of arthritis in the UK as well as in Egypt and its main symptom is {{c1::pain}}. {{c2::OA can appear radiographically without symptoms::Can OA appear radiographically without pain}}.
  • Radiographic and symptomatic OA are more common among the {{c3::African Americans}} compared to white people.

Differences between Primary and Secondary Osteoarthritis (slide 14)

  • Primary Osteoarthritis
    • Develops with{{c1::no underlying identifiable cause}}
    • Incidence increases with {{c1::age}}
    • Typical joint distribution (i.e. {{c2::hips, knees, DIPs, carpometacarpal joint of thumb}})
  • Secondary Osteoarthritis
    • Develops secondary to underlying causes either {{c3::congenital (e.g. epiphyseal dysplasias)}}, {{c3::metabolic (e.g. haemochromatosis, acromegaly, CPPD)}}, {{c3::traumatic or inflammatory (e.g. RA)}}
    • Can occur in {{c4::young}} ages
    • Atypical sites (e.g. {{c5::MCPs, wrists, elbows, shoulders, ankles}})

Symptoms and Signs (slides 15–17)

  • Pain
  • Stiffness
  • A {{c1::grating or grinding}} sensation (crepitus) when you move the joint
  • Soft or hard swellings

Figure: Nodes (slides 16–17)

  • How to know if its Rheumatoid arth vs osteo
    Osteo has the {{c2::DIP}}

Primary OA comes in the {{c1::medial or central}} compartments of the joints
Secondary comes {{c1::anywhere}}

OA Treatment Summary (slide 24)

  • {{c1::Exercise}} (local muscle strengthening and general aerobic fitness)
  • {{c2::Paracetamol}} and {{c2::topical NSAIDs}} first
  • {{c3::Arthroscopic}} lavage and debridement should not be offered routinely, only in {{c4::mechanical locking}}
  • Standard {{c5::NSAID}} or a {{c5::COX-2}} inhibitor should be co-prescribed with a {{c6::proton pump inhibitor (PPI)}}
  • Joint replacement surgery only if substantial impact on their quality of life and are refractory to non-surgical treatment (mostly in stage 4 of the disease)

Raynaud’s phenomenon

Definition and pathophysiology

  • Raynaud’s phenomenon is a common medical condition that represents an {{c1::exaggerated vasoconstrictor response to normal stimuli}}
    1. {{c2::Digital arterial vasospasm::What causes}}⟶ pallor, numbness
    2. {{c2::Deoxygenation of static venous blood::What causes}}⟶ cyanosis, suffusion, and/or pain and paraesthesia
    3. {{c2::Reactive hyperaemia::What causes}}⟶ return of blood flow
  • Most commonly caused by {{c3::exposure to cold temperatures or stress}}
  • {{c4::Digital::Which}} vessels:
    • More sensitive to cold
    • Close at higher temperature
    • Delayed recovery following cold exposure

Raynaud’s phenomenon

Epidemiology

  • {{c1::1 in 10::How many}} females
  • Less common in males
  • A small proportion of patients with Raynaud’s will have an underlying connective tissue disease

Is it primary or secondary?

  • History
  • Physical examination
  • Investigations
    • Thermography
    • {{c2::Capillaroscopy}} of nail fold capillaries
    • Autoimmune profile – {{c2::ANA}}

Raynaud’s phenomenon

Primary Raynaud’s phenomenon: good vs bad signs

  • Good signs
    • {{c1::Teenage and early 20’}}s onset
    • {{c1::Stable}} symptoms
    • {{c1::Familial}} Raynaud’s
    • {{c2::Normal}} nail fold capillaries
    • {{c3::Negative}} antinuclear antibodies
  • Bad signs
    • Male
    • Late onset {{c4::35}} years
    • {{c4::Increasingly severe}} symptoms
    • {{c4::No}} family history
    • {{c5::Abnormal}} capillaries
    • {{c6::ANA+/ENA+::Which antinuclear antibodies}}

Raynaud’s phenomenon

Primary vs Autoimmune Raynaud’s Phenomenon

  • Primary Raynaud’s
    • Occur in women between ages of {{c1::15–30}} years
    • {{c2::Fingers}} are more affected than {{c2::toes}} but thumbs are frequently spared
    • Symmetric manifestations
    • {{c3::Don’t develop trophic complications as digital ulcers, pitting and gangrene::Do they develop trophic complications}}
    • Nail fold capillary examination and serology are {{c4::negative}}
  • Autoimmune Raynaud’s Phenomenon
    • Onset in {{c5::third and fourth}} decades
    • Occur in {{c5::males and females}}
    • Signs and symptoms of underlying autoimmune disease
    • More prone to {{c6::trophic}} complications
    • Nailfold capillary examination and serologies can be {{c7::abnormal}}

Raynaud’s phenomenon

Autoimmune Raynaud’s phenomenon: risk

  • Raynaud’s phenomenon with positive {{c1::ANA}} and/or abnormal nailfold capillaries
  • 10–15% likelihood of developing a defined connective tissue disease

Secondary Raynaud’s Phenomenon: associated conditions

  • Connective tissue disease associated
    • {{c2::Systemic sclerosis}} – 96%
    • {{c2::Systemic lupus erythematosus}} – 20%
    • {{c2::Dermatomyositis/polymyositis}} – 20%
    • {{c2::Sjögren}}syndrome – 20%
    • Undifferentiated connective tissue disease
    • Overlap syndromes
    • {{c2::Rheumatoid arthritis}} <5%
  • Drug-induced
    • {{c3::Ergo-alkaloids}}
    • {{c3::β-blockers}}
    • {{c3::Bromocriptine}}
    • {{c3::Chemotherapeutics}}
  • Haematological conditions/hyperviscosity syndrome associated
    • {{c4::Polycythaemia}}
    • {{c4::Leukaemia}}
    • {{c4::Lymphoma}}
    • {{c4::Cryoglobulinaemia}}
    • {{c4::Cold agglutinin}} disease
    • {{c4::Myeloma}}
    • {{c4::Waldenström macroglobulinaemia}}
  • Endocrine syndromes associated
    • {{c5::Hypothyroidism}}
    • {{c5::Pheochromocytoma}}
    • {{c5::Acromegaly}}
  • Other associated conditions
    • {{c6::Vibration white}} finger
    • {{c6::Thoracic outflow}} syndrome
    • {{c6::Carpal tunnel}} syndrome
    • {{c6::Reflex sympathetic}} dystrophy

Raynaud’s phenomenon

Differential diagnosis – Raynaud’s-like conditions

  • {{c1::Vasculitis}}
  • {{c1::Antiphospholipid antibody}} syndrome
  • {{c1::Buerger’s}} disease
  • {{c1::Macrovascular}} disease – embolic, thrombotic or atherosclerotic complications

Raynaud’s phenomenon

Therapy – Raynaud’s phenomenon

  • Single short attacks
    • {{c1::Warmth, heating appliances, smoking cessation, Vitamin C and E}}, etc.
  • Frequent long attacks interfering with daily living, digital ulcers
    • {{c2::Calcium channel}} blockers (Nifedipine, Diltiazem)
    • {{c3::Angiotensin II receptor}} antagonists (Losartan, Irbesartan)
    • {{c4::Serotonin reuptake}} antagonists (Fluoxetine)
    • GTN patches
  • Severe attacks, ulcers
    • {{c5::IV Iloprost (a synthetic prostacyclin), sympathectomy}}
  • Gangrene
    • {{c6::Auto amputation or surgical amputation}}
  • Secondary infection
    • Antibiotics, surgery

Systemic Sclerosis (SSc)

Epidemiology

  • A rare connective tissue disease
  • Incidence: 3.7–56 cases/million/year
  • Prevalence: 31–399 cases/million
  • Peak onset {{c1::30–50}} years of age
  • Female predominance: 75%–96%
  • Predisposing factors
    • Genetic
    • Environmental (epoxy resin, rape seed oil, silica, drugs – bleomycin)

Systemic Sclerosis (SSc)

Classification: Limited vs Diffuse Scleroderma

  • Limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis (lcSSc)
    • Skin sclerosis {{c1::distal to the elbows and knees, over the face and neck::Where is it}}
    • Could also be called {{c2::CREST}}
    • {{c3::Anti-centromere}} Ab (very specific to limited)
  • Diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis (dcSSc)
    • {{c4::Distal and proximal::Where}} skin sclerosis
    • Once it affects the {{c4::trunk}}
    • Affects the chest a lot
    • {{c5::Anti-Scl 70}} Ab (very specific to diffuse)

Skin pathology

  • Skin sclerosis occurs as a result of collagen fiber accumulation in the {{c6::deep}} dermis which gradually invades the subcutaneous adipose layer.
  • With disease progression, skin atrophies with thinning of epidermis and accumulation of hyalinized collagen bundles in the dermis.
