  • The collagen deposition affects {{c7::skin, blood vessels and smooth muscles}}.

Systemic Sclerosis (SSc)

Clinical manifestations

  • Raynaud’s phenomenon
  • Sclerodactyly, Microstomia
    • Microstomia: {{c1::the mouth is super stiff and can barely open her mouth::What is it}}
  • Calcinosis
    • {{c2::Calcium deposits under the skin::What is it}}
  • Telangiectasia
    • Common in the {{c3::limited}} type, {{c4::red spots in the face (made up of capillaries, could blanche if pressed)::Describe it}}
  • GIT involvement – Oesophagus, Bowel, Ano-rectum (both subsets)
    • Lack of {{c5::peristalsis}}
  • Lung involvement
    • Pulmonary fibrosis ({{c6::lcSSc<dcSSc::Where is it more common}})
    • Pulmonary hypertension ({{c7::lcSSc=dcSSc::Where is it more common}})
  • Renal involvement ({{c8::lcSSc<dcSSc::Where is it more common}})
  • Cardiac problems ({{c9::lcSSc<dcSSc::Where is it more common}})
  • Arthralgia/tenosynovitis (tendon friction rub; {{c10::lcSSc<dcSSc::Where is it more common}})

Systemic Sclerosis (SSc)

Gastrointestinal problems

  • Oesophagus – {{c1::dysmotility, ↓sphincter pressure with reflux, Oesophagitis (leading to erosions, bleeding, strictures and possibility of Barrett’s oesophagus)::What happens to the oesophagus}}
  • Stomach – {{c2::early satiety, post-prandial distension, gastric antral vascular ectasia (GAVE) – {{c3::watermelon}} stomach::What happens to the stomach clinically}}
  • Small intestine – {{c4::hypomotility, pseudo obstruction, bacterial overgrowth (abdominal distension, diarrhoea, malabsorption, weight loss)::What happens to the small intesine}}
  • Large intestine – {{c5::hypomotility, pseudo obstruction::What happens to the large intestine}}
  • Ano-rectum – {{c6::sphincter}} involvement with anal incontinence, rectal prolapse
    • If there is diarrhoea then it’s {{c7::small intestine}} issue, if constipation then {{c7::Large}}, if alternating then both

Systemic Sclerosis (SSc)

GI investigations

  • Endoscopy
  • {{c1::Barium}} swallow
  • Oesophageal {{c1::scintiscan}}
  • {{c1::Hydrogen breath}} test
  • Barium follow through - caution in patients with bowel hypomotility – may cause {{c2::pseudo-obstruction}}
  • Colonoscopy
  • Air contrast CT enema
  • Anal manometry

Treatment of GI symptoms

  • {{c3::Small}} meals
  • Bed head {{c3::raise}}
  • {{c4::Proton pump}} inhibitors – omeprazole, lansoprazole
  • {{c4::Prokinetics}}– domperidone, metoclopramide
  • Yearly endoscopy for Barrett’s oesophagus
  • {{c5::Cyclical antibiotics}} for bacterial overgrowth
  • Laxatives
  • Surgery for ano-rectal disease
  • Ambulatory {{c6::TPN}} is sometimes required

Systemic Sclerosis (SSc)

Lung disease in SSc – pulmonary fibrosis

  • {{c1::Scl-70 (diffuse)}} association
  • Symptoms: Dyspnea, dry cough
  • Signs: {{c2::Late inspiratory crackles}}
  • Investigations
    • Lung function tests ({{c3::FVC, DLCO}})
    • X-ray/HRCT lung - basal fibrosis
  • Treatment – depends on severity
    1. {{c4::N-acetylcysteine}}(mucus dissolvement)
    2. {{c5::Mycophenolate mofetil or Azathioprine }}(immunosuppression mainly)
    3. {{c6::IV Cyclophosphamide}} (monthly) + oral steroids
    4. {{c7::Rituximab}} (SLE)
    5. +/- anti-fibrotic ttt

Systemic Sclerosis (SSc)

Lung disease in SSc – pulmonary hypertension

  • +/- pulmonary fibrosis
  • Symptoms – {{c1::dyspnoea}}
  • Signs – {{c2::RV Failure}}
    • {{c3::Raised}} JVP, ankle {{c3::oedema}}, parasternal {{c3::heave}}, {{c3::loud}} P2, tricuspid {{c4::regurgitation murmur}}
  • Investigations
    • Lung function ({{c5::FVC, DLCO}})
    • {{c5::Echo}}
    • {{c5::Rt. heart}} catheterization
  • Treatment
    • General – {{c6::diuretics, anticoagulation}}, {{c7::Oxygen and Digoxin}} for the treatment of heart failure
    • Advanced therapies – {{c8::phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors}} (sildenafil and tadalafil), {{c9::prostanoids}} (epoprostenol, treprostinil and iloprost) and {{c10::endothelin 1 receptor antagonists}} (bosentan and ambrisentan)

• Phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil):
    ↑ cGMP → vasodilation of pulmonary arteries → lowers pulmonary pressure.
  • Prostanoids (epoprostenol, treprostinil, iloprost):
    Mimic prostacyclin (PGI₂) → cause vasodilation, inhibit platelet aggregation, and reduce vascular remodeling.
  • Endothelin-1 receptor antagonists (bosentan, ambrisentan):
    Block endothelin-1, a potent vasoconstrictor → prevent vasoconstriction and reduce vascular smooth muscle proliferation.

Systemic Sclerosis (SSc)

Cardiac problems

  • {{c1::Pericarditis}}- effusion - rarely requires fenestration
  • {{c1::Myocarditis}}– heart failure
  • Arrhythmias
  • Heart block

Systemic Sclerosis (SSc)

Renal crisis

  • Affects ~5% of SSc patients
  • 80% of the cases have {{c1::dcSSc::Diffuse or local}}
  • 60% of the patients carry the {{c2::anti-RNA polymerase}} Ab
  • Screen at risk patients
    • Education, monthly BP, 6-monthly GFR in dcSSc < 3yrs from onset
  • Avoid high doses of {{c3::steroids}} in dcSSc patients in first 3 years

Systemic Sclerosis (SSc)

Diagnosis of scleroderma renal crisis
  • New onset elevated {{c1::BP (>150/85}})
  • Decrease of estimated GFR (eGFR) ≥{{c2::30}}%, ↑ {{c2::creatinine}}
  • {{c3::Microangiopathic hemolytic}} anemia on blood smear
  • Retinopathy typical of {{c4::acute hypertensive}} crisis
  • {{c5::Protein}}uria/{{c5::hema}}turia
  • {{c5::Olig}}uria or {{c5::an}}uria
  • Flash pulmonary {{c6::edema}}
  • Renal biopsy showing characteristic changes ({{c7::thrombotic microangiopathy}})

  • End-organ effect especially {{c8::encephalopathy}}

Systemic Sclerosis (SSc)

Management of established renal crisis

  • Control {{c1::seizures}}
  • Stop {{c1::nephrotoxic}} drugs
  • {{c1::Gentle blood pressure}}control
  • {{c2::CVP}} usually needed (monitoring)
  • {{c3::Review/initiate ACE inhibitor}} (most important ttt)
  • Start {{c4::iloprost}} — continuous low dose if tolerated
  • Renal biopsy when BP {{c5::controlled}}
  • Dialysis — haemo initially, peritoneal maintenance
  • Transplant — at least as successful as SLE
  • Relatively good long-term outcome
Iloprost is a prostacyclin that does VD

Systemic Sclerosis (SSc)

Muscle problems

  • Mild – proximal {{c1::myopathy}} with normal {{c1::CK}}
  • Myositis overlap
    • Proximal muscle {{c2::weakness and fatigue}}
    • Elevated muscle {{c3::enzymes}} – CK
    • Investigations: EMG, muscle biopsy, myositis specific autoantibodies as PmScl
    • Treatment - {{c4::steroids and methotrexate}}

Other Problems

  • Raynaud’s phenomenon
  • Calcinosis
  • Impotence
  • Depression
  • Arthralgia/tenosynovitis (tendon friction rubs)
  • Overlap syndromes
    • Rheumatoid arthritis
    • SLE
    • Sjogren’s syndrome
    • Myositis

Definition of SpA ‘family’

  • ‘Sero-negative’ = negative auto-antibody (RF or anti-CCP).
  • Spectrum of diseases: Historically 4 main groups, now 5 (USpA):
    • {{c1::1- Psoriatic arthritis (PsA)
    • 2- Ankylosing Spondylitis (AS)
    • 3- Reactive arthritis
    • 4- Enteropathic (Ent)
    • 5- Undifferentiated (USpA)}}
  • Characteristic features of this family:
    • 1- {{c2::Negative}} auto-antibody (RF or anti-CCP)
    • 2- {{c2::Inflammatory}} arthritides (peripheral, axial, tendons, entheses).
    • 3- Other organs: Eyes, GIT, oral mucosa, Aortic valve, Pericarditis, Apical Fibrosis / Restrictive PFT Defects, Skin.
    • Metabolic/CVD: esp {{c3::PsA/Psoriasis}} patients.
    • Note :Entheses is site of tendon insertion, if entheses pain then its characteristic of {{c4::Sero negative SpA}}

Axial Spondylo-Arthritis (axSpA)

  • Definition: Inflammatory disease where main symptom is {{c1::back pain}}, and where the x-ray changes of {{c2::sacroiliitis}} may or may not be present.
    • Within this axial SpA group, there are 2 subgroups:
      • {{c3::Ankylosing Spondylitis (AS)}}– diagnosis when the x-ray changes are clearly present.
      • {{c3::Non-radiographic axial spondyloarthritis (nr-axSpA)}}– diagnosis when x-ray changes are not present but clinical symptoms and MRI (sacroiliitis) findings of inflammation are present.
    • The term axial SpA attempts to include both those with x-ray changes (AS) and those with MRI-only changes (nr-axSpA).

Inflammatory Back Pain

  • You may have inflammatory back pain if you answer yes to at least 4 of 5 statements below.
  • Back pain >3 months is inflammatory if:
    1. Age at onset <40 years
    2. {{c1::Insidious}} onset
    3. Improvement with {{c1::exercise}}
    4. No improvement with {{c1::rest}}
    5. Pain at {{c1::night}} (with improvement on getting up)

Epidemiology of this family

  • {{c1::PsA}} most common: 1% population
  • AS – 0.25% population
  • ReA – 4–5 per 100,000 population …
  • EntA – variable (IBD 0.05% pop, 10%)
  • {{c2::HLA-B27}} association across spectrum
  • {{c3::AS}}– strongest genetic association

Pathophysiology

  • Genetic: {{c1::HLA-B27}}, positive Family History important (Ps, PsA, AS, IBD).
    • {{c2::Only ~5}}% with HLA-B27 positive status develop AS.
  • Environmental: STD ({{c3::ReA}}), Klebsiella, enteropathic infections ({{c3::ReA}}), smoking.
    • +/− trauma, skin infection for {{c4::PsA}}.
  • Cytokines: IL-12, IL-23, IL-17, TNF-α, T cells (CD4 and 8).

1) Psoriatic Arthritis (PsA)

  • Epidemiology / Impact
    • Up to 1% of the general population.
    • 5% of the UK population have Psoriasis.
    • {{c1::40%}} of patients may develop erosive and deforming arthritis.
  • Five Types of PsA (can coexist; most cases separate):
    • {{c2::Distal Interphalangeal: small joints of fingers or toes, e.g. Dactylitis (“sausage finger”)}} (55–70%)
    • {{c3::Asymmetrical Oligoarthritis: large joints (<5 joints affected)}} (30–50%)
    • {{c4::Symmetrical Polyarthritis}} – resembles RA (15–70%)
    • {{c5::Arthritis of the Sacroiliac Joints and Spine (Spondylitis)}} (5–33%)
    • {{c6::Arthritis Mutilans}} – rare, deforming and destructive (3–5%)
    Notes: oligo = 2–4 joints, poly = 5 or more.
  • Poor Prognostic Factors for PsA
    • A strong family history of {{c7::psoriasis}}
    • Disease onset < age {{c7::20}} years
    • Expression of {{c8::HLA-B27, HLA-Cw6, or HLA-DR4}} alleles
    • {{c9::Polyarticular}} disease
    • {{c9::Erosive}} disease
    • {{c10::Extensive skin}} involvement

1) Psoriatic Arthritis (PsA)

Classic’ PsA Clinical Features
  • Nail involvement: {{c1::pitting, separation from the nail bed (onycholysis), yellow-pink discoloration (oil-drop sign), ridging::Describe it}}
  • Dactylitis: {{c2::Sausage digits}}
  • Enthesopathy/Enthesitis: Inflammation at the sites of {{c3::ligamentous and tendinous insertions}}
  • {{c4::DIPJ }}involvement -> v imp; v imp.

2) Ankylosing Spondylitis (AS)

  • Key associations and demographics
    • {{c1::HLA-B27}} positive in 95%.
    • Male > Female {{c2::3:1}}.
    • Onset usually aged {{c2::15–25}} years.
    • Delayed diagnosis (average 8 yrs!).
    • Weight {{c3::loss (early),}} {{c3::Fatigue}}, {{c3::Feverish}}, Night {{c3::sweats}}: can occur, so not just Axial.
    • {{c4::Sacroilitis}}: radiographic grading system.
  • The 4 A’s (extra articular manifestations)
    • {{c5::Anterior Uveitis}}(40%)
    • {{c5::Aortic valve/root disease}}(AR)
    • {{c5::Apical}}fibrosis
    • {{c5::Amyloidosis (renal impairment, proteinuria, raised CRP/ESR)}}
  • Clinical signs
    • {{c6::Question-Mark}} Posture:
      • {{c7::
      • Thoracic Kyphosis
      • Loss of cervical lordosis::describe question mark posture}}
    • {{c8::Reduced}}lumbar flexion
  • Special clinical tests
    • Modified {{c9::Schober’s}} test: Mark the patient’s back at {{c10::5}} cm below and {{c10::10}} cm above the dimples of Venus. Then ask the patient to bend forward with knees straight and measure the new distance between your two marks. This distance should normally increase from 15 cm to 20 cm or more. --> if it’s under 20 then it’s {{c11::limited lumbar flexion}}
    • Cervical Test: {{c12::Occiput}} to wall or {{c12::Tragus}} to wall (cm).
    • SIJ Stress Test = {{c13::FABERs test (Flexion, Abduction, External Rotation)::What test is used}}.
    • Thoracic test: Chest expansion in cms ({{c14::3–15}} cm is the normal).
  • Imaging
    • X-ray SIJs = {{c15::Sacroiliitis}}.

    • {{c16::MRI}} SIJs
      • {{c17::Shiny Corner}} sign
      • {{c18::Gold}} standard

3) Reactive Arthritis (ReA)

  • Course and features
    • Onset: often within {{c1::1–3}} weeks of infection.
    • Most people's symptoms will improve within {{c1::3–12}} months.
    • {{c1::15–50}}% get recurrent attacks.
    • {{c1::30}}% can progress to chronic arthritis.
    • Historically: {{c2::Reiter’s}} Triad ({{c3::arthritis, urethritis, conjunctivitis}}).
    • Bulleted proportions:
      • {{c4::50}}% Eye
      • {{c4::20–40}}% (Genito-urinary system infection secondary to sexually transmitted diseases such as chlamydia trachomatis, Neisseria gonorrhea, Mycoplasma genitalium, ureaplasma urealyticum).
      • {{c4::10}}% Aortic valve disease or pericarditis.
    • Note : Comes probably from an STD (genitourinary) or GIT, most likely not GIT cuz its hard.
  • ‘Pathongnomic’ features 
    • {{c5::Keratoderma Blenorrhagica}}

    • {{c6::Pustular Rash (Gon)}}
    • {{c6::Tenosynovitis of small joints (Gon)}}
    • {{c6::Conjunctivitis}}
    • {{c6::Hind-foot, Ankle, Knee, wrist}}

    • EXCLUDE {{c7::SEPTIC JOINT(s}})
  • Associated Infections for ReA
    • {{c8::Chlamydia, Gonorrhoea}}
    • Campylobacter, Shigella, Salmonella, Yersinia
    • Parvovirus B19
    • Streptococcal
    • Entero-viruses
    • Other cervicitis/urethritis (ureaplasma etc)
    • Infective Endocarditis related species
    • Hepatitis B/C/A/E, EBV, CMV
    • Tuberculosis ({{c9::Poncet’s, Pott’s, poncets is arthritis generally, Potts is vertebrae }})
    • HIV – can present with any type of MSK pathology & higher risk of psoriasis, bone density loss.

4) Enteropathic Arthritis

  • Associated conditions
    • Inflammatory bowel disease ({{c1::Ulcerative colitis and Crohns}})
    • {{c2::Coeliac}} disease, {{c2::Whipple’s}} Disease, {{c2::Intestinal}} Bypass (Jejuno-Ileal)
  • Distribution: any joint but often affects the {{c3::feet, ankles, knees (axial more likely with {{c4::Crohns}})}}
  • NB: can be associated with {{c5::vitamin D}} deficiency & Low {{c5::BMD (bone marrow density) especially in Crohn's }}
  • Look for other ‘sero-neg’ extra-articular manifestations
    • esp: Erythema nodosum, Pyoderma gangrenosum, Oral ulcers, Clubbing, Uveitis, Iritis

Investigations for sero-negative SpA

  • Bloods: ESR/CRP/FBC/renal/liver/vit. D/calcium
  • Blood cultures
  • Serology: RF, Anti-CCP, ANA, Ig, Anti-TTG
  • Viral screen: HIV, HBV, HCV, EBV, CMV, Parvovirus B19
  • Stool analysis, {{c1::calprotectin}} in stools
  • GUM screen: Blood, urine, swabs, PCR
  • Synovial aspirate: Microscopy, culture and sensitivity, crystals, PCR
  • X-ray Hands & Feet, SIJ, spine
  • Ultrasound for peripheral joints.
  • MRI sacroiliac joints with STIR technique.
  • Echocardiography.
  • Bone Densitometry (DXA).
  • Ophthalmology

Medical management: Peripheral SpA

  • DMARDs (oral):
    • {{c1::MTX}} (PsA & Psoriasis, AS with peripheral arthritis, Ent, ReA)
    • {{c1::SSZ (Sulfasalazine)}} (PsA without psoriasis, ReA, Ent, AS)
    • {{c1::LEF (Leflunomide)}}
      		(PsA)
  • NSAIDs
  • Steroids: intra-articular, systemic (po or IM)
  • Biologics ({{c2::if failed or intolerant of 2 × DMARDs::When do we give biological treatments}}):
    • Anti-TNF blockers ({{c3::Etanercept, Infliximab, Adalimumab, Golimumab}})
    • {{c4::Ustekinumab}} (IL-12/23 blockade): Psoriasis, PsA
    • {{c5::Secukinumab}} (IL-17 blockade): PsA, AS but dont use in {{c6::entero arthritis }}

Medical management: Axial SpA

  • {{c1::NSAIDs}}: first line in axial Treatment (AS / axSpA / mild PsA)
  • Biologics: 2nd line after {{c2::2 × NSAID}} trial for Axial / AS.
    • {{c3::Anti-TNF}} blockers
    • {{c4::Ustekinumab}}(IL-12/23 blockade): Psoriasis, PsA
    • {{c5::Secukinumab}}(IL-17 blockade): PsA, AS
  • No Role for Steroids and DMARDs
  • Note (verbatim): screen {{c6::for hidden}} infections before giving biologics.

Introduction:
  • Vasculitis is defined as inflammation of blood vessels.
  • It may result in vessel wall {{c1::thickening, stenosis, and occlusion}} ⟶ subsequent ischemia and necrosis.
  • Necrotizing inflammation can completely {{c2::destroy segments of the wall and may lead to bleeding}}.

When to suspect vasculitis:
  • Suspect vasculitis if:
    • {{c1::Unexplained systemic illness (no sepsis, no malignancy, no drugs).
    • Multiple organ involvement with no obvious cause.
    • Symptoms of organ ischaemia.
    • Other suggested features:
      • palpable purpura
      • Glomerulonephritis (Haematuria)
      • mononeuritis multiplex
      • lung infiltrates.}}

• Items that will be discussed in details (classification overview):
  • Primary Vasculitis
    • 1- Large vessel vasculitis:
      • {{c1::Giant cell (Temporal) arteritis (GCA)
      • Takayasu’s arteritis}}
    • 2- Medium vessel vasculitis:
      • {{c2::Polyarteritis Nodosa (PAN)
      • Kawasaki’s disease}}
    • 3- Small/Medium vessel vasculitis:
      • {{c3::Wegener’s granulomatosis (Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis)
      • Churg-Strauss syndrome (Eosinophilic Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis; EGPA)
      • Microscopic polyangiitis}}
    • 4- Small vessel vasculitis:
      • {{c4::Henoch–Schönlein Purpura
      • Essential mixed cryoglobulinaemia}}
    • 5- {{c5::Behçet's}} disease
  • Secondary Vasculitis
    • {{c6::Drugs
    • Infections
    • Malignancies
    • Connective tissue diseases}}

Giant Cell (Temporal) Arteritis (GCA)

  • General:
    • {{c1::GCA}} is the most common form of systemic vasculitis in adults.
    • GCA is classified as a large-vessel vasculitis but typically also involves {{c2::medium and small}} arteries: particularly the {{c3::superficial temporal}} arteries.
    • In addition, GCA most commonly affects the {{c4::ophthalmic, occipital, vertebral, posterior ciliary, and proximal vertebral}} arteries.
  • Clinical features:
    • Female: male ratio = {{c5::3:1}}
    • Constitutional
    • Headache
    • Temporal {{c6::tenderness (also carotid)}}
    • Jaw {{c6::claudications}}
    • Any visual/ocular symptoms
  • {{c7::ACR–EULAR}} classification criteria:

  • Investigations:
    • {{c8::Visual or ocular}} symptoms must be taken seriously, ophthalmology opinion if uncertain.
    • Inflammatory markers.
    • {{c9::Temporal}} artery biopsy (up to 2 weeks post treatment).
      • Negative biopsy {{c10::does NOT::does or does not}} exclude diagnosis.
  • Newer Imaging Techniques (ultrasound figure):
    • Narrowed Lumen
    • Hypoechoic halo
    • Turbulent flow

    • Figure key takeaway: {{c11::Halo}} sign with luminal narrowing supports cranial artery involvement in GCA.
  • Management of GCA:
    • Prednisolone {{c12::40–80 mg}} od
      • {{c13::40}}mg if no visual/ocular symptoms
      • 60–80 (1 mg/kg) if suspect visual/ocular involvement
    • Then gradual withdrawal of steroids.
    • {{c14::Bone}} protection ({{c15::Calcium, vitamin D and bisphosphonate}}).
    • {{c14::GI}} protection (e.g., {{c16::PPI}}).
    • always give bone and GI protection with cortisols [spelling corrected for clarity: corticosteroids].
    • bisphosphonate must be given if {{c17::more than 3 months on cortisols}} [spelling corrected for clarity: bisphosphonate; corticosteroids].
  • Management of relapses:
    • {{c18::Increase dose to previous level or slightly higher (always use minimum effective dose)}}.
    • Add {{c19::steroid-sparing agents (e.g., MTX, Tocilizumab).}}

Takayasu’s Arteritis

  • Arteriographic features (figure):

    • Cockscrew
    • Narrowing
    • Aneurysm
    • irregularity
    • Figure key takeaway: Segmental stenoses, aneurysms, and irregular lumens typify large-vessel involvement.
  • Newer Imaging Techniques:
    • PET-CT scan

  • Management:
    • {{c1::Prednisolone + steroid sparing agents (e.g., MTX)}}.
    • Monitor and treat cardiovascular risk factors.
    • Surgery – {{c2::reconstructive/angioplasty (after achieving remission or in Emergency)}}.

Medium Vessel Vasculitis (generated)

Polyarteritis Nodosa (PAN)

  • Inflammation and necrosis of {{c1::medium}}-size arteries.
  • Dominant features are {{c2::aneurysm and organ infarction}}.
  • American College of Rheumatology (ACR) 1990 criteria:

    • PAN = > {{c3::3}}/10 criteria
  • Nodules include:
  • {{c4::}}

Medium Vessel Vasculitis (generated)

Kawasaki’s Disease

  • {{c1::Mucocutaneos LN}} syndrome
  • Affects {{c2::infants and children}}
  • {{c3::Coronary vasculitis}} is major cause of morbidity and mortality

Small/Medium Vessel Vasculitis (generated)

Wegener’s Granulomatosis (Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis; GPA)

  • Triad of systemic necrotising vasculitis affecting:
    • {{c1::Upper airway (ENT)
    • Lung
    • Kidney}}
  • {{c2::cANCA (anti-PR3)}} positive (>95%)
  • very different to ANCA-P 
  • Pulmonary manifestations
    • {{c3::Nodules, vasculitis and hemorrhage
      }}
  • Kidney manifestations
    • {{c4::Variable
    • RPGN (Rapidly Progressive Glomerulonephritis)
    • Asymptomatic hematuria
    • Impaired renal function predicts poor outcome
      }}

Small/Medium Vessel Vasculitis (generated)

Churg–Strauss Syndrome (Eosinophilic Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis; EGPA)

  • Churg & Strauss (1591) – post-mortem series [as typed]
  • Triad of (Lanham, 1984):
    • {{c1::Asthma
    • Eosinophilia
    • Systemic vasculitis involving >2 extrapulmonary organs}}
  • {{c2::pANCA (Anti-MPO)}} positive

Small/Medium Vessel Vasculitis (generated)

Microscopic Polyangiitis (MPA)

  • {{c1::Necrotising}} vasculitis with few immune deposits predominantly affecting {{c2::small}} vessels.
    • (but can affect small and medium-sized vessels)
    • ({{c4::GN (glomerulonephritis)}} very common, pulmonary vasculitis common)
  • Often {{c3::pANCA (anti-MPO)}} positive.

Small Vessel Vasculitis (generated)

{{c1::Henoch–Schönlein Purpura (HSP)}}

  • Most common form of vasculitis of childhood.
  • A form of {{c2::leucocytoclastic}} vasculitis of the small vessels.
  • Often preceded by {{c3::upper respiratory tract}} infection.
  • Common clinical features:
    • {{c4::Symmetrical}} purpura
    • GIT – {{c5::colic, maelena, haematemesis}}
    • Joints – {{c6::arthritis/arthralgia}}
    • Renal – {{c7::haematuria, proteinuria}}

  • Investigations:
    • Diagnosis is {{c8::clinical}} in most cases.
    • Biopsy – perivascular {{c9::IgA}} deposition.
  • Management:
    • Usually, {{c10::self-limiting}}.
    • {{c10::Supportive}}.
    • {{c11::Corticosteroid, immunosuppressants, plasma exchange}} in severe cases.

Cryoglobulinaemia

  • Cryoglobulin = cold-precipitating Ig.

Essential Mixed Cryoglobulinaemia (Cryoglobulinemic Vasculitis)

  • Skin: {{c1::Palpable purpura (esp. exposed areas, extremities)}}
  • Joints: {{c1::Arthritis/arthralgia}}
  • Neuro: {{c2::Peripheral neuropathy}}
  • Lung: {{c2::alveolitis}}
  • Kidney: {{c3::Glomerulonephritis}}
  • Constitutional manifestations
  • Low {{c4::C4::Which complement factor}}
  • Positive {{c5::rheumatoid factor}}
  • Positive {{c6::cryoglobulins}}
  • Management:
    • Treat {{c7::underlying conditions (HCV, Hematological malignancies)}}.
    • {{c7::Immunosuppression}}.
    • {{c8::Plasma exchange}}in organ-threatening or life-threatening conditions.

Behçet’s Disease

  • Diagnosis:
    • {{c1::Recurrent oral}} ulcers plus 2 of the following:
      • Recurrent {{c2::genital}} ulcers
      • Eye: {{c2::uveitis, retinal vasculitis}}
      • Skin lesions: {{c3::erythema nodosum, pseudo-folliculitis, papulopustular lesions, acneiform nodules}}
      • {{c4::Pathergy}} test (skin hypersensitivity to needle prick)
  • Behçet’s — Skin (figure):

  • Other clinical features:
    • Vasculopathy
      • Venous
        • {{c5::Thrombophlebitis
        • Thrombosis}}
      • Arterial
        • {{c5::Aneurysm}}
    • Neurological
    • Epididymitis
  • Management:
    • {{c6::Corticosteroid}}– local or systemic
    • {{c7::Colchicine}}: mucocutaneous
    • Immunosuppressants
    • {{c8::Anti-TNF}}: in refractory cases.
    • (Anti-platelet/anti-coagulation)

Gout

  • Definition and epidemiology
    • Clinical syndrome caused by inflammatory response to {{c1::Monosodium Urate Monohydrate}} crystals which develop secondary to hyperuricemia
    • Prevalence 8.4/1000
    • Sex/age: 8× more common in males; uncommon in premenopausal women; rare in children; age of onset 40–50 years in males, >60 in females 

  • Hyperuricemia
    • Definition: > {{c2::6.8}} mg/dl (> {{c2::360}} µmol/l)
    • Biochemistry: Purines → hypoxanthine → xanthine → uric acid
    • Uricase: Humans (unlike animals) have {{c3::inactive uricase}}, which oxidizes uric acid to highly soluble allantoin (inactive in humans)

Gout

Classification of Hyperuricemia

  • Underexcretion (90%)
    • Idiopathic or genetic
    • Chronic renal failure
    • Drugs (CAN’T LEAP): Cyclosporin, Alcohol, Nicotinic Acid, Thiazides, Lasix((DM medication
      frusomide), Ethambutol, Aspirin (low dose), Pyrazinamide

      Aspirin low dose creates gout
      High dose treats it
  • Overproduction (10%)
    • Polycythaemia
    • Leukemia
    • Pernicious anemia
    • Chronic hemolytic anemia
    • Lymphoproliferative disorders
    • Psoriasis
    • Cytotoxic drugs used in malignancy (tumour lysis syndrome)

Gout

Classification of Hyperuricemia

  • Underexcretion (90%)
    • Idiopathic or genetic
    • Chronic renal failure
    • Drugs {{c7::(CAN’T LEAP): {{c2::Cyclosporin}}, Alcohol, Nicotinic Acid,{{c3::Thiazides}}, {{c4::Lasix}}((DM medication frusomide), {{c5::Ethambutol}}, {{c6::Aspirin}} (low dose), Pyrazinamide}}
  • Overproduction (10%)
    • {{c8::Polycythaemia
    • Leukemia
    • Pernicious anemia
    • Chronic hemolytic anemia
    • Lymphoproliferative disorders
    • Psoriasis}}
  • Cytotoxic drugs used in malignancy ({{c9::tumour lysis}} syndrome) 
    • N.B Aspirin {{c10::low}} dose creates gout
    {{c10::High}} dose treats it

Gout

Hyperuricemia and Gout: risk context

  • Asymptomatic hyperuricemia is {{c1::10}}× more common than gout → suggests other pathogenic factors
  • Associated factors: Body mass index, Alcohol, Hypercholesterolemia, Menopause, Renal dysfunction, Diuretics → part of {{c2::metabolic}} syndrome with abdominal obesity and insulin resistance
Metabolic syndrome (abdominal obesity, insulin resistance, dyslipidemia, hypertension) → decreased renal urate excretion and increased uric acid production → hyperuricemia → gout.

Gout

Acute Gout

  • Typical sites
    • {{c1::Base of 1st MTP (podagra)}} in 50% of cases; other lower limb joints
    • Non-articular deposition: {{c2::olecranon bursa, Achilles tendon}}
  • Pattern
    • Monoarticular in 90%; progresses to polyarticular with disease progression, in females and {{c3::myelo/lymphoproliferative}} disorders
  • Onset
    • Rapid pain, swelling, erythema
  • Other
    • May coexist with other monoarthritic conditions
    • May have fever, raised WCC, ESR; may have {{c4::normal}} urate level
    • Precipitants: Acute illness, trauma, surgery, Alcohol (Beer, wine), starvation, Drugs – {{c5::Allopurinol}}
Must diffrentiate from septic arthritis!!
• In acute attack, the urate goes to the joints thus making it normal in blood

Gout

Chronic Gouty Arthritis and Tophaceous Gout

  • Natural history and tophi
    • Untreated gout eventually progresses to chronic inflammation and/or establishment of {{c1::tophi}}
    • Tophi: urate embedded in a matrix of {{c2::lipids, proteins, mucopolysaccharides}} → persistent {{c3::inflammatory}} state; tophi damage bone, cartilage, tendons, and soft tissues
  • Figures / clinical images
    • Middle-aged man hopping with a slipper on one foot suggestive of gout

    • Swollen red shiny appearance of toe in early gout

    • Olecranon bursa: large fluid swelling in acute attacks; in chronic gout, large crystal deposits with white tophaceous patches; white urate crystals seen on aspiration

    • Tophi on helix, finger, eyelid in chronic gout

    • Severe hand deformities in chronic gout

    • Figure key takeaway: Chronic gout can produce visible tophi and deformity at multiple sites; aspiration may reveal white urate crystals.
  • Crystals (microscopy)

    • {{c4::Urate crystals}} (needle-shaped) appear bright-colored under polarized light microscopy (strongly birefringent)

Gout

Radiological Diagnosis (Gout)

  • Descriptions
    • Acute on chronic gout; chronic changes include characteristic ‘{{c1::punched-out’, well-defined}} erosions, {{c1::subarticular}} cysts, and {{c1::joint space}} narrowing

{{c2::Acute gout: soft tissue swelling around the first MTP; label: Soft tissue swelling::Describe the photo above}}

Gout

Management – Treatment (overall framing)

  • Acute attack — “Agents to put out fire”
  • Prophylaxis of acute attack — “To make matches damp”
  • Treatment of hyperuricaemia — “To remove matches from body”
  • {{c1::Do not introduce/withdraw hypouricaemic drugs during acute attacks without Colchicine/NSAID as changes in urate levels may increase duration of acute attacks::Can we introduce/withdraw hypouraicaemic drugs during acute attacks?}}

Gout

Treatment of Acute Gout

  • Non-pharmacologic
    • {{c1::Cold pack, Rest}}
  • Anti-inflammatory options
    • A) {{c2::Colchicine}}
      • Inhibits intracellular {{c3::microtubule}} formation
      • Given at {{c4::regular intervals and not as per BNF}}
      • Adverse effects: {{c5::GI intolerance}}
    • OR B) Short course of {{c6::oral steroids}}
    • OR C) {{c6::NSAIDs}}
  • Guideline note
    • 2020 ACR: initiation of hypouricemic drugs during the gout flare is conditionally recommended over delaying until flare resolution

Gout

Indications for Hypouricemic Treatment (ULT)

  • Start/continue ULT for:
    • {{c1::Tophaceous}} gout
    • {{c1::Radiographic}} erosions
    • Uric acid {{c1::nephrolithiasis}}
    • Urate {{c1::nephropathy}}
    • Prophylaxis of chemotherapy-induced gout
    • Recurrent acute episodes (e.g., {{c2::2}} attacks/year)
  • Asymptomatic hyperuricaemia: {{c3::does not require prophylactic treatment::Does it require prophylactic treatment}}
  • Continuation: Continuing hypouricemic drugs indefinitely is {{c4::conditionally recommended::recomennded or not}} over cessation (ACR, 2020)

Gout

Allopurinol

  • Mechanism: Competitive {{c1::xanthine oxidase inhibitor used in both overproducers/underexcretors}}
  • Pharmacokinetics: Active metabolite {{c1::oxypurinol, slowly cleared by renal elimination, half-life 24 hours}}
  • Dosing: {{c2::Once daily; can be used with reduced renal function, titrate to creatinine clearance}}
  • Duration: {{c3::Life-long; cessation → recurrence of symptoms}}

Gout

Febuxostat

  • Mechanism: {{c1::Xanthine oxidase inhibitor (oxidized and reduced forms)}}
  • Clearance: {{c1::Hepatic; can be used in renal impairment}}
  • Safety: Associated with higher risk of {{c2::CVD-related}} death and all-cause mortality (driven by CVD deaths) vs allopurinol (ACR, 2020)

Gout

Novel Therapies and General Recommendations

  • {{c1::Uricosuric}} drugs
    • Use in those with normal renal function
    • Risk of {{c2::renal calculi}}
    • Examples: {{c3::Sulfinpyrazone, Losartan, Fenofibrate}}
  • {{c4::Recombinant}} uricase
    • Useful for {{c5::debulking of tophi}}
    • Benefit limited by emergence of {{c6::neutralizing antibody}}
  • {{c7::URAT-1}} inhibitor; {{c7::IL-1}} antagonist (during acute flare)
  • General Recommendations (ACR, 2020)
    • Limiting alcohol intake
    • Limiting purine intake
    • Limiting high-fructose corn syrup intake
    • Using a weight loss program

Summary of Gout Management

  • Conservative measures
    • Limiting {{c1::high-fructose corn syrup}}
    • Limiting {{c1::alcohol}}
    • Limiting {{c1::purine}} intake
    • {{c1::Weight loss}} program
  • Acute attack
    • {{c2::Colchicine}}
    • Short course of {{c2::oral}} steroids
    • {{c2::NSAIDs}}
  • Hypouricemic TTT

    • {{c3::Xanthine oxidase}} inhibitors (allopurinol or febuxostat)
    • {{c3::Uricosuric}} drugs
    • {{c3::Recombinant}} uricase
    • {{c3::URAT-1}} inhibitor
  • Prophylactic therapy note (as stated)
    • “Prophylactic therapy is given with {{c4::hypouricemic}} drugs till uric acid is normal (Steroids or colchicine or NSAIDs)”

Barriers / Suboptimal Treatment

  • …yet gout treatment remains suboptimal
    • Adherence to treatment
    • Toxicity of treatment
    • Lack of understanding of preventive strategy

Drug–Drug Interaction (case)

  • Case: 66-year-old woman on Colchicine prophylaxis for chronic gout; renal impairment background; admitted with community-acquired pneumonia; penicillin-allergic → started Clarithromycin → developed pancytopenia
  • Mechanism: Inhibition of CYP3A4 increases colchicine levels
  • Recommendation: Use {{c1::azithromycin}} and not erythromycin; risk of rhabdomyolysis

Calcium Pyrophosphate Dihydrate (CPPD) Crystal Deposition Disease

  • Definition & spectrum
    • Inflammatory arthropathy associated with {{c1::calcium pyrophosphate dihydrate}} crystal deposition in hyaline and fibrocartilage
    • Spectrum: Pseudogout, Pseudo-osteoarthritis
    • Epidemiology: {{c2::Female}} preponderance; increases with age
  • Sites of deposition
    • {{c3::Knee, Wrist, Pubic symphysis, Shoulders, Intervertebral discs}}

Calcium Pyrophosphate Dihydrate (CPPD) Crystal Deposition Disease

  • Clinical features
    • Pseudogout
      • {{c1::Acute}} knee {{c1::synovitis}} in female elderly
      • {{c1::Pain, swelling, erythema, fever}}
      • Precipitants: Trauma, Illness, Surgery, Spontaneous
    • Pseudo-osteoarthritis
      • Background of {{c2::generalized osteoarthritis}}
      • {{c2::Wrist, shoulder, knees}}
      • Marked {{c2::inflammatory}} response

Calcium Pyrophosphate Dihydrate (CPPD) Crystal Deposition Disease

  • Investigations
    • Synovial fluid analysis: {{c1::Turbid, blood-stained; intracellular crystals on compensated polarized light microscopy}}
    • Figure:

      Polarized light micrograph of a clump of {{c2::pyrophosphate}} crystals (Rhomboid-shaped) in {{c3::blood-stained}} synovial fluid (weakly birefringent)
  • Radiological diagnosis (CPPD)

    • Calcified {{c4::hyaline}} and {{c4::fibrocartilage}} with {{c5::linear}} and {{c5::spotty}} appearances and {{c6::well-preserved}} joint space

Calcium Pyrophosphate Dihydrate (CPPD) Crystal Deposition Disease

  • Management
    • In CPPD attacks, treatment is {{c1::symptomatic}} to relieve pain and inflammation (NSAIDs or colchicine)
    • {{c2::Colchicine}} may be given as prophylactic therapy to prevent frequent attacks
    • There is no treatment to dissolve crystals or prevent precipitation as in gout

Trimodal Peak of Traumatic Death
  • First peak ({{c1::50}}%): Immediate death.
    • Lacerations of brain stem & upper spinal cord.
    • Lacerations of heart & great vessels.
    • Preventable trauma death.
  • Second peak ({{c1::30}}%): Within 2–3 hours — early death (“the golden hour”).
  • Third peak ({{c1::20}}%): Days or weeks after injury — {{c2::MODF secondary to infection or SIRS}}.

• Importance of Second Peak Mortality
    • Definitive surgical care within the shortest possible period could effectively decrease that peak (Regionalization & training).
    • 10% of traumatic deaths in the USA is due to failure to {{c1::recognize or control bleeding from intra-abdominal organs}}.
    • Failure to manage abdominal injuries successfully accounts for the majority of preventable deaths following multiple injuries.

Features and Phases of Effective Trauma Care

  • Features of Effective Trauma Care
    • Rapid therapy & proper prioritization of injuries & the required interventions.
    • Timely & adequate diagnosis of all injuries & comorbidity.
    • Comprehensive team management.
    • Extend from initial field care till complete rehabilitation.
    • Priorities are:
      • {{c1::Airway}}.
      • {{c1::Bleeding}}.
      • Then usually {{c1::subdural haematomas}}.
      • Limb evidence doesn’t matter awy UNLESS {{c2::THERE IS VASCULAR INJURY}}.
  • Phases of Trauma Care
    1. {{c3::Access.
    2. Prehospital phase:
      • First aid resusc.
      • Triage.
      • Transport.
    3. ER resusc.:
      • Primary survey.
      • Investigations.
      • Secondary survey.
    4. Operative phase:
      • Urgent.
      • Less urgent (priority).
    5. Postoperative phase:
      • ICU, Ward.
      • Tertiary survey.
    6. Rehabilitative phase}}
  • Systems That Sustain Damage in Trauma from most to least important
    • {{c4::Craniospinal.
    • Chest.
    • Abdomen.
    • Limb injuries}}.

Abdominal Injuries: Overview and Clinical Considerations

  • General
    • Usually associated with other injuries (25% isolated abdominal injuries).
    • Association of CNS, chest & limb injuries may obscure injury of abdominal contents & their symptoms.
    • Intra-abdominal injuries carry high morbidity & mortality because they are often not detected (anatomical considerations) or severity is underestimated, especially in blunt trauma where there may be few or no external signs.
    • Always have a high index of suspicion of abdominal injury when the history suggests {{c1::severe trauma}}.
  • Immediate Surgical Triggers (verbatim notes)
    • First thing to do is to {{c2::resuscitate}}; if he is haemodynamically unstable despite resuscitation then a sign for {{c3::exploration}}.
    • Also if he has {{c4::peritonitis}} → {{c5::surgery}} right away.
    • btw, if he has FAST positive, i.e., he’s bleeding, but he’s haemodynamically stable then {{c6::no need for laparotomy::Need for lapratomy or not?}}.
  • Clinical Re-evaluation in Blunt Abdominal Trauma
    • Mandatory; why?
      • Muscle guarding resulting from intraperitoneal injury can be due to {{c7::abdominal wall injuries (cause of non-therapeutic laparotomy in the haemodynamically stable patient) (ie it can mimic peritoneal irritation, so have to revaluate to make sure its not just abdominal wall injury)}}.
      • Signs of {{c8::peritoneal}} irritation after rupture of a hollow viscus can be slow to develop as in small intestinal injury.
    • {{c9::20}}% of polytrauma patients require abdominal operations.
  • Blunt vs Penetrating
    • Broadly divided into blunt & penetrating injuries; direct implication on diagnostic workup & therapy. However, initial assessment & resuscitation if needed are the {{c10::same}}.
    • Wartime abdominal injuries are mainly {{c11::penetrating}}.
    • Civilian injuries are mainly {{c11::blunt}}.
  • Causes of Blunt Abdominal Trauma
    • {{c12::Motor vehicle crashes}} 75% (pedestrians & passengers).
    • {{c12::Falls}}(vertical deceleration).
    • {{c12::Motorcycle & bicycle}} crashes.
    • {{c12::Assaults}}.
  • Causes of Penetrating Abdominal Trauma
    • {{c13::Stab wounds}}.
    • {{c13::Gunshot wound}} (GSW).
    • {{c13::Shotgun wounds}}.
    • {{c13::Impalement injuries}}.
  • Management Issues
    • Is there an injury? Details are less important.
    • Do we need laparotomy?
      • Is there peritonitis?
      • Is he haemodynamically unstable post resuscitation?
        • then {{c14::yes}}

Anatomical Considerations in Abdominal Trauma

  • General
    • The abdomen encompasses a large area from the diaphragm (level of the nipples) superiorly to the infragluteal fold inferiorly.
    • This is specially relevant to penetrating injuries that can transgress the chest or thighs to involve intra-abdominal organs.
    • Note (verbatim): basically things enter thorax/glutes and end up in the abdomen.
    • The abdomen is divided into four areas:
      • Intrathoracic abdomen.
      • True abdomen.
      • Pelvic abdomen.
      • Retroperitoneal abdomen.

  • Intrathoracic Abdomen
    • Rib cage poses difficulty in examination.
    • {{c1::Chest}} stabs can traverse the diaphragm.
    • DPL (Diagnostic Peritoneal Lavage), FAST & CT are of diagnostic value.
  • Pelvic Abdomen

    • Difficult abdominal assessment.
    • {{c2::Thigh & buttock penetrating}} injuries can affect intra-abdominal organs.
    • {{c2::Pelvic}} fractures are frequently associated with organ injury.
    • {{c3::PR & PV (peri rectal peri vaginal)}} are mandatory in assessment.
    • Ancillary imaging is frequently needed: {{c4::urethrocystography & sigmoidoscopy}}.
    • Verbatim notes:
      • might need to insert catheter to make sure no rupture of the {{c6::bladder}}; could also do {{c5::FAST}} scan.
      • if the prosatet is more then {{c7::2}} phalanx deep no urethra insertion. (Since there is some sort of issue with the urethra)

Anatomical Considerations in Abdominal Trauma

  • True Abdomen
    • {{c1::Accessible}} for clinical examination.
    • Plain X-ray may detect {{c2::free air}}.
    • DPL, FAST & CT are of diagnostic significance.
  • Retroperitoneal Abdomen
    • Few physical findings.
    • DPL & FAST are {{c3::unreliable::reliable or unreliable}}.
    • {{c4::CT }} in the haemodynamically stable patient is the best imaging modality.
    • Evaluation needs:
      • {{c5::Serum amylase}}.
      • {{c5::Radiography & angiography}}.
    • Verbatim notes:
      • only thing that shows the retroperitnoeal abdomen is {{c6::CT scan with a dye}}, FAST scan does not do.
      • zone {{c7::1}} trauma always open; zone {{c7::2}} not needed; zone {{c7::3}} try not to open.

Surgical Pathology of Abdominal Trauma

  • Mechanisms in Blunt Abdominal Trauma
    • Compression causing a {{c1::crush injury}}.
    • An abrupt shearing force causing {{c1::tears of organs or vascular pedicles}}.
    • A sudden rise in intra-abdominal pressure causing {{c1::rupture of a viscus}}.
  • Patient Factors
    • More elastic tissue of young victims tolerate trauma better than the less resilient or fixed tissues of the older persons.
    • Since wearing seat belts was made compulsory, number of fatal head injuries has declined but seat belt injuries has emerged; {{c2::avulsion injuries of the mesentery of the small bowel::What injury can be caused by small intestine}}.
  • Penetrating Injuries
    • Abdominal stabs are associated with 20–30% significant organ injuries: need of patient selection for laparotomy.
    • Gunshot penetrating wounds are associated with 80–95% significant organ injury due to higher kinetic energy, {{c3::cavitatory}} effects, {{c3::heat}} effects; mandatory exploration of {{c4::all}} cases.

Role of Surgeons in Trauma Management and the 3R Framework

  • Role of Surgeons in Trauma Management
    • {{c1::Decrease preventable mortality:
      • First aid to keep life.
      • Prevention or alleviation of secondary pathology.
      • Definitive management of immediate life endangering pathology.
    • Decrease morbidity:
      • Prevention of secondary pathology complicating issues.
      • Definitive management according to priorities.
      • Final rehabilitation.}} 
  • The 3R Respective Management from most to least important
    1. {{c2::Resuscitate: A B C}}.
    2. {{c2::Review}} (D E):
      • History & Examination.
      • Investigations & Set Priorities.
    3. {{c2::Repair}}.

Resuscitation in Abdominal Injuries

  • General
    • As the regular resuscitation in polytraumatized patients.
    • Little can be done for blunt abdominal injuries in the field except keeping {{c1::IV}} lines in upper limb.
      • Verbatim note: never in lower limb.
    • In penetrating wounds: apply {{c2::sterile dressings}} even if evisceration occurs.
  • Embedded Foreign Bodies
    • Any embedded FB in the trunk should {{c3::not be removed::removed or nah}} to avoid major bleeding thereafter.
    • This involves highly trained persons at extrication.

Review: History and Clinical Assessment

  • History
    • In penetrating injuries:
      • {{c1::AMPLE: allergies, medications, past medical history, last oral intake, events about the injury.
      • Detail of the weapon.
      • Position of patient at time of injury::What history to take}}.
    • In blunt injuries:
      • {{c2::AMPLE.
      • Mechanism:
        • RTA; pedastrian or passenger, horizontal decleration, lateral crash, capsize, ejection, secondary projectiles.
      • Severity (amount of energy transfer).
      • Conditions before extrication (trapped, crushed, prolonged exposure related to asphyxia, hypothermia & rhambdomyolysis).}}
      • Verbatim note: crush injuries usually cause {{c3::renal}} issues.

Review: History and Clinical Assessment.

  • Clinical Assessment
    • Aim of clinical examination is to {{c1::identify the need for laparotomy}}; precise determination of organ injury is not necessary.
    • Accordingly we have two working diagnoses viz:
      • {{c2::Abdominal bleeding.
      • Peritoneal irritation}}.
  • Working Diagnoses in Abdominal Trauma
    • Abdominal bleeding
      • D.D.: {{c3::extrabdominal sources of blood loss}}.
      • If haemodynamically unstable despite resuscitation with no evident cause — {{c4::laparotomy}}.
      • In trauma centres:
        • If haemodynamically stable — Is there a role for conservation (CT evidence)?
    • Peritoneal irritation
      • D.D.: {{c5::tenderness & spasm due to lower rib fractures, pelvic fractures, & abdominal wall contusions}}.
      • Nearly one half of patients without obvious findings on physical examination will have positive findings at laparotomy — Why?
  • Why Physical Exam Can Be Misleading
    • Intraperitoneal injuries are either vascular, solid, or hollow organ.
    • Interpretation of physical findings is often a function of {{c6::time}} that each type requires to create peritoneal irritation:
      • Rapid presentation by {{c7::hypovolaemia}} with no physical findings in abdomen (e.g., {{c8::mesenteric vessel}} injury).
      • Rapid: {{c8::Immediate peritoneal irritation following injury of the stomach or colon}}.
      • Slow to develop: {{c9::Small intestinal injury}} produce significant intra-abdominal findings within 24 hours.
    • Interpretation is further confounded by:
      • Altered conscious level.
      • Drug intoxication.
      • Spinal cord injuries.
      • Pain & tenderness could not be elisted.

Review: History and Clinical Assessment.

  • Clinical Examination in Trauma to Abdomen
    • Skin bruising ({{c1::tyre marks, seat belt mark, scrotal, perineal & back bruising}}).
    • Movement of abdominal wall with {{c1::respiration}}.
    • {{c1::Rigidity, tenderness & masses}}.
    • Abdominal {{c1::distension}}.
    • Bowel {{c1::sounds}}.
    • {{c1::PR & PV}}.
    • Verbatim notes:
      • {{c2::tyre marks and seat belt}} marks cannot be missed.
      • if doing a PR and theres blood, this is {{c3::sigificant}}, also can feel {{c3::fractures}} in PR.
  • PR Examination
    • Tone of {{c4::sphincter}}
    • Intact {{c4::anorectal}} wall.
    • {{c5::Over-riding}} prostate.
    • Tenderness or masses.
    • Sacrum & coccyx.
    • {{c6::Blood}} on finger.

  • PV Examination
    • {{c7::Lacerated}} vaginal wall.
    • {{c7::Fractured}} pubic rami.

Review: History and Clinical Assessment.

  • Clinical Monitoring
    • Ryle tube:
      • {{c1::Bleeding}} in penetrating wound is diagnostic. (aspirated through the tube)
      • Guard against {{c1::vomiting}}.
      • Monitoring during DPL (Diagnostic Peritoneal Lavage).
    • {{c2::Urinary catheters}} after precautions.

Investigations and Imaging

  • Adjunctive Studies
    • Laboratory.
    • Imaging.
    • Ancillary diagnostic procedures: {{c1::DPL, FAST, CT}} scan.
    • Verbatim note: maybe also {{c1::portable X-ray}} for chest and pelvis.
  • Laboratory Investigations
    • {{c3::HCT.}}
    • Urine (in blunt):
      • If > 30–50 RBC — Do {{c2::IVU (Intravenous Urography)}}.
    • {{c3::Amylase}}.
    • Others (Baseline): WBC, creatinine, blood glucose, electrolytes, blood gases.
  • Imaging Overview
    • Arteriography.
       v uncommon unless there is {{c4::super uncontrolled bleeding}}
    • CT.
    • FAST.
    • Cystography.
    • IVU.
    • Plain Abdomen & Chest.
    • Urology: Urethrography.

Investigations and Imaging

  • Plain Abdomen & Chest
    • Fractures ribs — {{c1::liver or spleen}} injury.
    • Fracture transverse process (esp lumbar)— {{c2::ureteric}} injury.
    • Retroperitoneal stippling associated with {{c3::duodenal}} injuries.
    • Loss of psoas shadow — {{c4::retroperitoneal}} haemorrhage.
    • Opaque FB as bullets.
    • Confirms the position of nasogastric tube.
    • In cases of absent radiologic evidence of spinal or pelvic fractures, it is better to do {{c5::sitting}} chest X-ray than a {{c5::supine}} abdomen or chest X-ray to show:
      • Diaphragmatic {{c6::rupture}}.
      • {{c6::Free air}} intraperitoneal.

Investigations and Imaging

  • CT
    • {{c1::Helical CT}} studies are the state of art diagnostic procedure in haemodynamically stable abdominal injuries.
    • Detects {{c2::retroperitoneal}} injuries (superior to DPL & FAST).
    • Allows grading of solid organ injuries.
    • Low sensitivity to: {{c3::bowel & diaphragmatic}} injuries.
    • Solid organ injury grading by CT sometimes does not correlate with operative findings.
    • Thus decision to operate depend upon:
      • Haemodynamic stability.
      • Transfusion requirements.
      • Metabolic derangement.
      • Physical examination.
  • Arteriography: Indication
    • To diagnose {{c4::renal artery}} thrombosis.
    • During conservation on a CT-confirmed liver haematoma there is drop of {{c5::HCT}} or increased {{c5::volume}} of the haematoma — arteriography & embolization.

Priorities in Trauma Management

  1. Support {{c1::cervical}} spine.
  2. Clear {{c2::airway}} & maintain {{c2::ventilation}}.
  3. Arrest {{c3::external haemorrhage}} & treat {{c3::shock}}.
  4. Examine the injured fully.
  5. Splint {{c4::broken bones}}.
  6. Deal with {{c5::extradural haemorrhage}}.
  7. Attend to {{c6::abdominal injuries}}.
  8. Limb injuries, bones vessels, tendons & nerves.

Repair: Indications of Laparotomy

  • Penetrating Injuries
    • Haemodynamically {{c1::unstable}}. -> lapratomy
    • {{c1::Evisceration}}.-> lapratomy
    • {{c1::All}} gunshot wounds.-> lapratomy
    • Stable penetrating anterior stab wound with physical findings: {{c2::DPL & laparoscopy}}.
    • Back stabs:
      • Signs of peritonitis — {{c3::Laparotomy}}.
      • No signs of peritonitis — either:
        • {{c4::Triple-contrast CT}}.
        • {{c4::Selective}} management.
    • Flank stabs:
      • Signs of peritonitis or evisceration — {{c5::Laparotomy}}.
      • No signs of peritonitis — {{c6::local wound exploration}} — according to direction:
        • Anterior — treat as anterior.
        • Posterior — treat as posterior.

Repair: Indications of Laparotomy.

  • Blunt Abdominal Trauma
    • Haemodynamically unstable:
      • Injury confined to abdomen — {{c1::Laparotomy}}.
      • Multiple injuries uncertain of abdominal contribution — {{c2::FAST}} in trauma centres; {{c2::DPL}} otherwise.
    • Haemodynamically stable:
      • Overt signs of {{c3::peritoneal}} injury — Laparotomy.
      • Multiple injuries uncertain of abdominal contribution or operated upon for other lesions — {{c4::FAST}} in trauma centres & {{c4::CT}}; {{c4::Laparoscopy}}; {{c4::DPL}} otherwise.

Transversalis Fascia & Groin-Adjacent Concepts (Slide 9)

  • The transversalis fascia is the fascial envelope of the abdomen; the competency of the deep inguinal ring depends on its integrity.
    • Tear at the ring ⟶ descent of contents in a previously non-detected oblique inguinal sac (indirect).
    • {{c2::Direct}} hernia presents after a tear in the fascia transversalis in {{c1::Hesselbach’s}} triangle.

Functional Segments & Intersections (Slides 16–17)

  • Functional failure patterns
    • Upper respiratory zone failure ⟶ {{c1::epigastric}}  hernia (tearing of fibers attachment of the diaphragm at {{c2::second tendinous intersection}}).
    • Buffer umbilical zone failure ⟶ {{c3::infantile true umbilical}} hernia, {{c3::adult true umbilical}} hernia, {{c3::paraumbilical}} hernias (opening of umbilical canal or tear in the {{c4::medial attachment of the third tendinous intersection}}).
    • Belly support area failure ⟶ {{c5::groin}} hernias in {{c6::Fruchaud’s myopectineal}} orifice (away from the enforced midline).
    • Upper respiratory + buffer umbilical zones failure ⟶ {{c7::diastasis of the recti}}.

Abdominothoracic Rhythm & Visceroptosis (Slide 18)

  • Abdominal muscles contract and relax with respiration; the wall conforms to viscera.
  • Abdominothoracic rhythm:
    • Inspiration: sternum moves forward, chest expands, anterior abdominal wall moves forward.
    • If chest expands but anterior abdominal wall remains stationary or contracts inward ⟶ {{c1::probable parietal peritoneum inflammation causing reflex abdominal muscle contraction::What is the cause}}.
  • Muscle tone and wall shape:
    • Middle-aged woman with poor abdominal muscles after multiple pregnancies may be incapable of supporting viscera, thus the lower anterior abdominal wall protrudes forward = {{c2::visceroptosis.}}
    • Not to be confused with abdominal tumor (e.g., ovarian cyst) or excess fat.

Ventral Hernias — Overview & Classification (Slides 19–23)

  • Ventral hernia: Congenital or acquired
    • Common:
      1. {{c2::Epigastric

      2. Umbilical (congenital, infantile, adult)

      3. Paraumbilical (second common)
      4. {{c1::Incisional}} (most common; 80% of ventral hernias; secondary hernia)
        }}
    • Uncommon:
      1. {{c3::Spigelian}}(junctional primary)
      2. {{c3::Lumbar}}(junctional primary)
      3. {{c4::Traumatic}}(secondary)
      4. {{c4::Parastomal}}(secondary)
Ventral hernia
In ubmilical hernia, the {{c5::“stalk”}} of the umbilicus is present on the head of the hernia.

Epigastric Hernia (Slides 24–28)

  • Occurs through the {{c1::widest part of the linea alba}}, anywhere between {{c2::xiphoid and umbilicus}}.
  • Usually small; starts as protrusion of extraperitoneal fat between linea alba fibers.
    • Over months/years, fat is forced farther through linea alba and eventually drags a small peritoneal sac.
    • Sac often contains a small piece of {{c3::greater omentum}}.
  • Common in {{c4::middle-aged manual workers::Which demographic}}.
  • Examples
    • Non-complicated epigastric hernia.

    • Features of {{c5::strangulation}}

  • {{c7::“Swiss cheese” multiple}} defects may occur.

  • Note the location of epigastric hernia above the umbilicus. Initially it is sacless but later {{c6::sac}} with content develops

Umbilical Herniae (Slides 29–31)

  • Congenital umbilical hernia (exomphalos/omphalocele)
    • Failure of {{c1::part of midgut to return}} to abdominal cavity from extraembryonic coelom during fetal life.
    • {{c2::No umbilical scar, no skin over the hernia::Describe umbilical scar and skin over hernia}}
  • Acquired infantile umbilical hernia
    • Small hernia due to {{c3::weakness in the umbilical scar in the linea alba}}.
    • Most become smaller and disappear {{c4::without treatment as the abdominal cavity enlarges::How do they disappear}}.
  • Acquired adult umbilical hernia
    • Adult with {{c5::ascites (indirect herniation through an umbilical canal)}}

  • A. {{c6::Congenital umbilical hernia}}
  • B. {{c6::Infantile umbilical hernia}}
  • C. {{c7::Paraumbilical hernia}}
