Paraumbilical Hernia (Slides 32–36)

  • Hernial sac does not protrude through the umbilical scar, but through the {{c1::linea alba in the region of the umbilicus}}
  • Gradually increases {{c2::in size }}and hangs {{c2::downward}}.
  • Neck may be {{c3::narrow}}; body of sac often contains coils of {{c4::small and large intestines and omentum}}.
  • Adults: Prone to {{c5::irreducibility, obstruction, and strangulation (narrow neck vs large contents)}}.
  • Development (Comparison of paraumbilical and umbilical)
    • Acquired umbilical hernia develops when {{c6::umbilical scar is stretched by raised intraabdominal pressure}}.
    • Paraumbilical hernia develops just {{c6::above or just below the umbilicus::Where}}.

  • Operative views
  • Paraumbilical defect — laparoscopic (posterior) view.

  • Massive paraumbilical hernia — operative (anterior) view.

Incisional Hernia — Incidence, Features, and Predisposition (Slides 37–40)

  • Incidence
    • {{c1::10–50}} % after laparotomy
    • 1–5% of laparoscopic port-site
  • Causation due to patient, wound, and surgeon factors
    • Preoperative, operative, and postoperative causes.
  • Features
    • Wide variation in size
    • Often multiple defects within the same scar
    • {{c2::Obstruction}} is common (adhesions within the sac) but {{c2::strangulation}} is rare (relatively wide neck)
    • Open and laparoscopic repairs possible.
  • Predisposing factors for incisional hernia development
    • Patient factors
      • Obesity
      • Malnutrition
      • General poor healing due to immunosuppression or steroid therapy
      • Chronic cough
      • Cancer
    • Wound factors
      • Poor quality tissues
      • Wound infection
    • Surgical factors
      • Inappropriate suture material
      • Incorrect suture placement (creating tension)
  • Remember
    • An incisional hernia usually starts as disruption of the {{c3::musculofascial layers of a wound in the early postoperative period}}.
    • Often unnoticed if the {{c4::overlying skin has healed securely (concealed burst)}}.
    • Classic sign of wound disruption: {{c5::serosanguinous}} discharge.
    • Many incisional hernias may be preventable with {{c6::good surgical technique}}

Incisional Hernia — Detailed Risk Stratification (Slides 40–41)

  • Predisposing factors (themes: impaired healing, subclinical dehiscence, wound infection)
    Preoperative
    • Age >70 years
    • Obesity, esp. morbid obesity (very strong risk factor)
    • Malnutrition
    • Cancer
    • General poor healing due to immunosuppression/steroid therapy (less important than morbid obesity)
    • Diabetes
    • Chronic cough (COPD)
    Operative
    • Emergency surgery (independent risk factor)
    • Reoperation for a postoperative complication (independent risk factor)
    • Type of incision: more frequent after {{c1::vertical}} than {{c1::transverse/oblique}} incisions
    • No prophylactic antibiotic cover during primary operation ⟶ increased infection risk
    • Creation of a {{c2::stoma (colostomy, ileostomy, urinary conduit) ⟶ parastomal hernias}}
    Postoperative
    • Postoperative abdominal distension
    • {{c3::Wound infection}}: most important etiological factor, mainly technical causes (hemostasis, tissue handling, tension repair, dead space) and contamination

Diastasis (Divarication) of Recti & Large Ventral Hernias (Slides 43–46)

  • Separation of the Recti Abdominis
    • Occurs in {{c1::elderly multiparous women with weak abdominal muscles::Which type of patients}}.
    • Aponeuroses forming the rectus sheath become excessively stretched.
    • On coughing/straining, the recti {{c2::separate widely}}, and a large hernial sac, containing abdominal viscera, bulges forward between medial margins of the recti.
  • Diastasis (Divarication) of the recti (image)

  • Large ventral hernia (front and side views)


    • Needs {{c3::preperitoneal mesh repair}}.

Treatment of Incisional Hernia (Slides 52–55)

  • Techniques of repair:
    1. Primary repair
    2. Primary repair with relaxing incisions
    3. Primary repair with onlay mesh reinforcement
    4. Onlay mesh only
    5. Inlay mesh placement
    6. Retrorectus mesh placement
    7. Preperitoneal mesh placement
    8. Intraperitoneal mesh placement
  • Evidence summary
    • Primary fascial repair techniques ⟶ unacceptably high recurrence (up to 50%) ⟶ largely abandoned in most centers.
    • {{c1::Tension-free}} repair by a {{c1::synthetic mesh}} ⟶ much lower recurrence (2–10%) ⟶ general use.
  • International Hernia Collaboration — consensus naming (mesh position)
    • A. Onlay
    • B. Inlay
    • C. Retrorectus (retromuscular)
    • D. Preperitoneal
    • E. Intraperitoneal

  • Laparoscopic incisional hernia repair
    • Mesh secured around the periphery with tacking sutures placed through stab incisions in the skin.
    • Sutures brought through all layers of the abdominal wall, through the mesh, and back through the mesh and abdominal wall.
    • Sutures are then tied.

Rare, Junctional, and Pelvic Herniae (Slides 56–62)

  • Obturator hernia
  • Through {{c1::obturator canal in obturator foramen}}.
  • Note: also shows locations of inguinal and femoral hernias.

Spigelian & Richter’s Herniae (Slides 63–66)

  • Spigelian hernia
    • Can occur {{c1::above (10%) or below (90%) the umbilicus, Laterally to the rectus abdominus::Where}}.
    • Below the umbilicus: occurs at the junction of {{c2::linea semilunaris and linea semicircularis (wider and weaker point)}}.
    • Defect is formed by {{c3::internal oblique and transversus abdominis; external oblique is outer to the hernial sac::Which muscles cause the deformity and which muscle is external to it}}.
    • Clinical features
      • Common in {{c4::females}} after 50 years of age
      • {{c5::Soft, reducible::Describe the}} mass {{c6::lateral to the rectus muscle and below the umbilicus, with impulse on coughing::Where is it and mention whether it has an impulse on coughing or not}}
      • {{c7::Strangulation}} is common in spigelian hernia
      • Precipitating factors: obesity, chronic cough, old age, multiple pregnancies

Spigelian & Richter’s Herniae (Slides 63–66)

  • Richter’s hernia
    • Common in {{c1::femoral and Spigelian}} hernia.


{{c2::Strangulated femoral hernia, Richter’s type with perforation
    • Patient underwent resection and anastomosis}}.

When to Admit?

  • If suspected, consider referral to hospital
    • {{c1::Obstruction}}
    • {{c1::Peritonitis}}
    • Time (more than {{c1::24}} hrs)
  • Absolute criteria (all or none)
    • {{c2::Trauma}}
    • {{c2::Shock}}
    • Disturbance of {{c2::body fluids}}
  • Contextual remarks
    • Circumstantial evidence is relevant; if the patient is going out of his way to be examined then it’s probably something. (EX -> Chines patients can be slightly tougher than indian)

Working Diagnosis: Purpose and Method

  • Why?
    • To put a {{c2::management plan irrespective of the correct pathologic diagnosis at that stage}}.
  • How?
    • By assessment of symptoms and signs.
    • N.b. --> If G.I. symptoms not G.I. affect then it’s {{c1::Genitourinary}}; if not either then it’s {{c1::medical or maybe aneurysm}}.

Working Diagnosis by Systems: Symptoms and Signs

  • Symptoms sorted in order
    • {{c1::Pain}}
    • {{c1::G.I.T (Good negative)}}
    • {{c1::Urinary Tract}}
    • {{c1::Genital Tract}}
  • Signs
    • {{c2::General}}
    • {{c2::Parietal}}
    • {{c2::Abdominal}}
      • Peritonitis
      • Obstruction
    • {{c2::Peri-rectal}}

Pain Analysis: Colicky vs Peritonitis vs “Other”

  • {{c1::Colicky (“So-called colicky}} pain”)
    • Intermittent, periodic, gripping, poorly localized.
    • Patient {{c2::doubled up}}.
    • {{c3::Tender}}abdomen.
    • If pain comes and goes ⟶ {{c4::obstruction}}.
  • {{c5::Peritonitis}} (Continuous pain)
    • Continuous pain, worse with movement or coughing.
    • Patient {{c6::lies still}}.
    • {{c7::Tenderness, guarding, (rebound tenderness::Describe the abdomen}}).
    • If general peritonitis then {{c8::no intestinal}} sounds; in local then {{c8::maybe}}.
    • Peritonism is debilitating: {{c9::can’t}} move, {{c9::can’t}} focus, {{c9::can’t}} shout.
  • Other (G.I.T. manifestations not dominating)
    • Symptoms referable to {{c10::urinary}} tract.
    • {{c11::Gynaec ± vaginal}} bleeding.
    • Neither nor
      • {{c12::Aneurysm}}
      • Extra-abdominal causes
        • Includes {{c12::lobar pneumonia and DKA}}.

Continuous Pain (Peritonitis): Localized vs Generalized

  • Localized peritonitis
    • {{c1::Weak but present}} bowel sounds.
    • {{c1::Gynaecological}} symptoms predominate ⟶ septic conditions (e.g., {{c2::salpingitis}}).
    • G.I.T. manifestations predominate:
      • Right upper quadrant ⟶ {{c3::Acute cholecystitis}}
        • Calculous
        • Acalculous
      • Right iliac fossa ⟶ {{c4::Acute appendicitis}}
      • Left iliac fossa ⟶ {{c4::Acute diverticulitis}}
  • Generalized peritonitis
    • More serious than localized peritonitis.
    • Question: Laparotomy or not?
      • Pancreatitis usually {{c5::does not need laparotomy}}.
    • History heuristic
      • Acute ⟶ {{c6::pancreatitis}}.
      • Sudden ⟶ {{c6::perforation}}.
    • Investigations: {{c7::Serum amylase + Erect chest X-ray.}}

Generalized Peritonitis: Investigations and Etiologies

Test pair Result pattern Implication
Serum amylase + Erect chest X-ray Slightly raised + Air under diaphragm {{c1::Perforated viscus}}
Serum amylase + Erect chest X-ray Markedly raised + No free gas {{c1::Acute pancreatitis}}

Generalized Peritonitis: Investigations and Etiologies

  • Etiologies (generalized peritonitis)
    • Perforated viscus
      • Mainly localized; rarely generalized
        • {{c1::Appendix}}
        • {{c1::Colonic diverticulum}}
      • Mainly generalized
        • {{c2::Perforated ulcer
        • Perforated malignancy
        • Perforated Meckel’s}}
  • Vascular accidents (organ vascular insults)
    • {{c3::Strangulation: hernias (external or internal) or volvulus.
    • Mesenteric ischaemia}}.
    • They do not show {{c4::free gas}} in chest X-ray; serum amylase {{c4::maybe slightly raised}}.
  • Extension of severe inflammation leading to peritonitis (rare)
    • Occurs in organs where the {{c5::greater omentum could not isolate them}}:
      • {{c6::Meckel’s diverticulum
      • Crohn’s ileitis}}
    • Again, {{c7::no free gas}} in chest X-ray; serum amylase maybe slightly raised.
  • Pancreatitis could also cause gen. per. but {{c8::serum amylase}} has to be 3x

Intermittent Gripping Pain: Biliary, Intestinal Obstruction, Ureteric Colic

  • Band-like pain, upper abdomen (foregut), nausea, intolerance to fatty meals
    • {{c1::Biliary}} system involved ⟶ Sonography.
  • Typical colicky (can't sit still)
    • Central (midgut), lower (hindgut)
    • Vomiting, constipation, abdominal distension, tympanic abdomen, tinkling bowel sounds
    • Intestinal obstruction
      • Small ⟶ {{c2::Observation}}.
      • Large
        • {{c2::P.R. Sigmoidoscopy
        • Obstructing lesion}}
  • Typical colicky loin pain in the back radiating to groin and testis
    • {{c3::Ureteric}}colic
      • {{c4::Urine microscopy, plain U.T. (KUB)
      • IVP + ultrasound}}

• Simulating generalized peritonitis
    • Periodic peritonitis
    • Autoimmune disease, looks like appendicitis, so do {{c1::prophylactic appendectomy}} to make sure

Antipsychotics

Indications
  • Start antipsychotics once {{c1::psychosis (or schizophrenia)}} is diagnosed.
  • Do not delay: delaying {{c2::worsens}} negative symptoms.
  • Treatment includes more than medication → psychosocial interventions + family support.
  • If concordance is an issue:
    • {{c3::Depot injections (long-acting)}}preferred once dosage is established.
    • More reliable than oral (short-acting) forms.
Mechanism of Action
  • {{c4::Block (or partially block) dopamine receptors in the mesolimbic pathway}}.

Classes of Antipsychotics

Generation Mechanism Key Features / Side Effects Examples
First Generation (FGA / Typical) {{c5::D2 antagonists}} {{c6::High risk of extrapyramidal side effects (EPSE) via nigrostriatal D2 blockade}}
 {{c7::Haloperidol, Trifluperazine}}
Second Generation (SGA / Atypical) {{c5::5HT2A + D2 antagonists}} {{c6::Lower EPSE risk, but more metabolic side effects}}
 {{c8::Clozapine, Olanzapine, Quetiapine, Risperidone}}
Third Generation (TGA) {{c5::Dopamine partial agonists}} Newest group {{c9::Aripiprazole (first licensed), Brexipiprazole, Cariprazine, Lumateperone}}

Side Effects of Antipsychotics

Neurological (mainly FGA)
  • Acute dystonic reaction (emergency): severe muscle spasm.
  • Parkinsonian-like: rigidity, bradykinesia, tremor.
  • Akathisia (emergency): inner restlessness, urge to move.
  • Tardive dyskinesia (serious, prolonged use): involuntary movements (face, mouth, tongue).
  • Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (emergency): fever, rigidity, tachycardia, ↑ CPK > 1000.
Non-Neurological
  • Anticholinergic: dry mouth, blurred vision, hot flushes, constipation, urine retention.
  • Cardiac: arrhythmias (anticholinergic effect), orthostatic hypotension.
  • Weight gain (antihistaminic effect).
  • Endocrine: impotence, amenorrhea (↑ prolactin).
Side Effects in Newer Drugs
  • Weight gain → ↓ compliance, ↑ risk of CV events and diabetes.
  • Sexual dysfunction: erectile dysfunction, ↓ libido, anorgasmia (from ↑ prolactin).
  • Cardiovascular: postural hypotension (α1 blockade, esp. first 3 months), long QTc, myocarditis/cardiomyopathy (clozapine).
  • Daytime drowsiness.
  • Agranulocytosis / neutropenia (clozapine).

Antidepressants

Indications

  • Evidence: Helpful in {{c1::major depression}}, especially in preventing relapse.
  • Prescription approach:
    • Discuss choice of drug + non-pharmacological therapy.
    • {{c2::CBT}} is as effective as antidepressants in mild–moderate depression.
    • Combination (drug + CBT) > either alone.
    • Discuss side effects.
    • Warn: possible {{c3::initial worsening of symptoms in first weeks → persevere until effect seen}}.
    • Note: not all side effects are undesirable (e.g., SSRIs may help premature ejaculation).
  • Principles after medication agreed:
    • Antidepressants take ≈ {{c4::6}} weeks for full efficacy.
    • Monitor closely for increased suicidality during this period.
    • Assess at ≥ 4 weeks:
      • If effective → continue ≥ {{c5::6}} months after recovery.
      • If stopped too soon → {{c6::50}}% relapse risk.
  • If inadequate response:
    • Minor/low response: titrate dose upwards.
    • Partial recovery: switching > augmenting (though combinations are used in practice).
    • No response after 4 weeks / poor tolerability: switch to a different class.
    • Avoid excessive dose escalation — little evidence for dose-response beyond therapeutic range.

Mechanism

  • Neurotransmitter receptor hypothesis:
    • Clinical effects after weeks due to {{c7::desensitization / downregulation}} of receptors, not just ↑ neurotransmitter at synapse.
  • Monoamine hypothesis (gene expression):
    • Synaptic ↑ neurotransmitter triggers gene regulation.
    • Leads to altered expression of {{c8::receptors and proteins → antidepressant effect}}.
  • Serotonin pathway (illustration):
    • Normal: serotonin released → received by next nerve cell → some reabsorbed by presynaptic cell.
    • Depression: {{c9::↓}} serotonin availability linked to depression/anxiety.
    • SSRIs: block reuptake → {{c9::↑}} serotonin at synapse → improved neurotransmission.

Classes

  • General Principles:
    • Antidepressants have largely equal efficacy.
    • Choice depends on: side effect profile, patient preference, prior response, safety in overdose, drug interactions.
    • SSRIs recommended (favourable risk–benefit).
    • Escitalopram and dual-action (e.g., venlafaxine) {{c10::not significantly::Significanlty or not}} superior.
First Line: SSRIs
  • Start at low dose, titrate as needed.
  • Continue ≥ 6 months after recovery.
  • Examples: flu{{c11::oxetine, citalopram (or escitalopram), sertraline (best in IHD)}}.
  • Monitoring:
    • {{c12::FBC (risk: GI bleed anaemia).
    • Avoid concurrent NSAIDs.
    • U&E (risk: hyponatraemia).
    • {{c13::Citalopram}}: dose-dependent QTc prolongation → ECG monitoring (risk: torsades de pointes)}}.
Other Antidepressants (Commonly 3rd Line)
  • {{c15::NaSSA (Noradrenergic & Specific Serotonergic): mirtazapine}}.
    • Drowsy at low dose → useful for {{c14::insomnia}} (give at night).
  • {{c16::SNRI (Serotonin & Noradrenaline Reuptake Inhibitor): venlafaxine}}.
    • Effective in {{c17::mixed anxiety}}.
    • Requires baseline {{c18::BP and ECG (CVS side effects)}}.
  • Adjunct: {{c19::lithium (effective but toxic)}}.
Older Antidepressants (not commonly used until fourth line)
  • {{c20::Tricyclic antidepressants (TCA): nortriptyline, clomipramine.
  • Monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOI): moclobemide}}.

Side Effects

  • Common across antidepressants:
    • {{c21::Nausea.
    • ↑ appetite, weight gain.
    • Sexual dysfunction (↓ desire, erectile dysfunction, ↓ orgasm).
    • Fatigue, drowsiness, or insomnia (→ usually avoid nighttime dosing).
    • Anticholinergic effects: dry mouth, blurred vision, constipation}}.

Mood Stabilisers

Indications

  • Used to treat {{c1::bipolar affective disorder}}.
  • Treatment divided into three overlapping phases:
    • {{c2::Acute manic episode.
    • Depressive episode.
    • Prophylactic treatment}}.
  • Only {{c3::⅓}} of bipolar patients experience adequate relief with monotherapy.
Onset of Action
  • {{c4::Lithium}}: may take ≥ 1 week to work.
  • {{c5::Anticonvulsants (valproate)}}and {{c5::antipsychotics (olanzapine, aripiprazole)}} act more rapidly for mania.
  • Until mood stabiliser effect occurs, {{c6::antipsychotics or sedatives}} may be used short-term.
  • Additional medications may be added if mania or depression breaks through despite stabiliser use.

Classes

{{c9::Lithium}}
  • Among the first mood stabilisers used.
  • A simple ion (like sodium); available as {{c7::lithium carbonate}} (immediate-/controlled-release) or {{c7::lithium citrate}}.
  • Effective for {{c8::treating mania}} and {{c8::preventing relapse}} of mania/depression.
Mechanism
  • May affect signal transduction via:
    • {{c10::Inhibition of secondary messenger enzymes.
    • Modulation of G-proteins.
    • Inhibition of GSK3 and other downstream pathways}}.
  • Requires {{c11::blood monitoring}} to maintain therapeutic (non-toxic) levels.
{{c12::Anticonvulsants (Antiepileptics)}}
  • Also used in bipolar affective disorder.
  • Examples: {{c13::sodium valproate, carbamazepine, lamotrigine}}.
{{c14::Sodium Valproate}}
  • First-line for bipolar disorder, especially {{c15::mixed state or rapid cycling}}.
  • Blocks {{c16::voltage-sensitive Na⁺ channels; increases brain GABA levels (mechanism not fully understood)}}.
  • Contraindicated in {{c17::women of childbearing age due to teratogenic risk: spina bifida, heart abnormalities, cleft lip}}.
{{c18::Carbamazepine}}
  • Used in {{c19::acute mania and prophylaxis}}.
  • Less effective overall than lithium.
  • Mechanism: {{c20::blocks Na⁺ channels → ↓ glutamate release; ↓ norepinephrine/dopamine turnover; facilitates 5-HT neurotransmission}}.
{{c21::Lamotrigine}}
  • More effective for {{c22::bipolar depressive episodes}}.
  • Less commonly used than other anticonvulsants.
  • Mechanism: {{c23::voltage-gated Na⁺ channel antagonist → inhibits Na⁺ influx, ↓ glutamate release}}.

Side Effects

General / Lithium
  • {{c24::GI distress.
  • Polyuria, polydipsia.
  • Lethargy.
  • Weight gain.
  • Poor coordination.
  • Tremor, acne.
  • Renal: oedema (linked to Na⁺ retention).
  • Thyroid: enlargement with ↓ function (hypothyroidism).
  • Avoid dehydration (raises lithium concentration → toxicity, potentially fatal)}}.
Carbamazepine
  • {{c25::GI upset.
  • Sedation.
  • Ataxia.
  • Severe bone marrow depression.
  • Induces hepatic P450 enzymes → ↓ efficacy of other drugs (oral contraceptives, warfarin)}}.
Lamotrigine
  • {{c26::Strong warning for life-threatening skin reactions (e.g., Stevens–Johnson Syndrome).
  • Risk greatest in first 2–8 weeks or if stopped then restarted at normal dose}}.
Valproate
  • {{c27::Side effects linked to Na⁺ channel action:
    • Sedation.
    • Tremor.
    • Headaches.
    • Weight gain.
    • Lipid dysregulation.
    • Alopecia.}}

Anxiolytics

Indications

  • Used to treat multiple conditions:
    • {{c1::Panic disorder.
    • Generalized anxiety disorder (GAD).
    • Substance-related disorders and their withdrawal.
    • Insomnias and parasomnias}}.
  • In anxiety disorders, often combined with {{c2::SSRIs or SNRIs}} for treatment.

Classes

{{c3::Benzodiazepines}}
  • Commonly used in insomnia, parasomnias, and anxiety disorders.
  • Side effects: {{c4::somnolence, amnesia, disinhibition}}.
  • Risk of dependence and tolerance → difficult to {{c5::discontinue}}.
  • {{c6::Not a::is it a}} long-term solution for anxiety.
  • Best used {{c7::sparingly}} and infrequently for acute distress and agitation.
{{c8::β-Blockers}} (e.g., {{c9::Propranolol}})
  • Improve {{c10::somatic}} symptoms of anxiety: {{c10::tremulousness, sweating, etc}}.
  • Useful in {{c11::specific situations (e.g., public speaking)}}.
  • {{c12::Do not::does or not}} improve cognitive anxiety.
  • May be detrimental: patient still feels anxious but appears outwardly calm.
{{c13::Buspirone}}
  • Primarily used in {{c14::generalized anxiety disorder (GAD)}}.
  • Mechanism: {{c15::high-affinity 5-HT1A receptor agonist}}.
    • Works independently of endogenous serotonin release.
    • {{c16::Not a::is or is not}} GABA receptor agonist.
  • Advantages:
    • {{c17::Not sedative → better tolerated}}.
  • Limitations:
    • Takes up to {{c18::4}} weeks for full effect.
    • Ineffective for {{c19::short-term anxiety or panic disorder}}.
    • Will not reduce anxiety in patients accustomed to benzodiazepines.

Compliance

No Improvement
  • If a patient does not appear to be improving, there could be many reasons; start by considering the medication prescribed.
  • Monitor the medication for efficacy and tolerance and adjust as indicated.
  • If the patient does not improve, step back and rethink your diagnosis and treatment plan.
  • Keep an eye on {{c1::drug–drug}} interactions.
  • If there is still no improvement, make sure the drug is actually being taken.
Why Is Compliance Poor?
  • For some patients, medications play an instrumental role in the management of mental health symptoms.
  • Getting patients to take medications regularly on their own has its challenges. Psychiatric patients have similar reactions to taking medication as other chronically ill patients.
Patients may refuse medication for many reasons:
  • They believe the side effects of the medication are worse than their disease.
  • They want to avoid the stigma of taking medication.
  • They refuse to take medication because it is a reminder of being ill.
  • They believe the medication doesn’t work.
Improving Compliance
Patients should be encouraged to ask:
  • What is the name of the medication?
  • What is the medication supposed to do? And what are its side effects?
  • How and when should I take it?
  • What should I do if I miss a dose?
  • When and how should I stop taking it?
  • Will it interact with other medications I take? What should I avoid?
  • …and you should be prepared to answer them all.

History Taking in Dermatology

Introduction (patient encounter)
  • Greet the patient
  • Introduce yourself
  • Explain the purpose of consultation
  • Gain consent
  • Take personal history
Chief complaint ({{c1::open}}-ended)
  • Prompt: “What brought you today?”
  • Examples provided by patients: blisters, pimples, breakout, hair loss, hair fall, etc.
{{c2::Present history
  • Onset, Duration, Course
  • Precipitating factors: contact history / sun exposure / trauma / prior systemic illness / upper respiratory tract infection / emotional distress
  • Exacerbating and relieving factors
  • Analysis of skin lesions:
    • {{c3::Site at onset, Distribution, Spread
    • Other sites involvement: mucosa, hair, nail affection
    • Seasonal variations
    • Previous episodes::What specifically for skin lesions}}
  • Associated symptoms:
    I. Itching
    II. Burning
    III. Pain
Drug history
  • Any topical or systemic preparations used prior to the eruption of skin lesions
  • Drug allergies
  • OTC medications or herbal remedies
  • Regular or newly introduced medications
Review of other systems affection
  • {{c4::Screening for cardiovascular problems
  • Screening for TB
  • Thyroid disorders symptoms
  • Liver disease: yellowish discoloration of skin or sclera
  • Chronic kidney disease
  • Menstrual history::Like what}}
Past history
  • {{c5::DM or Hypertension
  • Previous dermatologic diseases
  • Allergic conditions: atopic dermatitis / eczema / asthma / food allergies / allergic rhinitis and conjunctivitis
  • Previous surgeries
  • Previous admissions to the hospital/ICU::What do you ask in this section}}
Family history
  • Family history of other medical conditions: DM/HTN
  • Dermatological, autoimmune or rheumatological diseases
  • Allergic conditions
Social & occupational history
  • Smoking, alcohol and substance use
  • Working in an outdoor environment
  • Exposure to irritants (work, household detergents, hobbies)
  • Special habits of medical importance: {{c6::Pet ownership (Tenia)}}
Ending the clinical encounter
  • Summarize key findings
  • Address ideas / concerns / expectations
  • Explain briefly the next step in the management plan
  • Thank the patient:: Describe the history taking checklist}}

Examination

Primary lesions by elevation & size (generated)
  • Describe the lesion height:
    • Raised
      • {{c1::Papule}}: < 0.5 cm
      • {{c1::Nodule}}: > 0.5 cm

    • Flat
      • {{c2::Macule}}: < 0.5 cm
      • {{c2::Patch}}: > 0.5 cm

• Fluid-filled lesions:
    • {{c1::Vesicles}}: < 0.5 cm in diameter
    • {{c1::Bulla}}: > 0.5 cm
    • {{c1::Pustule}}: Pus-filled lesion

Special types of primary lesions
  • {{c1::Burrow::What is it called}}: A tunnel dug in the skin by the {{c6::scabies}} mite
  • {{c2::Comedone}}: The primary lesion of {{c5::acne}}
  • {{c3::Wheal}}: The primary lesion of {{c4::urticaria}}
Secondary lesions
  • {{c7::Scale}}: {{c11::Visible, palpable flake of aggregated epidermal cells::What is it}}
  • {{c8::Crust}}: {{c12::A dried exudate on the skin surface — serum, blood, pus, or a combination}}
  • {{c9::Excoriation marks}}: {{c13::Superficial ulceration secondary to scratching}}
  • {{c10::Ulcer}}: Loss of epidermis (+/− underlying structure)

Patterns of Skin Lesions

  • Symmetrical vs Asymmetrical
  • Flexural vs Extensor
  • eg. Flexural : {{c5::inside of the joint in Atopic dermatitis}}

  • eg. Extensor surface: {{c6::outside of a joint, e.g Psoriasis}}

  • Groups: {{c1::Herpes simplex}}

  • Linear: {{c2::Psoriasis}}
  • Dermatomal: {{c3::Herpes Zoster}}

  • {{c4::Serpiginous}} (looks like it snakes across the skin)
  • {{c4::Arcuate}} (arc shaped lesions)
  • {{c4::Annular}} (complete ring with central clearing)
    Annular example : {{c7::tinea corporis}}

Koebnerization
  • Definition: {{c8::Appearance of new skin lesions on previously unaffected skin secondary to trauma}}
  • Seen in: {{c9::Lichen planus, psoriasis}}

Monomorphic: All lesions look the same—same size, shape, and stage. Suggests a single process.

Pleomorphic: Lesions vary in appearance—different types or stages. Suggests multiple processes or evolving disease

Examples of Malfunction of the Skin

  • {{c1::Pemphigus vulgaris, TEN, Burns}} ⟶ fluid, protein and heat loss, & portal of entry for infection

  • Loss of physical barrier in {{c2::atopic patient & 2ry Staph infection}}

  • Abnormal cutaneous cell-mediated immunity in atopic patient with {{c3::HSV::What bacteria}}

  • Increased UV penetration in an {{c4::albino child with squamous cell carcinoma}}

  • Failure of immune surveillance: {{c5::malignant melanoma}}

Skin layers
Physiological variation in thickness

1. Epidermis: varies in thickness with site, from< 0.1 mm on the eyelids to about 1 mm on acral sites

2. Dermis: varies in thickness with site, from 1 mm on the face to about 4 mm on the back

3. Subcutaneous fat: varies extensively in thickness (up to multiple cm) depending on site and gender:
• Site: {{c1::thick in the abdominal wall, thin in the eye lids}}
• Gender: In females → thick in {{c2::pubic and thigh skin}}; in males: in {{c2::upper arm and posterior neck}}

1. The epidermis

Type of epithelium: Keratinizing stratified squamous epithelium

{{c2::Cells within the epidermis:
1. Keratinocytes (main cell component)

2. Dendritic cells : {{c1::a. Melanocytes (Pigment cells) b. Langerhans cells (cells from the macrophage family) c. Merkel cell (nerve cell)}}.}}

Function of Keratinocytes

Formation of keratin, yet, Every layer has a specific function:

a. {{c1::Basal cell layer}}: The site of mitosis and regeneration
b. {{c1::Spinous (prickle) layer}}: Non- mitotic, gives the epidermis its structure
c. {{c1::Granular layer}}: Keratin formation, water barrier
d. {{c1::Horny, keratinous}} layer: protective
Basal cell layer can only be seen by {{c1::IHC}}, sees the {{c2::collagen T4}}, or you can use {{c3::PAS}} stain

Layers of the Keratinocytes

a. Basal layer
 • A single layer of {{c1::elongated}} cells with palisade arrangement of nuclei.
 • Mitotically {{c2::active → regenerative}}
 • 60% of basal cells proliferate either rapidly or slowly.
 • Few areas of suprabasal cells are also mitotically active.

b. Spinous layer
 • After leaving the basal layer, keratinocytes become {{c3::postmitotic}} and start their structural differentiation
 • Cells develop keratin {{c4::filament-desmosome}} complex & a cornified envelope

Layers of the Keratinocytes

c. Granular layer
 • A cornified envelope gets its full strength in the granular layer
 • Accumulation of Kerato-hyaline granules; composed of {{c1::profillagrin}}
 • Profillagrin → (enzymatically) Fillagrin [Fillagrin= Keratin matrix protein (a keratin filament aggregating protein)]
 • Fillagrin→ decomposes → yielding derivatives that are: {{c2::1. Sun-shielding 2. Water- binding}}

Odland bodies: They are released from keratinocytes of the {{c3::upper spinous}} layer and move to the periphery of the granular layer where they release their contents within the intercellular space. They are lipid rich and hydrophobic, i.e. repel water

d. Horny layer
 • The anucleated, uppermost layer of the epidermis
 • It appears {{c4::basket- weave}} in formalin fixed specimens, since substances within the horny layer are not fixed by formalin and are washed away during processing

Function of dendritic cells

• Melanocytes: pigment cells
 Located in the {{c1::basal layer between keratinocytes in the upper layer}}
 Responsible for skin colour
 Protect the skin against {{c2::harmful UV rays}}

• Langerhans cells: antigen presenting cells (APCs)
 Located mainly within the {{c3::upper/ middle}} layers of the epidermis
 {{c4::Protective}} role against infective agents

• Merkel cells:
 Part of the {{c5::sensory}} nervous system connecting nerve fibers to keratinocytes
 Located at {{c6::basement membrane}} level
 {{c7::Hardly seen by H&E stain…Special stains to highlight their presence::is it seen or not by H&E stain}}

Melanocytes

A melanocyte origin is the {{c1::neural crest}}, it reaches the final destination within the {{c2::basal}} layer. Immunohistochemistry: positive for {{c3::S-100, HMB-45 and Melan A}}

After a melanocyte makes a melanosome, it passes the melanosome on to a keratinocyte . The keratinocyte stores the melanosomes over its nucleus. The melanin in the melanosome absorbs ultraviolet radiation before it reaches the nucleus (and therefore DNA) of the keratinocyte. Therefore, instead of the DNA absorbing harmful UV rays, the melanin in the melanosome absorbs it, where it does no harm.

Langerhans cells
• {{c1::Mid}}-epidermal, dendritic, antigen presenting cells (→)

 • {{c2::Bone marrow}} derived
 • Increased numbers in conditions with {{c3::increased antigen presentation (e.g. contact dermatitis)}}
 • Specifically positive for {{c4::CD1a
::}}
Looks just like keratinocyte but its in the middle of the epidermis

Merkel Cells

 • Part of the sensory nervous system (Essential for {{c1::light touch sensation}}) 
• Present at {{c2::basement membrane}} level
 • Have desmosomal contacts with keratinocytes

 Merkel cells produce: {{c3::Keratin (cytokeratin 20)}} and {{c3::neurosecretory granules (Immunohistochemical staining is necessary to visualize them {{c4::# CK 20}})}}

 Not detectable with routinely stained histologic sections
{{c1::A single Merkel cell is noted at the level of the basement membrane (→) (IHC, CK20)::Describe and mention the stain used}}

{{c1::

The idea is langerhand are in the middle, melanocytes are in the base}}

NOTE
Keratinocytes adhere to each other by {{c1::Desmosomes}}

The basal layer is attached to the underlying basal lamina by {{c2::half desmosomes}}

2. The dermis

• The middle layer of skin, deep to the epidermis and superficial to the subcutaneous layer
• Divided into two layers: {{c1::the papillary dermis and the reticular dermis}}

• Composed of cells, connective tissue and ground substance + blood and lymphatic vessels, nerves, glands, and hair follicles
• It ranges from 1-4mm in thick



Papillary dermis: Collagen bundles (TYPE {{c2::III}}) {{c2::thin & loose, less eosinophilic::Describe it}}

Reticular dermis: Collagen bundles (TYPE {{c3::I}}) {{c3::dense& irregular, eosinophilic::Describe it}}


• Elastic fibers are found in {{c4::both the papillary and the reticular dermis::Which layers}}
 • They are more numerous within the {{c4::reticular}} dermis

The skin vasculature is arranged in two plexi:
• The superficial plexus→ located within {{c5::the papillary dermis}}
 • The deep plexus→ located within {{c5:: the reticular dermis.}}

Most of the vessels in the skin are small sized, you could find medium sized ones in SC tissue
{{c1::Eccrine}} gland is on its own compared to the hair follicle and seb. gland

Function of epidermal appendages
 ➢Hair: {{c1::• Regulates body temperature • Helps evaporation of sweat}}
➢Sebaceous glands (*) secrete {{c2::sebum (fat) (lubricant for skin and hair)}}
➢ Eccrine sweat glands → {{c3::regulation of body temperature}}
 ➢ Apocrine glands → {{c4::Scent (odour) glands}} --> Fully developed at {{c5::puberty}}

Some common microscopic terms

• {{c1::Acanthosis::Define}}: Increased thickness of the spinous layer
• {{c2::Orthokeratosis::Define}}: Normal basket-weave appearance of the horny layer
• {{c3::Hyperkeratosis::Define}}: Thickening of the horny layer
• {{c4::Parakeratosis::Define}}: Retention of nuclei within the horny layer
• {{c5::Hypergranulosis::Define}}: Thickened granular layer
• {{c6::Acantholysis::Define}}: Separation of keratinocytes because of disruption of desmosomes leading to intra-epidermal blisters
• {{c7::Spongiosis::Define}}: Intercellular (only) edema, excessive spongiosis can lead to intraepidermal vesicles
• {{c8::Reticular degeneration::Define}}: inter- and intracellular (both) oedema with keratinocyte rupture leaving behind a reticulum of remaining cell membranes
• {{c9::Ballooning degeneration::Define}}: Accumulation of intracellular fluid in response to cell injury

A blister is a fluid- filled cavity either intra- or sub-epidermal
 
{{c1::Vesicle}} -> less than 0.5cm
 {{c1::Bulla}} - > More than 0.5cm

Mechanisms of blister formation

{{c1::1. Spongiosis (Intercellular edema) → with associated Exocytosis


2. Reticular degeneration: Inter- and intracellular edema lead to cell rupture & a reticular meshwork

3. Acantholysis: Loss of cell cohesion→ formation of clefts and vesicles

4. Cytolysis: disruption of keratinocytes by friction or heat

5. Basement membrane zone disruption e.g. ballooning degeneration of basal layer

::What are the 5 mech}}

What is Eczema?
{{c1::• Dermatitis: Inflammation of the skin}}

• Characterized Clinically by {{c2::erythema and itching}} 
Histologically by {{c3::spongiosis and inflammation}}

Spongiotic dermatitis = spongiosis with associated inflammation

What is Spongiosis?

{{c1::It is accumulation of extracellular fluid (intercellular edema) causing disruption of desmosomes.
 There is an associated inflammatory infiltrate both perivascular, within the dermis as well as extending into the epidermis→ Exocytosis}}

What is exocytosis?
{{c2::It means the presence of inflammatory cells within the epidermis with associated spongiosis}}
Green arrows inflammation, eosinphils could come from allergy, parasites, or drug induced

What is reticular degeneration?

It means the presence of oedema {{c1::both within and between cells::Where}}
 It leads to cell rupture→ a reticular meshwork * (remnants of the ruptured cell wall)


Q. What is the expected clinical presentation of reticular degeneration? 
{{c2:: [A vesicle inside the epidermis ( intra- epidermal vesicle)]}}
