Eczema/dermatitis Classification

2. Cause/ aetiology

Eczema, especially atopic dermatitis, often appears in the first {{c1::six months to 5 years}} of a child's life

 Most common types of eczema that affect children:
{{c2::Atopic dermatitis (atopic is the most)
Contact dermatitis
 Dyshidrotic eczema
 Seborrheic dermatitis [known as “cradle cap” in infants]}}

Atopic eczema/ dermatitis
 • What is Atopy?
 It is the predisposition to {{c1::develop asthma, allergic rhinitis and atopic dermatitis}}

• Increased sensitivity to {{c1::pruritic}} stimuli
• Incidence: 1—20% of children
• 70% have a positive family history of atopy

Definition:
{{c2::It is a chronic, relapsing, pruritic dermatitis in patients with a family history of atopy. It can be associated with other atopic disorders like allergic rhinitis/sinusitis and asthma}}

Atopic dermatitis

Pathogenesis:
A Genetically controlled disorder.

Related it to loss of {{c1::tight junctions within the cornified layer either through loss of function mutation in the Fillagrin gene or to reduced expression of tight junction protein {{c2::claudin-1}}}}

It is a type “{{c3::I}}” hypersensitivity reaction.

Atopic dermatitis
Clinical presentation

• Common sites:

With infants and toddlers, atopic dermatitis usually starts on the {{c1::face or on the elbows and knees easy areas to scratch and rub as they are crawling.}} ---> It may spread to other areas of the body

Clinicopathological correlation
Acute Atopic dermatitis

Clinically: {{c2::Red, dry, itchy patches on the skin that result from inflammation.
Itching may be severe and constant.
With frequent scratching, the skin may develop blisters, oozing, crusting, or sores from infection}}


Microscopically: {{c3::Acute dermatitis -- First slide is spongiosis, second is intraepidermal vesicle, could rupture if scratched which would ooze and cause an infection::Describe and diagnose}}

What is the microscopic site of the blister? {{c4::Intraepidermal}}

Chronic atopic dermatitis
• Clinically:

{{c1::Lichenification ( thick skin with prominent skin markings) on top of chronic rubbing}}

{{c2::• The chronic itch- rub cycle leads to lichenification of the skin manifested by thick skin with prominent markings +/- postinflammatory hyperpigmentation::What causes the lichenification}}

• Microscopically:

{{c3::Chronic dermatitis/ lichen simplex chronicus, • Microscopically it is a chronic dermatitis with marked epidermal hyperplasia and dermal fibrosis}}

What is the microscopic feature taking place on top of chronic rubbing?
{{c3::Dermal fibrosis [*]}}

Atopic dermatitis

Prognosis
 • {{c1::50}}% clear by 3 years
 • {{c1::66}}% clear by 6 years
 • {{c1::90}}% clear by 20 years

 Investigations and management -> {{c2::Clinical sessions}}

SEBORRHEIC DERMATITIS Age group:
• Babies younger than {{c1::3 months}} and adults from {{c1::30 to 60}} years of age 

Clinical presentation:
{{c2::• Greasy, white, flaky scaling over erythematous patches
• It may itch, but is usually not painful, unless infected or irritated}}

Pathogenesis:
• Infectious: Possible link to {{c3::Malassezia}} species
• Increased {{c3::Pityrosporum orbiculare}} yeasts
• IN ADULTS: increased incidence in {{c4::Parkinson’s disease, facial paralysis, obesity, zinc deficiency and HIV}}

Common sites:

• {{c5::Scalp}}: when mild it is simply called dandruff
• In infants, it is called {{c6::cradle cap}}
• Other parts of the body ⟶
{{c7::    ◦ Face: eyebrows, eyelids, forehead and nasolabial folds
    ◦ Chest
    ◦ Creases of the neck, arms, legs, and groin}}

Cradle cap appears as patchy scaling or thick crusts on the scalp and greasy skin covered with flaky white or yellow scales

MICROSCOPIC PICTURE

• A subacute dermatitis with:
{{c8::    ◦ Spongiosis in epidermis and hair follicle epithelium
    ◦ Scales within follicular ostia &Neutrophils within the scales Epidermal spongiosis Dilated follicular ostea With scales Epidermal hyperplasia::Describe }}

Dyshidrotic Eczema (Pompholyx)

  • Definition: A form of eczema characterized by a vesicular rash on the hands and feet. The term "pompholyx" comes from the ancient Greek word for "bubble."
  • Demographics: Most common in teenagers and young adults.
  • Pathogenesis & Causes:
    • {{c1::Abnormal}} sweating.
    • Allergy to {{c1::nickel}}.
    • Aggravated by {{c1::stress}}.
    • Often related to {{c1::other skin diseases (e.g., atopic dermatitis, contact dermatitis, dermatophyte infection, bacterial infection)}}
    • Associated with {{c1::environmental and emotional stress}}.
  • Clinical Presentation:
    • {{c4::Pruritic (itchy) vesicular or bullous rash with a predilection for the hands and feet::Describe}}.
    • The rash is usually {{c2::bi}}lateral and may be {{c2::symmetrical}}.
    • The condition frequently develops or flares up in the {{c3::summer}}.
  • Microscopic Picture (Histopathology):

    • Early lesions show {{c5::acute spongiotic dermatitis with intraepidermal vesicles.}}
    • A key histological feature indicating acral skin (e.g., from feet) is the presence of {{c6::a thick stratum corneum.}}

Contact Dermatitis — Pathogenesis of Allergic and Irritant (Slides 39–41)

Allergic Contact Dermatitis (ACD) — Definition (Slide 39)
  • Inflammatory skin disorder initiated by {{c1::contact with an allergen to which the person has already been sensitized}}.
  • Caused by cutaneous type {{c1::IV}} cell-mediated delayed hypersensitivity reaction.
Irritant Contact Dermatitis (ICD) — Definition (Slide 39)
  • Inflammatory skin condition produced in response to {{c2::non-immune–mediated direct toxic effect of a chemical or physical irritant damaging the skin barrier}}.
  • Mechanisms of irritant damage include:
    • {{c3::Removal of surface lipids
    • Damage of cell membranes
    • Denaturation of epidermal keratins
    • Cytokine release
    • Direct cytotoxic effect}}

Allergic (examples) Irritant (categories & examples)
Cause Exposure to {{c1::poison ivy; Nickel, rubber, cosmetics, fragrances}} {{c1::1) Chemical burns “alkalis and acids” 2) Acute irritants: cosmetics 3) Chronic irritants: detergents}}
Mechanism Cell-mediated ({{c2::type IV}}) hypersensitivity reaction Direct epidermal damage through: {{c2::a. Removal of lipid barrier b. Damaged cell membrane c. Denatured keratin cytoskeleton}}
Pathology {{c3::Inflammation initiated within papillary dermis ⟶ Exocytosis by monocytes & eosinophils}}
 {{c3::Direct damage to the epidermis; Inflammation within upper half of epidermis}}

Pathogenesis Details: Allergic vs Irritant (Slides 41–42)

Allergic Contact Dermatitis — Type IV Hypersensitivity Reaction
  • Type IV hypersensitivity reaction (Slide 41).
  • Poison ivy exposure model (Slide 42):
    1. {{c2::First exposure: no reaction; sensitization stimulates {{c1::helper T}} cells ⟶ memory helper T cells (reactivated on future exposures).
    2. Secondary exposure: memory helper T cells reactivated ⟶ inflammatory cytokines ⟶ stimulate macrophages and cytotoxic T cells to induce an inflammatory lesion at the exposed site::Describe 1st vs 2nd exposure}}.
Irritant Contact Dermatitis — Barrier Penetration Cascade (Slide 41)
  • When the irritant penetrates the stratum corneum, it leads to:
    • Stimulation of cytokine expression
    • Chemotaxis of PNLs within the epidermis
    • Damage of papillary dermal blood vessels
    • Direct cytotoxic effect

Clinical Presentations (Slides 43–45)

Allergic Contact Dermatitis — Clinical (Slides 43–44)
  • {{c1::Allergic contact dermatitis on top of a Tattoo::Describe the photo below}}.

    • Figure key takeaway: ACD can localize to tattooed skin following allergen exposure.
  • {{c2::Nickel}} allergy is one of the most common causes of contact dermatitis in children.
    • Infants: skin lesions corresponding to the location of snaps on garments.
    • Older children: reactions to watches, chains, belt buckles, earrings.
    • Periumbilical allergic contact dermatitis to Nickel from trousers belt.

    • Nickel contact dermatitis from necklace.

    • Figure key takeaway: Site-specific eruptions map to metal contact points (belt area, jewelry sites).
Irritant Contact Dermatitis in Children — Clinical (Slide 45)
  • Most common ICD in infants: {{c3::diaper rash (diaper dermatitis).}}
    • Mechanism: skin reaction in the diaper area caused by prolonged contact with natural chemicals in urine and stool.

    • Figure key takeaway: Chronic moisture/chemical exposure in occluded areas drives ICD without prior sensitization.

Microscopy of Contact Dermatitis (Slides 46–47)

Allergic Contact Dermatitis — Histopathology (Slide 46)
  • {{c1::Acute spongiotic dermatitis; infiltrate reveals {{c2::eosinophils}}.
  • Subacute phase.
  • Chronic phase: features similar to {{c3::lichen simplex chronicus}}::What are the three phases}}.
Irritant Contact Dermatitis — Histopathology (Slide 46)
  • Features vary by irritant nature and duration of exposure.
  • Highly concentrated irritants: {{c4::marked keratinocytic ballooning degeneration with variable necrosis (single cells to confluent zones within upper epidermis); neutrophils present in areas of epidermal necrosis::what does it look like microscopically}}.
  • Lower irritant concentrations: {{c5::spongiosis, perivascular infiltrates, and dermal edema similar to ACD::what does it look like microscopically}}.
Labeled Micrographs & Captions (Slides 46–47)
  • {{c6::ACUTE SPONGIOTIC DERMATITIS — {{c7::Intraepidermal microvesicles}} (*)::Describe and diganose}}.

    • Figure key takeaway: Marked spongiosis with microvesiculation typifies acute spongiotic dermatitis.
  • {{c8::IRRITANT CONTACT DERMATITIS — Upper epidermal necrosis (→) with spongiosis (→)::Describe and diagnose}}.

    • Figure key takeaway: Upper epidermal necrosis with neutrophils favors ICD (especially with strong irritants).
  • Microscopic picture of contact dermatitis — Allergic Contact Dermatitis vs Irritant Contact Dermatitis (labels).
    • Figure key takeaway: ACD: eosinophils, lower-epidermal spongiosis; ICD: upper-epidermal injury, neutrophils.

Microscopic Feature Allergic Irritant
Ulceration & Erosions {{c1::Usually absent}} {{c1::May be present}}
Early site of affection {{c2::Lower epidermis}}
 {{c2::Upper epidermis}}
Spongiosis {{c3::Marked ⟶ vesicles}}
 {{c3::Slight ⟶ no vesicles}}
Ballooning degeneration {{c4::Slight}}
 {{c4::Marked}}
Necrotic keratinocytes {{c5::Absent}}
 {{c5::Present}}
Type of inflammatory cells {{c6::Lymphocytes & eosinophils}}
 {{c6::Neutrophils}}
Superficial perivascular infiltrate {{c7::Lymphocytes and eosinophils, no neutrophils}}
 {{c7::Sparse, made of lymphocytes and neutrophils, no eosinophils}}

Nummular Eczema (Slide 49)

  • Clinical picture: {{c1::scaly, crusted, pruritic, coin-shaped plaques}}.
  • Microscopic picture: {{c2::Subacute, spongiotic dermatitis}}.

children.name: ICC2 VM 137 approach to multiple trauma patients

fields:
TTL Approach

  • A B C D E – Vertical Approach
    • A – Airway
    • B – Breathing
    • C – Circulation
    • D – Disability (neurological assessment)
    • E – Exposure / Environment
  • TTL (Trauma Team Leader): coordinates and directs the trauma resuscitation.
  • Horizontal Approach: emphasizes simultaneous, team-based task execution with delegated roles.
  • For multiple Trauma patients has to be a {{c1::horizontal}} approach

Team Organization

Airway Team
  • {{c2::Airway assessment.
  • Airway management.
  • C-spine control.
  • Neurological assessment}}.
Breathing Team (TSP 1)
  • {{c2::Chest assessment.
  • Chest drain insertion.
  • Monitoring.
  • Blood samples.
  • Removal of clothes.
  • Temperature control.
  • Assisting with procedures}}.
Circulation Team (TSP 2)
  • {{c2::Hemorrhage control.
  • Assessment of circulation.
  • Abdominal, pelvic, limb assessment.
  • Vascular access}}.
Team Leader Assistant
  • {{c2::Documentation}}.

Emergency Department Alert

Prehospital Information
  • {{c2::Age, sex, relevant history.
  • Time of incident.
  • Mechanism of incident.
  • Suspected injuries.
  • Signs and symptoms.
  • Treatment given.
  • Estimated time of arrival (ETA)}}.
Team Introduction & Brief
  • Task allocation: competencies, roles.
  • Formulate {{c3::Plan A and Escape plan}}.
  • Equipment preparation and check.
  • Alert additional services (radiology, blood bank, specialists).
  • Universal precautions.

Arrival of the Patient

Team Leader’s Initial Actions
  • Ensure {{c4::safe transfer}}.
  • Perform “{{c4::5-second round}}”.
  • Take handover from prehospital team.
Handover Content
  • {{c5::Age.
  • Timings.
  • Mechanism of injury.
  • Injuries.
  • Signs and symptoms.
  • Treatment}}.

Primary Survey and Resuscitation

  • Use the {{c6::ABCDE}} approach.
  • Goal: Identify and treat all immediately life-threatening injuries.
  • Same priorities for all patients.
  • Special considerations required for:
    • Elderly patients.
    • Children.
    • Pregnancy.

ABCDE Approach

  • {{c1::Catastrophic hemorrhage: identify and control immediately.
  • Airway with cervical spine control.
  • Breathing and ventilation.
  • Circulation & control of hemorrhage.
  • Dysfunction of the CNS.
  • Exposure & environment}}.

Airway

Initial Steps
  • Airway personnel establish contact with the patient.
  • Check airway patency:
    • {{c2::Patent.
    • Obstructed.
    • At risk}}.
  • Provide airway management as {{c3::necessary (including RSI) and anticipate airway}} difficulty.
  • Provide restriction of {{c3::cervical spine}} motion (with help of B person).
  • Consider analgesia (with help of the C person).
  • Carry out focused {{c4::neurological assessment}}.
  • Obtain {{c5::AMPLE (Allergies, Medications, Past medical history, last meal, Events leading to presentation) history}}.

Airway Management

  • Open: {{c6::jaw thrust}}.
  • Clear: {{c6::suction}}.
  • Maintain:
    • {{c6::OPA (oropharyngeal airway).
    • NPA (nasopharyngeal airway)}}.
  • Oxygenate: {{c6::high-flow O₂}}.
  • Ventilate: if needed, via BVM.
  • Secure:
    • {{c7::SGD – Supraglottic Device (LMA– Laryngeal Mask Airway).
    • ETT– Endotracheal Tube (with RSI- rapid seq induction).
    • Surgical cricothyroidotomy (FONA- Front of Neck Access)}}.
  • Monitor: {{c7::SpO₂, ETCO₂}}.

Anticipating Airway Difficulty (LEMON approach)

  • {{c10::L – Look externally (obvious facial trauma, beard, large tongue)}}.
  • {{c10::E – Evaluate 3-3-2 rule:
    • {{c8::
    • 3 fingers in mouth.
    • 3 fingers from mentum to hyoid cartilage.
    • 2 fingers from floor of mouth to thyroid cartilage}}.::What is the whole approach}}
  • {{c10::M – Mallampati score:
    • {{c9::Class I – full visualization of soft palate.
    • Class II – uvula completely visible.
    • Class III – only base of uvula visible.
    • Class IV – soft palate not visible}}.}}
  • {{c10::O – Obstruction: presence of pathology (e.g., epiglottitis, abscess, trauma)}}.
  • {{c10::N – Neck mobility: assess cervical spine mobility (trauma, arthritis, etc.)}}.

Breathing

Breathing personnel should:
  • Assess breathing pattern and examine chest & neck:
    • Inspection
    • Palpation
    • Percussion
    • Auscultation
  • Ensure {{c1::SpO₂ and ECG}} monitors are attached.
  • Support breathing if inadequate (by assisting A person).
Exclude and treat six life-threatening thoracic conditions V V V IMPORTANT:
  1. {{c2::Laryngo-tracheal injury.
  2. Tension pneumothorax.
  3. Open chest wound.
  4. Flail chest.
  5. Massive hemothorax.
  6. Cardiac tamponade}}.
  • These are critical causes of traumatic cardiac arrest.

Circulation

Assume {{c3::hypovolemia}} in trauma shock.
  • “Look for blood on the {{c4::floor}} and {{c4::four more” → chest, abdomen, pelvis, long bones}}.
Circulation personnel should:
  • Control external bleeding: {{c5::direct compression, tourniquet}}.
  • Identify and quantify severity of shock:
    • Assess {{c6::vital signs (HR, BP, CRT, PP, RR, urine output, mental status)}}.
    • Insert {{c6::urine catheter}}.
  • Treat shock:
    • Establish {{c7::peripheral IV access and take blood samples + ABG}}.
    • Start {{c7::fluid resuscitation, give blood products}}.
    • Apply {{c7::pelvic}} binder if indicated in pelvic injury.
  • Find the cause:
    • Examine {{c8::abdomen, pelvis, long bones}}.
    • Perform {{c8::e-FAST (if competent)}}.

Definition of Shock

  • Shock: {{c9::An abnormality of the circulatory system that results in inadequate organ perfusion and tissue oxygenation}}.

children.name: FCP [032]Acid Base Balance (Arterial Blood Gas Interpretation)

fields:
T or F
should water use and excretion be balanced
{{c1:: True
}}

Daily Water Requirement

Basis of Calculation
  • Metabolic rate: 1 ml / kcal.
  • Body surface area (BSA): 1.5 L / m².
  • Weight-based: {{c1::30}} ml / kg (most important)

Insensible Water Loss

  • Source: Evaporation from skin and lungs.
  • Excludes: Sweat (salty exocrine secretion).
  • Allowance: {{c2::50}} ml / hr in unstressed patients.
  • Adjustment: Increases by {{c2::10}}% per degree of pyrexia.

Sodium

  • Principal extracellular cation: 140 mmol / L.
  • Distribution: Freely diffuses between blood and tissues.
  • Exclusion: Blocked by cell membrane via Na⁺-K⁺ ATPase.
  • Requirement: ~{{c3::1.5}} mmol / kg / day.

Potassium

  • Predominant intracellular cation: 150 mmol / L.
  • Distribution: Freely diffuses between blood and tissues.
  • Permeability: Freely crosses cell membrane.
  • Requirement: ~{{c4::1}} mmol / kg / day.

Intravenous Fluids

Who Needs Them?
  • Patients unable to take {{c1::sufficient oral fluids}}:
    • {{c1::Peri-operative fasting.
    • GI pathology.
    • Excessive losses::List 3 examples}}.

Daily Fluid Requirements

  1. Basal maintenance.
  2. Pre-existing deficits.
  3. Ongoing losses.
1. Basal Maintenance
  • Water: {{c1::30}} ml / kg.
  • Sodium (Na⁺): {{c1::1.5}} mmol / kg / day.
  • Potassium (K⁺): {{c1::1.0}} mmol / kg / day.
For Paediatric Patients
  • First 10 kg: {{c2::4}} ml / kg / hr.
  • Next 10 kg: {{c3::2}} ml / kg / hr.
  • Subsequent weight: {{c4::1}} ml / kg / hr.
Example Calculation (25 kg child)
  • {{c5::4 × 10 = 40 ml / hr.
  • 2 × 10 = 20 ml / hr.
  • 1 × 5 = 5 ml / hr.
  • Total = 65 ml / hr::How much maintenance fluid is required}}.
2. Pre-Existing Deficits
  • Factors to consider:
    • {{c6::Duration of NBM (nil by mouth).
    • Vomiting / nasogastric (NG) aspirate.
    • Diarrhoea.
    • Pyrexia.
    • Third space losses::What are different examples}}
3. Ongoing Losses
  • {{c6::Haemorrhage.
  • Drains.
  • Fistulae.
  • NG aspirate.
  • Insensible losses.
  • Third space losses::What are different examples}}.

Third Space Fluid

  • Also called transcellular water.
  • Sites:
    • {{c1::CSF.
    • Gut (ileus).
    • Bladder (retention).
    • Serous cavities (effusions)}}.
  • Can account for {{c2::large, unseen losses}}.

General Approach to Fluid Problems

  1. {{c1::Identify cause and type of deficit}}:
    • {{c2::NBM duration, vomiting, diarrhoea, pyrexia, blood loss, surgery}}.
  2. Assess magnitude of deficit through:
    • {{c3::History.
    • Symptoms of dehydration.
    • Examination (signs of ↓ perfusion).
    • Investigations (blood + urinalysis)}}.

Clinical Features of {{c1::Dehydration}}

Symptoms
  • {{c2::Thirst.
  • Fatigue.
  • Headache.
  • Nausea}}.
Signs
  • {{c2::↓ Skin turgor.
  • Dry mucosae.
  • Pallor.
  • ↑ Heart rate.
  • ↓ Blood pressure.
  • Oliguria.
  • ↓ Central venous pressure (CVP)}}
Test Results
  • {{c3::↑ Haematocrit
  • ↑ Urea.
  • ↑ Albumin.
  • Urine SG ≥ 1.02.
  • Urine osmolarity > 500.
  • [Na⁺] ≤ 1 (low sodium concentration)}}
Inc HC, Urea, and albumin because the blood concentrations increase

{{c1::The oesophageal Doppler}} measures blood flow
 by ultrasound

Doppler Guided Fluid Administration

Process Flow :
  1. {{c1::Measure Stroke Volume.
    • Initial assessment step.::What are the 4 steps of this flow from administrating the fluid }}
  2. {{c1::Fluid Challenge.
    • Administer intravenous fluid bolus to assess patient response.::What are the 4 steps of this flow from administrating the fluid }}
  3. {{c1::Evaluate Response:
    • If Stroke Volume rises → continue monitoring stroke volume.
    • If Stroke Volume does not rise → proceed to check for stroke volume fall::What are the 4 steps of this flow from administrating the fluid }}
  4. {{c1::Check Stroke Volume Fall:
    • If Yes (stroke volume falls) → repeat fluid challenge.
    • If No (no rise, no fall) → monitor stroke volume::What are the 4 steps of this flow from administrating the fluid }}
The Doppler helps: 
 {{c2::• To give just enough fluid to maintain intravascular filling 
 • Without giving excess}}
If stroke volume is getting higher on the doppler then he needs further fluids, if it doesnt then stop and monitor, if it falls then he needs more fluid

Intravenous Fluids – Crystalloids and Colloids

{{c1::Crystalloids}}
  • Definition: {{c2::Aqueous fluids with dissolved sugars or salts, with properties of a crystal}}.
  • Examples:
    • {{c2::Normal saline.
    • Hartmann’s solution (Ringer’s lactate).
    • 5% dextrose.
    • Dextrose-saline}}
{{c1::Colloids}}
  • Definition: {{c3::Dispersions of small particles in a continuous fluid phase; cannot be separated by centrifuge}}.
  • Key property: {{c4::Exert osmotic pressure across endothelium}}.
  • Examples:
    • {{c5::Gelatins.
    • Starches (hydroxyethyl starches).
    • Albumin.
    • Dextrans}}.

Properties of Common Fluids

Normal Saline
  • {{c1::150}} mmol / L NaCl.
  • {{c1::Is}}osmotic.
  • pH ≈ {{c1::5}}.
  • Note: “Nothing ‘normal’ about it.”
5% Glucose
  • {{c2::50}} g / L glucose (≈ 200 kcal / L).
  • {{c2::Is}}osmotic.
  • Rapidly metabolized to {{c2::CO₂ + H₂O → effectively free water}}.
{{c3::Hartmann’s}} Solution ({{c3::Ringer’s}} Lactate)
  • Na⁺ 131 mmol / L.
  • K⁺ 5 mmol / L.
  • Ca²⁺ 2 mmol / L.
  • Cl⁻ 111 mmol / L.
  • Lactate 29 mmol / L → metabolized to HCO₃⁻ in liver.
{{c4::Gelatins}}
  • Polypeptides (degraded animal collagen).
  • Gelofusine: {{c5::4% succinylated gelatin}}.
  • Haemaccel: {{c5::3.5% urea-linked gelatin}}.
  • Relatively small MW ≈ 30,000.
  • Inexpensive.
  • Might cause allergy for patients
{{c6::Hydroxyethyl}} Starches
  • Synthetic polymers from amylopectin.
  • MW = 250–450 kDa.
  • Can impair {{c7::coagulation}}.
  • Low incidence of allergy.
  • More expensive.

Distribution of Infused Fluids

1000 ml 5% Dextrose
  • Distributes to all compartments.
  • {{c1::67% → Intracellular fluid (ICF) (which is good for case of hypoglaycamiea)::Describe the ICF ECF etc}}
  • {{c1::33% → Extracellular fluid (ECF) = 333 ml}}.
    • {{c1::2/3 of this → Interstitial fluid (ISF)}}.
    • {{c1::1/3 remains in plasma ≈ 111 ml. (which is not good for volume expansion)}}
1000 ml Normal Saline
  • {{c2::Confined to ECF}}.
  • {{c2::2/3 → ISF (helps replenish sweating)}}
  • {{c2::1/3 remains in plasma ≈ 333 ml. (Also not horrible for blood expansion)}}

Principles of Fluid Therapy

  • Described as a dynamic process requiring continuous evaluation of clinical and haemodynamic indices.
  • Practical summary: {{c1::Give fluid and observe response}}.

Replacement of Blood Loss (IMPORTANT)

  • Initial: {{c2::Salty crystalloid (e.g., Hartmann’s), given at 3× estimated loss}}.
  • Large/acute losses: {{c2::Colloid (e.g., Gelofusine)}}.
  • Massive losses: {{c2::Blood transfusion}}.
    • General transfusion trigger: Hb < {{c3::8}} g/dL.

Stress Response (Post-Operative)

  • Hormonal changes: Increased ADH, aldosterone, cortisol.
  • Effect:
    • {{c4::Retention of water and Na⁺}}.
    • {{c4::Loss of K⁺ and nitrogen}}.

Step-by-Step Examination for Fluid Status

WIPER
  • {{c1::W: Wash your hands.
  • I: Introduce yourself.
  • P: Ask for permission to examine the patient to check fluid status.
  • E: Remove top and bottom clothing (leave underwear).
  • R: Position patient at 45°}}.

Inspection

  • Patient:
    • {{c2::Oedema.
    • Shortness of breath (respiratory rate).
    • Catheter.
    • Stoma}}.
  • Paraphernalia:
    • {{c2::Pain indicators.
    • Drip stands.
    • NBM (nil by mouth) signs.
    • Vomit bowls}}.

Questions

  • {{c3::Do you feel thirsty?
  • Do you feel dizzy on standing?
  • Have you had any vomiting or diarrhoea?}}

Peripheries

  • {{c4::Warm or cool. (Cool could be because of hypotension or fever or hyopthermia)::How do we think abt temp}}
  • Capillary refill: normally {{c4::< 2}} seconds.
  • {{c4::Peripheral}} cyanosis.
  • Pulse:
    • Rate.
    • Character (e.g., thready).
  • Oedema.

Arm

  • Palpate {{c5::brachial}} pulse.
  • Blood pressure (lying/standing):
    • If dizzy, take {{c6::lying and sitting}} BP.
    • Measure lying BP → stand patient → wait 3 minutes → recheck BP.
    • Postural drop:
      • {{c7::10}} mmHg diastolic drop.
      • {{c7::20}} mmHg systolic drop.
  • Assess axilla: wet or dry?

Face

  • Eyes:
    • {{c8::Sunken.
    • Conjunctival pallor (low Hb from haemorrhage).
    • Periorbital oedema::What are Eye signs of dehydration}}.
  • Mouth:
    • Dry.
    • Wet.

Neck

  • JVP (jugular venous pressure):
    • Estimate central venous pressure.
    • Raised if > {{c9::4}} cm vertical height.
    • {{c10::Abdominojugular reflux: abdominal pressure → JVP visible > 15 s = right heart failure::What indicates right heart failure}}.
    • Cannon v-waves: {{c11::tricuspid regurgitation → fluid overload::What does it mean}}.
  • Carotid pulse.

Chest

  • Central skin {{c12::turgor}}.
  • {{c12::Central capillary refill::Which Capillary refill do we use}}.
  • Heart sounds: listen for {{c12::3rd heart sound or tricuspid regurgitation}}.
  • Lung bases: auscultate for {{c12::crackles}}.
  • Sacrum: check for {{c12::oedema}}.

Abdomen

  • Lie patient flat.
  • Percuss: {{c13::ascites::What are we checking for}}.
  • Auscultate: {{c13::bowel sounds}}.
  • Scrotum: assess for {{c13::oedema}}.

Legs

  • Examine for {{c14::oedema}} sequentially:
    • Foot.
    • Lower calf.
    • Upper calf.
    • Thigh.

Closing the Examination

  • Help patient get changed.
  • State further steps:
    • Review observation chart.
    • Review fluid balance chart.
    • Check daily weights.
    • Bloods / urine:
      • {{c15::FBC (if suspected bleed/infection).
      • U&E (creatinine clearance, sodium).
      • LFT (albumin).
      • Urinary sodium}}.

Common Findings

Dehydration
  • Obs: T 36.9, BP {{c1::95/60}}, HR {{c1::110}}, RR {{c1::22}}, SpO₂ {{c1::97% (OA)}}.
  • CVS: {{c2::Cool peripheries, capillary refill = 4 s, JVP not visible}}.
  • RS: {{c3::Chest clear}}.
  • GI: {{c4::Nil abnormal}}.
  • Renal: {{c5::Urine output 20 ml/hr}}.
  • Neuro: {{c6::Drowsy}}.
Fluid Overload
  • Obs: T 36.6, BP {{c1::140/85}}, HR {{c1::70}}, RR {{c1::30}}, SpO₂ {{c1::94% (OA)}}.
  • CVS: {{c2::Warm, well-perfused, oedema to mid-calf and sacrum, JVP 8 cm}}.
  • RS: {{c3::Bibasal crepitations}}.
  • GI: {{c4::Moderate ascites}}.
  • Renal: {{c5::Urine output 50 ml/hr}}.
  • Neuro: {{c6::Agitated}}.

Prescribing Skills

Two Main Aims
  1. Maintenance.
  2. Resuscitation.
Maintenance Fluid
Daily Losses (~1000 ml):
  • Sweat: {{c1::500}} ml.
  • Faeces: {{c1::250}} ml.
  • Breathing: {{c1::250}} ml.
Urine Output:
  • 1 ml/kg/hr.
  • 70 kg patient = 70 ml/hr.
  • {{c2::70 × 24 = 1680 ml/day}}.
Total Fluid Requirement:
  • 1680 ml (urine) + 1000 ml (insensible + stool) = {{c3::2680}} ml/day.
Electrolyte requirement
  • Na {{c4::1.5}} mmols / Kg / day 
  • K {{c4::1}} mmol / Kg / day

Prescribing Skills

Two Main Aims
  1. Maintenance.
  2. Resuscitation.
Maintenance Fluid
Daily Losses (~1000 ml):
  • Sweat: {{c1::500}} ml.
  • Faeces: {{c1::250}} ml.
  • Breathing: {{c1::250}} ml.
Urine Output:
  • 1 ml/kg/hr.
  • 70 kg patient = 70 ml/hr.
  • {{c2::70 × 24 = 1680 ml/day}}.
Total Fluid Requirement:
  • 1680 ml (urine) + 1000 ml (insensible + stool) = {{c3::2680}} ml/day.
Electrolyte requirement
  • Na {{c4::1.5}} mmols / Kg / day 
  • K {{c4::1}} mmol / Kg / day

Prescribing Skills

Two Main Aims
  1. Maintenance.
  2. Resuscitation.
Maintenance Fluid
Daily Losses (~1000 ml):
  • Sweat: {{c1::500}} ml.
  • Faeces: {{c1::250}} ml.
  • Breathing: {{c1::250}} ml.
Urine Output:
  • 1 ml/kg/hr.
  • 70 kg patient = 70 ml/hr.
  • {{c2::70 × 24 = 1680 ml/day}}.
Total Fluid Requirement:
  • 1680 ml (urine) + 1000 ml (insensible + stool) = {{c3::2680}} ml/day.
Electrolyte requirement
  • Na {{c4::1.5}} mmols / Kg / day 
  • K {{c4::1}} mmol / Kg / day

Fluid (1000 ml infused) Intracellular Extracellular Interstitial Intravascular (BP-effective) and where is it effective 
{{c4::5% Glucose}}
 667 ml (2/3) 333 ml (1/3) 222 ml (2/3 of ECF) {{c1::111 ml (1/3 of ECF, i.e. 1/9th total) EFFECTIVE FOR HYPOGLYCAEMIA}}
{{c3::0.9% Normal Saline}}
 0 ml 1000 ml 667 ml (2/3 of ECF) {{c1::333 ml (1/3 of ECF) IN THE MIDDLE}}
{{c2::Gelofusine}}
 0 ml 1000 ml 0 ml {{c1::1000 ml (100%) EFFECTIVE FOR HYPOVOLEAMIC SHOCK}}

Parameter Stage 1 (<15% blood loss) Stage 2 (15–30% blood loss) Stage 3 (30–40% blood loss) Stage 4 (>40% blood loss)
Capillary refill {{c1::< 2}} s {{c1::> 2}} s {{c1::5}} s {{c1::> 5}} s
Peripheries {{c2::Warm}} {{c2::Cool}} {{c2::Colder}} {{c2::Coldest}}
Heart rate (HR) {{c3::< 100}} {{c3::> 100}} {{c3::> 120}} {{c3::> 140}}
Blood pressure (BP) {{c4::Normal}} {{c4::Normal or narrow pulse pressure}} {{c4::Low}} {{c4::Very low}}
Respiratory rate (RR) {{c5::14–20}} {{c5::20–30}} {{c5::30–40}} {{c5::> 35}}
Urine output (ml/hr) {{c6::> 30}} {{c6::20–30}} {{c6::5–15}} {{c6::Negligible}}
Consciousness {{c7::Normal}} {{c7::Slightly anxious}} {{c7::Confused / lethargic}} {{c7::Unresponsive}}
Other signs {{c8::Peripheral vasoconstriction}} {{c8::Postural hypotension}} {{c8::Recumbent hypotension}} {{c8::Tachycardia}}

Legs

  • Raise legs to {{c1::45}}°.
  • Assess blood pressure and pulse to check for responsiveness.

Assessment

  • Use {{c1::ABCDE}} to assess for hypovolaemia.
  • Trends to monitor:
    • A/B: Respiratory rate > {{c2::20}}.
    • C: HR > {{c2::90}} bpm, capillary refill > {{c2::2}} s, cold peripheries, NEWS > {{c2::4}}.
  • Passive leg raise (45°): suggests {{c3::fluid responsiveness}}.

Fluid Resuscitation

  • Identify deficit.
  • Fluid bolus: {{c4::500}} ml crystalloid (Na⁺ 130–154 mmol/L) over 15 min.
  • Reassess.
  • If needed: further bolus 250–500 ml.
  • If signs of shock → seek expert help.
  • If > 2000 ml given → seek expert help.

Maintenance

  • {{c5::30}} ml / kg / day water.
  • {{c5::1}} mmol / kg / day potassium.
  • {{c5::1.5}} mmol / kg / day sodium.
  • {{c5::50–100}} g / day glucose (5% glucose = 50 g / 1000 ml).
  • Reassess.
  • Prefer {{c6::NG / enteral feeding if required > 3 days}}.

Replacement and Redistribution

Fluid Loss Causes
  • {{c7::Vomiting, diarrhoea.
  • Biliary drainage.
  • Haemorrhage.
  • Sweating, fever, dehydration.
  • Pancreatic / jejunal fistula or stoma loss.
  • Urinary loss (polyuria)}}.
Redistribution Causes
  • {{c7::Gross oedema.
  • Severe sepsis.
  • Hypernatremia / hyponatremia.
  • Renal, liver, and cardiac impairment.
  • Post-operative fluid retention / redistribution.
  • Malnourishment / refeeding}}.

children.name: ICC [047] Blood components and complications of blood transfusion

fields:
Blood Products

1. Packed Red Cells
  • Volume: About 280 ml in a unit.
  • Dosage Effect:
    • {{c1::3}} ml/kg increases the Hb by 1 g/dL.
    • Expect an increase of more than 1 g/dL Hb per unit in adults (depends on patient size and donor unit).
  • Storage:
    • Stored at 4° Celsius.
    • Can be stored for up to {{c2::35}} days.
  • Indications:
    • {{c3::Active bleeding with fluids likely to be insufficient.
    • Bone marrow failure.
    • Abnormal red cells need suppressing or exchanging (e.g., thalassaemia and sickle cell disease).
    • Rarely haematinic deficiency (though usually best managed with replacement)}}.

Blood Products

2. Platelets
  • Description:
    • {{c1::1}} pool from 4 donors (= standard adult dose) or from {{c1::1}} donor by apheresis (using a cell separator machine).
  • Storage:
    • Stored at {{c2::22°}}C (Room temperature) under constant agitation.
    • Shelf life is {{c2::7}} days only (due to risk of bacterial infection, though units are now screened).
  • Compatibility: Need to know the patient's {{c3::blood group; no crossmatch is needed::what do we need to know and is cross match needed}}.
  • Indications & Dosing:
    • Thresholds for transfusion depend on the clinical scenario (examples are not rigid):
      • {{c4::Bleeding or Pre-Operative: < 80 x 10⁹/L
      • Bleeding in the eye or brain: < 100 x 10⁹/L
      • Bone marrow failure: < 10 x 10⁹/L
      • Neonates: < 30 x 10⁹/L
      • (ie if they have less than 80 x 10/L then we give platelets }}
    • Sometimes needed for {{c4::platelet}} dysfunction (e.g., on {{c6::cardiopulmonary}} bypass).
    • Dosing: {{c5::10}} ml/kg or {{c5::1}} pool (whichever is least) is usually enough; rarely need more.

Blood Products

3. Fresh Frozen Plasma (FFP)
  • Description: 1 unit from 1 donor (~300ml); small packs are available for children.
  • Storage:
    • Stored at {{c1::-30}}°C.
    • Shelf life is {{c1::1}} year.
  • Handling:
    • Must be thawed for approx. {{c2::20-30}} mins before use (if too hot, proteins denature).
    • Must be given {{c3::ASAP - within 1 hour of thawing or else coagulation factors degenerate::Within how long}}.
  • Dosing: {{c4::12-15}} ml/kg = usually 2 or 3 units for an adult.
  • Compatibility: Must know the {{c5::patient's blood group; no crossmatch, just choose the same ABO group::what should know regarding crossmatch and blood group}}.
  • Indications:
    1. {{c6::If bleeding + abnormal coagulation test results (e.g., prolonged PT, APTT). Monitor response clinically and with repeat coag tests.
    2. Reversal of warfarin (e.g., for urgent surgery).
    3. Other conditions occasionally.}}

    • NB: {{c7::Not to be used just to replace volume/fluid loss::Can we use it as a replacement for volume/fluids}}

Blood Products

4. Cryoprecipitate
  • Description: From frozen plasma thawed at 4-8ºC; the overnight residue remains.
  • Contents: Contains {{c1::fibrinogen and factor VIII}}.
  • Storage: Same as FFP - store at -{{c2::30ºC}} for 1 year.
  • Standard Dose: From {{c3::10}} donors (5 in a pack).
  • Indications:
    • {{c4::If massive bleeding and fibrinogen is very low.
    • Rarely hypofibrinogenaemia}}

Blood Products

Special Requests for Blood Products
  • Age of blood: {{c1::Young::young or old}} blood lasts longer, less cation leakage.
  • Specific phenotype requests (usually Rh and Kell): For {{c2::multiply transfused (ie people who get lots of transfusions}} patients (e.g., {{c3::sickle cell and thalassaemia}}).
  • Irradiated: For {{c4::depressed T-cell function (e.g., post-BMT, DiGeorge syndrome, Hodgkin lymphoma, certain drugs like fludarabine)}}.
  • CMV (cytomegalovirus) negative: For {{c5::pregnancy and IUT (Intrauterine Transfusion) because CMV can affect the uterus. Other requirements differ between institutions}}.
  • Others: e.g., Washed, plasma depleted, T-antigen negative.

Why irradiated?
To prevent transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease (TA-GvHD)—a deadly complication where donor T-cells attack the recipient’s tissues. Irradiation inactivates those T-cells, making transfusion safer for patients with weakened immune systems.

Rapid Transfusion Protocol
There are choices depending on clinical urgency. You should wait as long as is safe but no longer:
  • {{c1::Flying Squad O-negative}}: In certain sites within the hospital (immediate, universal donor).
  • {{c1::Group specific}}: i.e., no antibody screen or crossmatch (faster, but less safe).
  • {{c1::Full Crossmatch}}: Safest, but takes the longest.
  • This decision should be made in discussion with the on-call haematologist and the blood bank biomedical scientist

Major Haemorrhage: Definitions

  • Replacement of entire blood volume in {{c1::24}} hours.
  • Total transfusion >{{c2::8–10}} packed RBC units in 24 hours.
  • Replacement >{{c3::50}}% blood volume in 3 hours.
  • Blood loss at {{c4::150}} ml/min or greater.
  • Transfusion of {{c4::4}} or more RBC units in 1 hour with ongoing haemorrhage.
  • Patient predicted to need {{c4::8}} or more RBC units within 2 hours.
  • Avoidable deaths of patients with major haemorrhage are well recognised.

Transfusion Reactions

A. Immune Reactions
Acute:
  • {{c1::Acute Haemolytic Transfusion Reactions (AHTR)
  • Transfusion Associated Graft vs. Host Disease (TA-GvHD)
  • Anaphylaxis}}
Delayed:
  • {{c2::Delayed Haemolytic Transfusion Reactions
  • Post-Transfusion Purpura (PTP)
  • Neonatal alloimmune disorders including Rh disease of the newborn.}}
B. Non-Immune Reactions
  • {{c3::Infective}}: Bacterial, viral, prion.
  • {{c4::Iron}} Overload:
    • 200mg Fe per unit of PRBC.
    • A major cause of morbidity and mortality in people with transfusion-dependent diseases.
  • Infective Shock (from Bacterial Contamination):
    • Risk: {{c5::Platelets >> RBC > FFP}}.
    • Signs and symptoms usually occur during the {{c6::transfusion of the first 100ml}}.
    • {{c7::High}} risk of mortality.
    • {{c8::::What are the precacutions}}

• Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury (TRALI)
    • Caused by {{c1::antibodies (often HLA)}} in the transfused product reacting to the recipient's {{c2::WBCs}} (in 50-70% of cases).
    • Leads to {{c3::pulmonary capillary leakage, causing acute hypoxia and pulmonary infiltrates with no evidence of heart failure}}
    • Occurs within {{c4::4}} hours of a transfusion.
    • Life-threatening; death in 30-50% of cases.
    • Not easily distinguished from {{c5::ARDS}}.
    • Implicated donors are often {{c6::parous women}}.

Anaphylactic Reaction

  • Rarity & Severity: {{c1::Rare but life-threatening}}.
  • Cause: Caused by a severe patient sensitivity to antigens or allergens present in the donated blood component.
  • Risk Factor: The risk is higher with blood components that contain plasma.
  • Mechanism: Typically mediated by {{c2::IgE}} antibodies (occasionally IgG) directed against a specific allergen.
  • Specific Case: {{c3::IgA}} Deficiency: Patients with immunodeficiencies (e.g., IgA deficiency) can develop Antibodies to {{c3::IgA}}, which can trigger this reaction.
  • Anaphylactoid Reactions: Less severe, non-antibody mediated reactions can also occur (e.g., if the donor has consumed something the recipient is allergic to).

Management of Specific Reactions

1. Management of Severe Acute Transfusion Reaction
  • {{c1::Urgent medical review (+/- ITU review).
  • Inform the blood bank and the haematology transfusion registrar.
  • Stop the transfusion, but keep the IV line open with saline.
  • Check blood component against patient ID.
  • Take blood cultures and send the blood component to the laboratory.
  • Monitor: Temp, pulse, BP, respiratory rate, O2 sats (blood gases), urine output.
  • ABO incompatibility may require haemofiltration.
  • Supportive care: Antibiotics, steroids, adrenaline +/- inotropes, bronchodilators, high-flow O2::generally what do i do}}.

Post-Transfusion Purpura (PTP)

  • Most often seen in {{c1::female}} patients.
  • Caused by platelet-specific alloantibodies causing an immune-mediated thrombocytopenia.
  • Usually occurs 5–12 days following transfusion.
  • Signs: {{c2::Severe thrombocytopenia and bleeding}}.

Transfusion Associated Graft vs. Host Disease (TA-GvHD)
  • Caused by an immune reaction of {{c1::donor T-cells}} against the recipient (who is often immunodeficient).
  • Onset is usually {{c2::4 days to 6}} weeks after transfusion.
  • Signs: {{c3::Fever, skin rash, pancytopenia, liver failure, renal failure}}.
  • {{c4::Usually fatal (mortality 75-90%). No effective treatment::fatal or not and whats the treatment}}.

Acquired Haemolytic Anaemias

  • Immune
    • Autoimmune
    • Alloimmune
    • Hyperhaemolysis
  • Non-immune
    • Hypersplenism
    • Paroxysmal Nocturnal Haemoglobinuria (PNH)
    • Secondary to renal or liver disease
    • Miscellaneous: chemicals, toxins, infections
Features of Haemolysis
  • Depends on whether it's intravascular or extravascular (may be both).
  • Extravascular: {{c1::RES (reticuloendothelial system)}} picking up damaged RBCs.
  • Intravascular: {{c1::Haemoglobinaemia, haemoglobinuria. Usually associated with IgM or complement (e.g., PNH and ABO incompatible transfusions)}}
  • Common Signs:
    • {{c2::Anaemia
    • Reticulocytosis (polychromasia on blood film)
    • Macrocytosis (young cells are larger)
    • Unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia
    • Morphological changes (e.g., spherocytes, fragmented cells)
    • Marrow hypertrophy and hyperplasia}}
Free Haemoglobin
  • Binds to haptoglobin → low haptoglobin is a sensitive indicator of {{c4::intravascular haemolysis}}.
  • Binds to albumin → {{c3::forms methaemalbumin (detected by Schumm's test).}}
  • Lost in glomerular filtrate → absorbed by tubular cells → cells desquamate → positive urinary {{c5::haemosiderin}}.

Product What it is When to use
Fresh Frozen Plasma (FFP) Plasma separated from whole blood and frozen within hours; contains all clotting factors, fibrinogen, albumin, and other plasma proteins. - Coagulopathy due to multiple factor deficiencies (e.g., liver disease, DIC) - Massive transfusion protocols - Reversal of warfarin effect if PCC unavailable - TTP (as plasma exchange)
Cryoprecipitate Cold-insoluble portion of plasma; rich in fibrinogen, factor VIII, factor XIII, vWF, and fibronectin. - Hypofibrinogenemia (<100 mg/dL, e.g., massive hemorrhage, DIC, liver disease) - Factor XIII deficiency - Occasionally used in uremic bleeding (via vWF)
Packed Red Blood Cells (PRBCs) Concentrated red cells (most plasma removed); hematocrit ~60–70%. - Symptomatic anemia - Acute blood loss - To improve oxygen delivery in severe anemia or hemorrhage
Platelets Platelet concentrate derived from whole blood (random donor units) or apheresis (single donor, higher dose). - Thrombocytopenia with bleeding or before invasive procedures - Platelet dysfunction with bleeding - Prophylaxis if platelets <10,000/µL (even without bleeding)

Body Oxygen Content & Stores

Total Body O₂ (Textbook 70-kg adult)
  • ~1.5 L O₂ total, approximately:
    • ~{{c1::850}} ml in blood
    • ~{{c1::250}} ml bound to myoglobin
    • ~{{c1::450}} ml contained in FRC (21% of 2.4 L)
  • Reference link (as provided): http://www.partone.litfl.com/GLOSSARY.html
  • (Slides 10–12)
Sites of O₂ Storage & Practical Limits
  • Oxygen is difficult to store in the body; total O₂ stores last only a few minutes.
  • Sites:
    • Lungs — in the {{c2::FRC (~2.5 L)}}
    • Haemoglobin
    • Myoglobin — tightly bound
    • Body fluids — physical solution
  • (Slide 38)
O₂ Stores — Volumes & Duration (Air vs 100% O₂)
ConditionLungs (ml)Blood (ml)Total (ml)Approx. duration
Air (21% O₂)50010001500~6 mins
100% O₂250010753575~14 mins
  • (Slide 39)

Oxygen Carriage in Blood

Dissolved vs Hb-bound
  • 1 L plasma → ~3 ml O₂ dissolved
  • 1 L blood (Hb 15 g/dl) → ~200 ml O₂ bound to Hb
  • (Slide 12)
Haemoglobin Structure & Binding
  • Hb A (adult): 2 α chains + 2 β chains (total 4 subunits)
  • Each subunit binds 1 molecule O₂
  • Positive co-operation (cooperativity)
  • (Slide 13)

Oxygen Content (CaO₂)

  • Definition: {{c1::CaO₂ = O₂ bound to Hb + dissolved O₂}}
  • Formulae (both notations preserved):
    • {{c1::CaO₂ = (Hb × Saturation × Hüffner Constant) + (Partial pressure O₂ × Solubility coefficient)
    • (Also appears as Huffner in later use; both preserved)}}\
  • Approximate total O₂ content: ~200 ml·L⁻¹
  • (Slide 14)

Oxygen Delivery (DO₂)

  • Definition: Rate of O₂ delivery to tissues
  • Formula: {{c1::DO₂ = (O₂ content of blood) × (cardiac output)}}
    • Expressed as DO₂ = {{c1::(Hb × saturation) × cardiac output}}
    • Example calculation: 200 ml·L⁻¹ × 5 L·min⁻¹ = 1000 ml O₂·min⁻¹
  • (Slide 15)

Oxygen Utilisation (VO₂) & Metabolic Context

  • VO₂ = {{c2::O₂ delivery − O₂ returning to lung}}
  • VO₂ ≈ 250 ml·min⁻¹
  • Aerobic vs anaerobic states differ in utilisation
  • Biochemical context:
    • Aerobic: glucose → pyruvate → CO₂ + H₂O + 38 ATP
    • Anaerobic: glucose → pyruvate → lactate + 2 ATP
  • Balance of supply vs demand (DO₂:VO₂) determines if aerobic metabolism can be sustained (normal ~{{c3::4:1}})
  • (Slides 16–17)

Hypoxia — Definitions & Categories

  • Core definition (variants preserved):
    • A relative or absolute deficiency of oxygen
    • A relative or absolute deficiency of cellular oxygen
  • {{c1::Increased demand::What causes(In hypoxia context)}}: e.g., seizures, pyrexia
  • {{c1::Decreased supply::What causes (In hypoxia context)}}: e.g., anaemia, CCF, IHD
  • {{c1::Abnormal usage::What causes (In hypoxia context)}}: e.g., cyanide poisoning
  • Four major categories (multiple instances preserved/merged from Slides 20–22, 31, 33–34):
    1. {{c2::Hypoxaemic — low arterial partial pressure
    2. Anaemic — low/abnormal haemoglobin
    3. Ischaemic — {{c5::low cardiac output (C.O.), interrupted blood supply}}
    4. Histotoxic — e.g., {{c4::cyanide poisoning, CO}}}}
      • Note (clarified phrasing for accuracy): Hb has a {{c3::higher::Higher or lower}} affinity for CO than O₂ (stated as “Hb has a higher affinity in CO than O₂”).
  • (Slides 19–22, 31, 33–34)

Hypoxaemic (Hypoxic) Hypoxia — Mechanisms

(Synonyms preserved: “Hypoxaemic Hypoxia” and “Hypoxic Hypoxia” (lists are identical across slides).
Merged from Slides 23–24, 26, 28, 30.)

Causes/examples:
  • {{c1::Low inspired oxygen (FiO₂)
  • Alveolar hypoventilation
  • Impaired pulmonary diffusion
  • V/Q mismatch / Shunt
    • Intrapulmonary eg. {{c6::PE}}
    • Extrapulmonary eg. {{c6::shunt}}}}
  • Explanatory note:
    {{c2::Ventilation}} moves air in/out of lungs, {{c2::diffusion}} transfers O₂ from alveoli to blood (and CO₂ out), and {{c2::perfusion}} delivers oxygen-rich blood to tissues — any mismatch between these leads to poor gas exchange.
Low Inspired Oxygen — Examples
  • {{c3::Oxygen-depleted environment
  • Dilution of inspired oxygen
  • Altitude}}
  • (Slide 25)
Alveolar Hypoventilation — Causes
  • Central:
    • {{c4::Drugs
    • Hypocapnia (↓CO₂)
    • Intracranial pathology — head injury, tumour
    • Removal of hypoxic drive / COPD}}
  • Peripheral:
    • {{c4::Airway obstruction
    • Restriction — pain, obesity, ascites, bandaging
    • Muscular/neurological — spinal injury, MND, muscular dystrophy, drugs
    • Mechanical — flail chest, pneumothorax, COPD}}
  • (Slide 27)
Impaired Diffusion — Examples
  • {{c5::Pulmonary oedema
  • Drowning
  • Pulmonary fibrosis}}
  • (Slide 29)

• Hypocapnia (↓CO₂) leads to alveolar hypoventilation cause: Low carbon dioxide levels can suppress the respiratory drive, especially in patients with chronic CO₂ retention (e.g. COPD), where the body relies more on oxygen levels to regulate breathing.

Anaemic Hypoxia — Quantitative Illustration

  • CaO₂ formula (notation as given):
    CaO₂ = ({{c1::Hb × SaO₂ × Huffner Constant) (+ solubility coeff × PaO}}₂)
  • TLDR;
  • Example values:
    • 19.8 ml·dl⁻¹ when Hb = 15 g·dl⁻¹
    • 9.3 ml·dl⁻¹ when Hb = 7 g·dl⁻¹
  • (Slide 32)

Clinical Hypoxia — Symptoms & Signs

Symptoms
  • {{c1::Light-headedness
  • Euphoria, (For clarity: “you’re more likely to laugh when you’re hypoxic”)
  • Tiredness
  • Ataxia
  • Confusion
  • Nausea
  • Tingling
  • Coma}}
Signs
  • {{c1::Tachycardia
  • Low saturations
  • Cyanosis (unreliable)}}

  • (Slides 35–36)

Oxygen Therapy — How Much & When

  • IN ALL EMERGENCIES → {{c1::high}} concentration
  • To correct hypoxia → {{c2::known}} concentration
  • Pre-operative → {{c3::100}}%
  • Post-operative supplement → ~{{c4::30}}%
  • Therapeutic hyperoxia → {{c5::high concentration / hyperbaric}}
(Slide note clarified): Hyperbaric chambers deliver higher O₂ concentrations than normal to improve recovery (spelling/grammar standardized)

Oxygen Therapy — Delivery Methods (“How”)

  • Nasal prongs
    • Set at {{c1::~2}} L·min⁻¹
    • Delivers low O₂ concentration ~{{c2::24–35}}%
  • Variable-performance oxygen mask
    • Delivers ~{{c3::35–60}}%
  • High Air Flow Oxygen Enrichment (HAFOE) Venturi mask
    • Delivers {{c4::prescribed O₂ concentrations irrespective of respiratory pattern}}
    • Fixed performance
  • Hudson mask with non-rebreathing bag
    • Delivers ~{{c5::60–80}}+%
    • Effective for {{c6::short-term treatment}}
  • Anaesthetic mask & circuit
    • Only way to deliver {{c7::100}}% oxygen
  • (Slides 43–46)

Monitoring During Oxygen Therapy

Signs of Inadequate Oxygenation
  • {{c1::↑ Respiratory rate
  • ↑ Pulse rate
  • Confusion, agitation, or reduced conscious level
  • Cyanosis (unreliable)}}
Methods of Monitoring Oxygenation
  • {{c1::Observation of clinical state
  • Pulse oximetry
  • Arterial blood gases (ABG)}}
  • (Slide 47)

Avoiding Respiratory Depression (CO₂ Narcosis Risk)

  • High index of suspicion in {{c2::COPD::Which diseases}}
  • Start with {{c3::low}} O₂ concentration via {{c3::fixed-performance}} mask ({{c3::24–28}}%)
  • Serial arterial blood gases
  • Monitor for signs of ↑CO₂:
    • Peripheral vasodilatation / bounding pulse
    • Coarse flapping tremor / twitching
    • Confusion, drowsiness, coma
  • (Slide 49)

Oxygen Therapy — Dangers

  • Oxygen toxicity — {{c1::lung, brain, eye}}
  • {{c1::Respiratory}}depression (CO₂ narcosis)
  • {{c1::Drying}}effect
  • {{c1::Fire}}hazard — facial burns and deaths reported when patients smoke during O₂ therapy
    • Reference as given: BMJ 2002;325:666
  • (Slides 50–51)

What are Helminths?

  • Helminth = general term meaning worm
  • Characteristics: invertebrates with elongated, flat, or round bodies
  • Living forms: parasites or free-living in aquatic/terrestrial environments
  • Classification (by shape):
    • {{c1::Flatworms (platyhelminths): flukes (trematodes), tapeworms (cestodes)
    • Roundworms (nematodes)}}
  • Further subdivision: by {{c2::residing host organ (e.g. lung flukes, intestinal roundworms)}}
StudyFix Simplification: Worms are either flat (flukes/tapeworms) or round; some live freely, others parasitize humans/animals.
StudyFix Clarification: [Not needed]

Classification of Helminths

Flukes (Trematodes)

  • {{c1::Leaf}}-shaped, length: few mm to {{c2::8}} cm
  • Hermaphroditic (except blood flukes, which are bisexual)
    • {{c3::Both self- and cross::What kind of}}-fertilisation possible
  • Life cycle:
    • Eggs pass in feces/urine/sputum → aquatic environment → hatch to ciliated larvae
    • Larvae penetrate/eaten by snail intermediate hosts
    • Within snail: miracidium → sporocyst → cercariae
    • Cercariae migrate out → penetrate definitive host → transform directly into adults

Common Trematodes

  • {{c4::Lung}}: {{c11::Paragonimus westermani}}
  • {{c5::Intestine}}: {{c10::Fasciolopsis buski, Heterophyes heterophyes, Metagonimus yokogawai}}
  • {{c6::Liver}}: {{c9::Clonorchis sinensis, Opisthorchis species, Fasciola hepatica}}
  • {{c7::Blood}}: {{c8::Schistosoma mansoni, S. haematobium, S. japonicum}}

Tapeworms (Cestodes)

  • Flat, hermaphroditic, parasitic worms inhabiting {{c12::GI tract::Which part of the body}}
  • Hosts: some primarily human pathogens; others animal pathogens infect humans too
  • Adults: {{c13::elongated, segmented, flat; intestinal lumen; length 2 mm – 10 m; thousands of segments::What do they look like and how long}}
  • Larval forms: cystic or solid; {{c14::extra-intestinal::In which tissues do they reside}} tissues
    • Examples: {{c15::Taenia saginata, T. solium, Diphyllobothrium latum, Hymenolepis nana, Echinococcus species}}
  • Larvae develop into adults in definitive {{c16::(human)}} host

Roundworms (Nematodes)

  • Cylindrical, bisexual (require copulation)
  • Developmental stages: egg → larva → adult
  • Classification by infection mode:
    • Direct (egg transmission anus→mouth, no soil):
      • {{c17::Enterobius vermicularis (pinworm/threadworm)
      • Trichuris trichiuria (whipworm)::Examples}}
    • Modified direct (eggs incubate in soil):
      • {{c18::Ascaris lumbricoides (roundworm)::Examples}}
    • Skin penetration:
      • {{c19::Hookworms (Ancylostoma duodenale, Necator americanus)::Examples}}
StudyFix Simplification: Flukes use snails, tapeworms live in intestines, and roundworms spread via eggs or skin.
StudyFix Clarification: Snails act as key intermediate hosts in trematode life cycles.

Diagnosis and Treatment of Common Helminthic Diseases

Schistosomiasis (Bilharziasis)

  • Caused by {{c1::blood flukes}}:
    • Schistosoma mansoni, S. haematobium, S. japonicum, S. intercalatum, S. mekongi
  • Acute infection:
    • {{c2::“Swimmer itch” or Katayama fever::What does it cayse}} (4–8 weeks post-infection)
    • Symptoms: {{c3::fever, urticaria, angioedema, chills, myalgia, arthralgia, dry cough, diarrhea, abdominal pain, headache}}
  • Chronic infection: months–years later
    • S. mansoni: {{c4::hepatosplenomegaly, periportal fibrosis → portal vein occlusion → portal hypertension, splenomegaly, portocaval shunting, GI varices::What does it cause}}
    • S. haematobium: {{c4::sandy patches in urinary bladder, hematuria, bladder cancer::What does it cause}}
  • Treatment: {{c5::Praziquantel 20}} mg/kg, repeat {{c5::4–6}} hours later (children >4 years, adults)

Enterobiasis (Oxyuris vermicularis / Pinworm)

  • Most common helminthic infection in Western Europe
  • Symptoms: {{c6::pruritus ani}}
  • Transmission:
    • {{c7::Eggs deposited in perianal folds by gravid worms
    • Auto-infection via scratching → hand-to-mouth
    • Person-to-person: contaminated surfaces, food::How does it get transmitted}}
  • Treatment:
    • {{c8::Mebendazole 100}} mg immediate dose, repeat
    • Whole family treated on first infection
  • Prevention: {{c9::avoid scratching, clean nails, boil/iron bedding & clothing}}

Trichuris trichiuria (Whipworm)

  • Causes {{c10::trichuriasis}}
  • Symptoms:
    • Often {{c11::asymptomatic}}
    • Heavy infestations → {{c12::severe GI}} symptoms (colitis, dysentery)
    • Children: {{c13::iron deficiency anemia, growth retardation::Symptoms}}
    • Rare: {{c13::rectal prolapse::Symptoms}}
  • Diagnosis: stool microscopy, full blood counts (eosinophilia common)
  • Treatment:
    • {{c14::Albendazole 400 mg, twice daily × 3 days (unlicensed)}}
    • {{c14::Mebendazole 500 mg (adults); children 1–18 years: 100 mg twice daily × 3 days}}
StudyFix Simplification: Schistosomiasis damages liver/bladder, pinworms cause itching, whipworms cause dysentery and anemia.
StudyFix Clarification: Praziquantel is specific for schistosomiasis; mebendazole/albendazole cover common intestinal worms.

Prevention Advice for Travelers to Endemic Areas

  • Contaminated food/water = infection risk
    • Wash hands before eating, after bathroom, after contact with children/animals/feces
    • Avoid raw/uncooked foods (salads, unpeeled fruits, uncooked vegetables)
    • Drink safe water only (avoid tap water for cooking, brushing teeth, ice, etc.)
  • Avoid {{c1::contact with unsafe water (sewage, storm drains, heavy rainfall runoff, freshwater in schistosomiasis-endemic areas: Caribbean, South America, Africa, Asia)}}
  • Prevent hookworms: {{c2::wear shoes, avoid contaminated soil}}
  • Protection from bites: {{c3::DEET insect repellent, permethrin-soaked clothing, long clothing}}
StudyFix Simplification: Wash hands, avoid unsafe water/food, wear shoes, use repellents in endemic regions.
StudyFix Clarification: Schistosomiasis is linked to freshwater exposure in Africa, Asia, S. America.

Preventive Chemotherapy

  • WHO recommends as public health strategy in endemic areas
  • {{c4::Antihelminthics}}: used alone or in combination
    • Prevent morbidity, cover multiple helminths, reduce transmission
  • Should begin early in life
  • Every opportunity should be taken to reach at-risk populations
StudyFix Simplification: WHO advises mass deworming early in life to reduce transmission.
StudyFix Clarification: This is called Mass Drug Administration (MDA).

Helminthic Therapy

  • Definition: experimental immunotherapy = deliberate helminth infestation/egg administration
  • Hypothesis:
    • Helminths induce {{c5::Th2 anti-inflammatory response}}
    • This downregulates hyperactive {{c5::Th1/Th17}} immune responses (seen in autoimmune disease)
  • Target diseases: autoimmune and immune disorders
StudyFix Simplification: Infecting with worms may calm immune system and help autoimmune diseases.
StudyFix Clarification: [Outside Fact: Trials use Trichuris suis ova for Crohn’s disease (NICE 2024)].

Autoimmunity: Definition and Core Mechanisms

  • Definition
    • Autoimmunity occurs when immunological tolerance to self antigens breaks down.
  • Initiation and perpetuation
    • Cause is unknown in most cases, but {{c1::infection}} is often implicated in the initial breakdown of self-tolerance.
    • Autoimmune disease occurs when autoantigens are persistent ⟶ chronic inflammation.
    • Chronic inflammation ⟶ tissue damage ⟶ release of more autoantigens and recruitment of lymphocytes, macrophages, neutrophils ⟶ exacerbation of inflammation.
    • Previously unavailable autoantigens may become targets after the original attack.
  • Slides (anchors): 6–8

Genetics of Autoimmunity

  • Many genes affect autoimmunity.
    {{c1::::What are the genes affecting ankylosing spondyltiits, SLE, Rhema. arthritis}}
  • Genome-Wide Association Studies (GWAS)
    • Large numbers of individuals with a disease/trait.
    • Viewed as great hope of the research community.
    • So far… disappointing results.
    • Now additional steps are included to assess protein functionality.
  • Slides (anchors): 9–12

Environment and Autoimmunity

  • Environmental triggers in autoimmune rheumatic diseases
    • Based on lack of concordance in identical twins.
    • Regional differences in prevalence after migration.
    • For most diseases the trigger is unknown, but {{c1::infectious}} agents are prime candidates.
  • Environmental factors affecting {{c2::SLE}} (suggested; anecdotal evidence)
    • Sunlight
    • Pesticides
    • Silica
    • Mercury
    • EBV infection
    • Hormones
  • EBV infection?
    • The paradox: ~{{c3::90% of adults are infected by EBV, yet SLE prevalence remains low, emphasizing multifactorial pathogenesis::What is the paradox}}.
  • Impact of hormones
    • Predominantly a disease of women of {{c4::childbearing}} age.
    • Anecdotal reports of {{c5::exogenous}} oestrogens exacerbating disease and/or increasing risk of development.
    • SELENA study: {{c6::increase in mild flare in SLE, but no overall increase in disease activity due to HRT use::what did it point out}}.
    • Contraceptive Pill: anecdotal evidence of severe disease flares not confirmed by randomized study.

Mechanisms Linking Triggers to Autoimmunity

  • Cross-reactivity and molecular mimicry
    • {{c1::Microorganisms/viruses with similar antigens as self antigens::What causes}}⟶ cross-reactivity.
  • Formation of neoantigens
    • Occurs by {{c2::chemical binding (e.g., drugs)}} or {{c2::tissue damage}}.
  • Superantigens
    • Produced by {{c3::microorganisms}} ⟶ stimulation of T cells and massive cytokine release.
  • Idiotype cross-reaction
    • {{c4::Antigenic epitopes on the antigen-binding portion of immunoglobulin; cross-reactivity between it and anti-viral antibody::Describe what}} can cause autoimmune response.
  • Release of {{c5::sequestered or cryptic}} antigens
    • Exposure to the immune system ⟶ reaction.
  • Dendritic cell apoptosis and clearance deficiency
    • Can lead to inappropriate {{c6::lymphocyte}} activation.

Rheumatoid Arthritis: Epidemiology and Impact

  • Affects 1% of the population (higher in >50 yrs).
  • Female:Male = {{c1::3:1}}.
  • Usually begins in {{c2::20–50}} age group.
  • Joint destruction leading to deformity and disability.
  • Life expectancy {{c3::shortened}}.

Rheumatoid Synovium: Cellular Components

  • Major cell types
    • {{c4::T lymphocytes
    • Macrophage-like (type A) synoviocytes
    • Fibroblastic (type B) synoviocytes}}
  • Less abundant but important
    • Dendritic cells
    • B cells
    • Plasma cells
    • Mast cells
    • Osteoclasts

RA Pathogenesis: Candidate Mechanisms

  • Possible components
    • Abnormal antigen presentation in the synovium.
    • Immune complex–mediated disease.
    • Dysregulation of T cells and/or macrophages in synovium with production of inflammatory cytokines.(autoreactive CD4 T cells activate macrophages, resulting in pro inflammatory cytokines which then produce {{c5::MMP and RANKL ligand}})
    • Role of TNF:
      • {{c6::Pivotal (but not exclusive) cytokine, acting directly in both pro- and anti-inflammatory networks, and indirectly via effect on interleukins (IL), especially IL-1 and IL-6}}.

Antibodies in Autoimmune Disease (generated)

  • {{c1::Physiologic (natural)::Which}} autoantibodies
    • Serve as first defense against infections.
    • Contribute to immune system homeostasis.
  • Pathogenic autoantibodies
    • Reflect a pathologic process where cell clearance (apoptosis), antigen signaling, and cell effector function are disturbed.
    • Tissue/organ injury via {{c2::antigen–antibody complexes and complement activation}} ⟶ perpetuates inflammation.

Rheumatoid Factor (RF) and Related Structures

  • RF: Antibodies to {{c3::IgG}}
    • High levels -> moderate sensitivity (>80%) for RA.
    • IgA and IgM RF present {{c4::years::How long}} before onset.
    • {{c5::Plasma}} cells make RF antibodies in the joint.
    • Lots of antigen everywhere! ).
  • Diagnosis and prognosis
    • Diagnosis of RA is supported by identification of {{c6::RF, an anti-IgG antibody present in >80% of patients}}.
    • Most RF are Ig{{c7::M}} subtype, with specificity for Fc portion of IgG.
    • High levels associated with worse prognosis, implicating pathogenic role.

Anti–Cyclic Citrullinated Peptide (anti-CCP)

  • Citrullination
    • Citrulline: non-standard amino acid created by deamination of arginine residues by {{c1::peptidylarginine deiminase (PAD)}}.
    • {{c1::PAD}} enzymes cause local citrullination of synovial proteins (e.g., fibrin).
  • Antigenic target and local response
    • {{c2::Citrullinated extracellular fibrin}} in RA synovium may be a major autoantigen driving local immune response, suggested by anti-CCP production in the joint.
  • Clinical utility
    • Important markers for diagnosis and prognosis in RA:
      • {{c3::As}} sensitive as and {{c3::more}} specific than IgM RF in early and established disease.
      • May predict {{c3::development into RA}} when found in undifferentiated arthritis.
      • Marker of {{c3::erosive}} disease in RA.
      • May be detected in healthy individuals {{c3::years}} before onset of clinical RA.

SLE: Classification (ACR 1982) and Diagnostic Framework

  • ACR 1982 criteria: To make a diagnosis of lupus, the patient must have had at least FOUR of these 11 manifestations at any time since disease onset:
    1. {{c1::{{c2::Malar}} rash: fixed red rash over the cheeks.
    2. {{c2::Discoid}}rash: red patches with scaling and plugging of hair follicles.
    3. Photosensitivity: rash after sunlight exposure.
    4. {{c3::Mucosal}}ulcers: small sores in mouth and nose.
    5. Serositis: inflammation of delicate tissues covering internal organs; abdominal pain.
    6. Arthritis: very common; pain in the joints.
    7. {{c4::Renal}} disorder: usually detected by routine blood and urine analysis.
    8. {{c4::Neurological}} disorder: seizures or psychosis.
    9. {{c4::Haematological}} disorder: haemolytic anaemia, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia.
    10. {{c4::Immunologic}} disorder: tests on LE cells, anti-DNA, and anti-Sm antibodies.
    11. {{c5::Anti-Nuclear}} Antibody (ANA) blood test:
      • When found in blood and the patient is not taking drugs, it is known to cause a positive test for lupus in most cases, but is not necessarily conclusive::What are the 11 criteria}}

SLE Immunopathogenesis (flow)

Aspects of SLE immunopathogenesis (diagram transcribed and organized)
  • {{c1::Genetic susceptibility + External triggers (e.g., infections) ⟶
    • B and T cells specific for self-nuclear antigens (failure of self-tolerance) ⟶
    • Apoptosis with defective clearance of apoptotic bodies (Increased burden of nucleur antigens)⟶
      • TLR stimulation of B cells and DCs by antigen–antibody complexes ⟶
      • Type I IFN (e.g., IFN-α) production ⟶
      • Persistent IgG anti-nuclear antibodies::Describe the whole process}}
    • Figure key takeaway: Impaired apoptotic clearance increases nuclear antigen exposure, driving TLR-mediated autoreactive B/T cell activation and sustained type I IFN and ANA production.

Immunological Diagnosis of SLE

  • {{c1::ANA}} testing
    • Useful diagnostic tool when combined with relevant clinical history and symptoms consistent with {{c2::CTD}} (e.g., SLE, scleroderma, Sjögren’s syndrome, polymyositis/dermatomyositis).
  • ANA by {{c3::indirect immunofluorescence (IIF)}}
    • .
    • Patterns: Homogenous/speckled ANA, high titre (noted under immunological diagnosis section).
  • Anti-dsDNA testing
    • Screen: Crithidia luciliae.

    • Monitoring: Quantitative ELISA.
      • Substrate gives colour reaction.
      • Anti-human IgG antibody with enzyme label.
      • Patient’s anti-dsDNA binds dsDNA.
  • Direct immunofluorescence (DIF)
    • Detects deposition of {{c4::immune complexes in skin and kidney}}.
    • Patient biopsy material probed with {{c5::anti-human IgG or C3}} antibody labelled with fluorescent probe.
    • Detects deposition of immune complexes in patient organs (kidney, skin).
  • Limitations of ANA testing
      • {{c6::ANA can be positive in healthy individuals, particularly the elderly, who never develop CTD.
      • Positive ANA also seen in diseases other than CTD, where it has no diagnostic or prognostic value.
      • High titre ANA are more likely to be markers of connective tissue disease, BUT:
        • ANA titre is not a marker of disease activity}}.
StudyFix Simplification: Use ANA (IIF) with clinical context; anti-dsDNA (Crithidia/ELISA) refines diagnosis; DIF shows immune complex deposition; ANA titre ≠ activity.
StudyFix Clarification: [Not needed]

Targeted Therapeutic Approaches in SLE

  • {{c1::Rituximab}} as therapy for B-cell lymphoma (context for B-cell depletion)
    • Approved (USA) for non-Hodgkin’s lymphoma in 1997.
    • Chimeric monoclonal anti-CD20 antibody.
    • Leads to B-cell depletion lasting 6 → 9 months.
    • Response rate approx 60%.
  • Severe Discoid Lupus—clinical images
    • Before B-cell depletion.

    • After 1 month BCD.
      .
  • Antibody levels after B-cell depletion
    • Antibody levels which fall:
      • {{c2::Anti-DNA
      • Anti-nucleosome
      • Anti-C1q
      • Anti-cardiolipin}}
    • Antibody levels which do NOT fall:
      • {{c2::Anti-Ro
      • Anti-La
      • Anti-Sm
      • Anti-RNP
      • Anti-measles
      • Anti-tetanus toxoid}}

Systemic Sclerosis (Scleroderma)

  • Definition and scope
    • Systemic disease characterized by {{c1::sclerosis of connective tissue}}.
    • Most obvious in the skin (skin thickening) but also affects kidney, lung, heart, and gut.
  • Clinical subsets
    • Diffuse, progressive scleroderma:
      • {{c2::Generalized skin thickening with rapidly progressive and often fatal visceral involvement::What does it present with}}.
    • Limited cutaneous scleroderma ({{c3::CREST}} syndrome):
      • {{c4::Calcinosis cutis
      • Raynaud's syndrome
      • Esophageal dysmotility
      • Sclerodactyly
      • Telangiectasias::What are the 5 main }}

Brucellosis

Introduction
  • Infectious disease caused by Brucella species.
  • {{c1::Endemic zoonotic}} disease affecting humans and animals (cattle, dogs, sheep, goats).
  • Caused by gram-{{c2::negative}} {{c2::aerobic coccobacillus}} bacteria.
  • Also known as:
    • {{c3::Remitting or relapsing fever
    • Undulant fever
    • Mediterranean fever}}
Reservoirs, Shedding, and Transmission
  • Bacteria are found in the {{c4::reproductive}} organs of host animals, causing {{c5::abortions and sterility}}.
  • Shed in {{c6::urine, milk, placental fluid, and other animal fluids}}.
  • Humans are incidental hosts infected through contact with animals and animal products.
Clinical Features (Acute)
    • {{c7::Disabling flu-like syndrome with nonspecific signs:
      • Undulating fever, sweating, chills, myalgia, arthralgia, fatigue}}.
Chronic Disease and Complications
  • If not properly diagnosed in the acute phase and left untreated, infection can become chronic and persist for years.
  • Chronic infection leads to localization of bacteria in various tissues and organs, causing debilitating complications:
    • {{c8::Osteoarticular involvement
    • Hepatobiliary involvement
    • Central nervous system (CNS) involvement
    • Cardiovascular involvement}}

Aetiology of Brucellosis

  • The Brucella genus currently consists of 12 species.
  • Of these, four species have moderate-to-significant human pathogenicity:
    • {{c1::Brucella melitensis}} (from sheep).
    • {{c1::Brucella suis}} (from pigs).
    • {{c1::Brucella abortus}} (from cattle).
    • {{c1::Brucella canis}} (from dogs).
  • Infectivity: as few as 10–100 organisms can cause disease in humans.
  • Characteristics: Brucella species {{c2::do not form spores or toxins::Do they form spores and toxins}}.
  • Virulence ranking:
    • Most virulent: {{c3::B. melitensis}}.
    • Next: {{c3::B. suis}}.
  • Most vulnerable occupational groups:
    • {{c4::Slaughterhouse workers.
    • Veterinarians.
    • Farmers.
    • Laboratory technicians (infected via animal or human specimens)}}

Transmission of Brucellosis

  1. {{c1::Direct contact with infected animals or their body fluids}}.
  2. {{c1::Consumption of contaminated products, such as raw or unpasteurized dairy products from infected animals}}.
  3. Inhalation of airborne agents:
    • Occurs in {{c1::certain occupational settings (e.g., slaughterhouses, meat processing facilities) where airborne transmission of Brucella bacteria is possible.}}
  4. Occupational hazard: high risk in professions such as {{c1::butchers and laboratory workers}}.
  5. Indirect transmission: through contact with {{c1::contaminated materials or environments}}.

Pathogenesis of Brucellosis

  • Incubation period: ranges from as few as {{c1::3 }}days to several weeks.
Entry and Early Infection
  • When ingested, Brucella organisms are {{c2::phagocytosed}} and enter the intestinal submucosa.
  • Transported to {{c3::lymphoid}} tissue by macrophages.
  • In the bloodstream, organisms are contained within {{c4::polymorphonuclear cells (PMNs) and macrophages,}} where they exploit mechanisms to avoid or suppress bactericidal responses.
Dissemination
  • Brucellae spread into the {{c5::lymphatic}} system and replicate locally.
  • May also replicate in:
    • Liver
    • Spleen
    • Kidney
    • Breast tissue
    • Joints
  • Leads to both {{c6::localized and systemic::Local or systemic}} infection.
Virulence Factors
  • Brucella lacks classical virulence factors (no exotoxins, cytolysins, or exoenzymes).
  • Pathogenesis involves:
    • {{c7::Invasion of host cells.
    • Immune evasion.
    • Establishment of chronic infection}}.
  • Unique ability to {{c7::penetrate and persist within host cells (e.g., macrophages)}}
  • Strategies include:
    • {{c8::Inhibiting phagocytosis.
    • Reducing bactericidal activity.
    • Diminishing endotoxic reactions.
    • Impeding antigen presentation}}.
Course of Illness
  • Symptoms may {{c9::persist for several months}}.
  • Chronic brucellosis may last for {{c9::years}}.

Clinical Picture of Brucellosis

History
  • A {{c1::detailed}} history is essential for diagnosis.
  • Important elements:
    • {{c2::Dietary}} history.
    • {{c2::Occupational}} history.
  • Symptoms vary from flu-like illnesses to {{c3::severe multi-organ complications.}}
Common Symptoms
  • {{c4::Headache.
  • Cyclical fever.
  • Migratory arthralgia.
  • Myalgia.
  • Asthenia.
  • Anorexia.
  • Fatigue.
  • Malaise.
  • Weakness.
  • Sweating.
  • Vomiting.
  • Diarrhoea.
  • Abdominal pain.
  • Miscarriage.
  • Focal manifestations depend on the involved organs (see complications)}}.
Complications
  • Rare if {{c5::treated timely and appropriately.}}
  • Risk increases if symptom duration > {{c6::1}} month before diagnosis.
Cardiovascular
  • {{c6::Endocarditis.
  • Myocarditis.
  • Pericarditis}}.
Genitourinary
  • {{c6::Orchitis.
  • Epididymo-orchitis.
  • Glomerulonephritis.
  • Pyelonephritis.
  • Abortion}}.
Central Nervous System (CNS)
  • {{c6::Meningitis.
  • Meningoencephalitis.
  • Papilledema.
  • Stroke.
  • Optic neuropathy}}.
Hematologic
  • {{c6::Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC)}}.
Musculoskeletal
  • {{c6::Sacroiliitis.
  • Arthritis.
  • Osteomyelitis.
  • Tenosynovitis}}.
Gastrointestinal / Hepatobiliary
  • {{c6::Hepatitis.
  • Hepatic abscess.
  • Acute cholecystitis.
  • Ileitis.
  • Colitis.
  • Abscess in spinal cord, spleen, thyroid}}.

Physical Examination in Brucellosis

  • {{c2::May be normal::Can it be normal}}.
  • Findings can include:
    • {{c1::Lymphadenopathy.
    • Splenomegaly.
    • Hepatomegaly}}.
  • Cardiac involvement:
    • Endocarditis → {{c3::new or changing murmurs}}.
    • Pericarditis → {{c3::pericardial rub}}.
  • Skin lesions:
    • {{c4::Maculopapular eruptions.
    • Erythema nodosum.
    • Abscesses}}.
  • Ocular findings:
    • {{c4::Uveitis.
    • Keratoconjunctivitis.
    • Iridocyclitis.
    • Optic neuritis.
    • Cataracts}}.
  • Neurologic findings (depending on disease presentation):
    • Meningitis: {{c5::nuchal rigidity, Kernig sign, Brudzinski sign::what are the signs}}.
    • Increased intracranial pressure (ICP) / brain abscess: {{c5::papilledema, cranial nerve palsy, focal neurologic deficits}}.

Relapses in Brucellosis

  • Incidence: {{c6::5%–15}}% after treatment.
  • Timing: usually within {{c7::6–12}} months of completing therapy.
  • Causes of relapse:
    • {{c8::Insufficient antibiotic treatment (short duration or nonadherence).
    • Localised areas of infection}}.

Diagnosis of Brucellosis

General Considerations
  • Symptoms and signs are nonspecific.
  • {{c1::Cultures and serology (especially serology to not overcontaminate)}} are usually necessary for diagnosis.
  • Certain laboratory findings may suggest the diagnosis.
Complete Blood Count (CBC)
  • Typically shows {{c2::neutropenia and anemia}}.
  • Thrombocytopenia also reported:
    • May occur secondary to {{c3::hepatosplenomegaly}}.
    • Or from {{c3::immune thrombocytopenia}}.
Inflammatory Markers
  • Raised {{c4::ESR, CRP, and LDH}}.
Liver Enzymes
  • Elevation in {{c4::ALT, AST, and Alkaline phosphatase}} is very common.
Culture
  • Blood cultures in {{c5::tryptose medium}} and {{c5::BACTEC}} system may detect Brucella.
  • Slow-growing nature: cultures may take ≥ 1 week to become positive.
  • Sensitivity: {{c6::10–90}}%.
  • Bone marrow culture: higher yield than blood culture, due to high concentration of brucellae in the reticuloendothelial system.
Serology
  • {{c7::Standard agglutination testing (SAT)}}: most common in endemic areas.
  • Indirect ELISA: {{c8::also useful::Useful or no}}.
  • {{c9::Repeated serologic}} testing recommended if initial titer is low.
  • Titers > 1:160 + compatible clinical presentation → suggestive of {{c10::infection}}.
  • Titers > 1:320 → more specific, especially in {{c10::endemic areas}}.
Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR)
  • PCR developed for {{c11::rapid detection and diagnosis}} of Brucella in blood specimens.
  • {{c12::Species-specific::Species specfic or not}}.
  • {{c13::Nucleic acid amplification tests (NAAT)}}: accurate and rapid.
Radiography
  • In brucellar spondylitis:
    • {{c14::Disc space narrowing.
    • Bone destruction.
    • Sclerosis}}.
Biopsy
  • Bone marrow aspiration and biopsy: {{c15::may be required in certain patients::IS it required}}.
  • Percutaneous liver biopsy: in patients with {{c16::liver granulomas}}.
    • Used to obtain specimens for diagnosis.
    • May show {{c17::granulomatous hepatitis}} and {{c17::hepatic microabscesses}}.

Treatment

Goal
  • {{c1::Control the disease as quickly as possible to prevent complications and relapses}}.
Antibiotics
  • {{c2::Doxycycline}}is used with either {{c2::streptomycin, rifampin, gentamicin}}, or {{c2::sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim}} to treat brucellosis.
  • As the bacteria reside within cells, {{c3::several weeks::how long}} of treatment are needed.
  • Avoid {{c4::single-agent}} therapy: high relapse rates when a combination is not used.
  • Uncomplicated infections:
    • {{c5::Doxycycline}}100 mg {{c6::PO twice daily for 6 weeks::Whats the dosage and for how long}} may be the most appropriate monotherapy; however, relapse ≈ {{c7::40}}% with such monotherapy, so {{c7::rifampin 600–900}} mg/day is usually added.
  • {{c8::Fluoroquinolones}} are considered secondary alternatives because of their high efficacy.
  • Pregnancy: {{c9::rifampin}} during pregnancy, with {{c10::trimethoprim–sulfamethoxazole}} added postpartum.
  • Spondylitis / sacroiliitis: {{c11::doxycycline}} + {{c11::rifampin}} + {{c11::an aminoglycoside (gentamicin)}} for the initial {{c12::2–3}} weeks, followed by {{c13::6}} weeks of {{c14::rifampin + doxycycline}}.
Additional Drugs
  • Symptomatic treatment with {{c15::antipyretics and analgesics}} must also be administered.
Surgical Intervention
  • Main roles of surgery in brucellosis:
    • Treatment of {{c16::endocarditis (infected valves may need replacement)}}.
    • Drainage of {{c17::pyogenic joint effusions or paraspinal abscesses}}.
    • {{c18::Surgical debridement}}and {{c18::bone grafting}} for spondylitis.
General Rules
  • Majority of patients managed as {{c19::outpatients}} unless there are complications.
  • Avoid contact with the source of infection.
  • {{c20::Serological}} monitoring required to ensure response to treatment.
  • Compliance with medical treatment is essential for full recovery.

Prevention

  • Avoid {{c21::infected animals}}; use {{c22::gloves and masks}} when dealing with infected animals; avoid potentially contaminated foods.
  • Adequate {{c21::cooking}} of meat and {{c21::pasteurization}} of dairy products.
  • Laboratory workers should maintain an appropriate level of {{c21::containment}}.
  • Animal {{c21::(livestock) vaccination}}: controlling animal brucellosis is the most efficient approach to preventing human infection.
  • No human vaccine currently available.

Prognosis

  • {{c23::Excellent}}for most patients.
  • Relapse risk: {{c24::5–15}}%.
  • In patients with comorbid disorders, the condition can be {{c25::disabling}}.
  • {{c26::Poor}} prognosis with congestive heart failure due to endocarditis.
  • Tends to improve with {{c27::physical activity}} rather than bed rest.
  • Recovery usually takes {{c28::3–6}} months.
  • Overall mortality (acute or chronic): {{c29::< 2%.}}

Case Presentation

  • Patient: Male, 20 years old, university student.
Initial Complaint
  • Fever for 1 week (up to 39°C) with headache and generalized malaise.
  • Took acetaminophen for fever.
First Medical Visit
  • On physical examination: swollen cervical lymph nodes.
  • Received non-specific treatment (assumed influenza).
Progression
  • After 2 days → developed neck stiffness and rigidity.
  • Possible meningitis suspected → referred to Fever Hospital.
Investigations
  • MRI (head and neck): ordered.
  • Diagnostic spinal tap performed.
CSF Analysis
  • Findings:
    • Lymphocytes present.
    • Low glucose.
    • Elevated protein.
