    • Consistent with aseptic meningitis.
  • Absence of neutrophils: excludes meningococcal meningitis.
  • Some CSF sample submitted to microbiology for culture.
Hospital Course
  • Hospitalized for 3 days.
  • Condition: not improved, but stable.
Revised History
  • Patient had visited brother’s farm where cows were raised.
  • Assisted in pulling a stillborn calf from the birth canal of a cow in difficult labor.

Differential Diagnosis

  1. {{c1::Brucellosis.
  2. Bacterial meningitis.
  3. ENT infections}}.

Diagnostic Confirmation

  • {{c2::Standard Tube Agglutination (STA)}} test for Brucella spp.: POSITIVE.
  • With supporting history and clinical features, diagnosis of brucellosis established.
  • Brucella antibodies also detected in {{c3::CSF}} sample.
  • Final diagnosis: Neurobrucellosis.

Treatment and Outcome

  • Treated with dual antibiotics for {{c4::6}} weeks.
  • Showed good response.

children.name: ICC FNM[003] Surgical Anatomy of External Abdominal Herniae

fields:
Definitions and General Concepts of Hernia

  • Etymology: The word hernia is derived from Latin meaning “a rupture.”
  • Definition: Abnormal protrusion of an organ or tissue through a natural or induced orifice into a surrounding space; may occur between two adjacent cavities (e.g., abdomen ↔ thorax).
  • Common site: Hernia can occur in various sites, most commonly through the abdominal wall.
  • Exception: The {{c1::pancreas}} is the only intra-abdominal organ that does not protrude through an abdominal wall defect (posterior retroperitoneal position).

I) Introduction

1) How scientists describe neoplasms
  • Neoplasm: a mass of cells that
    • Proliferate in an {{c1::atypical and relentless}} way.
    • Serve {{c1::no useful}} functions.
  • Cancer cells are psychopaths:
    • No respect {{c1::for the rights of other cells; they violate the democratic principles of normal cellular organization}}.
    • Proliferation is {{c1::uncontrolled}}.
    • Ability to spread is {{c1::unbounded}}.
    • Relentless progression destroys {{c1::first the tissue and then the person}}.
  • Cancer as a genetic ferment:
    • Cells divide without {{c1::proper checks and balances}}.
    • Mutations arise continually within tumours.
    ⟶ If they affect tumour suppressor genes → inhibit {{c2::apoptosis}} and encourage {{c2::further mutations}}.
    ⟶ {{c3::Some mutations lead to drug resistance:: T OR F, Some mutations lead to drug resistance}}.
  • • We all carry genetic sequences that, through mutation, can turn into active oncogenes and cause malignant transformation.
2) Epidemiology
  • One in four deaths in the USA is caused by cancer, second only to heart disease.
  • In the USA, cancer is the leading cause of death before age 65 in both sexes.
  • In the USA, although the absolute number of patients with cancer is increasing, the death rate is decreasing (declining since the early 1990s).
3) Types of neoplasms
  • Benign: cells do not invade surrounding tissues; remain local as a conglomerate.
  • Malignant: cells can invade and metastasize.
4) Further classifications of neoplasms
{{c4::

Cell of origin Tissue of origin Gross appearance
Epithelium (adenoma, adenocarcinoma) Skin Cauliflower mass
Mesenchymal / connective tissue (…oma or sarcoma) Breast Ulcerating
GIT Fungating
Lung Spreading (superficial or in the submucosa)
Urogenital Cicatrizing
Haematological
Nervous system
::How are they classified}}

II) Aetiology of cancer

  • Most cancers are caused by a complex combination of exogenous and/or endogenous factors rather than a single physical/chemical/biological factor.
Exogenous factors
  1. {{c1::Physical.
  2. Chemical.
  3. Infections (viral & parasites).
  4. Dietary carcinogens.
  5. Chronic inflammation}}.
Endogenous factors
  1. {{c1::Genetic.
  2. Hormones (promotors).
  3. Immunity}}.

III) Tumour biology

Functional capabilities of malignant cells
  • Growth capabilities
    • Self-sufficient growth
    • Limitless replication
  • Antigrowth factor evasion
    • Insensitivity to antigrowth signals
    • Evade apoptosis
  • Interaction & relationship with other elements
    • Sustained angiogenesis
    • Sustained tissue invasion
Self-sufficient growth
  • Cancer cells grow and proliferate in the absence of external stimuli.
  • Oncogenes (aberrant forms of normal cellular genes) are key factors.
  • Latent oncogenic sequences in humans can, via mutation, become active and cause malignant transformation.
Limitless replication (immortality)
  • Normal cells: finite divisions (~40–60).
    • Limitation imposed by telomere shortening (molecular clock).
  • Cancer cells: use {{c1::telomerase}} to rebuild telomeres each cycle → no telomeric shortening → lineage never dies → immortality.
Sustained tissue invasion
  • Mechanisms:
    1. {{c2::Rise in interstitial pressure from unrestrained proliferation and lack of contact inhibition → cells push beyond normal limits.
    2. Dissolution of extracellular matrix via collagenases and proteases; prostaglandins induce osteolysis (skeletal deposits). Modulation of cell-surface integrins permits detachment from ECM.
    3. Increased motility: abnormal integrins transmit environmental signals → mobility; epithelial–mesenchymal transition (EMT): epithelial cells behave as if mesenchymal}}.
Malignant transformation vs tumour development
  • Transformation ≠ inevitable tumour. Malignant transformation may be more frequent than clinical cancer.
  • Protective mechanisms:
    {{c3::a) Loss of transformed cells from surfaces by simple wastage (e.g., lumen via normal mucosal shedding).
    b) Immune surveillance destroys transformed cells before proliferation}}
Evasion of immune surveillance
  • Mechanisms:
    {{c4::a) Suppress expression of tumour-associated antigens.
    b) Co-opt parts of the immune system to help the tumour escape detection by other components}}
If immune surveillance breaks
  • Persistence of growth of transformed cells in host tissues.
  • Examples:
    a) Bone marrow/solid organ transplantation (induced immunosuppresion) associated with {{c5::Hodgkin’s disease, Kaposi sarcoma, skin, oropharynx, bone cancers.}}
    b) HIV/AIDS associated with {{c5::Non-Hodgkin’s lymphoma, Kaposi sarcoma, invasive cervical cancer}}
The growth of a tumour
  1. Number of cell doublings and doubling time duration determine total duration (assuming constancy).
  2. ~45 doublings → a 1 cm tumour (clinically occult → clinically manifest); +15 doublings → ~1 Kg tumour (1 billion cells stated) → likely fatal.
  3. Faster cell cycle → {{c6::shorter}} time between doublings → more rapid disease and shorter survival.
  • Growth kinetics:
    • Early growth is exponential; as size increases, growth rate slows (nutrition/oxygenation constraints; competition with host tissues and between tumour cells).
    • Best model: {{c7::Gompertzian}} (not purely exponential)
  • Basically most tumor growth happens {{c8::before}} clinical detection which is bad
Spread of cancer cells
  • Tissue disrespect: malignant cells breach basement membrane, enter blood & lymph → use body’s distributing systems.
  • Most circulating cancer cells do not form viable metastases; detectable in patients who never develop clinical metastatic disease.
  • Two steps to distant metastases: migration followed by implantation.
Migration
  • a) {{c9::Individual cell migration}}.
  • b) {{c9::Embolic clumping: outer cells protect inner cells from immune attack; outer protective cells may on occasion be normal lymphocytes}}.
Implantation in distant sites
  • Malignant cells express inflammatory cytokines that “fool” endothelium into activation → allow access to extravascular space.
  • Activated endothelium expresses receptors that bind to {{c10::integrins/selectins on malignant cells → transendothelial migration}}
Classical vs contemporary concepts of spread
  • Traditional: {{c11::centrifugal tissue spread → regional lymph nodes (first step, regional disease) → then bloodstream dissemination (second step)}}.
  • Contemporary: {{c11::even early stages can invade both lymphatic and blood vessels → widespread micrometastases early}}.
    ⟶ Changes many classical management concepts.
Conceptual changes in management (based on spread mechanisms)
  1. Need for {{c12::systemic}} treatment for long-term control.
  2. Curative locoregional radical resection challenged; reappraise {{c14::local treatment to decrease postoperative morbidity}}.
  3. {{c13::Regional lymph node}} involvement now an indicator that widespread dissemination has occurred (not merely a staging step).
  4. Concept of cure: from absence of residual disease (lifetime & postmortem) → {{c15::normal duration of life without further clinical evidence of disease (microscopic deposits may still be present)}}.

IV) Clinical features and diagnosis

Clinical features
  • Benign tumours: often unrecognized or cause symptoms due to local or general effects.
  • Malignant tumours: variable—some rapidly progressive and debilitating; in others, local effects predominate.
Local effects
  1. {{c1::Surface lesions: Bleed or discharge.
  2. Hollow viscus: Obstruction.
  3. Closed space: Pressure.
  4. Organ failure from invasion.
  5. Pain: Severe from invasion; can mimic benign pain (e.g., {{c2::gastric ulcer}})}}.
Systemic effects
  1. {{c1::General debility (anaemia, abnormal proteins/peptides).
  2. Appropriate secretion of hormones.
  3. Inappropriate/ectopic secretion of hormones}}.
Diagnostic procedures
  1. {{c3::Laboratory: routine; tumour markers in some cases.
  2. Imaging.
  3. Sampling and biopsy.
  4. Additional aids to diagnosis}}.
Tumour markers
  • Definition: substances produced by tumours or by the body’s response to the tumour.
  • Categories:
    1. {{c4::Proteins: enzymes, hormones, oncofetal antigens.
    2. Genetic mutations.
    3. Epigenetic changes}}.
  • Uses:
    1. Diagnosis.
    2. Staging.
    3. Response to treatment.
    4. Tumour recurrence.
Imaging: diagnostic procedures
  1. Radiology: plain; contrast studies of {{c5::GI and GU}} tracts; CT, virtual colonoscopy.
  2. Ultrasonography.
  3. Radioscintigraphy: {{c6::thyroid scan, bone scan, PET & PET-CT::What are examples}}.
  4. MRI: regular; dynamic enhanced MRI (DEMRI).
Sampling and biopsy
  • Sampling:
    • Hollow organs: {{c7::endoscopy::What do we do here procedure wise}}.
    • Cavities: {{c7::laparoscopy, mediastinoscopy, thoracoscopy::What do we do here procedure wise}}.
  • Biopsy:
    • FNAC → {{c8::cellular}} pathology.
    • Core needle biopsy → {{c8::histological}} diagnosis & tissue markers.
    • Incisional biopsy.
    • Excisional biopsy.
  • Decision-making: {{c9::multidisciplinary}} input essential; inappropriate biopsy method can compromise subsequent surgical therapy.
Additional aids to diagnosis
  • Frequency measurement of {{c10::cancer-specific genes}} can provide predictive and prognostic information and guidance for oncologists.
Staging and grading
  • Staging aids prognosis and treatment selection.
  • Clinical staging: pretreatment TNM.
  • Postsurgical histopathological staging: pTNM.
  • Histological grading (some cancers):
    • Grade 1: {{c11::well differentiated (low grade)}}.
    • Grade 2: {{c11::moderately differentiated (intermediate grade)}}.
    • Grade 3: {{c11::poorly differentiated / anaplastic (high grade)}}.
Prognosis
  1. Staging and grading.
  2. Cell-surface receptors: e.g., {{c12::lymphomas with immunoglobulin receptors respond better to chemotherapy}}.
  3. Cytoplasmic proteins: breast cancer with {{c13::ER (oestrogen receptor)}} positivity more likely to respond to hormonal measures; better prognosis.
  4. Lymphocytic infiltration: {{c14::sinus histiocytosis in regional lymph nodes associated with better prognosis (may represent host–tumour response)}}.

V) Management of cancer

Overview
  • Prophylaxis
    • Risk assessment: Genetic, Diseases, Environmental, Behavioural
    • Screening
  • Active treatment (MDT)
    • Surgery, Chemotherapy, Hormonal therapy, Radiotherapy, Photodynamic therapy, Immunotherapy, Biological (targeted) therapy, Gene therapy
  • Rehabilitation or palliative care ({{c1::different from end-of-life care::Is it different or not from end of life care}})
Prophylaxis after risk assessment
Cancer risk assessment
  • Genetic: Family history, hereditary cancer syndromes.
  • Diseases: Inflammatory bowel disease, hepatitis B and C.
  • Environmental: Physical or chemical carcinogens.
  • Life style: Smoking, alcohol, diet, exercise.
Prophylaxis types
  • Primary: {{c2::in an otherwise healthy person}}.
  • Secondary: those who have {{c2::premalignant conditions}}.
  • Tertiary: {{c2::avoiding second tumours in patients cured from their original disease}}.
Tools for prophylaxis
  • Avoid: Environmental pollution; harmful lifestyles.
  • Chemoprevention:
    • {{c3::Tamoxifen}} to prevent {{c4::breast cancer}}.
    • {{c5::Nicotinamide (vitamin B3)}} for {{c6::non-melanotic skin cancer}}.
    • {{c7::13-cis-retinoic acid}} to reverse {{c8::leucoplakia}}.
  • Risk reduction:
  • • Breast.
    • Gastrectomy.
    • Thyroidectomy.
    • Colectomy.
Current roles of surgery
  • Diagnostic
  • Curative
  • Palliative
  • Risk reduction (not prophylactic)
Risk reduction surgery examples
  • {{c9::Subcutaneous mastectomy}} in {{c10::BRCA-positive}} patients without evident disease.
  • {{c9::Total gastrectomy}} in {{c11::CDH1}} mutation carriers.
  • {{c9::Total thyroidectomy}} in {{c12::MEN2}}.
  • {{c9::Colectomy}} in {{c13::familial polyposis or ulcerative colitis}}.
Diagnostic roles of surgery
  • Biopsies for definite diagnoses: {{c14::LN in lymphomas}}.
  • Staging procedures: {{c15::Laparoscopy}} (gastric & pancreatic tumours), {{c15::mediastinoscopy}} (lung cancer).
  • Laparoscopic ultrasound: to avoid missing small {{c16::liver}} tumours.

Surgery with curative intent
  • {{c1::En-bloc}} resection. -> Tumor
  • {{c1::Lymphadenectomy}}: oesophageal, gastric, pancreatic, colorectal. -> nodes
  • {{c1::Sentinel lymph node biopsy}}: breast, melanoma. -> nodes
  • {{c1::Metastasectomy}}: hepatic metastases of colorectal cancer; pulmonary metastases from soft tissue sarcoma.-> Metastasis
En-bloc resection
  • {{c2::Removal of entire tumour and any adjacent structures/organs involved::What is it}}.
  • Aims: obtain {{c3::tumour-free margin → minimize local recurrence}}.
  • More aggressive/poorly differentiated cancers require {{c4::wider}} margins.
Metastasectomy — prerequisites
  1. Adequate {{c5::disease-free interval (time from initial treatment to recurrence)}}.
  2. {{c5::Organ of involvement}}.
  3. Ability to {{c5::render patient tumour-free with acceptable morbidity/mortality}}.
Palliative surgery (non-curative)
  • Provide {{c6::nutritional}} support.
  • Manage {{c6::complications}}.
  • {{c6::Vascular} }access for chemotherapy.
  • {{c6::Cytoreductive}} surgery.
Management of complications (palliative)
  • {{c7::Haemorrhage.
  • Obstruction.
  • Perforated viscus.
  • Pain due to nerve infiltration}}.

🧬 Lymphadenectomy

  • Definition: Surgical removal of multiple lymph nodes in a specific region.
  • Purpose: Done when cancer is known or strongly suspected to have spread.

🎯 Sentinel Lymph Node Biopsy

  • Definition: Removal of the first lymph node(s) that drain the tumor area.
  • Purpose: To check if cancer has started to spread.

Chemotherapy Settings
Setting Definition / Use Examples / Notes
Primary {{c1::Alone without additional therapies}} {{c2::Haematological malignancies: leukaemia, lymphoma}}
Adjuvant {{c1::After curative surgery but high risk of relapse}}
 e.g., {{c3::node-positive breast cancer}}
Neoadjuvant {{c1::Before surgery to downsize tumour → allow less radical operation or obtain clear margins}}

Chemotherapy Routes
Route Examples / Indications
{{c4::Oral}}
 Systemic
{{c4::IV}}
 Systemic
Regional — in spaces {{c5::Intraperitoneal chemotherapy (some GIT and ovarian tumours)}}
Regional — through blood supply {{c5::Isolated limb perfusion (extremity melanoma/sarcoma); Isolated hepatic artery infusion (hepatic metastases of colorectal cancer)}}

Hormonal therapy (for hormone-dependent cancers)

Cancer Surgical ablation Drugs
Breast cancer {{c6::Ovariectomy}}
 {{c6::Antioestrogen (tamoxifen); LHRH antagonist; Progestogens; Aromatase inhibitors (in cases with DVT)}}
Prostatic cancer {{c7::Orchiectomy}}
 {{c7::Antiandrogenic (flutamide); LHRH analogue; Stilbosterol}}

Radiotherapy

Setting Definition / Use Examples / Notes
Primary {{c8::Alone without additional therapies; preferred over surgery in laryngeal cancer, oropharyngeal cancer, basal cell carcinoma (face) for organ/tissue preservation}}
 {{c9::Seminoma, localized Hodgkin disease}}
Adjuvant {{c8::After breast lumpectomy to decrease local recurrence}}
 Some chemotherapeutics ({{c10::cisplatin, 5-fluorouracil}}) serve as radiosensitizers
Neoadjuvant {{c8::Before surgery to downsize tumour → less radical operation / clear margins}}
 e.g., {{c10::rectal adenocarcinoma}}
 
Radiotherapy Routes

Route Description / Examples
Systemic {{c11::Oral radioactive iodine}}
Teletherapy {{c11::External beam radiotherapy}}
Brachytherapy {{c11::Radiation source near/within target tissue (e.g., cervical, breast, prostate)}}

Photodynamic therapy (PDT)
  • Mechanism: {{c1::photosensitizer drug + light source → free radical formation (singlet oxygen) to destroy sensitized cells}}.
  • Singlet oxygen effects:
    a) {{c2::Destroy cells directly}}.
    b) {{c2::Destroy nearby blood vessels}}.
    c) {{c2::Activate immune system to attack affected tumour cells}}.
  • Uses:
    a) Surface lesions: {{c3::non-melanotic skin cancers, premalignant skin lesions (e.g., actinic keratosis)::Like in when}}.
    b) Symptom relief in {{c3::oesophageal cancer, non-small-cell lung cancer; precancerous mucosal lesions in Barrett’s oesophagus::Like in when}}.
Immunotherapy
Active
  • Non-specific: {{c4::BCG in superficial bladder cancer}}.
  • Specific: {{c4::Vaccines derived from tumour cells or antigen-presenting cells}}.
Passive
  • Cytokines: {{c5::Interferone alpha}} activates natural killer cells and dendritic cells.
Biologic (targeted) therapy
  • Concept: drugs/agents that block growth/spread by interfering with specific molecules in tumour growth.
  • Types (by mechanism):
    {{c6::a) Inhibit growth factor receptors (e.g., HER2 in some breast cancers).
    b) Inhibit intracellular signal transduction.
    c) Inhibit the cell cycle}}.
Gene therapy
  • Aim: alter {{c7::genetic program}} of cancer cells by introducing DNA/RNA.
  • Strategies:
    {{c8::a) Replace an altered tumour suppressor gene.
    b) Introduce genetic material to block tumour oncogene expression.}}

BCG refers to Bacillus Calmette-Guérin

🧪 What BCG Does

  • It’s a weakened strain of Mycobacterium bovis, originally developed as a tuberculosis vaccine.
  • When instilled directly into the bladder, BCG stimulates the immune system to attack cancer cells locally.

🧬 Role in Cancer Management

  • Listed under immunotherapy in the course material.
  • Used for non-muscle invasive bladder cancer, especially carcinoma in situ.
  • It’s not chemotherapy—it works by activating immune responses, not by directly killing cells.

Rehabilitation or palliative care for advanced malignancies

  • Therapeutic manipulations (local and/or systemic therapy to induce tumour regression):
    {{c1::• Radiotherapy, Chemotherapy.
    • Surgery: excision of a fungating tumour; management of dysphagia, intestinal obstruction, impending paraplegia.}}
  • Non-specific treatment (does not affect tumour growth but relieves symptoms):
    {{c1::• Pain, Dyspnoea, Lack of sleep.
    • Dysphagia (tube stenting), Moniliasis.
    • Vomiting.
    • Physiotherapy, Occupational and Speech therapy.}}
Non-therapeutic palliative care
  • Counselling & emotional support.
  • Nutritional support (advice & supplements).
  • Social support (patients, relatives, carers).
  • Financial support (entitlements, grants from charity).
  • Spiritual support.

End-of-life care

  • Patients treated palliatively may survive several years; end-of-life care concerns the last few months.
  • Problems specific to approaching death:
    a) Spiritual need.
    b) Profound fear.
    c) Specific needs of those facing bereavement.
  • Aim: patient should have a “good death”, preferably at home.

Remember

  1. Doctors and nursing staff must be prepared to {{c2::spend time to help the patient die with dignity}}.
  2. At least the relatives should be informed of the {{c2::inevitable outcome}}.
  3. A doctor’s attention must not cease after death. Words of sympathy from surgical staff can give great comfort to bereaved relatives.

children.name: ICC [146] History taking in patients presenting with a complaint related to the abdomen

fields:
History taking steps

Introductory
  1. Introduce yourself
  2. Personal and social history
Complaint
  1. {{c1::In his own words.
  2. Present history::Should i use his own words}}
Relevant histories
  1. Past history.
  2. Family history

Principles for achieving a provisional anatomic diagnosis

Categories
  • GIT symptoms (upper or lower)
  • Others
Patterns and clues
  • GIT (+ive):
    • {{c1::Masses}}
    • Symptoms related to obstruction or infection of the {{c2::urinary tract}}.
  • Hollow organs outside of the GIT (−ive):
    • {{c3::Masses}}
    • {{c4::Colicky}} pain
    • {{c5::Urologic::Where}} upper or lower symptoms
    • {{c5::Gynaecologic::Also where}} symptoms: discharge and bleeding
  • Solid organs outside of GIT (−ive):
    • {{c6::Masses}}
    • {{c7::Atypical}}pain, {{c7::Dragging or severe::What type of pain}} pain
    • Rupture {{c8::AAA}}
    • {{c8::Haemodynamic}}disturbance
  • Parietal (−ive):
    • {{c9::Masses}}
    • Diagnosed by {{c10::exclusion}}

Principles for achieving a provisional pathological diagnosis


Category Onset Course Duration
Congenital {{c1::Congenital}} {{c1::Insidious if not superficial}} {{c1::Stationary; rarely slowly progressive—}}
Acute inflammation {{c2::Rapid (along hours)}} {{c2::May be regressive}} {{c2::Short (acute presentation)}}
Haemorrhage, torsion and perforation {{c3::Sudden}} {{c3::Rapidly progressive if not managed}} {{c3::Short (acute presentation)}}
Chronic inflammation {{c4::Insidious}} {{c4::Stationary, sometimes increasing (acute exacerbation); regression may occur}}
 {{c4::Usually prolonged}}.
Neoplasia {{c5::Insidious}} {{c5::Progressive; it never regresses}} {{c5::According to the aggressiveness of the tumour}}

Introductory history taking (expanded)

  1. Introduce yourself and greet your patient: patients like to know to whom they are talking; Students should tell patients their name and explain why they are seeing them.
  2. Take the personal and social history: these data could be correlated with the patient complaint and might help the doctor to reach a provisional pathological diagnosis.

Personal and social history

  • Name (familiarise & document)
  • Demographic data
    • Age and sex (insight into pathology)
  • Occupation & residence (may give a clue to pathology)
  • Social
    1. Marital status
    2. Special habits: smoking, alcohol and illicit drugs (may give a clue to pathology)
{{c1::read}}

Generally, {{c3::anorexia nausua vomitting and weight loss}} are v v indicative of IGT

{{c1::altered bowel}} habits
are a huge red flag for lower GIT onset has to be smth frmo {{c2::3-6}} weeks

children.name: ICC [154] Homeostasis And Stress response to injury

fields:
Overview of the Stress Response Pathways
The response is initiated by neural, endocrine, and inflammatory pathways, often overlapping.
  • Inflammatory Response (Local and Systemic):
    • {{c1::Recruitment and activation of neutrophils, monocytes, macrophages, and lymphocytes.
    • Production of reactive oxygen and nitrogen intermediates.
    • Production of pro-inflammatory and anti-inflammatory cytokines.
    • Acute-phase changes in hepatic protein synthesis.
    • Upregulation of procoagulant activity.
    • Stimulates neural and endocrine responses}}.
  • Neural Response:
    • {{c2::Augments cardiovascular function via the autonomic nervous system.
    • Neuro-endocrine interaction: Activates the Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Adrenal Axis (HPAA).
    • Neuro-immune interaction}}.
  • Endocrine Response:
    • {{c3::Promotes glycogenolysis.
    • Catalyzes peripheral lean tissue and fat for gluconeogenesis.
    • Induces peripheral insulin resistance.
    • Endocrine-immune interaction: Cortisol depresses innate and adaptive immunity}}.
Clinical Note: Circulating mediators like adrenaline require functional receptors. Underlying conditions like {{c4::hypothyroidism (low thyroxine)}} or {{c4::renal failure (affecting cortisol metabolism)}} can impair the stress response, leading to shock.

Multi-Organ Failure (MOF) Origins 
  • Early Organ Failure: Results from {{c1::an inadequate response to stress (the systems fail to activate sufficiently)}}.
  • Late Organ Failure: Results from {{c1::an excessive response to stress (e.g., cortisol over-response knocks out the immune system, leading to secondary systemic infection)}}.
  • ICU Infections: Can originate from tubes or, most commonly, from {{c2::local Gut flora overgrowth due to loss of intestinal barrier function}}.

Specific Changes During the Stress Response
  1. {{c1::Pulse and Temperature Changes: Sympathetic-driven fever.
  2. Water and Salt Retention.
  3. Changes in Intermediary Metabolism.
  4. Changes in Blood Coagulation: Progresses from initial hypercoagulability to potential hypocoagulability}}.

Thermoregulation & Hormonal Release
  • Receptors: Core temperature detection occurs in the {{c1::preoptic area of the hypothalamus}}.
  • Stimuli:
  • 1) Alteration in ambient temperature eg.{{c2::hypothermia due to prolonged exposure of the viscera during open surgery}}
  • 2) Inadequate substrate supply eg {{c2::starvation}}.
  • 3) Loss of the normal thermal insulating barrier eg {{c2::burns}}
  • 4) Inadequate hepatic blood flow eg {{c2::hypovolaemia}}.
  • 5) Inadequate peripheral circulation VC or VD eg {{c2::sepsis}}
  • Effects of Stimulation: Leads to pituitary release of:
    • {{c3::Vasopressin (ADH)
    • ACTH → Cortisol + Aldosterone
    • TSH → Thyroxine}}
    • Also stimulates the adrenal medulla to release {{c3::catecholamines.}}

Water and Salt Retention Mechanisms
  • Vasopressin (ADH) from Posterior Pituitary:
    • Secreted by: {{c1::Cytokine stimulation, volume receptors in atria, osmoreceptors in anterior hypothalamus}}.
  • Aldosterone from Adrenal Cortex:
    • Secreted by: {{c2::CNS (via ACTH), Renin-Angiotensin System, direct effect of electrolyte concentration from the suprarenal}}

Changes in Intermediary Metabolism
  1. {{c1::Glycogen}}: Catabolized to increase blood glucose.
  2. {{c1::Fats}}: Catabolized to increase circulating glucose and Free Fatty Acids (FFAs), decreasing them in peripheral tissues.
  3. {{c1::Proteins}}: Catabolized to increase circulating glucose and amino acids for the immune response and healing. This leads to muscle mass loss, impacting functional recovery.
  • In {{c2::severe sepsis and burns}}, protein catabolism and energy expenditure are massively increased, fueled by intense free fatty acid oxidation.

Changes in Blood Coagulation Under Stress
  • {{c1::Hypercoagulability:
      • Increased platelet count and adhesiveness.
      • Increased fibrinogen.
    • Hypocoagulability:
      • 1) Increased fibrinolysins.
      • 2) Decreased fibrinogen}}

Initiation and Control of Inflammation
Initiation, maintenance, and termination involve numerous cell types and hundreds of humoral mediators. It is crucial to understand how the inflammatory response is both mobilized and controlled.
  • Neural Circuit: Localized injury messages are relayed to the {{c1::brain (Nucleus Tractus Solitarius)}}. The brain responds with {{c2::hormone release (ACTH, glucocorticoids)}} and {{c2::a sympathetic response}}.
  • Vagal Response: Rapidly induces {{c3::acetylcholine release at the injury site}} to curtail the inflammation from activated immunocytes. This is a real-time, site-specific anti-inflammatory mechanism.

17. Phases of the Stress Response
Sir David Cuthbertson (1930s) first described a biphasic response, later modified by Francis Moore (1970s).
  • Ebb Phase: Initial (<24 hours) {{c1::hypometabolic::Hypo or hyper metabolic}} phase. A coordinated response for immediate survival.
    • Features: Activation of {{c2::local coagulation/innate immunity}}. Systemic surge in plasma {{c2::catecholamines, cortisol, aldosterone}} → {{c3::tachycardia, tachypnea, vasoconstriction, ↓ cardiac output, ↓ O2 consumption, ↓ metabolic rate, Na+/H2O retention, blood shunting to vital organs, Acute-Phase Protein (APP) production::Clinically what does it look like}}
  • Flow Phase: If the patient survives, transitions to a hypermetabolic "flow" phase.
    • Catabolic Flow Phase: Characterized by explosive metabolic/immune activity for tissue repair and:
      • {{c4::Massive neuroendocrine flux (catecholamines, ADH, cortisol, insulin, glucagon, GH)}}.
      • Increased adrenergic stimulation increases {{c5::glucagon:insulin}} ratio → enhanced {{c6::proteolysis & lipolysis to fuel hepatic gluconeogenesis}}.
      • Proteolysis: Amino acids from skeletal muscle and visceral proteins catabolism are used by the {{c7::liver for gluconeogenesis and protein synthesis}} or used for {{c7::enzyme synthesis and collagen deposition at the site of injury}}
      • Energy needs of other tissues are met by {{c8::Free Fatty Acids (FFAs) and ketone bodies from lipolysis}}.
      • This increased lipolytic flux is marked by adipocyte glycerol release that is used as a glucose precursor by the liver, while the FFAs are available for {{c8::hepatic oxidation and energy production for these gluconeogenic processes}}
      • IE stuff that can use glucose uses glucose, stuff that can use FFA uses FFA
      • N.B --> Clearly this massive catabolic process requires augmented blood flow to the muscle, liver, and areas of tissue injury.
      • Clinical Signs: {{c9::Tachycardia, tachypnea, peripheral oedema, fever, hyperglycemia, leukocytosis, ↑ O2 consumption, ↑ CO2 production, ↑ minute ventilation, ↑ Resting Energy Expenditure (REE), negative nitrogen balance}}
      • All in All the liver  
        • Provides {{c10::substrates through gluconeogenesis and synthesis of ketone bodies}}, 
        • Detoxifies {{c10::nitrogenous}} waste via the synthesis of {{c11::urea}}
        • Elaborates a series of {{c10::APPs that bind metabolic by-products or limit the activity of proteolytic enzymes secreted by activated leukocytes}}.
      • Renal blood flow and glomerular filtration {{c12::increase}} and aid excretion of {{c12::nitrogenous}} by-products.
      • Cytokines and adipokines released from macrophages and adipose tissue, respectively, result in {{c13::disrupted capillary tight junctions}}, leading to {{c14::vascular leak allowing fluid and substrates to flow toward the avascular area of injury, as well as to the interstitium in other body parts}}

The Flow Phase

Clinical Manifestations and Compensatory Aims
The hypermetabolic state characteristic of the flow phase is a universal clinical finding in postoperative patients. It manifests through two primary compensatory mechanisms:
  1. {{c1::Fluid and Sodium Retention: The kidneys concentrate urine, leading to the retention of water and sodium}}.
  2. {{c1::Redistribution of Blood Flow: Preferential shunting of blood occurs towards vital organs (e.g., heart, brain)}}.
These physiological responses are adaptive measures designed to compensate for two key issues:
  • {{c2::Intravascular depletion secondary to widespread capillary leak}}.
  • {{c2::Possible external fluid losses incurred during the operative period (e.g., blood loss, insensible losses)}}.
If allowed to go unchecked, this catabolic response would deplete endogenous resources and become maladaptive.

{{c1::Systemic inflammatory response, severe metabolic depletion, and possible secondary infection::What}} can all cause damage to vital organs that were not initially compromised by the injury.

{{c2::Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), renal insufficiency, hepatic dysfunction, loss of gut epithelial barrier function, immunoparalysis, and sepsis::what}} may develop and the multiorgan dysfunction can be fatal.

Prognosis and Management
Fortunately, with appropriate support measures, the stress response nearly always {{c3::resolves itself without complications::What happen}}.

Anabolic Flow Phase

  • Timing & Transition
    • Intensity and duration correlate with extent/type of injury.
    • Catabolic process peaks at {{c1::48–72}} hours post-injury.
    • Transition to anabolic state occurs {{c1::5–10}} days post-injury if insult resolves.
  • Hormonal Changes
    • Surge in {{c2::insulin, growth hormone (GH), and insulin-like growth factor I (IGF-I)}}
  • Physiological Effects
    • Flux of {{c3::protein, fluid, and electrolytes}} into depleted intracellular space (esp. skeletal muscle).
    • Interstitial edema fluid {{c3::reabsorbed}}.
    • Excess fluid eliminated via {{c4::brisk diuresis}}.
    • Ion shifts: {{c5::K⁺, Mg²⁺, PO₄⁻}} move from blood into cells to restore electrochemical equilibrium.

Surgery and the Stress Response

General Features
  • Response extends beyond initial postoperative period.
  • Within {{c1::minutes}} of surgery: counter-regulatory hormones (cortisol, glucagon, catecholamines) rise.
  • Uncomplicated surgery: {{c2::short}}-lived endocrine response (24–48 hrs), initiating {{c3::protein catabolism}}.
  • Major surgery: {{c4::prolonged::What happens to}} catabolism (up to 1 month) due to proinflammatory cytokines:
    • TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6, IL-8
    • Act locally (injury site), via bloodstream, and CNS.
  • Cytokine imbalance between the pro and anti inflammatory contributes to:
    • {{c5::Anorexia
    • Pyrexia
    • Fatigue
    • Fat catabolism}}

Factors Initiating the Stress Response to Surgery

Intraoperative Factors
  • {{c1::Anaesthesia (hypotensive anaesthesia, drugs)
  • Emergency surgery (original disease, reperfusion injury)
  • Depletion of circulating volume::Give 3 examples}}
Postoperative Factors
  • {{c2::Pain
  • Inflammatory response (control of injury, prepare for healing)
  • Toxic factors}}

Factors Initiating the Stress Response to Surgery

Intraoperative Factors
  • Anaesthesia (hypotensive anaesthesia, drugs)
  • Emergency surgery (original disease, reperfusion injury)
  • Depletion of circulating volume
Postoperative Factors
  • Pain
  • Inflammatory response (control of injury, prepare for healing)
  • Toxic factors:
    • Endogenous
      • {{c1::Particulate: fat emboli, small platelet aggregations
      • Non-particulate (from injured/hypoxic tissue): ATP, Kinin, Myoglobin}}
    • Exogenous
      • {{c1::Endotoxin or exotoxin (initiate shock)}}

Why Surgical Patients Are Swayed Towards Catabolism

  1. Pre- and postoperative {{c1::starvation}}.
  2. Need for {{c1::wound healing (with/without infection) requiring catabolism of peripheral tissues}}.
  3. {{c1::Hormonal changes in intermediary}} metabolism provide the armamentarium for this catabolic shift.

Cytokines Post-Elective Surgery

  • Elective surgery with uneventful course: induces only minor systemic inflammatory changes.
  • Acute-phase response:
    • Proportional to tissue trauma or severity of procedure.
    • Inflammatory mediators (cellular, cytokines, APPs) peak at day {{c1::1–2}} post-injury.
    • Return to baseline by day {{c1::6–7}} post-injury.
  • {{c2::Persistent postoperative pain, stress, or a second insult}} will change that pattern.

Elective Surgery Emergency Surgery
Aim: reduce need for homeostatic response Aim: augment artificially the homeostatic response (resuscitation)
Achieved by: {{c1::minimal access surgery; stress-free perioperative care (enhanced recovery)}} Achieved by: {{c1::timely intervention to primary insult (e.g., drainage of abdominal collections); organ support (critical care) until homeostasis restored}}

How can infection initiate or modify the stress response to injury

Directly
 • Production of {{c1::endotoxin}}

Indirectly
 Secondary to changes in:
{{c1:: 1) Blood volume.
 2) Oxygen concentration.
 3) Substrate concentration.
 4) Pain .
 5) Cytokines released from inflammatory cells}}

What is Shock?

  • Definition : {{c1::Acute haemodynamic disturbance — systemic low tissue perfusion due to dysfunction of cardiovascular homeostatic mechanisms (relation between blood volume and vascular capacity) thus failing tomeet metabolic needs of the cell; initially reversible, becomes irreversible if tissue perfusion deficiency is prolonged or severe}}
  • Clinical syndrome due to {{c2::inadequate tissue perfusion unable to sustain normal metabolism/nutrition}} ⟶ impaired {{c2::cellular metabolism}} + serious pathophysiological abnormalities.
Key Point
  • Ischaemia = localized inadequate tissue perfusion.

Types of Shock

Normal Cardiovascular Homeostatic Mechanisms
  • Pump (heart) → {{c1::Cardiogenic}} shock.
  • Circulating fluid → {{c1::Hypovolaemic}} shock (cold shock).
  • Vessels → {{c1::Low resistance}} shock (warm shock).
  • Microcirculatory factors → {{c1::Irreversible}} shock

Causes of Major Shock Types

Cardiogenic Shock
  • Primary:
    • {{c2::Infarction.
    • Arrhythmia.
    • Myocardial depression}}
  • Secondary (Obstructive):
    • {{c2::Cardiac tamponade.
    • Pulmonary embolism}}.
Hypovolaemic Shock
  • Loss of:
    • {{c3::Blood.
    • Plasma.
    • Extracellular fluid}}.
Low Resistance (Distributive) Shock
  • Vasogenic (Septic): {{c4::endotoxin release + immune activation}}.
  • Neurogenic: {{c4::failure of sympathetic outflow (bradycardia)}}.
  • Anaphylactic: {{c4::histamine release (bronchospasm)
  • Vasodilation in both neurogenic and anaphalactic}}.
Important Reminder
  • {{c5::Hypovolaemia}}= most common form of shock.
  • Often component of other shock types.
  • Must be excluded or treated in all shock management.

Septic Shock

Definition
  • Combination of distributive shock + organ dysfunction from:
    • {{c1::Host inflammatory mediators (cytokines, complement).
    • Direct bacterial toxins (e.g., staphylococci, Gram-negative bacilli from colonic leak)}}.
Pathophysiology
  • {{c2::↓}} Oxygen usage (cardiorespiratory impairment, microcirculatory imbalance, mitochondrial dysfunction).
  • {{c2::Metabolic acidosis::ABG reading}}.
  • Multiple organ {{c2::dysfunction}}
  • Coagulation abnormalities:
    • {{c2::↓}} Anticoagulants (e.g., {{c5::alpha-1-antitrypsin}}).
    • {{c2::↑}} Procoagulants (e.g., {{c4::tissue factor}}).
    • Inhibition of {{c3::fibrinolysis}}.
  • Result: {{c6::disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC), microvascular occlusion, large vessel thrombosis, ischaemia}}
Phases of Septic Shock
  1. {{c7::Extensive vasodilatation ⟶ relative hypovolaemia (Hyperdynamic)}}.
  2. {{c7::Endothelial damage ⟶ ↑ capillary permeability, massive fluid leakage, hypotension with normal/high cardiac output (hypodynamic) }}.
  3. {{c7::Myocardial contractility depression (hypodynamic)}}.
Special Case: {{c8::Toxic Shock}} Syndrome
  • Associated with {{c9::staphylococcal/streptococcal}} infection.
  • Linked to {{c10::super-absorbent tampon}} use.

⚡ Hyperdynamic Stage (Early Phase)

  • When: Early in septic shock.
  • Why: Endotoxins and inflammatory mediators cause widespread vasodilation, leading to relative hypovolaemia.
  • Effect: The heart compensates by increasing cardiac output; CVP may also rise due to increased venous return.
  • Clinical signs: Warm, flushed skin; bounding pulses; high cardiac output but poor tissue oxygen extraction.

🧊 Hypodynamic Stage (Late Phase)

  • When: Later, if shock isn’t controlled.
  • Why: Ongoing inflammation leads to myocardial depression, capillary leak, and fluid loss into tissues.
  • Effect: Cardiac output and CVP drop; tissue perfusion worsens.
  • Clinical signs: Cold, clammy skin; weak pulses; low blood pressure; organ failure.

Special Types of Shock

Traumatic Shock
  • Combination of:
    • {{c1::Hypovolemia secondary to blood loss.
    • Activation of the inflammatory cascade by tissue injury, similar to septic shock}}
Endocrine Shock
  • Hypothyroidism
    • Similar to neurogenic shock due to:
      • {{c2::Disordered vascular and cardiac responsiveness to circulating catecholamines.
      • Associated cardiomyopathy}}
  • Thyrotoxicosis
    • Leads to {{c3::high-output heart failure}}.
  • Hypoadrenal Shock
    • Causes:
      • {{c4::Hypovolemia}}.
      • Poor response to {{c4::circulating and exogenous catecholamines}}.
      • Pre-existing {{c5::Addison’s}} disease, history of {{c5::steroid}} therapy, or relative adrenal insufficiency due to {{c5::systemic sepsis}}.

Pathophysiology of Shock

Pathways Leading to Decreased Tissue Perfusion
  1. Direct causes:
    • {{c1::Hemorrhage / Hypovolemia.
    • Cardiac failure.
    • Neurologic injury}}.
    • Decreased tissue perfusion and cellular injury can then result in immune and inflammatory responses
  2. Indirect causes:
    • {{c2::Infection: elaboration of microbial products (e.g., LPS = lipopolysaccharide).
    • Tissue injury: release of endogenous cellular products (e.g., HMGB1 = high mobility group box 1).
    • These products activate cells, influencing tissue perfusion and driving shock progression through RAGE = receptor for advanced glycation end product pathways}}

Central Role of Decreased Tissue Perfusion
  • Direct cause: from the initial shock aetiology
    (e.g., {{c1::hypovolemic, haemorrhagic, cardiogenic, neurogenic}}).
  • Secondary cause: from molecules or cellular products leading to endothelial and cellular activation
    (e.g., {{c1::septic shock, traumatic shock}}).
Causes of Reduced Tissue Perfusion by Shock Type
  • Cardiogenic Shock and Hypovolemic Shock:
    • Reduction in {{c2::cardiac output}}.
  • Septic Shock:
    • Inflammatory mediators cause:
      • {{c3::Decreased cellular oxygen uptake}}.
      • {{c3::Opening of precapillary arterio-venous shunts, which increases venous return}}.
      • Leads to ineffective increase in cardiac output during the {{c4::hyperdynamic (early compensated)}} stage leading to:
        • No preferential blood flow to vital organs (e.g., brain, heart).
        • Worsening cellular dysfunction.

The Vicious Cycle of Shock

  • Regardless of aetiology, decreased tissue perfusion triggers a {{c1::feed-forward}} loop:
    • Exacerbates cellular injury.
    • Leads to progressive tissue dysfunction.

Cellular Changes Due to Decreased Tissue Perfusion

  1. Oxygen Deprivation
    • Switch to anaerobic respiration → accumulation of {{c1::lactic}} acid.
  2. Local Effects
    • Hypoxia + Acidosis activate {{c2::complement and prime neutrophils}}.
    • Release of {{c2::oxygen free radicals and cytokines → capillary endothelial injury}}.
    • Microvascular changes worsen perfusion.
  3. Systemic Effects
    • Widespread underperfusion → {{c3::metabolic acidosis}}.
  4. Glucose Depletion
    • Once intracellular glucose is exhausted:
      • Anaerobic respiration {{c4::ceases}}.
      • Failure of {{c4::sodium-potassium}} pumps in cell membrane:
        • Leads to cell swelling and organelle dysfunction.
      • Intracellular lysosomes release autodigestive enzymes → {{c5::cell lysis}}.
      • Intracellular contents, including potassium, are {{c5::released into the bloodstream}}.

Compensatory Mechanisms for Metabolic Acidosis

  • Metabolic acidosis + sympathetic activation:
    • Increase {{c1::respiratory rate and minute ventilation}}.
    • Enhances {{c1::CO₂ excretion → produces a compensatory respiratory alkalosis}}.

Physiologic Responses to Shock

Major Types of Responses
  • {{c1::Neuroendocrine response}} (rapid).
  • {{c1::Metabolic}}response (slower but short-lived).
  • {{c1::Immune/Inflammatory}} signaling (slower, prolonged).
Body’s Defense Against Shock
  • {{c2::Nervous}}system (rapid, seconds).
  • {{c2::Endocrine}}system (second line).
  • {{c3::Inflammatory}}response (prolonged).

Afferent Impulses

Sources of Afferent Signals
  • Loss of {{c1::circulating blood volume}} (initial inciting event).
  • {{c1::Pain}}: from injured tissue → spinothalamic tracts → hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis + autonomic nervous system (ANS) → sympathetic stimulation of adrenal medulla → catecholamine release.
  • {{c1::Hypoxemia, hypercarbia, acidosis:: ABG, O2, CO2 levels}}.
  • {{c1::Infection, temperature}} changes.
  • Emotional {{c1::arousal}}.
  • {{c1::Hypo}}glycemia.
Effect of Afferent Impulses
  • Processed in CNS, activating reflexive efferent responses.
  • Goals of efferent responses:
    • {{c2::Expand plasma volume.
    • Maintain peripheral perfusion and tissue O₂ delivery.
    • Restore homeostasis}}

Compensatory Mechanisms for Extracellular Fluid Volume

  • Neural factors.
  • Endocrine factors.
  • Transcapillary refill.

Physiological Response to Reduced Tissue Perfusion (e.g., Hemorrhage)

Capillary Refill and Haemodilution
  • Increases {{c1::blood volume}}.
Initial Pressure-Related Phase (Sympathetic Effect)
  • Reduced {{c2::blood volume + arteriolar constriction → decreased capillary hydrostatic pressure → net reabsorption into capillaries}}
Overlapping Osmotic Phase (Hormonal + Cytokine-Mediated)
  • Mobilization of intracellular fluid due to increased extracellular osmolarity caused by:
    • {{c3::Hyperglycemia.
    • Soluble products of glycolysis, lipolysis, proteolysis (metabolic hormones)}}.

Neural Responses to Reduced Perfusion

Key Receptors
  • Stretch receptors: {{c1::right atrium::Where are they}}.
  • Baroreceptors: {{c1::aortic arch, carotid sinus}}.
  • Chemoreceptors: {{c1::aortic and carotid bodies (sensitive to O₂ tension, H⁺, CO₂)}}
Stimulus Afferent Pathway Effects of Activation
Baroreceptors in atria (low volume, mild haemorrhage) {{c2::Vagus}} nerve Decreased atrial pressure → reduced baroreceptor output → {{c3::disinhibition of ANS → ↑ sympathetic outflow from vasomotor centers → peripheral vasoconstriction::What happens to ANS, and vessels}}.
Baroreceptors in aortic arch & carotid sinus (larger volume/pressure drops) {{c2::Vagus}} nerve Same effect as atrial baroreceptors.
Chemoreceptors in aortic & carotid bodies (changes in O₂, H⁺, CO₂) {{c2::Vagus}} nerve {{c4::Coronary vasodilation, bradycardia, vasoconstriction in splanchnic & skeletal circulation}}.

Normal Cardiovascular Homeostasis

  • Majority of blood volume resides in {{c1::venous}} system at rest.
  • Venous return → ventricular {{c2::end-diastolic}} wall tension → major determinant of cardiac output.
  • Gravitational shifts corrected rapidly by {{c3::venous capacity}} changes.
  • Cardiac output usually depends on {{c4::preload::Which load, pre or after}}.
  • Preload effect influenced by:
    • {{c5::Coordinated atrial activity. Cuz coordinated atrial activity contributes 20-30% to ventricular filling
    • Tachycardia}}.
  • Sympathetic tone increases:
    • {{c6::Modest::What type of}} effect on skeletal muscle beds.
    • Strong reduction in {{c7::splanchnic}} blood volume (normally ~20% of total).

Blood Volume Homeostasis

Acute Response
  • Intravascular volume changes corrected by:
    • {{c8::Venous tone. (VC)
    • Systemic vascular resistance. (Arterial VC)
    • Intrathoracic pressure to inc venous return}}.
  • Hormonal changes less important early.
Kidney-Mediated Response
  • Normal circulating blood volume maintained by:
    • {{c9::Renal salt and water}} balance with external losses via systemic and local haemodynamic
    • Hormones: {{c9::renin, angiotensin, ADH}}.
  • These slower responses → maintain preload by {{c10::altering blood volume}}.

Direct Neural Sympathetic Responses

On the Heart (Pump)
  • Hemorrhage → {{c1::↓ venous return → ↓ cardiac output}}.
  • Compensation:
    • {{c2::↑}} HR and contractility (β₁-adrenergic stimulation).
    • Venous + arterial vaso{{c2::constriction}}
  • Side effect: {{c3::↑}} myocardial O₂ consumption → risk of dysfunction if O₂ supply not maintained.
On Peripheral Vessels
  1. Arterioles (α₁-adrenergic)
    • Vaso{{c4::constriction → ↑ systemic vascular resistance and BP::Also mention the systemic effect}}.
    • But it is not uniform as:
      • {{c5::Blood shunted from non-essential organs (intestine, kidney, skin).
      • Autoregulation preserves flow to brain and heart}}.
  1. Veins
    • Veno{{c6::constriction → ↓ capacitance → accelerates venous return::Also mention the systemic effect}}.

Catecholamine/Neural Surge

  • ↑ Sympathetic output → adrenal medulla releases catecholamines.
  • Peak: {{c7::24–48}} hours post-injury.
  • Return to baseline unless {{c8::noxious afferent stimuli persist}}.
  • Sources:
    • Epinephrine: {{c9::adrenal medulla}}.
    • Norepinephrine: {{c9::sympathetic synapses}}.

Neurohormonal and Endocrine Response to Hypovolemia

  • Trigger: Hypovolemia → ↓ arterial baroreceptor activity → CNS response.
Pathways:
  • Rapid response
    • {{c1::↑ Sympathetic activity → release of epinephrine & norepinephrine
    • Peripheral vasoconstriction
    • ↑ Cardiac activity}}
  • Sustained response
    • {{c2::↓ Renal blood flow & ↑ sympathetic activity → release of renin, angiotensin, aldosterone → arterial vasoconstriction + Na/H₂O retention}}
    • {{c2::Pituitary release of ACTH & AVP (arginine vasopressin) → ↑ cortisol, Na/H₂O retention}}

Endocrine Response

  • Renin-Angiotensin System
    • Triggered by {{c3::↓ renal artery perfusion}}, {{c3::β-adrenergic}} stimulation, and {{c3::↓ tubular sodium}}.
    • Renin → converts angiotensinogen (liver) → Angiotensin I → (ACE, lungs) → Angiotensin II.
    • Angiotensin II effects:
      • {{c4::Potent vasoconstrictor of splanchnic & peripheral beds.
      • Stimulates secretion of aldosterone, ACTH, ADH}}.
    • Aldosterone: promotes {{c5::Na⁺ reabsorption, water retention, K⁺ and H⁺ excretion}}.
  • Hypothalamic Response
    • Cortisol works with epinephrine & glucagon in catabolism:
      {{c6::a. ↑ Gluconeogenesis & insulin resistance → hyperglycemia.
      b. ↑ Muscle protein breakdown → gluconeogenic substrates.
      c. ↑ Lipolysis → NEFA & glycerol → hepatic gluconeogenesis.
      d. Retains Na⁺ & water in kidneys}}.
    • Severe hypovolemia: {{c7::ACTH secretion independent of cortisol feedback::Is the ACTH dep or indep of negative feedback}}.
  • ADH (Vasopressin)
    • Released from pituitary in response to {{c8::hypovolemia, baroreceptor activity, ↑ plasma osmolality}}.
    • Actions:
      • Acts on {{c9::distal tubule & collecting duct → ↑ water reabsorption → ↑ water retention}}.
      • Potent {{c9::mesenteric vasoconstrictor → shunts blood from gut during shock → risk of ischemia, bacterial translocation}}.
      • {{c9::↑}} Hepatic gluconeogenesis & glycolysis.

Endocrinal Response

  • In septic states, endotoxin directly stimulates {{c10::arginine vasopressin (but its not that relevant in terms of VC)}} secretion, independent of:
    • Blood pressure
    • Osmotic changes
    • Intravascular volume changes
  • {{c11::Pro::pro or anti}}inflammatory cytokines also contribute to arginine vasopressin release.
  • Patients on chronic ACE inhibitor therapy:
    • At higher risk of {{c12::hypotension and vasodilatory shock}} during open-heart surgery.
    • These patients have {{c13::low}} plasma levels of arginine vasopressin confirmed.
Endocrinal Factors summary
  • {{c14::Catecholamines}}
    • Pump
    • Vasomotor tone
  • {{c14::ADH (Vasopressin)}}
    • Water reabsorption
    • Vasoconstriction in severe hemorrhage
  • {{c14::Renin–Angiotensin System}}
    • Renin → Angiotensin I → Angiotensin II
    • Vasoconstriction within 20 minutes
    • Sodium and water retention
    • Direct action on kidney
    • Aldosterone secretion
  • {{c14::Metabolic Hormones}}
    • Anti-insulin hormones: ACTH, cortisol, GH, glucagon
    • Cortisol sensitizes cells to catecholamines
    • Catecholamines inhibit insulin

3) Inflammatory Response
  • Cellular dysfunction due to shock → systemic inflammatory response.
    • Dangerous positive feedback cycle → multiorgan failure, jeopardizing survival.
  • Types of shock:
    • {{c1::Hypovolaemic, cardiogenic, neurogenic}} → decreased tissue perfusion → systemic inflammation.
    • {{c1::Septic, traumatic}} → systemic inflammatory response directly, then via cellular dysfunction.
  • Phases of Systemic Inflammatory Response Syndrome (SIRS):
    • {{c2::Proinflammatory phase
      • Restore tissue function
      • Eradicate invading microorganisms
    • Anti-inflammatory (counter-regulatory) phase
      • Prevent excessive proinflammatory activity
      • Restore homeostasis}}

Microcirculation Homeostasis
  • Microvascular circulation regulates cellular perfusion, significantly affected in shock.
  1. Sympathetic innervation → profound effect on larger arterioles.
    • {{c1:: Usually in Hemorrhage → vasoconstriction.
    • Sepsis/neurogenic shock → vasodilation::What happens, VC or VD}}.
  2. Vasoactive proteins: vasopressin, angiotensin II, endothelin-1
    • {{c2::Vasoconstriction limits perfusion to skin, skeletal muscle, kidneys, GI tract
    • Preserves perfusion of myocardium and CNS::Describe it perfusion wise}}
  3. Endothelial dysfunction in shock
    • Capillary {{c3::leak}}
    • Intracellular {{c3::swelling (Multifactorial but Majorly Na⁺/K⁺ pump dysfunction)}}
    • Extracellular {{c3::fluid deficit (↓ capillary hydrostatic pressure cause of the changes in blood volume, ↑ fluid uptake)}}
  4. Capillary bed flow becomes heterogeneous due to:
    • {{c4::Endothelial swelling
    • Endothelial dysfunction
    • Endothelial activation (recruitment of leukocytes, platelets)}}
    • → Results in diminished capillary perfusion, which may persist even after resuscitation.
  5. No-Reflow Phenomenon
    • {{c5::Endothelial dysfunction + inflammatory mediator}} activation in {{c6::septic or traumatic}} shock causes:
      • {{c7::Enhanced endothelial swelling
      • Capillary leak
      • Leukocyte adherence → capillary occlusion}}
    • This leads to no-reflow (capillaries fail to reperfuse after resuscitation).
    • Consequences:
      • {{c8::Ongoing ischaemic injury
      • Release of further inflammatory cytokines
      • Compounded tissue injury}}
    6) Experimental Evidence
    • Animal studies:
      • Neutrophil depletion in haemorrhagic shock → fewer capillaries with no-reflow.
      • Associated with lower mortality.

Pathophysiology of Shock (Microvascular Changes)

1) Capillary Endothelial Injury

  • {{c1::Leaky capillaries → tissue oedema → exacerbates cellular hypoxia → ↑ lactic acid production::What are the two main things that happen}}.
  • Leads to further activation of:
    • {{c1::Immune system
    • Coagulation system → Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC)}}.

2) Ischaemia–Reperfusion Syndrome

  • Trigger: {{c2::Systemic hypoperfusion}}.
During hypoperfusion:
  1. Accumulation of:
    • {{c3::Acid load
    • Potassium load}}
  2. Activation of cellular & humoral elements:
    • {{c4::Complement system
    • Neutrophils
    • Microvascular thrombi}}
When circulation is restored (reperfusion):
  • “Flush back” of accumulated products into systemic circulation →
    1. {{c5::Direct myocardial depression + vascular dilatation → worsens hypotension}}.
    2. {{c5::Endothelial injury in distant organs → Multi-Organ Failure Syndrome (MOFS)::How does it affect heart and endothelium}}.
⚠️ Remember: Reperfusion injury can only be attenuated by {{c6::reducing extent & duration of tissue hypoperfusion}}.

n.b. This is different to no reflow..

3) Haemorrhagic Shock vs Septic Shock

Haemorrhagic Shock
  • Correction of haemodynamic parameters + restoration of O₂ delivery → leads
    to restoration of:
    • Tissue O₂ consumption
    • Tissue O₂ levels
Septic Shock
  • Even after correction of haemodynamics & O₂ delivery:
    • {{c7::Regional tissue dysoxia often persists}}.
  • Causes:
      • {{c8::Heterogeneous impairment of the microcirculation (cause of the endothelial damage)
        • Intraparenchymal shunting.
      • Impaired tissue parenchymal cell oxidative phosphorylation (cause of endotoxins)
        • Failure of mitochondrial O₂ consumption → unresolved dysoxia}}.

Clinical Recognition of Shock

  • Shock = Clinical Syndrome

Key Clinical Features

  • {{c1::Apprehension & irritability → due to brain hypoperfusion (may be late).
  • Pulse and BP changes → reflect cardiac output changes.
  • Urinary output → important indicator of generalized tissue perfusion.
  • Skin temperature & colour → reflect peripheral resistance.
  • Venous filling & nail bed colour → reflect peripheral blood flow::List the effects on brain heart urine skin and nail bed color}}.

Early Recognition Systems

  • Developed to detect patients at risk of deterioration early.
  • Aim to emulate the simplicity, reliability, and clinical value of the Glasgow Coma Scale.
  • Based on routinely recorded physiological data.
  • Most are modifications of the Early Warning Score (EWS).
  • Extremely useful for spotting high-risk patients needing urgent medical attention.
  • Have become standard of care for surgical patients.
