Stage Features
Compensated (≤15% blood volume loss) • {{c1::Adequate central blood volume and perfusion to brain, lungs.• Occult hypoperfusion: metabolic acidosis despite normal urine output and vitals.• Manifestations: tachycardia, cool periphery.• If prolonged >12h without correction → MOF (multi-organ failure) + ischaemic reperfusion syndrome (gut, muscle beds)}}.
Decompensated – Mild • {{c1::Mild anxiety.• Tachycardia, tachypnea.• BP maintained, ↓ pulse pressure.• Mild oliguria.• Cool, sweaty extremities.• Prolonged capillary refill}}.
Decompensated – Moderate (30–40% volume loss) • {{c1::Drowsy, mildly confused.• More tachycardia.• BP falls.• Oliguria}}.
Decompensated – Severe • {{c1::Unconscious, laboured breathing.• Profound tachycardia.• Profound hypotension.• Urine output = 0}}.

Principles of shock treatment

Step Approach
1 {{c1::Secure airway}}
2 {{c1::Ensure adequate ventilation}}
3 {{c1::Control haemorrhage in bleeding patient}}
4 {{c1::Restore vascular volume & tissue perfusion}}

Management of Shock

Changing Concepts in Management

  • As resuscitation strategies evolved, survival of patients with haemorrhage improved → new challenges of sustained shock emerged.
  • Vietnam War experience:
    • Aggressive resuscitation with red blood cells + crystalloid solution or plasma → patients survived who previously would have died of haemorrhagic shock.
    • Renal failure became less frequent.
    • New complication: {{c1::acute fulminant pulmonary failure}}:
      • Early cause of death after apparently successful haemorrhage control surgery.
      • Initially termed Da Nang lung / shock lung.
      • Later recognized as Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS).
      • Led to new methods of prolonged mechanical ventilation.
  • Current concept: ARDS is part of the spectrum of multiple organ system failure (MOSF).

Principles of Treatment

Haemorrhagic / Hypovolaemic Shock
  • Mainstay: {{c2::volume resuscitation with blood products}}.
  • In haemorrhagic shock → timely bleeding control is essential, outcome-determining.
  • Prevent:
    • {{c3::Hypothermia
    • Acidaemia
    • Coagulopathy}}
  • Lethal triad of death in trauma: {{c4::hypothermia + acidaemia + coagulopathy}}.
Septic Shock
  • Mainstay:
    • {{c5::Fluid resuscitation.
    • Early appropriate antibiotic therapy}}.
    • Source control:
      • {{c6::Drainage of infected collections.
      • Removal of infected foreign bodies.
      • Débridement of devitalized tissues}}.

Resuscitation

  • {{c7::Do not::Do or do not}}delay resuscitation to make a definitive diagnosis.
  • Timing/nature depends on shock type + timing/severity of insult.
  • Rapid clinical exam often provides sufficient initial clues.
  • If cause uncertain:
    • Assume {{c8::hypovolaemia}}.
    • Start fluid resuscitation.
    • Assess response via monitoring.

Monitoring

Why
  1. {{c1::Detect response to therapy}}.
    • {{c1::Prevent complications of hypovolaemia:
      • Pump failure.
      • MODF (multi-organ dysfunction/failure) syndrome}}.
How
  • Non-invasive (if fit & responsive to challenge):
    • {{c2::Clinical exam.
    • Urinary catheterization.
    • ECG.
    • Pulse oximetry}}.
  • Invasive (if {{c3::old, cardiac disease, moderate/severe shock, or mild shock non-responsive}}):
    • {{c3::CVP.
    • Invasive BP.
    • Blood gases.
    • Swan-Ganz catheter (cardiac output + mixed venous O₂ saturation)}}.

Resuscitation of Shock — Fluid Challenge

Mild Shock (750–1500 ml blood loss in adult)
  • 2 L lactated Ringer’s challenge.
  • Responses:
    • Definite improvement → Responders.
    • Moderate/transient improvement → Transient responders.
      • Suspect:
        • {{c4::Continuing moderate fluid loss (haemorrhage or fluid shifts).
        • Myocardial insufficiency.
        • Inadequate replacement}}.
    • Deterioration → Non-responders.
      • In trauma suspect:
        • {{c4::Tension pneumothorax
        • Cardiac tamponade}}

Moderate & Severe Shock
  • 2 L lactated Ringer’s challenge while awaiting blood transfusion.
  • Responses:
    • Definite improvement + low CVP → Continued losses → {{c5::Exploration}}.
    • No improvement + rising CVP → “The Killers”:
      • {{c5::Tension pneumothorax.
      • Cardiac tamponade.
      • Cardiac failure}}.

Timing of Surgical Intervention

Actively Bleeding Patients
  • Proceed rapidly to {{c1::surgery }}after initial resuscitation.
  • Rationale:
    1. High-volume fluids → ↑ BP → ↑ bleeding (permissive hypotension preferred).
    2. Fluid therapy → cools patient + dilutes coagulation factors.
Hypovolaemia from Third-Space Loss (bowel obstruction) & Sepsis
  • Surgery adds injury + hypovolaemia.
  • Risks:
    • Exacerbates inflammatory activation.
    • Increases incidence & severity of end-organ insult.
  • → Ensure {{c1::adequate resuscitation before surgery}}.

Endpoints of Resuscitation

  • Use physiologic {{c2::parameters + organ perfusion/tissue oxygenation}} markers.
  • {{c3::Occult}} hypoperfusion:
    • Normal vitals but continued underperfusion.
    • Inflammation + coagulation ongoing.
    • Risk of {{c4::ischaemic reperfusion}} injury because we are not aware of the ischaemia.
  • Traditional endpoints (may be misleading):
    • Normal pulse.
    • Normal BP.
    • Normal urine output.
    • These reflect organs preserved until late (brain, lungs, kidneys).
  • Global perfusion endpoints:
    1. {{c5::Base deficit
    2. Lactate
    3. Mixed venous O₂ saturation}}

Vasopressor and inotropic support.

  • Septic shock → vasodilation → give {{c1::noradrenaline (vasopressor)}}.
  • Cardiogenic shock → weak pump → give {{c2::dobutamine (inotrope)}}.
  • Hypovolaemic shock → empty tank → {{c3::fluids first, NOT inotropes/pressors (they worsen ischemia).}} because Inotropes without adequate preload can lead to irreversible shock(decreased coronary perfusion in diastole and depletion of myocardial stores).

children.name: ICC [177] Insights in Vascular Anatomy and Physiology

fields:
• Blood is a fluid connective tissue that circulates through the arteries to reach the body’s tissues and then returns to the heart through the veins.
 • When blood is “spun down” in a centrifuge tube, the RBCs precipitate to the bottom of the tube, where they account for about {{c1::45}}% of the blood volume. This is called the haematocrit and normally ranges from 40% to 50% in males and {{c1::35}}% to {{c1::45}}% in females.
 • The next layer is a “buffy coat,” which makes up slightly less than {{c2::1}}% of the blood volume and includes {{c2::WBCs (leukocytes) and platelets.}}

 • The remaining 55% of the blood volume is the plasma and includes {{c3::water, plasma proteins, clotting factors, and various solutes (serum is plasma with the clotting factors removed).}}

• Determination of {{c1::right atrial::Which part of the heart}} pressure is important in the management of the critically ill patient; this can be measured by a catheter introduced into the internal jugular or subclavianvein via the superior vena cava into the right atrium.

 Obstruction of the superior vena cava by a tumour leads to diversion of its venous blood into {{c2::subcutaneous veins of the chest wall and, via them, into veins of the anterior abdominal wall. From there, the diverted blood drains into the inferior vena cava and azygos system of veins}}

• Lymphatic vessels transport lymph from everywhere in the body major 
lymphatic channels.
 • The majority of lymph (about 75-80%) ultimately collects in the {{c1::thoracic}}
 duct for delivery back to the venous system (joins the veins at the union of 
the left internal jugular and left subclavian veins)
 • A much smaller right lymphatic duct drains the right upper quadrant of the 
body lymphatics to a similar site on the right side.
 • Along the route of these lymphatic vessels, encapsulated lymph nodes are
 strategically placed to “filter” the lymph as it moves toward the venous
 system. Lymph nodes form a key site for phagocytosis of microorganisms
 and other particulate matter, and they can initiate the body’s immune
 responses.

Myocardial circulation from branches of the ascending aorta

The branches of the {{c1::ascending::which part of the}} aorta are the coronary vessels.

The right coronary artery arises from the ascending aorta and generally supplies the {{c2::right atrium, most of the right ventricle, the diaphragmatic surface of the left ventricle, part of the atrioventricular septum, the sinuatrial node (60% of the time), and the atrioventricular node (80% of the time)}}.

The left coronary artery also arises from the ascending aorta, and its branches usually supply {{c2::the left atrium, most of the left ventricle, some of the right ventricle, most of the interventricular septum, the sinuatrial node (40% of the time), and the atrioventricular node (20% of the time).}}

The {{c3::coronary sinus}} is the largest vein draining the heart. It receives blood from the great, middle, and small cardiac veins and drains into the right atrium

Coronary veins of the heart – those lying posteriorly are shown by {{c4::dotted}} lines. The coronary sinus drains into the right atrium near to the opening of the inferior vena cava

{{c1::True, Although anastomoses exist between the two coronary arteries, sudden occlusion of a major branch may result in ischaemia and death of some heart muscle (myocardial infarction), and if the area affected includes the conducting system or is large, the patient may die:: T or F, Although anastomoses exist between the two coronary arteries, sudden occlusion of a major branch may result in ischaemia and death of some heart muscle (myocardial infarction), and if the area affected includes the conducting system or is large, the patient may die.}}

Lesser degrees of ischaemic damage diminish the heart’s work capacity, and pain (angina) may be felt on exertion.

Narrowing of the coronary arteries may, by diminishing blood supply to the cardiac muscle, also cause angina, a pain usually felt in the substernal region. Ischaemia of the myocardium stimulates nerve endings within it. Sensory impulses are carried,largely on the left side, in the sympathetic branches to the thoracic segments T1–T4. Pain arising in the heart is felt substernally but is often referred to the left arm and neck.

•Clinical: Forty percent to 50% of cases of coronary artery occlusion lead to cardiac ischaemia and potential myocardial infarction in the {{c2::proximal portion of the left anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery::Which part in the cornoary circ}}

Clinical
 The most common cause of subarachnoid haemorrhage (bleeding into the subarachnoid space) is the rupture of a {{c1::saccular,or berry, aneurysm of one of the arteries of the cerebral and brainstem circulation.}}

 Berry aneurysms commonly occur at artery branch points, with about 85% occurring between the anteriorcerebral, internal carotid, and middle cerebral branches.

Regional Specializations (Slide 18)

  • Aorta:
    • Elastic tissue to handle pulsatile force → recoil & forward flow
    • Loss → {{c2::aneurysm}}
  • Muscular arteries/arterioles:
    • Concentric smooth muscle → regulate caliber, flow, BP
  • Capillaries:
    • Single endothelial layer + sparse pericytes → diffusion of O₂ and nutrients
  • Veins:
    • Thin media, high capacitance
    • Stasis in dilated bed → thrombus

Blood Vessels (Slides 20–23)

  • Arteries carry blood away; veins carry blood back
  • Arteries = more smooth muscle, major vascular resistance
  • Veins = ~64% of blood volume, capacitance vessels
  • Pulse points: superficial arteries over bone
    • Most distal: {{c1::dorsalis pedis (foot), posterior tibial (ankle)}}

Myocardial Circulation

Coronary Arteries (Slide 41)

  • Right coronary artery (RCA):
    • From {{c1::ascending aorta}}
    • Supplies {{c1::RA, most of RV, diaphragmatic LV surface, part of AV septum}}
    • {{c1::SA node (60%), AV node (80%)::Which nodes does it supply}}
  • Left coronary artery (LCA):
    • From {{c2::ascending aorta}}
    • Supplies {{c2::LA, most LV, some RV, most of interventricular septum}}
    • {{c2::SA node (40%), AV node (20%)::Which nodes does it supply}}
  • Venous drainage:
    • {{c3::Coronary sinus (largest), drains great, middle, small cardiac veins → RA}}

Coronary Veins (Slide 42)

  • Posterior veins shown by dotted lines
  • {{c1::Coronary}} sinus drains into RA near opening of IVC

Clinical Notes — Coronary Circulation (Slides 43–44)

  • Anastomoses exist between coronary arteries, but sudden occlusion → {{c2::ischaemia & myocardial infarction}}
  • Severe damage → arrhythmia, death
  • Lesser damage → ↓ work capacity, angina (on exertion)
  • Coronary narrowing → angina (substernal pain, referred to left arm & neck)
    • Pain via sympathetic nerves (T1–T4)
Clinical fact:
  • 40–50% of coronary artery occlusions → {{c3::ischaemia & potential MI in the proximal portion of the left anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery.}}

Branches of the Aortic Arch (Slide 50)

  1. {{c1::Right brachiocephalic artery → right subclavian + right common carotid
  2. Left common carotid artery
  3. Left subclavian artery}}
Clinical:
  • Area just distal to left subclavian origin = frequent site of:
    • {{c2::Blunt traumatic rupture (deceleration injuries)
    • Aortic dissection (hypertension)}}

Clinically (Slide 56)

  • The branches of the external carotid arteries anastomose across the {{c1::midline neck (superior thyroid arteries)}} and the face to provide collateral circulation should the arterial blood supply be compromised by occlusion or lacerated in trauma.
  • The small branches of the {{c2::superficial temporal}} artery supply the scalp, which bleeds profusely when cut because the small arteries are held open (rather than retracted into the subcutaneous tissue) by the tough connective tissue lying just beneath the skin (epidermis and dermis
Transient ischemic attack is the less than {{c1::24}}hrs, usually {{c1::15 minutes to 1 hour}}

Clinical (Slide 62)

  • The most common cause of subarachnoid haemorrhage (bleeding into the subarachnoid space) is the rupture of a {{c1::saccular, or berry,}} aneurysm of one of the arteries of the cerebral and brainstem circulation.
  • Berry aneurysms commonly occur at artery branch points, with about 85% occurring between the anterior cerebral, internal carotid, and middle cerebral branches.

Clinical (Slide 65)

  1. The small bronchial arteries (usually {{c1::1}} right and {{c1::2}} left arteries) are the nutritive arteries, delivering essential nutrients and oxygen to the pulmonary visceral pleura, bronchi and bronchial glands, connective tissue of the lungs, and walls of the larger intrapulmonary arteries.
  2. The {{c2::posterior intercostal}} arteries supply the spinal cord, especially at the second and eleventh space. These vessels may be jeopardized in cases of aortic dissection or surgery for aortic aneurysms.

The abdominal aorta (Slide 66)

  • Suprarenal:
    • Starts at the aortic hiatus in the diaphragm
    • Branches:
      1. {{c1::Anterior branches (visceral GIT)
      2. Lateral branches (parietal)}}
  • Infrarenal:
    • Ends by dividing into two common iliac branches
    • Branches:
      1. {{c1::Anterior branch (visceral, inferior mesenteric)
      2. Lateral branch (parietal, gonadal)
      3. Posterior branch (parietal)}}

Foregut Midgut Hindgut
Extension Lower oesophagus, stomach till the middle of the second part of duodenum below the ampulla of Vater From the middle of the second part of the duodenum, rest of small intestine, right colon till the junction of the right two third of the transverse colon From the end of the midgut to the anus
Arterial blood supply {{c1::Coeliac trunk that divides to left gastric, common hepatic and splenic arteries. Lymphatics follow the arterial drainage back to the main trunks originating from the aorta}} {{c2::Superior mesenteric artery. Lymphatics follow the arterial drainage back to the main trunks originating from the aorta}} {{c3::Inferior mesenteric artery. Lymphatics follow the arterial drainage back to the main trunks originating from the aorta}}
Venous drainage {{c1::To the portal vein direct or through the splenic vein. Portosystemic anastomosis at lower end of the oesophagus}} {{c2::Superior mesenteric vein that forms the portal vein after joining the splenic vein}} {{c3::Inferior mesenteric vein joins the splenic vein. At the rectum anastomosis with tributaries of the middle rectal vein proceeding towards the lateral pelvic wall as well as tributaries of the inferior rectal veins another site of portosystemic anastomosis — rectal varices}}

The liver parenchyma (Slide 77)

  • Organized in hexagones where hepatocytes modify contents of arterial and portal blood.
  • In each corner is a hepatic triad consisting of distal portal vein, hepatic artery, and proximal bile ductule.
  • The arrows indicate the direction of flow.
  • This counter-current system allows for hepatocytes to absorb substances for modification from the blood and excrete metabolites along with bile constituents into the bile ductule.
  • Excess fluid in the {{c1::space of Disse}} beneath endothelial cells drains into the lymphatic ducts.

Hepatic portal system (Slide 78)

  • Venous blood from the digestive tract, pancreas, gallbladder, and spleen enters the inferior surface of the liver through the {{c3::large hepatic portal}} vein.
  • This vein then ramifies like an artery to distribute blood to small endothelial-lined hepatic sinusoids, which form the vascular exchange network of the liver.
  • After passing through the sinusoids, the blood collects in a number of short hepatic veins, which drain into the inferior vena cava just before the inferior vena cava penetrates the diaphragm and enters the right atrium of the heart.
  • Normally, vascular beds drained by the hepatic portal system interconnect, through small veins, with beds drained by systemic vessels, which ultimately connect directly with either the {{c2::superior or inferior vena cava}}.

Comment (Slide 83)

  • The subclavian artery is divided into 3 parts relative to the anterior scalenus muscle.
    • 1st part: medial to the muscle
    • 2nd part: behind the muscle
    • 3rd part: lateral
  • Branches of the subclavian artery include:
    • Vertebral artery
    • Internal thoracic (mammary) artery
    • Thyrocervical trunk
    • Costocervical trunk
    • Dorsal scapular artery
  • Vertebral artery: ascends through the C6–T1 transverse foramina, enters the foramen magnum.
  • Internal thoracic: descends parasternally.
  • {{c1::Thyrocervical}} trunk: supplies the thyroid gland (inferior thyroid artery), lower neck (transverse cervical artery), and dorsal scapular region (suprascapular artery).
  • {{c1::Costocervical}} trunk: supplies the deep neck (deep cervical artery) and several intercostal spaces (supreme intercostal artery).
  • {{c1::Dorsal scapular}} branch: inconstant; may arise from transverse cervical artery.

Clinical (Slide 84)

  • The branches of the subclavian artery anastomose with:
    1. Branches of the {{c2::axillary}} artery around the shoulder joint
    2. Branches of the {{c2::thoracic aorta}}
    3. (intercostal branches) along the rib cage
    4. Across the midline of the neck and face via branches from both {{c3::external carotid arteries}}
    5. With the {{c4::internal carotid}} arteries and the {{c4::vertebral}} branches (circle of Willis on the brainstem)
  • These interconnections are important if the vasculature in one region is compromised.

Forearm Arteries (Slide 87)

  • The radial artery is the continuation of the brachial and thus common site of upper limb embolization.
  • The {{c1::interosseous}} arteries represent the axial artery of the upper limb.

Radialis indices artery (Slide 88)

  • The radialis indices artery is the site of small size embolization that could lead to a {{c1::blue finger}} syndrome.

What helps venous return to the heart (Slide 93)

Site of veins
  • Superficial system in the limbs:
    1. {{c1::Fasciomuscular pump direct the blood from the superficial to the deep system
    2. Valves prevent retrograde flow under the effect of gravity}}
  • In torso:
    1. {{c1::Negative intrathoracic pressure
    2. Valves prevent retrograde flow}}
  • Deep system in the limbs:
    1. {{c1::Intimate relation of the venae comitantes to the pulsating artery
    2. Valves prevent retrograde flow}}

Clinically (Slide 94)

  • Superficial veins of the upper limb is important in formation of vascular axis for {{c2::haemodialysis}}.
  • Superficial veins of the lower limb become varicose:
    • Primary cases: {{c3::due to lack of support}}
    • Secondary cases: {{c3::due to deep venous thrombosis (DVT) or arteriovenous fistulae}}

The satisfactory transport of blood throughout the body depends on the successful integration of three linked systems (Slide 96)

  1. The pumping action of the heart.
  2. Maintenance of a suitable pressure gradient in the arterial tree to allow forward flow.
  3. Continuous priming of the pump from the venous reservoir to support further perfusion.

(Slide 97)

  • In major injuries it is important to preserve {{c1::brain}} functions through maintaining adequate ventilation (Oxygenation) and adequate blood flow (Haemoglobin and fuel).
  • Under stress adequate blood flow to the brain involves changes in the circulation favouring increase in blood flow to the {{c2::myocardium}} as well as the brain, usually on the expense of less favoured systemic circulation (example the skin) or portal circulation.
  • Importance of oxygen: important for the functions of the mitochondria which leads to formation of ATP (cellular energy source) through formation of metabolic H₂O.

Control of Blood Pressure

Cardiac output — Vascular resistance (Slide 98–99)

Control of blood pressure
  • Control of cardiac output
    • {{c4:: {{c3::Venous return}} → Stroke volume
    • Heart rate}}
  • Control of vascular resistance
    • Vascular resistance at the level of arterioles
    • Neural influences
    • Hormonal influences
    • Blood volume in turn is regulated mainly by {{c5::renal sodium excretion or resorption}}.

Surgical Infection Management (s.5–8)

Present Armamentarium
  • 1) Source control
  • 2) Antimicrobials
  • 3) Organ support in advanced cases to control multiple organ dysfunction
Future Approach (under research)
  • Enhance resistance with infection (conceptual emphasis).
Shared Features in Surgical Infections
  • {{c1::Barrier transgression: breach of skin/GIT/respiratory/urologic epithelium by surgery, trauma, tumour, or ischaemia.}}
  • {{c1::Hollow organ obstruction: e.g., appendicolith, bowel obstruction, CBD stone, ureteral stone}}.
  • Some infections (e.g., subcutaneous abscess, superficial SSI) may resolve with drainage alone (no antibiotics). Others require {{c2::anatomic manipulation + antimicrobials}}.

Source Control in Surgical Infection

  • Definition: {{c1::Physical treatment of a surgical infection = source control}}.

Types of Source Control

  • Surgical procedures:
    • Extensive: {{c2::débridement of necrotizing soft tissue infection}}.
    • Simple: {{c2::removal of sutures from recent incision → skin falls open → surgical site infection (SSI) drains}}.
    • Removal of infected organs/segments:
      • {{c3::Appendectomy}}for appendicitis.
      • Closure of {{c3::perforated ulcer}}.
      • {{c3::Segmental colectomy}} for diverticulitis.
  • Endoscopic / Gastroenterology interventions:
    • Removal of {{c4::common bile duct (CBD) stone}} in cholangitis.
    • Placement of {{c4::stent across ampulla}}.
  • Radiologic interventions:
    • Interventional radiologist inserts {{c5::percutaneous}} drain into a {{c5::subphrenic}} abscess.
  • Prosthetic infections:
    • Usually require removal of {{c6::prosthesis for resolution (definitive source control)}}.

Antimicrobial Therapy

  • Any source control procedure → in most cases requires {{c7::concomitant antimicrobial treatment}}.

Clinical and Pathological Concepts

Core Concepts

  • Contamination = mere presence of micro-organisms in tissues.
  • Infection = manifestation of a disturbed {{c1::host–bacterium equilibrium}} in favor of bacteria within vascularized tissues that show inflammation.
    • Balance = between host resistance factors and bacterial actions:
      • Entry.
      • Growth.
      • Metabolic activities.
      • Pathophysiologic effects.
    • Stable equilibrium → host resistance overcomes contamination.
    • Upset equilibrium → infection may occur due to:
      • {{c2::Impaired host defense mechanisms.
      • Increased bacterial inoculum.
      • Increased bacterial virulence}}.
  • Sepsis:
    • Occurs when infection (bacterial, fungal, mycobacterial, or viral) triggers a {{c3::poorly regulated systemic pro-inflammatory reaction}}.

Definitions (Unified)

  • Contamination:
    • Mere presence of micro-organisms in tissues.
  • Infection:
    • Proliferation of micro-organisms in body tissue.
    • Associated with adverse physiological consequences.
  • Sepsis:
    • Infection triggering systemic, dysregulated pro-inflammatory response.

What is Contamination? (Slides 12–13)

  • Humans and other animals harbor a complex microbiome important for health and disease.
  • Estimated: normal human body harbors 10× more microbial cells than human cells.
  • Commensals coexist with host, occupying niches to prevent pathogens and help prevent infectious disease.
  • When host defenses are breached/attenuated, even “healthy” flora may cause symptomatic infections and can be fatal.
Distribution of Normal Adult Flora (Slide 14)
  • {{c1::Staphylococcus epidermidis}} is the most common coagulase-{{c2::negative}} staphylococcus frequently found on skin. Density of colonization varies greatly with age and site.
  • The most common yeast is {{c3::Candida albicans}}.

How Normal Flora Could Cause Infection (Slide 15)

Infection = proliferation of micro-organisms in body tissue with adverse physiological consequences.
Mechanisms:
  1. Mucosal/skin breaches ⟶ normal flora infect sterile sites.
  2. Overgrowth of potentially pathogenic flora with composition changes, e.g., after {{c1::antimicrobial treatment, local pH changes (vagina, stomach).}}
    • Commensals limit pathogen virulence by {{c2::nutrient deprivation, preventing adherence, and producing signaling substances interfering with pathogen activities}}.
  3. Irradication of commensals by antibiotics ⟶ replacement by {{c3::pathogens (e.g., {{c4::Klebsiella colonize upper respiratory tract ⟶ ventilation-associated pneumonia}}).}}
    • Broad-spectrum antibiotics ⟶ {{c5::Clostridium difficile}} replacement in colon ⟶ potentially life-threatening {{c6::diarrhoea}} in postoperative patients.
  4. Defective immunity (e.g., AIDS, immunosuppressive treatment).

What is Infection? What is Inflammation? (Slides 16–18)

Infection (Slide 17)
  • Manifestation of disturbed host–bacterium equilibrium in favour of bacteria.
  • Defined by presence of microorganisms in host tissue or bloodstream with pathophysiologic consequences.
Inflammation & Role in Sepsis (Slide 18)
  • Inflammation: response of vascularized tissues to infections/damage ⟶ brings defense cells & molecules to needed sites ⟶ eliminate offending agents.
  • Protective and essential for survival; removes initial causes (microbes, toxins) and consequences (necrotic cells/tissues).
  • Mediators: {{c1::phagocytic leukocytes, antibodies, complement proteins; recruited from blood or resident in tissues.}}
  • Process delivers/activates cells & proteins to remove harmful substances.
  • Without inflammation: infections unchecked, wounds never heal, injured tissues become festering sores

Invasion & Inflammatory Response (Slides 19–20)

Invasion of the body by microorganisms results in:
  • {{c1::1) Localized or 2) Spreading infection}}.
Inflammatory response:
  • {{c1::1) Localized inflammation
  • 2) Systemic response}}
Bacterial factors (Slide 20)
  • {{c2::Size of inoculum
  • Virulence
  • Bacterial products:
    • a) Exotoxin
    • b) Endotoxin
    • c) Haemolysin
    • d) Factors favouring spread: streptokinase, elastase, hyaluronidase}}

Bacterial Factors — Inoculum & Virulence (Slides 21–24)

  1. Inoculum size: smaller numbers more easily removed by host immune response.
  2. Virulence:
    • More virulent (e.g., Streptococcus pyogenes vs Escherichia coli in soft tissue) require {{c1::lower inoculum}} to establish infection.
    • {{c2::Pathogenic, noncommensal}} organisms cause most infectious diseases; virulence spans wide range.
    • Highly infectious microbes cause disease in many healthy individuals at {{c3::very low}} doses.
    • Minimally pathogenic microbes require {{c4::large exposures + breaches}} of defenses.
    • Establishment of a “{{c5::beachhead}}” in host initiates inflammatory response.
    • Entry routes: breach epithelial surfaces, inhalation, ingestion, sexual transmission.
    • In healthy persons:
      • Respiratory/GI/GU infections ⟶ virulent organisms able to {{c6::damage/penetrate epidermis/mucosa}}.
      • Skin infections ⟶ mainly less virulent organisms entering via {{c6::superficial injuries}}.
    • In immunocompetent: invasion determined by {{c7::virulence × inoculum}}.
    • In immunocompromised: low-virulence organisms or commensals can cause serious infections.
  3. Bacterial products (Slide 24):
    • a) Exotoxins: act {{c8::locally/regionally/systemically via bloodstream, lymphatics, along nerves (e.g., tetanospasmin ⟶ tetanus)}}.
    • b) Endotoxin (LPS): Gram-{{c9::negative}} cell wall; released on {{c10::lysis}}. Stimulates {{c11::endothelial cells & macrophages ⟶ cytokines ⟶ inflammatory response, septic shock. ({{c14::Lipoteichoic}} acid is Gram-positive equivalent)}}.
    • c) Other factors: haemolysins ({{c12::destroy RBCs}}); streptokinase, elastase, hyaluronidase ({{c12::damage connective tissues}}).

Host Resistance (Slide 25)

Microorganisms are normally prevented from causing infection by:
  1. Mechanical barriers: {{c13::intact epithelial surfaces (notably skin); broken by trauma, surgery, tumours, ischaemia}}.
  2. Other protective mechanisms:
    • a) Chemical: {{c13::low gastric pH}}
    • b) Humoral: {{c13::antibodies, complement, opsonins}}
    • c) Cellular: {{c13::phagocytic cells, macrophages, polymorphonuclear cells, killer lymphocytes}}
  • All natural mechanisms may be compromised by surgical intervention and drugs for other diseases.

Endotoxins

  • Source: Gram-negative bacteria (component of outer membrane, lipopolysaccharide—specifically lipid A).
  • Release: Only when bacteria die or lyse.
  • Potency: Less potent (higher dose needed for toxicity).
  • Heat stability: Stable even after autoclaving.
  • Clinical effects: Fever, shock, hypotension, DIC (via cytokine storm).
  • Specificity: Non-specific (same effects regardless of bacteria).

Exotoxins

  • Source: Mostly Gram-positive (but also some Gram-negative). Proteins, usually enzymes.
  • Release: Secreted actively by live bacteria.
  • Potency: Very potent, small doses lethal.
  • Heat stability: Heat-labile (denatured by heat).
  • Clinical effects: Diverse—neurotoxins, enterotoxins, cytotoxins (e.g., tetanus, diphtheria, cholera).
  • Specificity: Highly specific to target cells/tissues.
👉 Quick memory hook:
  • Exo = Exit (secreted, specific, strong).
  • Endo = End (released at bacterial death, general effects, endotoxin shock).

Local Inflammatory Reaction (Slides 28–31)

Components:
  • Vascular
  • Cellular
  • Chemical mediators
Typical inflammatory reaction — Sequential steps (Slide 29)
  1. {{c1::Offending agent in extravascular tissues recognized by host cells/molecules.
  2. Leukocytes & plasma proteins recruited from circulation to site.
  3. Leukocytes & proteins activated ⟶ destroy/eliminate offending substance.
  4. Reaction controlled/terminated.
  5. Damaged tissue repaired}}.
Figure (Slide 30): Sequence of events in an inflammatory reaction


  • Key takeaway: Tissue-resident cells recognize microbes/damage ⟶ mediators trigger vascular and cellular inflammatory reactions.
Figure (Slide 31): Key events occurring at site of tissue injury

  • Key takeaway: Local mediators orchestrate vascular permeability, leukocyte recruitment, and activation.

Inflammatory Manifestations (Slide 32)

  • Local: rubor, calor, dolor at sites like skin/subcutis.
  • In normal individuals with intact defenses: local signs plus systemic (↑ temperature, ↑ WBC, tachycardia, tachypnea).
  • These systemic manifestations = {{c1::Systemic Inflammatory Response Syndrome (SIRS)}}.

Local and Systemic Inflammation — Acute-Phase Response (Slides 34–35)

  • Local reactions can have systemic manifestations (e.g., {{c2::fever with pharyngitis}}).
  • Systemic Effects of inflammation (acute-phase response) are {{c3::cytokine}}-induced.
  • Mediators: TNF, IL-1, IL-6; type I interferons also contribute.
  • Figure/Section (Slide 35): The acute-phase response consists of several clinical and pathologic changes
    • Fever: cytokines (TNF, IL-1) stimulate production of {{c4::prostaglandins}} in hypothalamus.
    • Production of acute-phase proteins:
      • Includes {{c5::C-reactive}} protein and others.
      • Synthesis stimulated by cytokines (IL-6, others) acting on liver cells.
    • Leukocytosis:
      • Cytokines ({{c6::colony-stimulating factors}}) stimulate production of leukocytes from precursors in bone marrow.
    • Septic shock (in some severe infections):
      • {{c7::
      • Fall in blood pressure.
      • Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC).
      • Metabolic abnormalities.
      • Induced by high levels of TNF and other cytokines
        • }}.

What is Sepsis? (Slides 36–41)

Remember (Slide 37)
  • Sepsis is not synonymous with infection.
Definition of Sepsis (Slide 38)
  1. Sepsis
    a. Occurs when an infection (bacterial, fungal, mycobacterial, or viral) triggers a {{c1::poorly regulated systemic proinflammatory reaction}}.
    b. A {{c2::noninfectious}} process (e.g., {{c3::acute pancreatitis}}) may also trigger a similar dysregulated immune response.
  2. Early sepsis: {{c4::hyper}}immune responses may contribute to {{c5::organ dysfunction, MODS, hypotension, DIC, death}}. Early stage may be {{c6::reversible::Reversible or irreversible}}.
  3. Later sepsis: patients may be {{c7::hypo}}immune. Hypoimmunity may also contribute to infection and death.
Pathophysiology—From Local to Systemic (Slides 39–41)
  • Gram-{{c8::negative}} local infection ⟶ inflammatory cytokines release.
  • A similar response: {{c9::large necrotic tissue volume (e.g., gangrenous leg), worse if infected::Like in when}}.
  • Local paracrine responses are beneficial ({{c10::↑ blood flow, ↑ permeability ⟶ dendritic/macrophage influx; neutrophil activation ⟶ degranulation/oxygen radicals::Why are they beneficial}}).
  • If stimulus is great ⟶ {{c11::dysregulated cascade ⟶ sepsis & MODS}}.
  • Superoxide radicals and other factors damage cells systemically ⟶ {{c12::vasodilatation, ↑ vascular permeability ⟶ hypotension, circulatory collapse}}.
  • Myocardial {{c13::depression}} and {{c13::disrupted}} cellular metabolic functions occur.
Triggers of Dysregulated Response (Slide 41)
  • Pancreatitis, polytrauma, malignancy, transfusion reaction, infection.
  • Sepsis (SIRS caused by infection) mediated by proinflammatory cascade in response to microbial products:
    • a) {{c14::LPS (endotoxin) ⟶ Gram-negative}}
    • b) {{c14::Peptidoglycans, teichoic acids ⟶ Gram-positive}}
    • c) {{c14::Mannan (yeast, fungi), many other cell wall components}}
Figure (Slide 41): Relationship between infection and SIRS
  • Key takeaway: Sepsis = {{c15::intersection of infection and SIRS}}; SIRS can arise without infection ({{c16::trauma, aspiration, etc}}.). Severe sepsis & septic shock are subsets of infections.

Multiple Organ Dysfunction & SIRS (Slides 42–45)

MODS/MOF (Slide 42)
  • Recognized since mid-1970s: major physiological insult ⟶ organ failure remote from initial disease.
  • Underlying condition: unrestrained SIRS, initiated by:
    • {{c2::a) Trauma
    • b) Infection
    • c) Inflammation
    • d) Ischaemia or ischaemia–reperfusion injury}}
  • Most common reason for surgical patients to stay {{c1::>5}} days in ICU.
Clinical Conditions Leading to SIRS & MODS (Slide 43)
  • Include {{c4::infection and endotoxaemia (Gram-{{c3::negative}} sepsis)}} in 50–70% of cases, retained necrotic tissue, shock.
  • Mechanisms initiating distant organ failure:
    • {{c5::a) Excessive endogenous cytokine release
    • b) Disrupted oxygen delivery to tissues
    • c) Impaired intestinal barrier ⟶ translocation of intestinal bacteria/endotoxin to portal/systemic circulation
    • d) Damage to reticulo-endothelial system}}
  • Acute pancreatitis organ failure = {{c6::combination}} of these factors.
Sequence of Organ Failures (Slide 44)
  • {{c7::Pulmonary first (ARDS; ↑ permeability ⟶ non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema)
  • Hepatic: ↑ bilirubin, SGOT, LDH; may result from {{c8::hypotension}}
  • Intestinal: stress bleeding (requires transfusion); ischaemic bowel permeable to bacteria ⟶ translocation ⟶ further septic insult
  • Renal: ↑ plasma creatinine, low urine output; Acute kidney injury due to:
    • (1) Hypotension
    • (2) Renal vasoconstriction
    • (3) ↑ TNF
  • Cardiac: ↑ work ⟶ ischaemia, failure in end-stage shock (low CO, hypotension)
  • CNS: Altered mental states (confusion) due to poor perfusion
  • Haematological: DIC — clotting cascade activation, microclots, bleeding as factors consumed; ↓ protein C among mediators}}.
MODS Mortality (Slide 45)
  • Mortality related to number of failing organs:
    • 1 organ: {{c9::40}}%
    • 2 organs: {{c9::60}}%
    • ≥3 organs: >{{c9::90}}%

Why Hypotension? (Slide 46)

Mechanisms:
a) {{c1::Vasodilation (↓ SVR)}} mediated by ↑ NO, ↑ prostacyclin, low vasopressin ⟶ ↓ BP.
b) Cardiac output (CO) can be ↑ or ↓ in sepsis.
  • {{c2::(1) ↓ SVR ⟶ ↓ afterload ⟶ often ↑ CO
  • (2) Capillary leak ⟶ ↓ venous return ⟶ ↓ CO
  • (3) Myocardial depression ⟶ ↓ CO::What are causes of decreased and increased CO}}
  • Typical initial response: {{c3::↓ SVR and ↑ CO (especially after fluid resuscitation)}}.

Classical Spectrum of Sepsis — Sepsis-1 (1991) (Slides 47–48)

  • Sepsis = SIRS + infection.
  • Clinical spectrum:
    • Sepsis ({{c1::SIRS + infection}})
    • Severe sepsis ({{c1::organ dysfunction}})
    • Septic shock ({{c1::hypotension requiring vasopressors}})
  • Outcomes improved with organized therapy: rapid resuscitation, antibiotics, source control.
Figure (Slide 48): Clinical spectrum of infection and SIRS (Sepsis-1, 1991)
  • Key takeaway: Progression from sepsis to severe sepsis to septic shock.

Sepsis-3 (2016) Updates (Slides 49–55)

  • New definition: Sepsis = {{c2::life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection}}.
  • Abandoned SIRS criteria for identification; eliminated the term severe sepsis.
  • Eliminated concept of sepsis without organ dysfunction; redefined clinical criteria for genuine sepsis and septic shock. Aim: improve epidemiology, hospital coding, outcomes.
Scoring Systems (Slides 50–55)
  • SOFA: predicts {{c3::mortality}} risk in ICU based on lab/clinical data.
  • qSOFA: bedside quick SOFA to identify patients likely to have poor outcomes from infection ⟶ {{c4::trigger further workup/treatment for sepsis}}.
    • Limitation: {{c5::poor sensitivity ⟶ likely excludes its use as early sepsis screening (when treatment is most effective)}}
Criteria for Assessment (Slide 52)
SIRS (Sepsis-1) — 2 of these criteria:
  • {{c6::Tachypnoea (> 20) or PaCO₂ < 4.25 KPa (32 mmHg) (hyperventilation to combat metabolic acidosis)
  • Tachycardia (> 90)
  • Fever or hypothermia (> 38 or < 36)
  • Leukocytosis (> 12,000/mm³) / Leukopenia (< 3,000/mm³) / Bandaemia (> 10%)}}
qSOFA (Sepsis-3) — 2 of 3 criteria:
  • {{c7::Respiratory rate > 22/min — 1
  • Altered mental status — 1
  • Systolic blood pressure < 100 mm Hg — 1}}
If qSOFA score is 2 — {{c8::start sepsis care pathway}} (Slide 53)
    • {{c16::Oxygen:
      • High flow 15 L/min via non-rebreather mask.
      • Target saturations: >94%.
    • Blood cultures:
      • Take at least one set.
      • Include all relevant blood tests: FBC, U&E, LFT, clotting, glucose.
      • Consider urine/sputum/swab samples.
    • IV antibiotics:
      • As per hospital guidelines.
    • Fluid resuscitation (if hypotensive):
      • Give boluses of 0.9% saline or Hartmann’s.
      • 20 ml/kg up to max of 60 ml/kg.
    • Serum lactate and haemoglobin (Hb):
      • Ensure Hb >7 g/dl.
    • Catheterization:
      • Commence fluid balance chart::What are the 6 Qsofa treatment steps}}.
PLUS
  • a) Call Outreach Team if appropriate.
  • b) Discuss with Consultant.

Organ system SOFA 0 SOFA 1 SOFA 2 SOFA 3 SOFA 4
Respiratory (PaO₂/FiO₂, mmHg (kPa)) {{c10::≥ 400 (53.3)}} {{c10::< 400 (53.3)}} {{c10::< 300 (40)}} {{c10::< 200 (26.7) with respiratory support}} {{c10::< 100 (13.3) with respiratory support}}
Coagulation (Platelets/mm³) {{c11::≥ 150,000}} {{c11::< 150,000}} {{c11::< 100,000}} {{c11::< 50,000}} {{c11::< 20,000}}
Liver (Bilirubin mg/dL) {{c11::< 1.2}} {{c11::1.2–1.9}} {{c11::2.0–5.9}} {{c11::6.0–11.9}} {{c11::> 12.0}}
Cardiovascular {{c12::MAP ≥ 70 mmHg}} {{c12::MAP < 70 mmHg}} {{c12::Dopamine < 5 or Dobutamine (any dose)ᵇ}} {{c12::Dopamine 5.1–15 or epinephrine ≤ 0.1 or norepinephrine ≤ 0.1ᵇ}} {{c12::Dopamine > 15 or epinephrine > 0.1 or norepinephrine > 0.1ᵇ}}
CNS (Glasgow coma scale) {{c12::15}} {{c12::13–14}} {{c12::10–12}} {{c12::6–9}} {{c12::< 6}}
Renal (Creatinine mg/dL / Urine output mL/d) {{c12::< 1.2}} {{c12::1.2–1.9}} {{c12::2.0–3.4}} {{c12::3.5–4.9 / < 500}} {{c12::> 5.0 / < 200}}

  • A score of 2 or more is associated with a {{c9::10}}% or greater increase in mortality.
Septic Shock (Slide 55)
  • Redefined as {{c13::sepsis that requires vasopressors to keep MAP > 65 mm Hg (despite adequate fluid resuscitation) and lactate > 2.0 mmol/L}}.
How inflammatory mediators lead to septic shock:
  • {{c14::↑ metabolic needs, massive vasodilation.
  • ↓ SVR ⟶ hypotension despite ↑ CO}}.
  • Clinical appearance: {{c15::unwell, hypotensive, flushed cheeks, warm peripheries, bounding pulses}}.

Lactic Acidosis (Slide 56)

  • Common in sepsis; causes:
    a) {{c1::Microcirculatory lesions ⟶ impaired oxygen delivery
    • {{c2::1) Dysregulation of supply vs demand
    • (2) Microvascular occlusion::Why}}
  • b) Hypotension ⟶ impaired oxygen delivery
    c) Mitochondrial injury ⟶ impaired oxygen utilization
    d) Decreased hepatic clearance of lactate}}

Incidence of Sepsis (Slide 57)

  • Sepsis (with organ dysfunction) ≈ 750,000/year in the United States; ~30% mortality.
  • Increasing incidence, likely due to:
    1. More complex surgeries in older patients with multiple comorbidity
    2. Use of complex invasive procedures
    3. Efficient resuscitation/ICU care ⟶ emergence of new problems (e.g., MOF)
    4. Emergence of more virulent organisms due to inappropriate antibiotic use
    5. Potent immunosuppressive agents (transplantation)
    6. AIDS prevalence with acquired immunosuppression

Host–Pathogen Interaction — Outcomes (Slides 58–63)

Possible outcomes after microbial invasion & host interaction (Slide 59):
{{c1::(a) Eradication
(b) Containment ⟶ purulence (hallmark of chronic infections, e.g., furuncle, abscess in organ/parenchyma/potential space)
(c) Locoregional infection (cellulitis, lymphangitis, aggressive soft tissue infection) with/without distant spread (metastatic abscess)
(d) Systemic infection (bacteremia or fungemia)}}
  • Systemic infection = failure of local defenses; significant morbidity/mortality.
  • Disease progression may combine serious locoregional + concurrent systemic infection.
  • Chronic abscess may intermittently drain and/or be associated with bacteremia.
Primary goal of surgical therapy (Slide 60):
  1. {{c2::Reduce bacterial concentration (incision & drainage)}}
  2. {{c2::Improve local environment (debridement)
    ⟶ Shift balance away from invasion; reestablish opportunity for host defenses to eradicate microorganisms}}
Outcome schema (Slide 61–62)

Local
  • Eradication ⟶ Resolution
  • Containment ⟶ {{c3::Suppuration}}
  • Organization and healing by fibrosis; Chronicity
Spread
  • Regional:
    1. {{c4::Cellulitis
    2. Lymphangitis & Lymphadenitis
    3. Thrombophlebitis}}
  • Distant:
    1. {{c4::Bacteraemic
    2. Toxaemia
    3. Septicaemia
    4. Pyaemia and metastatic abscesses}}
Acute inflammation and its sequelae (Slide 63):
  • Resolution / Suppuration / Organization & repair
    • Exudation:
      • Resolution: {{c5::Regression}}
      • Suppuration: {{c5::Excessive exudation of neutrophils}}
      • Organization/repair: {{c5::N/A}}
    • Tissue destruction:
      • Resolution: No evidence
      • Suppuration:
        • If localized: due to {{c6::cystic action of enzymes released from leucocytes = abscess}}
        • If spreading: due to {{c6::bacterial products, either cellulitis or necrotizing soft tissue infection (associated with sloughs)}}
      • Organization/repair:
        • Following destruction of tissue by inflammation, {{c7::phagocytosis}} removes damaged cells, granulation tissue grows.
        • Integrity can be completely restored (e.g., {{c8::ulcerated mucosal lesion}}) or area can become {{c8::fibrosed/scarred}}.

Natural History — Interaction of Invasion & Tissue Reaction (Slides 64–66)

  • Causes of local tissue death:
    • {{c1::a) Bacterial exotoxin (e.g., Cl. perfringens)
    • b) Increased tissue tension (tissue oedema in limited space or vein thrombosis)}}
  • Forms of LTD: {{c1::Gangrene, Abscess, Sequestrum, Sloughs}}
Routes of entry & dissemination of microbes (Slide 65)
  • Entry by {{c2::penetrating epithelial/mucosal}} barriers. Infection may remain {{c2::localized or spread}}.
  • Most microbes spread via {{c3::lymphatics or bloodstream (free or within inflammatory cells)}}.
  • Some viruses and bacterial toxins may travel through nerves.

  • Key takeaway: Multiple anatomic routes enable local-to-systemic progression.
Definitions (Slide 66–67)
  • Toxaemia: {{c4::circulation of bacterial toxins (endo/exo) in blood causing pathological & clinical manifestations}}.
  • Bacteraemia: {{c4::transient circulation of small number of bacteria without toxic manifestations}}.
  • Septicaemia: {{c4::circulation & multiplication of large number of virulent bacteria & their toxins in blood stream}}.
  • Pyaemia: {{c4::circulation of septic emboli in the blood & their arrest in different organs ⟶ metastatic abscesses}}.
  • Septic shock: {{c4::clinical syndrome due to acute haemodynamic disturbance leading to generalized dysfunction of normal cardiovascular homeostatic mechanisms (relation between blood volume & its bed)}}.
  • Difference between bacteraemia and septicaemia: Both have viable bacteria in blood; {{c5::septicaemia is associated with a systemic response}}.

Antibiotics & Infections (Slides 69–74)

Principles (Slide 70)
  • Effective antibiotics tip the balance towards host defense victory vs invading micro-organism.
  • Once local tissue death occurs, antibiotics are not effective; {{c1::removal of dead tissue by drainage/debridement}} is the principle line of treatment.
  • Resistance can be acquired by previously sensitive bacteria via {{c2::plasmid}} transfer.
Broad Categories of Antibiotics — Mechanism of Action (Slide 71)
  • {{c3::Cell Wall Active Agents
  • Protein Synthesis Inhibitors
  • Disrupt Nucleic Acids}}
Cell Wall Active Agents (Slide 72)
  • β-Lactam Antibiotics: {{c4::penicillins, cephalosporins, monobactams, carbapenems}}.
  • Several agents combined with {{c5::β-lactamase}} inhibitors ⟶ broaden spectrum.
  • Subgroups: several generations of {{c6::cephalosporins, penicillinase-resistant penicillins}}.
Protein Synthesis Inhibitors (Slide 72)
  • Classes bind {{c7::bacterial ribosomes}}, inhibit {{c7::protein synthesis}}; divergent spectra/structures but mechanistically linked.
  • Examples: {{c8::Aminoglycosides, Tetracyclines, Linezolid, Clindamycin}}.
Drugs that Disrupt Nucleic Acids (Slide 73)
  • {{c8::
  • Fluoroquinolones
  • Cytotoxic Antibiotics: Metronidazole
  • Trimethoprim–Sulfamethoxazole
    • }}

Classifications of Surgical Infections (Slides 75–79)

Practical categorization (Slide 76)
  1. {{c1::Community-acquired infections
  2. Nosocomial (hospital)-acquired infections:
    • (a) Common postoperative infections
    • (b) Infections in critically ill patients (ICUs)}}
Distinction importance:
  • (a) Varying spectrum of infectious disease
  • (b) Frequent immune deficiency in hospital patients
  • (c) Higher prevalence of antibiotic-resistant organisms in hospital-acquired infections
Established Infection (Slide 77)
  • Community acquired: active inflammatory processes present before admission; often the reason for treatment (e.g., {{c2::peritonitis due to perforated colonic diverticulum}})
  • Hospital acquired / nosocomial: occur as a consequence or during treatment (operations in hospital) (e.g., {{c2::intraperitoneal abscess from colonic anastomotic leak}})
Remember (Slide 78)
  • Some nosocomial SSI manifest after leaving the hospital: {{c3::~1 }}week for skin infections, maybe {{c3::2}} weeks for postoperative intraabdominal abscesses.
  • Around {{c4::70}}% of postoperative infections present in the community after discharge.
Routes of Microbial Infection (Slide 79–80)
  • (Slide 79 title; paired with detailed route diagram earlier — see Slide 65.)

Community-Acquired vs Nosocomial — Examples by System (Slide 80)

Community-acquired infections
  • Skin & soft tissue:
    1. {{c1::Complicated skin and soft tissue infections
    2. Non-necrotizing cellulitis
    3. Necrotizing soft tissue infection
    4. Diabetic foot infection}}
  • GIT:
    1. {{c1::Toxigenic (cholera, staph food poisoning)
    2. Dysenteric infections
    3. Secondary peritonitis (appendix, diverticulum, perforated peptic ulcer)
    4. Pseudomembranous colitis (prolonged antibiotics)}}
  • Respiratory: {{c1::Pneumonia}}
  • Genitourinary: {{c1::Venereal infections}}
Nosocomial infections
  • Skin & soft tissue:
    1. {{c2::SSI (superficial, deep, or organ specific)
    2. Needle pricks
    3. Intravenous line infections}}
  • GIT:
    1. {{c2::Postoperative leakage
    2. Pseudomembranous colitis (prolonged antibiotics)}}
  • Respiratory: {{c2::Ventilation-associated pneumonia}}
  • Genitourinary: {{c2::Infection due to prolonged catheterization}}

Community-Acquired Infections — Surgical Focus (Slides 81–83)

  • Range:
    1. Primarily medical treatment is needed initially (e.g., respiratory infections, meningitis, AIDS, viral hepatitis, tropical infections)
    2. Surgical (e.g., abscesses, appendicitis, peritonitis)
  • Surgical community-acquired infections typically require {{c1::emergency surgical treatment}}.

Skin Infections (Slides 84–91)

Basic anatomy — three main layers of skin (Slide 87)
  1. Epidermis: four or five layers of squamous epithelium.
  2. Dermis: connective tissue.
    • Papillary dermis (upper; between rete ridges)
    • Reticular dermis (lower)
    • May contain hair follicles, sweat & sebaceous glands
    • Contains blood vessels and nerves
  3. {{c1::Subcutis}}: fat and connective tissue supporting upper layers and linking to muscles/bones
Impetigo (Slide 88)
  • Superficial skin infection by Gram {{c2::positive}} bacteria, commonly {{c3::Staphylococcus aureus}}.
  • {{c4::Large epidermal}}blisters: filled with clear fluid or neutrophils ⟶ {{c5::pustule}}.
  • Pustule ruptures ⟶ {{c6::yellow}} crust; heals {{c6::without::With or without}} scarring.
  • Crust/scab: {{c7::coagulated plasma, cell debris, extravasated blood cells::What does it contain}}.
Staphylococcal Scalded Skin Syndrome (Slide 89)
  • Some staphylococci produce potent toxin ⟶ extensive disruption of {{c8::epidermis, widespread confluent blistering, separation of upper epidermal layers with little inflammation histologically}}.
  • Original infection may be {{c9::occult}} or in {{c9::upper respiratory tract}} or {{c9::middle ear}}.
  • Prognosis: {{c10::relatively good}} in infants; rare adult cases carry {{c10::considerable mortality}}.
Folliculitis (Slide 90)
  • Bacterial infection of {{c11::hair follicles with pus around hair shaft}}.
  • Most often S{{c12::. aureus}}; involves buttocks & thighs, bearded face, scalp.
Furuncles/Boils (Slide 91)
  • {{c13::Superficial folliculitis with suppuration extending into parafollicular soft tissue ⟶ abscesses = furuncles/boils::What causes it}}.
  • Confluent boils ⟶ {{c14::carbuncles}}; common in {{c15::poorly controlled diabetes}}.
Figures: Community-Acquired Infections (Slides 86, 93, 95, 96)
  • Carbuncle (Slide 93) — Key takeaway: Confluent furuncles forming indurated mass; diabetes predisposes.

  • Infected sebaceous cyst (Slide 95) — Key takeaway: Secondary infection of a {{c16::blocked sebaceous unit}}.

  • Hidradenitis suppurativa (Slide 96) — Key takeaway: {{c17::Chronic, relapsing apocrine gland disease with abscesses/sinus tracts}}.

Acne (Slide 94)
  • Acne vulgaris = disorder of {{c18::pilosebaceous}} units.
  • Multifactorial:
    • a) Overproduction of {{c19::sebum (androgen-driven; more prominent in men)}}
    • b) Obstruction of {{c19::pilosebaceous canal by keratin}}
    • c) Distension of canal by {{c19::sebum & keratin}}
    • d) {{c19::Anaerobic}} bacteria proliferation ({{c20::Propionibacterium}} acnes)
    • e) Secondary infection by {{c19::pus-forming bacteria ⟶ folliculitis & abscess}}
Cellulitis (Slide 97)
  • Diffusely spreading infection of dermis & soft tissues; most often {{c21::Streptococcus pyogenes}}.
  • Group {{c22::A β-haemolytic streptococcus}} secretes {{c23::lytic}} enzymes (streptokinase, DNAase, hyaluronidase) ⟶ facilitated spread through tissues.
Erysipelas (Slide 98)
  • {{c24::Superficial}} cellulitis with prominent {{c24::lymphatic }}involvement; usually {{c25::group A β-haemolytic streptococci::Which bacteria}}.
  • Most often in {{c26::face, arms, legs}}.
  • {{c27::Redness & swelling + regional lymphadenitis + systemic symptoms (high fever, chills)::What does it look like clinically}}
SC Spreading Infections (Slide 99)
  • {{c28::Non-necrotizing:
    • Cellulitis
    • Erysipelas (Cellulitis + inflammation of dermal lymphatics)
  • Necrotizing::What are the types}}
Figures (Slides 100–101)
  • {{c29::Streptococcal cellulitis of the leg following minor puncture wound — Key takeaway: Rapid spread along tissue planes via enzymatic factors::Identify the picture below}}.

  • {{c30::Necrotizing soft tissue infection (late necrotizing fasciitis & myositis due to β-haemolytic streptococcal infection) with hypotension — Key takeaway: Fulminant NSTI can cause septic shock; requires urgent debridement + antibiotics::Identify the picture below}}.

Staphylococcal Infections (Gram Positive Cocci) (Slides 102–108)

Clinical spectrum (Slide 103)
  • S. aureus: {{c2::boils, carbuncles, impetigo, scalded-skin syndrome, abscesses, sepsis, osteomyelitis, pneumonia, endocarditis, food poisoning, toxic shock syndrome::What does it cause}}.
  • S. aureus: pyogenic {{c1::Gram}}-positive {{c1::cocci}} in {{c1::grape}}-like clusters.
  • {{c6::Coagulase-negative staphylococci (e.g., S. epidermidis)}}: {{c3::opportunistic}} infections in {{c3::catheterized patients, prosthetic valves, drug addicts}}.
    {{c5::S. saprophyticus}}: common {{c4::UTIs in young women}}.
Pathogenesis (Slide 103–104)
  • Virulence factors:
    • {{c7::i) Surface proteins (adherence, immune evasion)
    • ii) Secreted enzymes (degrade host structures)
    • iii) Secreted toxins (damage host cells)
    • iv) Antibiotic resistance proteins}}
  • Adherence:
    • {{c8::Clumping factor (fibrinogen receptor), fibronectin, vitronectin}}⟶ bind host endothelial cells.
    • {{c8::Polysaccharide capsule in prosthetic infections}}⟶ attach to artificial materials, resist phagocytosis.
    • {{c8::Protein A binds Fc portion of immunoglobulins}}⟶ escape antibody-mediated killing.
Figure (Slide 105): The many consequences of staphylococcal infection
  • Key takeaway: Toxins/enzymes and immune evasion mechanisms confer broad tissue tropism and clinical syndromes.

Toxins (Slide 106)
  • Membrane-damaging (haemolytic) toxins:
    • {{c9::α}}-toxin: {{c10::pore-forming ⟶ Ca²⁺ influx, cell death}}
    • {{c9::β}}-toxin: {{c10::sphingomyelinase}}
    • {{c9::δ}}-toxin: {{c10::detergent-like peptide}}
    • {{c9::γ}}-toxin & {{c9::leucocidin}}: {{c10::lyse RBCs and phagocytes}}
  • {{c11::Exfoliative A/B}} toxins: {{c12::serine proteases cleave desmoglein-1 ⟶ keratinocyte detachment, barrier loss ⟶ secondary infections}}
    • {{c13::Bullous impetigo}}(local exfolliative A/B infection) or {{c13::staphylococcal scalded-}}skin syndrome (widespread superficial epidermal loss)
Superantigens & Clinical Syndromes from staph (Slide 107)
  • Food poisoning, Toxic Shock Syndrome (TSS)
  • TSS associated historically with {{c14::hyperabsorbent tampons}}; can occur with {{c14::vaginal or infected surgical sites}}.
  • Clinical: {{c15::hypotension (shock), renal failure, coagulopathy, liver disease, respiratory distress, generalized erythematous rash, soft tissue necrosis; fatal if untreated}}.
  • Also caused by {{c16::Streptococcus pyogenes}}.
  • Mechanism: bacterial {{c17::superantigens bind MHC and TCR β chains (conserved regions) ⟶ stimulate up to 20% of T lymphocytes ⟶ massive TNF, IL-1 release ⟶ SIRS}}.
  • Superantigens also cause {{c18::vomiting}} (CNS/enteric nervous system effect).
Antibiotic Resistance (Slide 108)
  • MRSA: resistant to {{c19::nearly all penicillins & cephalosporins}}.
  • Once mainly healthcare-associated, now community-acquired MRSA is common in many areas.
  • Empirical treatment with penicillins/cephalosporins is less likely effective.
  • Treat with {{c20::Flucloxacillin and vancomycin}}

Streptococcal Infections (Gram Positive Cocci) — Diseases & Sensitivity (Slides 109–110)

Diseases Antibiotic sensitivity
Streptococcus pyogenus — {{c1::Upper airway; Wound infection: a) cellulitis b) lymphagitis c) Erysipelas d) NSTI}} {{c2::All strains sensitive to penicillin. Patients sensitive to penicillin: erythromycin, but some strains resistant}}.
Streptococcus faecalis — {{c1::Urinary tract infections; Acute cholecystitis; Wound infection}} {{c3::Enterococci sensitive to ampicillin; moderately resistant to penicillin; resistant to cephalosporin}}.
Streptococcus viridans — {{c1::Commensals; could cause endocarditis}} {{c4::Sensitive to penicillin }}
Streptococcus pneumonia (when encapsulated becomes virulent) — {{c1::Lower respiratory infections; Conjunctivitis; Sinusitis; Meningitis; Septicaemia (especially in splenectomized patients)}} {{c4::All strains sensitive to penicillin and erythromycin}}

Tetanus (Gram Positive Spore Forming Bacilli) (Slides 111–118)

Etiology & Pathogenesis (Slides 111–112)
  • Acute, often fatal disease due to {{c1::exotoxin by Clostridium tetani}} entering via {{c1::wound}}.
  • Incubation: mean {{c2::7–8 days (range 3–21).}}
  • In {{c3::Anaerobic}} conditions ⟶ spores germinate. Gram-{{c4::positive}} {{c4::anaerobic}} {{c4::rod}}.
  • Produces {{c5::tetanolysin and tetanospasmin}}.
    • Tetanospasmin = {{c6::neurotoxin}} causing clinical manifestations.
    • Acts at {{c7::peripheral motor end plates, spinal cord, sympathetic nervous system}}.
    • Blocks inhibitory neurotransmitter release ⟶ {{c8::unopposed muscle contraction & spasm}}.
Clinical (Slide 113)
  • {{c9::Generalized rigidity & convulsive spasms}} of skeletal muscles.
  • Jaw ({{c9::lockjaw}}) & neck ({{c9::trismus}}) initially, then generalized.
  • Back spasms may be so intense ⟶ {{c10::spontaneous vertebral fractures}}.
  • {{c11::Risus sardonicus}}(intense facial spasms).
  • {{c11::Laryngospasm/respiratory }}muscle spasm ⟶ breathing interference.
  • {{c12::No characteristic lab findings; culture is not helpful. Diagnosis is clinical::What abt lab findings?}}.
Treatment (Slide 114)
  • {{c13::Human tetanus immunoglobulin (TIG), airway protection (early tracheostomy), IV magnesium (spasm prevention), high calorie replenishment, benzodiazepines::What is the main line of treatment}}.
  • {{c14::No}}immunity after disease (toxin is extremely potent).
  • TIG recommended for persons with tetanus.
  • Active immunization with tetanus toxoid should begin/continue once stabilized.
  • Not transmissible person-to-person. Only vaccine-preventable disease that is {{c15::infectious but not contagious}}.
Tetanus Prophylaxis & Epidemiology (Slides 115–118)
  • Consider tetanus (Clostridium tetani) immunity in any {{c16::laceration}} in ED.
  • Rare in developed countries; major cause of mortality in developing communities.
  • Very low UK rates due to effective immunization and hygiene.
  • Aging populations with waning resistance due to historic immunizations.
Tetanus-prone wounds (including burns) (Slide 116):
  1. Sustained {{c17::> 6}} hours before surgical treatment
  2. Significant {{c17::devitalised tissue}}
  3. {{c18::Puncture}} injury
  4. Contact with {{c18::soil/manure}}
  5. Associated clinical {{c18::sepsis, foreign body, or compound fracture}}
Management by Immunization Status (Slides 117–118)
  1. Fully immunized/up-to-date ({{c19::5}} doses):
    • No further vaccine recommended (vaccine given at injury may not boost immunity within incubation).
    • If especially high risk (e.g., {{c20::stable manure contamination}}) ⟶ {{c21::human tetanus immunoglobulin}} for immediate protection.
  2. Not up-to-date/unknown:
    • Give reinforcing dose of {{c22::Td/IPV (if no contraindications); complete five-dose schedule as required}}.
    • {{c22::Immunoglobulin}}should be given for any tetanus-prone wound.
TIG dosing (Slide 118)
  • Preventative dose: {{c23::250 IU IM}} in most cases.
  • If > 24 hours since injury or heavy contamination or burns ⟶ {{c24::500 IU}}.
  • For other wounds with not up-to-date immunization ⟶ reinforcing {{c25::Td/IPV (or start full course).}}

Nosocomial Infections & Postoperative Surgical Infections (Slides 119–125)

Postoperative surgical infections (Slide 120)
  • Categories:
    1. {{c1::Systemic: postoperative chest and urinary infections
    2. Surgical site: infection at the operative site (usually abscess formation)
    3. Wound infection}}
  • Around 70% of postoperative infections present {{c2::after::After or before}} discharge

Medical Conditions Increasing Risk of Postoperative Infection (Slides 121–122)

  1. Patient factors:
    {{c3::a. Extremes of age
    b. Metabolic: malnutrition (including obesity), diabetes, hypocholesterolemia
    Organ dysfunction: uraemia, jaundice
    Chronic inflammation (e.g., IBD, psoriasis)
    ICU patients with bacterial translocation
    c. Disseminated disease (causing immunodeficiency): cancer, AIDS
    d. Iatrogenic immunosuppression: radiotherapy, chemotherapy, steroids}}
  2. Circumstantial:
    {{c3::a. Preoperative:
    • Coexisting infection remote to surgical site
    • Recent operation, especially chest or abdomen (second hit)
      b. Circumoperative:
    • Hypothermia
    • Hypoxemia}}
Figure (Slide 123): Gut failure, colonisation and translocation related to MODS and SIRS (IL, TNF)

  • Key takeaway: Gut barrier failure (loss of gut associated lymphoid tissue) ⟶ translocation ( ie bacteria grow and move around)⟶ (immune system flares up) SIRS/MODS amplification via cytokines.
Healthcare-Associated Infection (HAI) — 4 Main Groups (Slide 124)
  • {{c4::Respiratory infections (including ventilator-associated pneumonia)
  • Urinary tract infections (mostly related to urinary catheters)
  • Bacteraemia (mostly related to indwelling vascular catheters)
  • SSI (surgical site infection)}}

Components of Adequate Nutrition (slide 11)

  • {{c1::Proteins
  • Water
  • Energy
  • Electrolytes: Sodium (Na⁺), Potassium (K⁺)
  • Minerals: Calcium, Phosphorus, Magnesium; Trace elements: Copper, Iron, Selenium, Zinc
  • Vitamins: Water- and fat-soluble
    Requirements vary with patient and clinical condition.::What are the 6 main categories}}
entral route is thru the mouth, paraentral is veins, {{c2::entral::Which route}} is safer and better cuz the stomach helps, paraentral is IV fluids so comlpications arise, ie in a patient of shock (or any instability), i cant add the hypoerosmolar heavy calorie liquid and dilute the blood

Type Pathophysiology Manifestations Aetiology Characteristics
Marasmus (children); in adults protein-energy malnutrition (PEM) {{c1::Inadequate intake of an otherwise balanced diet; Impaired delivery}}
 {{c5::Loss of weight; erosion of muscle and fat stores}}

Cachexia {{c3::Interplay of altered metabolism and inflammation}}

Non-malignant GI disorders (benign obstruction, malabsorption, benign fistulae) {{c1::PEM due to inadequate intake, inadequate absorption, loss of fluids/electrolytes (external or third space)}}
 {{c5::Acute inflammation in phlegmons → mediators → worsen PEM and fluid/electrolyte deficit}}
